courts to discuss, deliberate and resolve questions of doctrine. We affirm that New
Jersey Presbytery, as a court of the church, acted within its constitutional bounds to
resolve this question of doctrine and that it did so consistent with past renderings of the
General Assembly. Therefore, the complaint is denied.
We affirm that New Jersey Presbytery, as a court of the church, acted within its
constitutional bounds to resolve this question of doctrine. Therefore, the complaint is
denied
The original proposed decision was written by the panel: RE Harrison
Brown, RE Eugene Friedline, and TE John McArthur. The Summary of the Facts
and the Reasoning and Opinion were done for the SJC by TE Dominic Aquila, TE
Paul Fowler and TE James Smith.

V. Vote of Standing Judicial Commission
Approved the decision: 12 Concurring, 9 Dissenting, 2 Recused, 1 Absent.

<a id="ga26-p108"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=110 printed_page=108 -->

NOTE: Pursuant to BCO 15-5(c) and SJC Manual 20.4, the Stated Clerk
received written notice from 9 members of the SJC who plan to submit a
minority report. Consequently, this case will be referred to the 26th General Assembly in St. Louis, which will make the final and definitive decision.
CONCURRING OPINION
CASE 97-5
1 We, the undersigned, respectfully submit this concurring judgment in support of
the majority decision in this case.
2. We support the Standing Judicial Commission’s judgment:
A. that the Westminster Confession of Faith, when it refers to “in the space
of six days” (WCF IV, 1) adopted substantially the language of the
English translation of the Bible, and
B. that the use of such scriptural language permits officers in the PCA
1 to interpret Genesis 1-3 as meaning that the world was created in
six consecutive 24 hour days, or
2. to hold other interpretations which affirm that God created the
world.
While there may be more than one way to interpret or understand the words, “in
the space of six days,” (which are not to be understood as exceptions to the
Confessional standards of the PCA), any purely naturalist evolutionary
interpretation is not compatible with our Confessional standards.
3. In support of the majority opinion we offer the following:
A. The issue in this case is whether there is only one acceptable
interpretation of the WCF phrase, “in the space of six days.”
1 The minority opinion holds that there is only one acceptable
view in the PCA - the “24 hour day” view.
2. This concurring opinion affirms that the “24 hour day” view,
while very plausible (indeed, a number of the undersigned hold
strongly to this view), is not the only possible faithful
interpretation.
B. The issue in this case is not about whether the Genesis account is
entirely true, factual and historical. It is! No one is questioning these
fundamentals:
1 that God created all things, by the word of his power, out of
nothing, and all very good,
2. that God created man male and female in his own image, endued
with righteousness and holiness, yet capable of sin,
3. that God created Adam and Eve without intermediaries, from the
dust of the earth, breathing life into them; mankind is not a
product of evolution, and
4. that Scripture does not err. What we read in Genesis 1 and 2 is
historically accurate and true to fact.

<a id="ga26-p109"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=111 printed_page=109 -->

While our interpretations may err, Scripture does not err. Everyone is seeking to
be faithful to the text of Scripture, and desires not to misinterpret it.
C the Westminster Confession of Faith itself acknowledges that “all
things in Scripture are not alike plain in themselves, nor alike clear to
all” (WCF, 1:7). This is one of those places. While we may argue
strongly for our view of “24 hour days,” we have to admit that the text is
not as clear as we would desire. There are questions raised in the text
itself that have led some of our brothers to hold differing views as to the
time element. For example:
1. Why do the sun and moon not appear until the fourth day9
Hence, how could there be a ‘literal’ evening and morning during
the first three days? Also, Genesis 1:14 reveals that the heavenly
bodies were made on the fourth day in order to measure days and
seasons and years.
2. Are Genesis 1:12 and 2:9 presenting us with instances of plants
growing gradually over a period of time? of course, God could
cause them to grow instantaneously, but if we are being asked to
take them according to the natural sense of the words, are we not
compelled to view their growth as taking place over a period of
time?
3. Are we to take the account of Genesis 2:5-25 as occurring in day
six of chapter one? If so, it was a rather long and full “24 hour
day.”
4 Is not the Hebrew word for “day” used in at least three different
ways in Genesis 1:1-2:4 alone? Would this not wave a caution
flag concerning the use of the word “day”?
5. How are we to understand the creation of light on two different
days, the first and the fourth?
6. How come the events in Genesis 2 are mentioned in a different
literary order than those in chapter one?
7. Are we to understand from the text and from the whole context of
Scripture that we are still in the seventh day; and if so, how does
that reflect on our understanding of the first six days?
these a rebut a few of the legitimate questions that rise, not from
science, but from the text; and not everyone is reaching the same
conclusions about the timing of the six days. All agree that God created
“in the space of six days,” but they do not agree that the word “day”
must only be interpreted as a “24 hour day.”
D it is not a question of orthodoxy, or of keeping the PCA from going
liberal. Otherwise, we would have to say that Charles and A. A. Hodge1
1 Archibald Alexander Hodge, A Commentary on The Confession of Faith, with questions for theological
students and Bible classes (Philadelphia: Presbyterian Board of Publication, 1869); reprint, with an
essay of the life of Hodge, re titled the confession of Faith. A Handbook of Christian Doctrine
Expounding the Westminster Confession (Edinburgh: Banner of Truth, 1983), pp 82-83. See also
Charles Hodge, Systematic Theology (Grand Rapids, MI: Wm. B. Eerdmans Publishing Cq ., 1979)
Volume I, pp 568-574.

<a id="ga26-p110"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=112 printed_page=110 -->

and bb Warfield2 and J. Gresham Machen3 and E. J. Young4 and
Donald Grey Barnhouse and Francis Schaeffer5 were all unwittingly
leading us into liberalism since they did not hold to the “24 hour day”
view.
E The framers of the 16th century Westminster Confession of Faith and
catechisms adopted substantially the words of the english translation of
the Bible without elaboration or interpretation, namely “in the space of
six days.”
1 The framers of the Confession did not adopt language which was
based upon any specific interpretation of the biblical language
They neither added to nor subtracted from words of Scripture.
2. The framers of the WCF would have been aware of some
differing views about the meaning of the word “day” in the
creation account in Genesis 1, for example, the view that the days
in Genesis 1 were “anthropomorphic days.” According to this
view “days” are God’s work-days, hence they are of an
unspecified relation to time as we experience time. This view
was considered by such church fathers as Athanasius,6
Augustine,7 and Anselm.8
3. while the divines of westminster were aware of various views of
the meaning of “day” in the creation account, they chose not to
give an official interpretation of the meaning of “day,” but rather
they chose to simply use the words of Scripture, namely “in the
period of six days.”
2 “Calvin's Doctrine of Creation,” in Calvin and Calvinism (NY: Oxford University Press, 1931) pp 287-349.
3 “Did God Create Man? in the christian view of man (NY: Macmillan Co., 1937) pp 130-131.
4In the Beginning, (Banner of Truth) pp 42-43.
5 “The Freedom and Limitation in Cosmogony as Set by the Bible.” in No Final Conflict, pp 131-135.
6 Discourse 2 against Arians, Sections 48, 60.
7 city of God, Confessions, and commentary on literal meaning of Genesis. See also Mitchell’s discussion of
Augustine quoted in Attachment A to this Opinion. Dr. William S. Barker notes “Augustine treats
Genesis 1 at least five times: in two early anti-Manichean works; in the Confessions, Books XI-XIII;
in On Genesis Literally Interpreted', and in the city of God, Books XI-XII. He is particularly
concerned with the philosophical issue of the relation of time and eternity. As a result, William Al
Christian can say, in “The Creation of the World,” a companion to the study of St. Augustine, ed.
Roy W. Battenhouse (New York: Oxford U. Press, 1955), p 319: “Augustine thinks that the six days
of the Genesis story are to be understood in ‘some other way’ than as literal days, defined by the
circuit of the sun. The story' of the six days is a dramatic representation of what took place at once
and as a whole ... These ‘parts’ of the creation are not to be taken as temporal parts, one succeeding
another in time; the things enumerated in temporal order in the story were all created ‘together,’
without any intervals of duration of periods of time. The creation of the world did not have any
temporal duration. It did not take time, nor was it in time. God created the world ‘in the beginning.’”
With this understanding of the creation as before time, Augustine can nevertheless say that
“according to Scripture, less than 6000 years have elapsed since He (man) began to be” (City of God,
XII, 12, ed. Whitney J. Oates, basic writings of St. Augustine [New York: Random House, 1948], II,
190) and yet can also say a few pages later, “I own that I do not know what ages passed before the
human race was created” (City of God, XII, 16, ed. Oates, II, 196). The point is, for our current
discussion, that Augustine did not take the six creative days of Genesis One as literal 24-hour days.
(From personal correspondence between Dr Barker and Dr. Robert M. Ferguson.)
8Cur deus homo? I. 18.

<a id="ga26-p113"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=113 printed_page=null -->

an extract of the authoritative discussion on this matter by Rev. Prof.
Alexander Mitchell, D.D. is set out in Attachment A. Also an extract of
J. Macpherson’s views are set out in Attachment B.
F The PCA, acting consistently with historic American
Presbyterianism, has sought to have candidates for office examined in
terms of whether or not their views were in accord with the system of
doctrine asset out in the Westminster Confession of Faith and
Catechisms.
1. We corporately and individually subscribe to these Confessional
standards, but we do not necessarily subscribe to every word
individually ( ipsissima verba)9
2. While the starting point for this examination would be the words
used in the Confession and Catechisms, the words are not the
determining factor. The determining factor is whether or not the
candidates’ views are in accord with the doctrine as set out in the
Confessional standards.
G. Under the BCO of the PCA, the responsibility of making this
judgment about the views of an officer candidate has been delegated
to the presbytery in the case of teaching elders and to the session in the
case of ruling elders and deacons. These same courts are also
empowered to determine theological questions and controversies within
their bounds ( BCO 13-9).
H. In answering the question, “Are the view sofa candidate out of accord
with the system of doctrine taught in the Westminster Confession of Faith and catechism when he holds to an interpretation of “in the space
of six days” which, while not being a naturalistic evolutionary
interpretation, is not the ‘24 hour day’ interpretation?”
1. the confession itself does not say that the six days of creation
are to be understood as six consecutive periods of 24 hours.
2. A wide variety of faithful American and British Puritans and
Presbyterians, men such as J. Macpherson ,10 william am es/7
Alexander F. Mitchell,12 T. Chalmers, and W. G. T. Shedd,13 A.
A. Hodge,14 and B.B. Warfield, and did not take the view that a

9 William S. Barker “On the Hemphill Case and subscription to the Westminster Standards.” in The Practice of
Confessional Subscription, ed. David W. Hall (Lanham, MD: University Press of America, 1997).
10 While there is some discussion about the personal views of Macpherson, he is a reliable commentator on the
Confession. the Westminster Confession of Faith, With Introduction and Notes, (in the Handbooks
For Bible Classes and Private Students series, edited by Marcus Dods and Alexander Whyte)
(Edinburgh: T. & T. Clark, 1881); reprint edition, (Edinburgh: T. & T. Clark, 1977), pp 52-53. The
full text of this extract is set out in Attachment B.
11 Macpherson cites William Ames Medulla theologica (1634), I.VIII.28 in his The Confession of Faith (1882),
P 53.
12 See Attachment A.
13 “Doctrine of God” in Dogmatic Theology, 3 Volumes, (Nashville, TN: Thomas Nelson Publishing, 1980)
Volume I, pp 474-479.
14 See footnote #1 above.
Ill

<a id="ga26-p112"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=114 printed_page=112 -->

“24 hour day” interpretation is the only acceptable interpretation
of the term “in the space of six days.”
3. Scholars within the PCA and our associated seminaries, men
such as Dr. Will Barker (WTS), Dr. Jack Collins (CTS), Dr
Willem Van Gemeran (TEDS), Dr Vern Poythress (WTS), Dr.
Paul Fowler (KTS), Dr. R. L. Harris, Dr. M. Kline have pointed
out in a wide variety of articles and debates on the floor of the
General Assembly and elsewhere, that the Confession’s use of
the words “in the space of six days” does not limit us to
interpreting these words to six consecutive periods of 24 hours.15
4 These Puritans and Presbyterians are in no way suggesting
that the Confession or Scripture gave room for a purely
naturalist evolutionary interpretation of “in the space of six
days.”
I. the General Assembly of the PCA has previously spoken, in part, to the
general issues in this case:
1. In Case 90-8 Bowen vs. Eastern Carolina Presbytery, the GA
adopted the following: “For instance, there has always been
great leeway in approving men for office in the PCA who hold
differing views of eschatology .... However, the church has
allowed men to become officers and remain in good standing
even though they hold differing views ... The same thing could
be said for the subject of divorce .... Other doctrines in which the
PCA has granted a measure of freedom are: in the area of
creation, where some may hold to a literal six, 24-hour day for
God’s creative acts, and others may hold to a form of 'age-day'
creation (italic added); in the area of worship, where some would
hold that WCF XXI-5 lists those elements of worship which god
has instituted and believe that other elements are improper,
including the singing of anything but psalms, while others would
allow for the singing of hymns and spiritual song sin worship ...”
2. The Fourth General Assembly adopted the following
recommendation from the Committee on Judicial Business:
“[BCO 12-2] gives church courts latitude to establish rules as
needed agreeable with Scripture and doctrines which are not in
violation of the Constitution of the Church.” [M4GA 4-71, III, A,
P 72]
J. In the PC A we have been careful to emphasize that matters of doctrine
should be decided through the use of study committees, debate
before the Assembly, and the formal process of amending our
standards.

15 Two illustrations are listed of PCA scholars discussing these issues: Dr. Jack Collins sets out a detailed
discussion of “anthropomorphic days” in his "How Old is the Earth?” (Presbyterian 20/2 (1994) pp
109-130). Dr. Vem Poythress has a chapter responding to Paul Nelson in J. P. Moreland and Robert
Reynolds, eds. maker of heaven an dearth (Downers Grove, IL: InterVarsity Press, to appear.)

<a id="ga26-p113"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=115 printed_page=113 -->

1 The Standing Judicial Commission was not established to
interpret theological issues but to deal with specific judicial cases
between parties. In so doing, the SJC must be careful not to
unconstitutionally amend the PCA Constitution by judicial
process.
2. The Ad Hoc Committee for Judicial Concerns was formed
recently to assure that the judicial system would not be abused by
deciding issues that properly should be determined through study,
debate and the legislative process.
3. The PCA has committed itself not to determine doctrine by
judgments handed down judicially. to attempt to do so should
be called by its proper name, judicial activism, and reminds us of
the methods of the justices of the Supreme Court of the United
States who try to write legislation through their decisions.
4. If those who hold that the “24 hour day” view is the only
acceptable view for the PCA want to establish that as the official
standard for the PCA, the proper approach would be through the
formal amendment process in BCO 26.
In setting forth the statement of “Affirmations and Denials Regarding the
Interpretation of Genesis One,” New Jersey Presbytery acted within the
authority of BCO 11-4 which provides, “Every court has the right to resolve
questions of doctrine and discipline seriously and reasonably proposed ...”
A. The statement, as adopted by Presbytery, does not transgress the measure
of freedom historically allowed by the PCA.
B. While a Presbytery does have the power to settle disputes, it does not
have the power to establish standards which establish extra-constitutional
standards.
C. Such extra-constitutional standards might be the removing of a freedom
of conviction about a doctrine which the Confession gave liberty. For
example, determining that the only acceptable view on eschatology is
Amillenianism.
The New Jersey Presbytery, like any Presbytery within the PCA, is
empowered by the BCO to both examine the views of candidates and to settle
within its bounds theological controversies.
A. The New Jersey Presbytery’s study committee brought a set of principles
by which the Presbytery and its Candidates Committee might create a
context within which a candidate’s view could be examined.
B these principles sought to set the examination in the context of “the
system of doctrine” and not a specific interpretation of the words “in the
space of six days.”
C. The Presbytery left the decision about a specific candidate’s views on
creation to a case-by-case assessment as to whether or not that
candidate’s views were in accord with the “system of doctrine.”
D. This action was, in our view, in accord with the constitutional
requirements of the PCA

<a id="ga26-p114"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=116 printed_page=114 -->

Respectfully submitted by:
Isl TE Dominic A Aquila Isl RE Mark Belz
/s/ TE Michael D. Bolus Is/ TE LeRoy H. Ferguson
Isl TE Robert M. Ferguson, Co-author Is/ TE Paul B. Fowler, Co-author
Isl TE Paul D. Kooistra Isl RE Tom Leopard
Is/ TE James L Smith, Jr. Isl TE L. Roy Taylor
Is/ RE John B. White, Jr. Isl RE W Jack Williamson

ATTACHMENT A TO CONCURRING OPINION
Rev. Prof. Alexander Mitchell, D.D
on
The Westminster Standards and Creation “In the Space of Six Days.”
One of the most respected authorities on the background to and the meaning of
the Westminster Confession of Faith is Dr. Alexander Mitchell (1822-1899).
Mitchell was a Church of Scotland ecclesiastical historian whose most valuable
contributions to church history were his studies of the westminster assembly and
Confession: The Westminster Confession of Faith (1866); The Westminster Assembly
(1883) and his edition, with J. s truthers of the minutes of ... the Westminster Assembly
(1874).
What follows is an unedited excerpt from “The theology of the reformed
Church with Special Reference to the Westminster Standards,” Report of the
Proceedings of the Second General Council of the Presbyterian Alliance, Convened at
Philadelphia, September, 1880, edited by John B. Dales, D.D. and R. M. Patterson,
D.D. (Philadelphia: Presbyterian Journal Company, and J. C. McCurdy & Co., 1880),
pp 474-484. The footnotes have been added for this extract.
“There are two or three topics of minor importance which i could
not take up in the introduction to the “Minutes of the Assembly,” but
which in consequence of prevalent misunderstandings i should like to
notice on this occasion. They really are matters of minor importance,
and it might fairly be said that no tone of them is essential or even
material to the reformed system of doctrine, or ought to be regarded as
imposed on the conscience in the same way as matters entering into the
sum and substance or the system of the Reformed doctrine. But this is
not all that can be said about them, or that it is expedient should be said
now that currency has been given to so many unguarded statements
about them. The first to which I advert is the question so often and
confidently propounded of late, that the Confession represents the
creation of the world as having taken place in six “natural or literal
days,” which almost all orthodox divines now grant that it did not. But
the whole ground for the assertion is furnished by the words “natural or
literal,” which they themselves inserted. the authors of the Confession,

<a id="ga26-p115"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=117 printed_page=115 -->

as Dr A. A Hodge has well observed,1 simply repeat the statements of
Scripture in almost identical terms, and any interpretation that is fairly
applicable to such passages of Scripture, as Gen ii. 3 and Exodus xx 11,
is equally applicable to the words of the Confession It is quite true, as
he adds, that since the Confession was composed, ... new arguments
have been furnished against interpreting the days mentioned in the above
passages of Scripture as literal days. But it would be a mistake to
suppose that the figurative interpretation of the word ‘days’ in these
passages originated in modern times, and was altogether unknown to the
men who framed the Confession To prove it a mistake it is not
necessary to have recourse to the ingenious conjecture, that some of the
Cambridge men in the assembly may have been acquainted with the
manuscript work of Dean Colet,2 preserved in their archives, and only
given to the public in our own time, in which the figurative interpretation
of the days of creation is maintained.”3
“There is no lack of evidence, in works published before the
meeting of the Assembly, and familiar to several of its members, to show
that the figurative interpretation had long before Dean Colet’s time
commended itself to several scholars and divines. If there was one
Jewish scholar with whose writings such men as Lightfoot, Selden,
Gataker, Seaman, and Coleman4 were more familiar than another, it was
Philo of Alexandria,5 and Philo has not hesitated to characterize it as
“rustic simplicity, to imagine that the world was created in six days, or,
indeed, in any clearly defined space of time.” Augustine,6 the great
Latin doctor, with whose works several of the westminster divines were
far better acquainted than most of their successors, in his Literal
Commentary on Genesis, maintains that the days of the creation-week
were far different from (longe dispares) and again, very unlike to
(multurn impares) those that now are in the earth.7 Procopius,8 a Greek
writer not unknown to some of the Westminster divines, teaches that the

1 A Commentary on The Confession of Faith ....(Philadelphia: Presbyterian Board of Publication, 1869);
reprint retitled The Confession of Faith ... .(Edinburgh: Banner of Truth, 1983), p 82.
(7) John Colet, c. 1466-1519. Dean of St. Paul’s whose critical scholarship and advocacy of clerical reform
helped prepare the way for the Reformation.
3 John Colet, letters tor ad u lp hus on the mosaic account of the Creation, with translation and notes by J. H.
Lupton (London, 1876), pp xx-xxii; 26-28.
4 John Lightfoot, 1602-1675, renowned Biblical and Rabbinic scholar; John Selden, 1584-1654, Jurist and
Orientalist, leading lay member of the Assembly; Thomas Gataker, 1574-1654, one of the most
learned men then in England; Lazarus Seaman, c. 1607-1675, a leading debater of the Assembly,
called “an ocean of theology;” Thomas Coleman, 1598-1646, called ‘Rabbi Coleman’ for the
proficiency of his Hebrew studies.
5 c. 20 B.C.-c. A.D. 50. Jewish thinker and exegete especially influential in the Alexandrine school of
Christian theology.
6 Augustine of Hippo, 354-430. Greatest of the Latin Fathers, post apostolos omnium ecclesiarum magister.
7 De Genesi ad Iiteram, iv. 27; cf. De Civitate Dei, XI. 6-9. See Eugene Portalie, A Guide to the Thought of
Saint Augustine (Westport, CT: Greenwood Press, 1975, pp 136-143).
Procopius of Gaza, c. 475-538. Rhetorician and Biblical exegete. The work cited is Commentary Octoteuch,
ed. C. Clauser (Tigur, 1555).

<a id="ga26-p116"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=118 printed_page=116 -->

number of six days was assumed not as a mark of actual time, but as a
manner of teaching the order of creation, while in certain commentaries
in that age, attributed to the Venerable Bede,9 and largely read in
England, though now deemed spurious, a similar opinion is said to be
found. [Most of these testimonies are referred to, and the opinion they
express is admitted to be probabilis, in the sense his sect used that term,
by Sixtus Senensis in his Bibliotheca Sancta, p 422.]10 The figurative
interpretation therefore of the six days of creation is no make-shift of
hard-pressed theologians in the nineteenth century. It was held by
respectable scholars and divines, from early times, and was known to the
framers of our Confession, and had they meant deliberately to exclude it
they would have written not six days, but six natural or literal days . . .”

ATTACHMENT B TO CONCURRING OPINION
John Macpherson
on
the Westminster Confession and “in the space of six days”.
John Macpherson (1847-1902) was a Free Church minister, writer and translator
of German theology. Macpherson’s theology was an Amyraldian type of Reformed
theology, defending the doctrines of total depravity, unconditional election and
substitutionary atonement. His Doctrine of the Church in Scottish Theology (1903) is
still the standard work on the subject and his Westminster Confession has been
frequently reprinted.
the following extract is his discussion of the variety views of the meaning of ‘in
the space of six days,” and is taken from The Westminster Confession of Faith, With
Introduction and Notes, (in the Handbooks For Bible Classes and Private Students
series, edited by Marcus Dods and Alexander Whyte) (Edinburgh: T. & T. Clark,
1881), reprint edition, (Edinburgh: T. & T. Clark, 1977), pp 52-53
“Much unreasonable opposition has been shown against the
retention in our Confession of the statement, ‘in the space of six days.’ It
might be answer enough to those who thus complain, to remark that their
complaint must tell equally against the Scriptures, and also that whatever
fair explanation can be given of the scriptural account is equally
available for that of the Confession. This has been from earliest times a
much debated point from the side of science and from the side of
theology. Philo regards the scriptural expression as indicating merely
the succession and order of events. This notion gained general
acceptance in the Christian church, and with sundry modifications is the

9 c. 673-735. Monk of Jarrow and “the Father of English history.”
10 1520-1564, Dominican scholar, Italian convert from Judaism. The work cited is Bibliotheca Sancta ex
Praecipuis Catholicae Eccl. Auctoribus Collecta (Venice, 1566), a study highly esteemed among both
Catholics and Protestants.

<a id="ga26-p117"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=119 printed_page=117 -->

view now generally entertained The early Protestant theologians are
commendably cautious here, and while generally not denying, do not
affirm the view that the world was created in six successive days. Some
(as Amesius, Medulla, 1. viii. 28) suggest that the active creative periods
were six natural days, with indefinite intervals between them. So also
Martensen says ( Christian Dogmatics, p 117): ‘Each new day dawned
when the time was full, when all the conditions and presuppositions of
its dawn had been developed.’ This theory is not in Scripture nor in the
Confession, but it is not inconsistent with either, and this ought to show
how little such a statement as that complained against in our Confession
excludes fairly-attempted schemes of reconciliation with discovered
facts of science However, it should be observed that the notion of the
six days as indicating the order of succession is still as obnoxious as ever
to men of science. One period seems to overlap the other, and it appears
utterly inconceivable, as well as unscientific, that the works represented
as belonging to one creative day should be declared finished, and then an
immense period intervene before the work of the next day should be
finished; - that creations should remain for ages in themselves perfect,
yet absolutely without the conditions of their efficient operation. The
student of God’s Word need not pledge himself to any theory. The
statement of the Confession is purely biblical ”

MINORITY REPORT
CASE 97-5

#### I. Summary of the Facts

At its May, 1996 Presbytery meeting, New Jersey Presbytery (NJP) erected a
committee to study possible exceptions to the Westminster Confession of Faith in the
area of creation (ROC, 4). This committee gave its report in September, 1996, issuing a
list of Affirmations and Denials in an attempt to circumscribe acceptable positions
(ROC, 5). Among the troubling aspects were:
II. C: “We deny any doctrine of the gradual evolution of human species
(emphasis added) from more primitive life forms” (which does not
exclude evolutionary origin for other species) and III. C: “We deny the
24-hour-day interpretation is the only exegetically possible
interpretation.” (See original complaint and the attached Resolution.)
Following discussion, this report was tabled until the November stated meeting
At its November, 1996 meeting (ROC, 6), NJP adopted “Affirmations and Denials
Regarding The Interpretation of Genesis One.” Immediately following the adoption of
this resolution, NJP also moved and carried “ that in regards to the Confession, the
Westminster divines probably understood Genesis 1 from a 24 hour view, and,
therefore, any other view could be taken as an exception. ” (ROC, 8)

<a id="ga26-p118"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=120 printed_page=118 -->

At the February, 1997 meeting of NJP, the session of Mt. Carmel PCA
complained against the adoption of the “Affirmations and Denials Regarding The
Interpretation of Genesis One” because it construed this to be a violation of the
Westminster Confession of Faith the heart of this alleged constitutional (rather than a
procedural) error is that, “The resolution denies the plain and ordinary sense of the
creation account revealed in Genesis One.” (ROC, 23) At no point did the complainants
object to the powers of presbytery to resolve its own matters, nor was BCO 11-4 raised
at all.
The complaint (ROC, 23-29) contends that the adopted resolution is
incompatible with the teaching in our standards, which uniformly employ the
unambiguous phrase - and no other - “in the space of six days.” (W CF IV: 1, WLC 15,
WSC 9) The complaint was denied by a 17-11 vote by NJP.
the session of mt car mel PCA church then submitted a timely filed complaint
to the Standing Judicial Commission which referred the case to a panel. Upon hearing
the case, the panel concluded that the NJP erred in adopting this resolution and that NJP
should reverse its error
The original panel recommended that the complaint be upheld and that the case
be remanded back to NJP for correction The SJC reheard the case on Jan. 8-9, 1998
and reversed the panel by a 12-9 vote. At that meeting, nine qualified members of the
SJC announced their intent to submit a minority report (BCO 15).

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Did the NJP act conform to our standards, specifically WCF IV: 1, WLC 15, and
WSC 99

#### III. Judgment

No. The Complaint is sustained and remanded back to NJP to be brought into
conformity with the plain language of scripture and our confession

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

Because of misconceptions which have arisen in this case, we think it is
important first to address what adoption of this minority report will NOT do. This is
not a complaint that raised the broad issue of system subscription, although some may
try to cast it as such. It deals with only one issue: whether NJP can create a document
that implicitly interprets our constitution contrary to its clear meaning. For purposes of
addressing the critical issue in this case, we assume arguendo that lower courts may
allow officers to take exceptions to our standards. We limit our opinion to the single
constitutional question raised by this complainant.
Nor do we believe this is a case where the SJC should decide who may be
ordained in the PCA and who may not be We take no position in this opinion as to
whether particular views to the Lord’s creation work are acceptable exceptions to our
standards. To address that issue in the context of this complaint would reflect a lack of
judicial restraint we strongly object ton jp creating a document of affirmations and .
denials which, in our opinion, creates extra-constitutional standards.

<a id="ga26-p119"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=121 printed_page=119 -->

In many ways the choice in this case is quite clear: The PCA either tailors the
interpretation of its constitution out of well-intended concern for modern scholarship or
in deference to esteemed personalities, or it sides with the time-tested doctrine of the
confession, and calls on all theories and personalities to submit to the only infallible
rule of faith and practice We either affirm our traditional doctrine or create a new
standard by well-intentioned default if the NJP action stands unaltered.
The language of the Confession, our constitutional standard, is quite clear and
the original meaning of “day” as a 24-hour day is even admitted by the act of New
Jersey Presbytery (ROC, 8). For the Standing Judicial Commission to presume the
power to ignore that original intent and uphold a presbytery study committee’s contrary
position - even if purporting to resolve only the incidental, procedural issue - is, in
effect, to create new standards in a critical area apart from the church’s commissioning,
apart from the constitutional process, and apart from Assembly’s deliberative study. To
so change (and by unilateral authority, at that) would permit or require the SJC to alter a
normal reading of the Confession and introduce a hermeneutic that renders uncertain the
meanings of other confessional phrases and sections.
This new proposal also does more than merely provide latitude for divergent
views of the length of the creation days. This new position could be heartily subscribed
by many (perhaps most) Theistic Evolutionists. In Denial II. C., which denies "“any
doctrine of the gradual evolution of human species from more primitive life forms”
(emphasis ours), the word “human” is critical. There is no denial of any evolutionary
process of any kind in the creation of the lower forms of life. neither do any of the
affirmations make any statement contradicting an evolutionary process in the
development of the microbes, plants, and animals. The only requirement is some belief
in the account of the special creation of Adam “out of the matter of the earth,” and of
Eve “out of the body of Adam.” such a position is far from the clear language of our
standards and of the Holy Scriptures.
This lapse in the precision of language of this proposed statement only serves to
underscore the danger of erecting subordinate standards without conforming to our
constitutional procedure for such acts. Only the constitutionally prescribed process of
amendment, with its methodical, deliberative checks and balances, can be trusted to
generate the results that protect against weakening our standards.
Despite sincere personal opinions held by some esteemed brothers, those
personal views should not supersede our constitution (W CF IV: 1, WLC 15) apart from
an amendment that would read something like:
“...to create, or make of nothing, the world, and all things therein
whether visible or invisible, i neither a space of six days ora lengthy
but undefined period; and all very good ”
This emendation might make the resolution approved by NJP conform to the
constitution. We invite such honest amendments to the Confession to be proposed and
voted on by the presbyteries. Further, we are of the opinion that then and only then, if
approved, could this court condone such new views as proposed. to do otherwise is to
have a small group - which we admire both for their candor and diligence in study -

<a id="ga26-p120"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=122 printed_page=120 -->

legislate for the church apart from the constitution Moreover, for the 24 member SJC
to enshrine such pluralism as orthodoxy for the whole church on such a key issue,
without the Assembly voting to alter its constitution, is a dangerous precedent at best.
The proposed opinion (by a slim 4 - 3 ratio) also fails to answer the complaint
raised. Nowhere does the Record of the Case represent this dispute as being about the
powers of presbytery to resolve its own matters, but the Record and the testimony of
both parties concerned doctrinal issues. While our decision does not create a new
doctrinal standard - we only repeat what has been held for centuries - the majority
opinion, by allowing the act of n jp that created a plurality of views to stand, does
inadvertently condone a new view.
We believe that the majority opinion which reflects a laissez faire approach
engages unwittingly in judicial activism by altering our standards. Had NJP approved a
certain view or range of exceptions for an individual candidate, that would have been
one thing It is another altogether to create a set of Affirmations and Denials on this
subject that legitimates such views in perpetuity. Other presbyteries could wrongly
appeal to this single action, thereby broadening or weakening the church’s views.
Further, we are convinced that to fail to uphold our standards at this point is to
invite future heterodoxy and would render the clear language of the confession
unenforceable (or unintelligible) in other presbyteries. While there are, no doubt, fine
men with these views who do and should serve in the PCA, other ways must be found to
accommodate their views if they differ with the constitution, rather than to ignore or
deconstruct the clear language of the Confession and Catechisms. The SJC simply does
not have the authority to legislate new views contrary to the constitution, no matter how
many vote to do so. Nor should the SJC create issues that have not been raised in the
complaint.
In addition, a human calculation of the potential effect or implications of this
case should not be elevated above the SJC’s charter to apply (not to create loopholes or
exceptions to) the constitution a sit is written. If personalities or other pragmatic
considerations may be held in abeyance momentarily, we are confident that a fair
comparison of then jp resolution with the clear language of the constitution will
indicate that the recently proposed view will be found out of conformity with the
standards. our opinion above prioritizes the objectivity of the constitution over either
evolving doctrinal standards or viewing the constitution as a “living document” to
accommodate the cultural positions of the day.
While the higher courts are normally to give great deference to the lower courts
(.BCO 39), those standards of review pertain to matters that are uniquely possessed by or
assigned to the lower courts. Our decision wholeheartedly supports that normal
standard of deference given to the lower court for observation, investigation, and
judgment. This case, however, is a matter of constitutional interpretation and doctrine.
this case involves interpretation of a constitutional issue to which a timely complaint
was made. the General Assembly reviews constitutional issues of this nature without
giving unlimited deference to the lower courts. Heretofore, the PCA has never
approved by overture, judgment in a judicial case, study paper, or amendment to the
constitution a general view ora lower court departing from the clear language of the
confession in the matter of creation.

<a id="ga26-p121"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=123 printed_page=121 -->

The lower courts have the authority to determine in each particular case whether
an individual’s views are in accord with our standards. Certainly, one man may ably
defend his position with sound exegesis, while a weaker, less-informed man may not be
able to do so. The weaker brother should not be able to benefit automatically from
blanket extensions and exceptions to our standards created and accepted by a lower
court. We firmly believe that will occur, if the majority opinion is adopted. Instead, the
lower courts must determine on a case-by-case basis whether each candidate’s views are
in accord with our standards. If they are not, then a decision must be made whether the
exceptions are acceptable or strike at the fundamentals of reformed doctrine. In each
case, the ordaining court, as the court of original jurisdiction, must initially decide what
actions to take after each examination. Those actions are subject to complaints (Cf.
BCO 43-1).
We do not believe that this judicial case, where the General Assembly cannot
debate or discuss these issues, is the best forum for developing new “official positions”
for the PCA on the issue of creation. the improper use of dicta from previous judicial
decisions by the majority in this case establishes this fear. We believe that the more
proper way to address these issues is for overtures to arise from lower courts wherein
the Assembly may fully debate and discuss these important issues. We fear that the
proposed decision will result in the creation of an official PCA position apart from such
deliberation. Because we believe the quasi-official position adopted by NJP and
condoned by a slim majority of the SJC creates extra-constitutional standards contrary
to the constitutional amendment process, we earnestly call upon the General Assembly
to adopt this minority report that exhibits judicial restraint.
We believe the proposed opinion, in its effort to seek what it perceived to be
neutral ground, has instead wrongly legislated on a critical constitutional issue The
proposed 97-5 opinion, if adopted a sit stands, will, we firmly believe, result in a serious
erosion of our standards
We fear, no matter how intent the present proposal is to allow personal liberty
and original jurisdiction to lower courts, that it is a concession to modernity or to a
particular scientific theory that is perhaps beginning to fade and certainly is antithetical
to the strongest of views on creation. We urge the church to stand strong, even in the
face of both friendly and hostile persons who oppose the language of Genesis.
we prefer to stand wi than earlier scientist nearer the time of the westminster
Assembly, whose take on the original intent of “day” clearly did not view the creation
days as non-literal. In 1681, Isaac Newton’s view on whether the 7th day was figurative
was expressed in these words: “methinks one of the Ten Commandments given by God
in Mount Sinai, pressed by divers of the prophets, observed by our Savior, his Apostles,
and first Christians for 300 years ... should not be grounded on a fiction.”
we sincerely believe that our proposed decision best preserves the role of the
Standing Judicial Commission to uphold the constitution and conform to the
Confession, rather than calling on it to create and condone new or possibly erroneous
views. Our approach is maximally careful - neither to forsake Scripture nor to rush into
a doctrinal channel that would abandon the past or injure the future.
The signers below urge the Assembly to reject any view that dilutes our clear
doctrinal teaching. One simple remedy is to remand this case to the presbytery of

<a id="ga26-p122"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=124 printed_page=122 -->

original jurisdiction with the instruction that this matter be brought into conformity with
the clear teaching of our Confession. The presbytery thus retains its original
jurisdiction AND the constitution is upheld simultaneously. To do less, we sincerely
fear, is to begin the process of official accommodation to secular views.
We urge the PCA to guard the deposit. We gladly extend charity to brothers of
differing interpretations, but let this Assembly not confuse personal charity with the
objective claims of our standards. Before going the way of many erstwhile great
denominations, we urge commissioners to uphold our decision above and study long
and hard before the PCA takes action to alter our standards; more importantly let us not
forsake the clear teaching of Scripture.
We believe it is dangerous and ill-advised to adopt new standards merely based
on what is “exegetically possible ” The fact that one passage or word is considered
“exegetically possible” should not be inscribed as a hermeneutic principle apart from
other considerations. There are, no doubt, many “exegetically possible” interpretations
of different words in Scripture, but some are obviously incorrect in light of the analogy
of Scripture
let us refuse to take steps off of the safe road of theological precision and
careful biblical exegesis, onto that slippery slope of culturally conditioned interpretation
which has brought too many once sound denominations to disaster. Such a step would
be particularly distressing during this, our anniversary celebration of God’s raising up
the PCA as a testimony to the in err an cy and infallibility of Scripture.
/s/ TE David W. Hall /s/ TE Ben W. Konopa, Jr Is/ TE John McArthur, Jr.
/s/ RE Harrison Brown /s/ RE Eugene Friedline /s/ RE Robert Miller
/s/ RE M. Dale Peacock /s/ RE Edward J. Robeson /s/ RE John Van Voorhis

### V. DISPOSITION OF OTHER CASES

The Standing Judicial Commission, therefore, reports the following disposition
of cases.

1. APPEAL, CASE 97-2
BRUCE NICKOLEY
VS.
ROCKY MOUNTAIN PRESBYTERY

#### I. Summary of the Facts

1. after a series of disagreements, protests and charges against various individuals,
local churches and the presbytery filed by Evergreen Presbyterian Church
(EPC), its pastor, its session and its members, Rocky Mountain Presbytery
(RMP), at its Stated Fall Meeting on September 26, 1996, dissolved the
relationship between the Evergreen Presbyterian Church (EPC) and Rocky
Mountain Presbytery (RMP). (Record of the case (ROC) IV -B1).

<a id="ga26-p123"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=125 printed_page=123 -->

2. Rev Bruce Nickoley, the Appellant herein, was and is still the pastor of EPC.
3. At said RMP Meeting, of september 26, 1996, the presbytery instituted by
indictment charges against Rev. Bruce Nickoley. These charges were that he
had violated his ordination vows Nos. 2, 3, and 4 (ROC II-B-1 through 4 and
IV-B-2). RMP set the date for trial of these charges at its Stated Winter Meeting
on January 22, 1997 at 1:00 P.M.
4 Rev Nickoley was allowed to labor out of bounds at EPC pending the outcome
of his trial. (ROC IV-B-2).
5 On January 22-24, 1997, RMP met for its Winter Stated Meeting at Deer Creek
Community Church in Littleton Co (ROC 1I-A-1).
6 On January 22, 1997, after some preliminaries, r mp moved to the trial of Rev.
Bruce Nickoley
7 Before beginning said trial, RMP voted to go into Executive Session, excepting
Dr. paul r gilchrist who was a visitor members of EPC, who had come to the
meeting as visitors, were required to leave during the Executive Session
8 As the trial of Rev. Bruce Nickoley proceeded, a presbyter interrupted to report
that “members/friends of EPC were picketing on the sidewalks outside with
signs indicating that deer creek church was conducting a secret trial of their
pastor.” (ROC II-A-4).
9. RMP then passed a motion requiring the Moderator:
“To direct TE Bruce Nickoley to instruct his congregation (EPC) to stop
picketing with signs that have the name ‘Deer Creek’ written on them ” (ROC
II-A-4)
10. The RMP minutes then recite that the “Orders of the Day” were called, and the
meeting adjourned until the next day
11 There is no evidence in the ROC that Rev. Bruce Nickoley complied with
RMP’s instructions, except in Appellant’s grounds for appeal he states that he
“. . . complied with this request and the instructions by TE Nickoley to the
picketers was witnessed by Rev Bernie Kuiper At this point everyone went
home ” (ROC 1-2).
12. On the next day, RMP reconvened with a season of prayer Then RMP resumed
the trial of Rev. Bruce Nickoley
13. At this point, TE Duane Cory, one of the presbytery members, informed the
presbytery meeting that the members of EPC had resumed their picketing in
front of the Deer Creek Church.
14. RMP then adopted the following motion:
. that Rocky Mountain Presbytery instruct TE Nickoley to encourage his
congregation to remove all picketing signs in light of the Ninth Commandment
and 1 Corinthians 6.” (ROC II-A-5).
15 The RMP minutes then record -
“. . . TE Bruce Nickoley refused to so instruct his congregation.” (ROC II-A-5).
16. The next item in the RMP minutes is:
“Moved, seconded and carried that TE Bruce Nickoley be cited for contumacy.”
(ROC II-A-5)

<a id="ga26-p124"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=126 printed_page=124 -->

17. Except for a few remarks which Appellant refers to in his Specification No. 3
for Appeal (ROC 1-7), there is no evidence in the ROC that RMP gave Rev.
Bruce Nickoley an opportunity to explain or defend his refusal to comply with
its instructions.
18 The next item in the RMP minutes is a Motion for judgment against Rev Bruce
Nickoley, which Motion carried with the following judgment:
“. . THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED that
(1) Rev Bruce Nickoley by refusal to submit to the
order of Rocky Mountain Presbytery admits his
contumacy (BCO 38-1).
(2) Rev Bruce Nickoley be immediately deposed
from ministry (BCO 34-4).” (ROC II-A-6).
19. It is from this judgment that Rev. Bruce Nickoley appeals.
20. RMP then took the following action of the interrupted trial of Rev. Bruce
Nickoley, to-wit:
“BE it resolved that any further proceedings of the trial of Mr. Nickoley as
to the violation of his ordination vows of No. 2, 3, and 4 is now declared moot.”
(ROC II-A-7).

#### II. Statement of the Issues

Did Rocky Mountain Presbytery err in receiving and immediately acting upon a
motion to cite Rev. Bruce Nickoley for contumacy and rendering the judgment to
depose him from the ministry without giving him reasonable opportunity to defend
himself under the due process provisions for a trial as outlined in the Book of Church
Order?

#### III. Judgment

Yes. This case is remanded to Rocky Mountain Presbytery with the
recommendation that it either (a) institute a prosecution against Rev. Bruce Nickoley as
a case under process on the charge of contumacy and/or other charges under the due
process provisions of the Book of Church Order for a trial, beginning with BCO §32-3.
Or (b) revoke the judgment of deposition from the ministry against Rev. Bruce
nickoley and reinstate him as a member of rocky mountain presbytery in good
standing.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

Appellee relies on several Book of Church Order (BCO) provisions in its
justification for its action in “deposing from ministry” Rev. Bruce Nickoley. In the
ROC and in Appelle’s brief, app el lee relies on its interpretations of BCO provisions
§38-1, 34-4, 32-6, and 35-12 as constitutional justification for this action. We are of the
opinion that r mp has misinterpreted the applicability of these BCO provisions to this
case. we shall give our reasoning and opinion as to the proper interpretation of these
BCO provisions to this case.

<a id="ga26-p125"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=127 printed_page=125 -->

BCO §38-1
BCO §38-1 deals with a case without process where a person “shall come
forward and make his offense known to the court.” RMP cites this provision as its
constitutional basis for judging that “Rev. Bruce Nickoley by refusal to submit to the
order of Rocky Mountain Presbytery admits his contumacy ” (ROC II-A-6). Likewise,
Appellee’s brief contains these arguments, to-wit:
1. “Because TE Nickoley continued to refuse to submit to RM P’s Order,
RMP found said refusal to be an admission of guilt for his contumacious
behavior.”
2. “TE Nickoley implicitly confessed his contumacy by continually
refusing to obey the Court’s order in the presence of all the presbyters.”
3. “Contumacious behavior is not an ordinary statement in the presence of
the Court It is sinful behavior which shows a lack of respect for the
Court Such rebellious and obstinate behavior is in actuality making his
offense known to the Court.”
4. “Failing to repent, TE Nickoley ‘made his offense known to the Court’
and left the presbytery no choice except to find him guilty of contempt .”
Thus by relying on BCO §38-1 as a justification for its judgment, RMP admits
that it did not follow the regular due process for a trial as prescribed by the BCO We
agree with Dr. Morton Smith’s interpretation of BCO §38-1 as he quotes 19th century
theologian Rev F. P. Ramsey, viz :
“In handling a confession of guilt it is essential that the person intends to confess
and permit the Court to render judgment without process. Statements made by him
in the presence of the court must not betaken as the basis of judgment without
process except by consent, for that would be to deprive him of his right ”
The facts in this case do not support a voluntary “coming forward” of rev
Bruce Nickoley. But, the facts in this case clearly contradict any voluntary confession
by him that would justify trying the case without due process The undisputed facts, in
the ROC, are:
1 “When TE Nickoley saw how BCO 38-1 was being used. . . he clearly
stated in unequivocal terms that he was admitting to no guilt whatsoever
regard the charge of contumacy ” (ROC 1-3).
2. “It was clear to RMP that TE Nickoley was not intending to confess to
the charge of contumacy and a full statement of the facts were never
given to the accused for his approval before the court proceeded to a
judgment ” (ROC 1-4).
3. “In the light of TE Nickoley’s clear denial of his guilt in the charge of
contumacy, the BCO is clear as to the process by which we must proceed
- if the accused . plead and take issue the trial shall proceed.” (ROC I-
6).
We are, therefore, of the opinion that since Rev. Bruce Nickoley did not invoke
BCO §38-1, rm phase r re din denying him aright to a trial, the right to be heard and
defend his actions, and the right to due process under the provisions of the BCO.

<a id="ga26-p126"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=128 printed_page=126 -->

BCO §34-4
BCO §34-4 deals with citations to a minister ina case under process. In the first
instance, this section is not applicable to this case for this is not a case “pertaining to
process against a minister,” but RMP deals with it as a case without process.
Furthermore, a “citation” is merely a notice to a party to appear a ta trial. This entire
section deals only with a minister being notified to attend a trial and the penalties that
may be imposed if the minister fails to appear For these reasons, this BCO §34-4 is not
applicable to the facts of this case. RMP then erred in citing this BCO §34-4 as the
constitutional basis for its judgment that “Rev. Bruce Nickoley be immediately deposed
from the ministry.” Rev Bruce Nickoley had not “failed to appear before the
Presbytery” or “refuse to attend” a trial (BCO §34-6) under the BCO provisions
“pertaining to process against a minister.” So we are of the opinion that BCO §34-4 is
not applicable to this case and cannot be relied upon by RMP as a constitutional basis
for its action.
BCO 6'32-6
BCO §32-6 deals with situations where an accused person “shall refuse to obey
a citation.”
The dictionary defines the word “citation” as:
“An official summons or notice given to a person to appear, as
before a tribunal of Justice.”
The BCO takes the same position as to the nature of a “citation” (BCO §32-2
(3), 32-4, 32-6, 34-4, Appendix G II and III). This is clear as these sections of the BCO
use the same notice to appear for a party and for witnesses, calling them “citation”.
BCO §34-4 provides that before a minister can be “deposed as contumacious” he must
have refused to appear for trial after having received 3 such citations.
Rev Bruce Nickoley did appear before RMP at its Stated Meeting on January
22-24, 1997, as he had been cited, to stand trial on charges that he had violated his
ordination vows It was during this trial that RMP gave him instructions relating to the
people picketing outside Deer Creek Community Church He was likewise present
when the motion was made and carried to convict him of contumacy and to depose him
from the ministry. He remained in the presbytery meeting until his trial for violation of
his ordination vows was declared “moot”. Then the RMP minutes read:
“Moved, seconded and carried to continue in Executive Session, with only Stated
Clerk Paul Gilchrist being allowed to stay. Mr. Nickoley was invited to leave the
meeting, which he did.”
There is absolutely no evidence that Rev. Nickoley violated any citations by
refusing or failing to appear
We are, therefore, of the opinion that RMP is in error in contending that Rev.
Nickoley violated BCO §32-6 and relying on this BCO provision as a constitutional
basis for its judgment against Rev. Nickoley
BCO §35-12
This provision deals with an “officer or member of the Church refusing to
testify” and on that basis, being censured for contumacy. This provision simply is not

<a id="ga26-p127"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=129 printed_page=127 -->

applicable to the facts in this case. The facts are undisputed that Rev Nickoley has not
“refused to testify.” He was attending the presbytery and testified in the case against
him for violation of his ordination vows.
RMP inferred that because he refused to obey its instructions and because he
refused to repent, that these could be considered as a “refusal to testify” and thus
violations of this BCO §35-12. We disagree. Rev. Nickoley remained in the presbytery
until he was asked to leave. The undisputed fact is as stated in the ROC:
“At no point in the proceedings did RMP give opportunity for TE Nickoley to
make a defense of his action. RMP did this because they refused the right of
trial to TE Nickoley.” (ROC 1-7).
powers of presbytery
Appellee makes a strong argument for its immediate judgment against TE
Nickoley based on the general powers of a presbytery under BCO §13-9a “to . .
remove and judge ministers.” In its brief, Appellee states its argument in such words
as:
(1) “When such (contumacious) action is demonstrated before the body
under which a person takes avow to submit, then immediate censure
should be forthcoming,”
(2) “The RMP which has the authority over Mr. Nickoley has the power to
immediately render discipline.”
(3) “Without the power of immediate censure for rebellious acts occurring
before it by a member, rm pis disenfranchised of its right to govern its
members.”
(4) “By logical inference therefore, when a person refuses a lawful order by
the presbyters who are his brothers in the Lord an immediate censure for
contumacy is allowed. . . Not allowing this would be handcuffing the
court and saying it has no immediate power of censure for unrepentant
and rebellious behavior occurring before its very eyes.”
(5) “The court has an inherent authority to protect the integrity of its
process. To allow men to manipulate the system would be a travesty of
justice.”
(6) “If a minister acts ina rebellious way to a lawful order directed to him
on the floor of presbytery, he must be immediately judged for contempt
in order to preserve the integrity and authority of the court ”
(7) “TE Nickoley continued to persist in his refusal to obey the lawful order
of RMP and was summarily charged and dealt with which is an inherent
power of the court for behavior contemptuous to it.”
(8) “Presbytery by virtue of its right to judge its member (BCO §13-
9a). . . and hearing from its member that he will not obey a lawful order
may issue a finding of contempt of actions of contumacy occurring
before its eyes. If presbytery is not allowed to do this, then justice is not
served in cases where ministers openly refuse to obey presbytery
decrees, and the power of dealing with contempt is stripped from the
authority being sinned against.”

<a id="ga26-p128"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=130 printed_page=128 -->

We agree with certain basic principles stated and inferred from these strong
arguments. We believe that a presbytery, under the general powers of BCO §13-9a, has
the right to determine its membership of teaching elders, to control the order and
decorum of its meeting, to issue instructions and orders to its members so long as such
are lawful, reasonable and in accordance with the general principles of biblical Polity.
this is the inherent authority and power of presbytery to protect the integrity of its
meetings and its actions. But the accused also has the right to be given due process to
explain and defend the charges against him.
As the ROC clearly shows RMP had, for several years, been engaged in a series
of controversies with EPC, its session and TE Nickoley. This resulted in the dissolution
of its relationship with EPC and charges being instituted against TE Nickoley. But in
spite of this background, RMP should have given TE Nickoley the full opportunity to
explain and defend his refusal to obey its order and instruction. The ROC does not
reveal that TE Nickoley was given such an ample opportunity to explain and defend his
action. In fairness and justice, the accused was entitled to this right.
CONCLUSION
We find no specific provision in the BCO which deals with the exact factual
situation in this case and would authorize such immediate action on a charge of
contumacy without a trial
Appellee acknowledges this fact but argues that RMP had such authority, by
inference, from certain BCO provisions and under the general powers given to
presbytery.
We have concluded that in the light of the lack of such a specific BCO
authorization and due to th every serious nature of the charge together with the drastic
effect of the judgment to depose TE Nickoley from ministry, that the accused should
have been given a trial under the due process provisions of the BCO in order for him to
have ample opportunity to answer this charge and defend his actions.
we are of the opinion that such would be in the interest of fairness and justice in
a court of our Lord Jesus Christ.
We do not say that RMP is deprived of its right and power to judge its member.
We see no reason why RMP could not now initiate such a trial and include in its
charges this issue of contumacy and the issue of TE Nickoley’s violation of his
ordination vows.
For these reasons, we have remanded the case back to RMP with our
recommendations that it choose one of the courses we set forth in our judgment.
Respectfully submitted,
/s/ Dr Michael D. Bolus Is/ Robert Miller /s/ W. Jack Williamson
NOTE: This preliminary decision was written by W. Jack Williamson with the
concurrence of Dr. Michael D. Bolus and Robert Miller.

#### V. Voting on Proposed Decision

Adopted by SJC: 17 Concurring, 1 Recused, 6 Absent.

<a id="ga26-p129"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=131 printed_page=129 -->

## 2. COMPLAINT, CASE 97-3

### BRUCE NICKOLEY
VS.
ROCKY MOUNTAIN PRESBYTERY

This case was determined to be out of order per SJC Manual 10.1. The following finding was unanimously approved by the full Standing Judicial Commission: “The Chairman and Secretary determined that the case was administratively out of order in that the matter about which the complaint was lodged was also the subject of an appeal. BCO 43-1 provides, ‘...no complaint is allowable in a judicial case in which an appeal is taken.’ In the matter at hand, to wit, the Rocky Mountain Presbytery action dissolving the relationship between itself and Evergreen Church, an appeal was granted to Evergreen Church and hearing of the appeal was docketed for the January 22 – 24, 1997, meeting of Presbytery.”

## 3. APPEAL, CASE 97-4

### MRS. NANCY A. CONRAD, et al.
VS.
CENTRAL CAROLINA PRESBYTERY

### I. Summary of the Facts

1. On January 27, 1996, Central Carolina Presbytery (CCP) sustained a complaint brought by the complainants, finding, among other things, that “although the Session of PPC may have had grounds to remove members from the roll, they failed to take the required action to remove them...” and, as a result, the Session of Prosperity Presbyterian Church (PPC) was in error when it dropped certain names from the membership roll (ROC 8b).

2. The Session of Prosperity Presbyterian Church was informed of Central Carolina Presbytery’s decision to sustain the complaint. On May 16, 1996, the following actions were taken:

(a) the Session of PPC approved the language of a letter to be sent out to Conrad, et al. acknowledging the procedural error on its part in their prior action of removing the names of Conrad, et al. from the rolls of PPC (ROC p. 4-5).

(b) the Session of PPC noted that the previous action in removing the names of certain members of PPC from the membership rolls is “null and void” (ROC p. 9). The CCP nullified the action because the clerk of PPC Session had acted apart from the Session of PPC taking any action to remove the names.

(c) the Session of PPC then voted to remove from the membership of PPC the names of Conrad, et al. (ROC p. 3-5).

<a id="ga26-p130"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=132 printed_page=130 -->

(d) the Session of PPC sent copies of “the preceding actions taken to this date regarding church rolls” to the Clerk of CCP (ROC p. 5).

(e) the Session of PPC approved sending the letter of notification to Conrad, et al., informing them of the actions of the Session of PPC on May 16, 1996 (ROC p. 9).

3. On June 12, 1996, Conrad, et al. brought a complaint to the Session of PPC against the “delinquency of the Session of PPC and their actions” in removing the names of Conrad, et al. from the rolls of PPC, maintaining that such action was “in defiance of the judicial decision rendered by the CCP at its January 1996 stated meeting...” (ROC p. 6).

4. The complaint of Conrad, et al. was denied by the Session of PPC on June 27, 1996 (ROC p. 11).

5. On July 22, 1996, Conrad, et al. brought their complaint to the CCP and sought relief from the May 16, 1996, action of the Session of PPC in removing the names of Conrad, et al. from the rolls of PPC (ROC p. 10).

6. On January 25-26, 1997, the CCP erected a Judicial Commission and heard the complaint. The CCP sustained the commission’s recommendation to deny the complaint (ROC p. 12).

7. A complaint was lodged with the Stated Clerk of the PCA, requesting that the SJC hear the case (ROC p. 1). The present case, 97-4, was filed on February 7, 1997, and referred to a panel which adjudicated the case on November 13, 1997, in Charlotte, NC.

## II. Statement of the Issue

Did the Central Carolina Presbytery act unconstitutionally on January 25-26, 1997, in denying the complaint of July 22, 1996?

## III. Judgment in the Case

No. The CCP did not act unconstitutionally in denying the complaint of June 27, 1996.

## IV. Reasoning and Opinion

Based on the ROC, the complainants did not demonstrate any unconstitutional procedure or violations of the PCA Book of Church Order committed by the Central Carolina Presbytery. The various actions taken by the parties were open to differing opinions and various interpretations, thus resulting in disagreement among the parties. In their oral argument, the Complainants desired to introduce material from previous cases which they believed had a direct bearing on the case. Nevertheless, the panel was limited in the scope of its consideration to those facts properly before us in the Record of the Case. On that basis, the panel’s finding is that no violation of the constitution was demonstrated by the evidence in the case.

This preliminary judgment was written by Jim Smith with the concurrence of TE David Hall and RE Ed Robeson.

<a id="ga26-p131"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=133 printed_page=131 -->

V. Vote on Decision
Approved by SJC: 21 Concurring, 0 Dissenting, 1 Recused and 2 Absent.
4. APPEAL, CASE 97-6

### E. L. FITZSIMMONS

VS.
EVANGEL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Summary of the Facts

The appellant, Mr. Edward L. Fitzsimmons, was a member of the presbyterian
Reformed Church of Pleasant Grove, Alabama. He was also an ordained deacon in that
church. For some period of time prior to May, 1996, Mr Fitzsimmons openly
expressed disagreement with the Session, primarily centering around the handling of the
missions budget and what Mr. Fitzsimmons considered to be the Session’s breach of
duty in failing to make required expenditures from that fund in 1995, and failure to
make a proper accounting of that fund to the congregation. He openly charged the
Session with mishandling the church’s finances
After numerous discussions with Mr. Fitzsimmons, by May, 1996, the Session
had reached the point of initiating formal disciplinary steps against him. On June 19,
1996, the Session charged Mr. Fitzsimmons with “bearing false witness against his
neighbor against the peace, unity and purity of the Church. . . [t]hat beginning August
1995 and continuing to the present time, the said Edward L. Fitzsimmons did falsely
accuse the session members of the presbyterian reformed church of pleasant Grove,
Alabama (both corporately and individually) of improprieties regarding the use of
church monies in written letters sent to various parties” (ROC 104, 105). By letter
accompanying the indictment, also dated June 19, 1996, Mr. Fitzsimmons was notified
that he was temporarily suspended from the Lord’s Table until “the charge against you
can be examined” (ROC 103). He was cited to appear for trial, before the Session, on
July 13, 1996 (ROC 106).
Trial was begun on July 13, but Mr. Fitzsimmons did not appear. The Session
appointed counsel for Mr. Fitzsimmons, and continued the trial proceedings to July 26,
1996 (ROC 49). It is unclear from the record as to whether Mr. Fitzsimmons got actual
written notice of this second trial date, or not. In any event, the Session proceeded to
trial on the charges against Mr Fitzsimmons again on July 26, finding him guilty as
charged (ROC 52, 53). The censure was excommunication.
Mr Fitzsimmons appealed the ruling of the Session by written notice to Evangel
Presbytery on August 6, 1996 (ROC 3, 4). The grounds for the appeal were essentially
as set forth above as the grounds for this appeal to the Standing Judicial Commission.
Evangel Presbytery received a written response to this appeal from the Session of
Presbyterian Reformed Church dated September 4, 1996 (ROC 6-12). On the same
date, the Session filed its record of the case in accordance with BCO 42-5 (ROC 23-
106).
At its regular meeting on September 24, 1996, Evangel Presbytery appointed a
committee to determine whether the appeal was in order (ROC 109). The appeal was

<a id="ga26-p132"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=134 printed_page=132 -->

found in order and a commission was appointed to hear Mr. Fitzsimmons’ appeal (ROC
110 , 111 ).
The Commission met on November 13, 1996, with commissioners Thompson,
Scruggs, Nevins and Scott present (ROC 113). the minutes of the commission do not
show that the appellant was present, nor does the Record show that the appellant was
notified of the meeting. It appears that the Commission likely considered the substance
of the appeal at that meeting, but it voted to “find the appeal out of order” (ROC 113).
The Commission reported this recommendation at the January 28, 1997 meeting
of Evangel Presbytery. At that meeting, Presbytery voted to rescind the previous action
of Presbytery finding the appeal in order and appointing a commission to hear and
adjudicate the case (ROC 117, 118)

#### II. Statement of the Issues

Appellant has set forth certain grounds for appeal, which may be summarized as
follows:
1. Did Presbytery hurry to a decision before reviewing all of the evidence in the
case, or before all the testimony was taken, or without affording the accused the
opportunity to meet with his accusers and defend himself?
2 did the session hurry to a decision before reviewing all of the evidence in the
case, or before all the testimony was taken, or without affording the accused the
opportunity to meet with his accusers and defend himself7
3. Did the Session refuse to set a date and time when appellant could be present at
trial, and if so, did it violate appellant’s rights under BCO 42-3?
4 Was the Session manifestly prejudiced?
5. Did the Session err by inflicting upon appellant the harshest censure of
excommunication under the record of this case?
HI. Judgment in the Case
1. Presbytery erred (a) in failing to notify the appellant of the time, date and place
for hearing the appeal and (b) in failing to adjudicate the appeal, once the appeal
was found to be in order. This case is remanded to Evangel Presbytery under
BCO 42-9.
2. We make no ruling as to Issues 2, 3, 4 and 5, which are properly the subject of
the disposition of this case by Evangel Presbytery on remand.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

Mr. fitzsimmons filed timely appeal to evangel presbytery of the Session’s
ruling against him. This was a proceeding under Chapter 42 of the Book of Church Order, and Presbytery initiated the processing of the appeal in a proper way. BCO 42-8
requires that a higher court must first decide that an appeal is “in order,” and Presbytery
found the appeal to be in order after a committee studied that issue. Also in accord with
BCO 42-8, Presbytery properly exercised its option to appoint a commission to hear the
appeal

<a id="ga26-p133"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=135 printed_page=133 -->

It is at this point that the first error occurs in Presbytery’s proceedings. Nothing
in the record indicates that Mr. Fitzsimmons was notified of the time and place set for
the hearing on the appeal, and the minutes of the Commission’s hearing on the appeal
do not indicate that he was present Rudimentary due process in our church courts
requires that such notice to the appellant and opportunity for the appellant to appear and
prosecute the appeal be given. The record must document the fact that proper notice
has been given, or that it has been waived. This error alone would merit our remanding
this case to Evangel Presbytery for rehearing of the appeal
However, Presbytery erred again at its meeting on January 28, 1997, when it
considered the report of the Commission. At this meeting, a motion was passed to
rescind the former action of Presbytery finding the appeal in order and appointing a
commission to hear it Thus, Presbytery effectively failed to adjudicate an appeal
properly before it. this is contrary to the provisions of BCO 42-8:
“After a higher court has decided that an appeal is in order and should be
entertained by the court, the court shall hear the case, or in accordance with the
provisions of BCO 15-2 and 15-3, appoint a commission to do so. . .The vote then
should be taken, without further debate, on each specification .
It is mandatory that once an appeal is found to be in order, a court adjudicate the
appeal in accordance with BCO Chapter 42. Evangel Presbytery found this appeal to be
in order at its meeting on September 24, 1996, but failed thereafter to fully adjudicate
the appeal. Thus, the case must be remanded to Presbytery for proper adjudication
/s/ TE Robert M. Ferguson, Chairman /s/ TE Ben W. Konopa, Jr
/s/ RE mark belz January 19, 1998.
[This opinion was written by RE Belz and approved by TEs Ferguson and Konopa]

#### V. Voting on Proposed Decision

Adopted by SJC: 17 Concurring, 1 Recused, 6 Absent

5. COMPLAINT, CASE 97-7
STEVE FARRIS
VS.
CENTRAL FLORIDA PRESBYTERY
The full Standing Judicial Commission found that this case was judicially out of
order since the complainant is not a member of a PCA church and, therefore, lacks
standing before the Commission.

<a id="ga26-p134"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=136 printed_page=134 -->

6. COMPLAINT, CASE 97-8
THERESA BRIDIE
VS.
CENTRAL FLORIDA PRESBYTERY
The full Standing Judicial Commission found that this case was judicially out of
order since the complainant is not a member of a PCA church and, therefore, lacks
standing before the Commission.

7. APPEAL, CASE 97-9
ROBERT M. SHIVE
VS.
CENTRAL CAROLINA PRESBYTERY

#### I. Summary of the Facts

Dr. Robert Shive is a psychiatrist by trade. Prior to joining Christ Covenant
Church, Dr Shive had illicit sexual relations with two of his patients. Because of this
unprofessional conduct, he was disciplined by the North Carolina Medical Board
(hereinafter “Medical Board”), which found his past conduct to have been sexually
exploitive of those whom he was entrusted to treat. In his previous practice of
psychiatry, Dr. Shive had employed a psychotherapeutic method referred to as
“bonding,” wherein he had engaged in physical contacts with some patients. This
purely secular method is a controversial practice within the professions of psychology
and psychiatry. As a result of his unprofessional conduct, Dr. Shive lost his medical
license in 1989, but re-obtained the privilege to practice medicine on a limited basis in
1993.
Dr. Shive joined Christ Covenant Church in 1995. He completed a new member
application and interview form on June 26, 1995, and it was processed by the church on
July 11, 1995. The interviewing elder was Pastor Harry Reeder. The form in the record
does not reflect that Dr. Shive signed the confessional and pledge portion. It is unclear
from the Record what was disclosed by Dr. Shive about his past when he was
interviewed by Pastor Reeder. Mr. Chris Dollar, Dr. Shive’s Advocate, stated during
the proceedings, “... the elder who interviewed Dr. Shive for membership met with him
in his home and did have a full disclosure of his background in that interview
process. ..” Pastor Reeder, in his testimony, states that “I’ve met with Mr. Shive one
time at his house when I went there, and he shared with me his past sins.” It is not clear
from the Record when this meeting took place. Subsequently, Dr. Shive was employed
at The Family Connection, a professional counseling center. In January 1996 the
Medical Board expressly restricted Dr Shive from engaging in psychotherapy, but he
was allowed to administer medication and to engage in diagnostic psychiatry.
In the course of his employment at the Family Connection, Dr Shive had
scheduled sessions with two female patients, who were also members of Christ

<a id="ga26-p135"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=137 printed_page=135 -->

Covenant Church. One patient had only one office visit with Dr Shive, this having
occurred on April 1, 1996. The other patient had three office visits in the same time
period, i.e., Spring, 1996. each of the patients had histories of sexual abuse. One had
previously been diagnosed as having a chemical imbalance and as having been
disassociative. She had been recently hospitalized The patients were referred to Dr.
Shive for purposes of diagnosis and medication management.
During those sessions, Dr. Shive used language with the two ladies which the
Session, in its indictment, called pornographic. Dr. Shive claimed that the language
