churches and other ethnic groups within the PCA, as well as with other true
believers in our local communities.
• utilize the resources of church vitality materials and consultation services.
• Recommend to church members that they subscribe (at no cost) to MNA
INTECESSOR AND MULTIPLY
Adopted
9. That Overture #10 from the Northern California Presbytery, Overture #16
from the Central Georgia Presbytery and Communication #8 from Northern
california presbytery concerning changing the boundaries of northern
california presbytery to include th estate of utah and additional counties in
Nevada be referred to the Inter-Presbvterv Committee for Church Development
in the West for its consideration and possible subsequent action by the
Presbyteries. Adopted
Note: Permanent Committee Recommendations #9 [see Appendix I, p. 441)
and #16 (p. 444) were considered by the Committee of Commissioners in
accordance with the manual of operations for committee of commissioners
III C.3 c regarding the grouping of similar overtures.
Grounds:
The Inter-presbytery Committee is in the best position to determine the
expansion o for creation of presbyteries in that thirteen state region. The
Committee, approved by General Assembly and in existence for seven years, is
comprised of 2 members from each of six presbyteries oft he west (Pacific
Northwest, Rocky Mountain, Southwest, Southcoast, Northern California and
Pacific) and has handled such matters in the past.

##### OVERTURE 10 From Northern California Presbytery

“Change boundaries of northern california presbytery to include
Additional Counties in Nevada and Utah”

Whereas, the Presbytery of Northern California has shown itself both
aggressive and effective in church planting, adding 10 churches and
missions from 1991 through 1996 and over 1,000 members, and

Whereas, three of the Northern California Presbytery’s new missions in the last
four years a RE in the state of Utah, which is outside the boundaries of any
presbytery, and

Whereas, the boundaries of northern california presbytery already include the
nevada counties of Douglas, Carson City, Storey, and Lyon and the
nevada counties of Washoe, Churchill, Eureka, White Pine, Elko,
Humboldt, Lander and Pershing are naturally connected by Interstate 80
and US Route 50,

<a id="ga26-p171"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=173 printed_page=171 -->

Therefore be it resolved that the 26th General Assembly ADD THE STATE OF
UTAH AND THE FOLLOWING COUNTIES IN THE STATE OF
NEVADA: Washoe, Churchill, Eureka, White Pine, Elko, Humboldt,
Lander and Pershing to the boundaries of the Northern California
Presbytery.
adopted at the stated meeting of northern california presbytery on february
11, 1998.
Attested by: /s/ Arthur R. Schick, Stated Clerk

##### OVERTURE 16 From Central Georgia Presbytery

“Deny Enlarging Northern California Presbytery”

Whereas, from its beginning, the Presbyterian Church in America has sought to
reach out to the entire nation, and

Whereas, the PCA’s philosophy in doing so has been to restrict geographical
boundaries of pres by teri est oa reasonable size and to allow all of the
presbyteries and churches to have responsibility for spreading the gospel
in the destitute areas of the church, and

Whereas, the 1984 General Assembly stated: “That our presbyteries be
encouraged to limit their boundaries to those geographic areas for which
they are able to take meaningful responsibility in evangelism and church
development, leaving other areas for orchestrated action of all
presbyteries working together through the General Assembly”; and

Whereas, Central Georgia Presbytery ordained a man in March 1998 to be a
church planter in the Provo, Utah, area, and

Whereas, Central Georgia Presbytery, in ordaining this man to undertake this
work, was acting in full accord with encouragement of the General Assembly and the examples of other Presbyteries, and

Whereas, Northern California Presbytery has overtured the General Assembly
to extend its boundaries so that Nevada and all of Utah would be
included within its bounds, thus adding close to 200,000 square miles to
its territory, and

Whereas, if this overture from Northern California were approved, no other
presbytery could lawfully plant churches in this vast area, and

Whereas, Northern California is a small presbytery numerically, as is evident
from the latest available denominational statistics (1,166 communicant
members, thus ranking 43rd out of 49 Anglo presbyteries in communicant
strength; total giving to the presbytery from the churches was
$21,247.00, thus ranking 39th out of 49 Anglo presbyteries), and

Whereas, as adopted by the General Assembly, the minimal ideal for a
presbytery is to have at least three churches with 125 communicant
members, and according to the latest denominational statistics, Northern
California has only two such churches; and

<a id="ga26-p172"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=174 printed_page=172 -->

Whereas, the ideal boundaries for a presbytery, as set forth by the General Assembly, include driving distances of no more than two and a half
hours, and

Whereas, the driving distance across Nevada and Utah along Interstate 80 is
more than 600 miles, and

Whereas, the driving distance between the Nevada-California border and San
Francisco is another 200 miles; and

Whereas, if the Northern California overture were adopted, Northern
California’s boundaries would stretch about 4,000 miles, from the
international dateline in the middle of the pacific ocean to the rocky
Mountains, and

Whereas, Utah has more in common with Colorado than with California, as is
evident culturally and geographically and as reflected in the fact that the
old skyline synod of the presbyterian church in the united states of
America encompasses Colorado and Utah.

Now therefore, be it resolved, that Central Georgia Presbytery hereby
overtures the 26th General Assembly to do the following:
Deny the request from Northern California Presbytery to extend
its boundaries so that the denomination may continue to bring its
entire resources to be aron the destitute regions of the Nation.
Adopted at the Spring Meeting of Central Georgia Presbytery on April 14, 1998
Attested by: Julian H. Davis, Stated Clerk
COMMUNICATION 8 From Northern California Presbytery
“Addendum of Grounds to Northern California Presbytery’s previous
Overture (#10) to the 26th General Assembly on Enlarging its
boundaries, dated February 11, 1998”

Whereas, the guidelines for ideal distances for the boundaries of a presbytery of
two and half hours do not apply well in the barren reaches of territory
between Reno, Nevada - the present eastern border of our boundaries -
and Salt Lake City, Utah, as evidenced by the width of the Southwest
Presbytery boundaries of more than 800 miles, and

Whereas, there is only one population center - Wells, Nevada - with a total of
1,256 people on a 209-mile stretch of that distance, and which contains
the major U.S.missile proving ground and the Bonneville Salt Flats, and

Whereas, the states of Nevada and Utah are more urbanized than the state of
New York, with Utah and Nevada at 87%, having a higher percentage of
city dwellers than New York, at 80%, according to a U.S. Census Bureau
ranking of people who live in places with at least 2,500 people or density
of 1,000 per square mile, and

Whereas, this indicates that the only significant population area that would be
added to the boundaries of northern california would bethe salt lake
City, Utah Metropolitan Area, where the Northern California Presbytery

<a id="ga26-p173"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=175 printed_page=173 -->

presently has 1 organized congregation and 2 mission churches, with 360
people in average worship attendance and growing, and

Whereas, the Northern California Presbytery has plans for 3 additional mission
congregations in the Salt Lake City Metro Area and

Whereas, the PCA teaching elders in Utah have shown their ability to be
meaningfully involved in Northern California Presbytery meetings in
that all 4 of our teaching elders in Salt Lake City have attended our last 2
stated meetings (we have 2 each year) in Northern California and Hawaii
and

Whereas, better oversight and care for future PCA congregations in Salt Lake
can be provided from churches in our Presbytery which are a few miles
away as compared to churches at a much greater distance and

Whereas, the ability of the Northern California Presbytery to plant growing
churches is amply demonstrated at the farthest reaches of what would be
our new boundaries in that we have started 3 churches in Hawaii and 3
churches in Utah in the last 5 years, and that we have seen an increase of
561 communicant members from 1992-1996 and

Whereas, the Northern California Presbytery had the highest percentage
increase (7.5%) of communicant members of any PCA presbytery in the
last statistical year (1996, during which the entire PCA averaged 2%)
and

Whereas, from 1980 to 1995 more than 3 times as many people migrated from
California (180,000) to Utah than the second must popular source of
immigration (60,000 people moved from Colorado to Utah in that
period) and

Whereas, in the last five years, the migration from California to Utah has been 5
times the number of immigration from Colorado, with more people
actually relocating from the states of Texas, Arizona and Idaho to Utah
in recent years than they did from Colorado and

Whereas, the Northern California Presbytery knew of no interest by any other
PCA pres byte ryi nth estate of utah when we drew up our overture
adopted at the called meeting of northern california presbytery on june 6,
1998 Attested by: /s/ Arthur R. Schick, Stated Clerk
10. That Overture #7 from Central Florida Presbytery requesting division of the
presbytery and the formation of the North Florida Presbytery be approved.
Adopted

##### OVERTURE 7 From the Presbytery of Central Florida

“Change Boundaries to Establish North Florida Presbytery”

Whereas, a presbytery confined to a smaller geographic area can lead to more
efficient oversight and cooperation. As our presbytery group has grown

<a id="ga26-p174"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=176 printed_page=174 -->

larger, it has experienced a declining spirit of unity and has become
nessarily less personal

Whereas, the history of Central Florida Presbytery supports the notion that a
healthy division can produce growth.

Whereas, greater regionalization will allow for greater focus on the unique
needs of a particular area

Whereas, our present presbytery’s geographic expanse is too large for efficient
involvement especially when Saturday meetings are involved.

Whereas, smaller presbyteries should equal shorter meetings.

Whereas, newer presbyteries will allow for fresh approach and possibly a better
approach to “doing” presbytery.

Whereas, greater geographic ownership in church planting should lead to
greater involvement and investment.

Whereas, this division meets all the guidelines established by the PCA for the
formation of a new presbytery.

Therefore be it resolved that General Assembly at its July 1998 meeting divide
the presbytery of central florida and form the north florida
Presbytery to include all churches and missions found within the
borders of Hamilton, Suwanee, Lafayette, Dixie, Columbia, Baker,
Union, Alachua, Gilchrist, Nassua, Duval, Bradford, Clay, Putnam, St.
Johns, Flagler counties and churches and missions affected are, Christ
Church (Jacksonville), Northshore (Jacksonville), Ortega (Jacksonville),
Pinewood (Middleburg), Ponte Vedra (Ponte Vedra), Redeemer
(Jacksonville), Westminster (Jacksonville), Cross Creek Mission (Fruit
Cove), Community (Live Oak), Faith (Gainesville, Good News (St.
Augustine), Grace Mission (Palm Coast) and Community (McIntosh,
within the borders of Marion county).
adopted at the stated meeting of presbytery of central florida on january 20,
1998 Attested by: /si Donald L. Mountan, Stated Clerk

Defeated

<a id="ga26-p175"></a>

That MNA Campus Ministries be directed to give highest priority to

14. That the General Assembly adopt the 1999 MNA budget when presented by the
committee of commissioners of the administrative committee and commend
such to the churches for their support. Adopted under the AC report
15. That the General Assembly approve the following response from the MNA
Committee to the exceptions taken to the MNA Minutes of June 1997, as
reported by the Committee of Commissioners for the Committee on Mission to
North America at the 25th General Assembly. Adopted
Exception a.
At several points (10-96-24 and 10-96-45 and 47) a teaching elder’s
status is changed. Such changes in terms of call need to be approved by the
man’s presbytery. This follows from BCO 20-1 and the precedent of the 23rd
ga with regard to the changes in terms of call for missionaries. There is no
evidence in the minutes of such involvement on the part of the relevant
presbyteries. This is especially serious in 10-96-45 where MNA “will dissolve
(a TE’s) pastoral relationship immediately” if a support deficit continues. The
TE’s presbytery must concur in such a dissolution. (BCO 23-l,cf.20-l).
the MNA committee is aware that all actions affecting the call of a
Teaching Elder must be approved by his presbytery, and the MNA Committee
operates within that understanding. In the cases cited, there is simply a failure
to note this understanding explicitly in the minutes. In the future, such motions
will include appropriate language, such as “pending presbytery approval, ” or
"with the concurrence of the presbytery. "
Exception b
while the minutes of the committee reflect the adoption of the various
recommendations to General Assembly, there is no evidence in the minutes that
the Committee ever saw or approved the “Report of the committee on mission
to North America,” nor that they authorized anyone else to write or approve the
report. if it is a committee report it needs to be adopted by the Committee.
(Robert’s Rule 50).
omission of approval of the full MNA report to the 25th GA was a
clerical error in the recording of the minutes. MNA March 1997 committee
meeting agenda materials include a printed recommendation for review and

<a id="ga26-p176"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=178 printed_page=176 -->

approval of the GA Report by the MNA Committee. Several MNA staff members
distinctly remember a motion for approval of the GA Report being passed. In
the process of producing the minutes this motion was somehow omitted.
Greater care will be taken in the future to prevent this error.
Exception c.
The Committee has one serving as Secretary who is not a member of the
Committee. Grounds: RAO 4-10.a. requires that the chairman of a
subcommittee must be a member of the permanent committee. It is irregular
that a person not elected by the GA to serve on the MNA Committee is serving
a san officer of the Committee. RAO 4-16 states that the secretary is an officer
of the permanent committee
the MNA committee makes extensive use of the provision in the RAO for
using co-opted members in the work of the Committee (RAO 4-10). The
secretary in question was such a co-opted member who had previously served as
an elected member and who served as secretary to the Committee during the
time of his elected term. In view of his faithfid and efficient service as secretary,
the Committee continued to elect him to this post , beginning several years ago,
even though he was a co-opted member. For reasons unknown, no one on the
MNA committee or previous committees of commissioners questioned this
practice. The MNA Committee acknowledges its error, and will take care to
make sure that the Committee elects its officers only from among the elected
members.
16. That Overture 17 from Central Georgia Presbytery, “Investigate Worship
Practices in Mission Churches in Utah”, and Overture 28 from Grace
Presbytery expressing support for Overture 17 be answered in the negative
based upon the assurances set forth in the MNA memorandum as follows.
Adopted as amended
June 29, 1998
To: MNA committee of Commissioners, 26th General Assembly
From: Philip Douglass, 1998-1999 MNA Chairman; Al LaCour, 1996-1998
MNA Chairman, and Cortez Cooper, MNA Coordinator
Over the last several weeks, we have labored to find the best ways to
address the issues that arise out of overtures 10, 16, 17 and 28. Our desire is
that MNA faithfully serve the General Assembly.
At the time of our MNA Committee Conference Call on June 1, it
appeared that the issues were primarily between Central Georgia Presbytery and
MNA/GA, with Grace Presbytery affirming that these issues needed to be
addressed. After that, considerably more public attention was brought to the
situation. Therefore we seek appropriate ways to respond further.
As MNA Committee Chairmen and Coordinator, we pledge ourselves to
take the following actions. In so doing, we speak only for ourselves, since there

<a id="ga26-p177"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=179 printed_page=177 -->

was not time for the MNA Committee to meet and deliberate further We pray
for the Lord’s leading in the challenges before you as a Committee, and we offer
to do whatever we can to assist you in the course of your work. In all of this, we
pray for a good spirit in dealing with difficult issues together, and we pray for
the advancement of the Lord’s Kingdom.
Answering the Overture in the negative in no way provides MNA with
an evasion of responsibility. Rather, it simply seeks to remand the conflict and
confusion to its original sources, so that the process becomes manageable and is
dealt with biblically in accordance with Matthew 18. We pledge ourselves to
lead the MNA Committee and Staff in doing everything possible with Central
Georgia Presbytery to address and resolve the grievances that provoked the
overtures. Committee members, as well as Staff, will be directly involved in
this process.
MNA requires the adherence of church planters and staff to the
following guidelines regarding worship (excerpted from MNA “Church
Essentials” document):
“The church is committed to the necessity of biblical worship and
the spiritual growth process of its people. The church is led in vita!
worship that balances the vertical and the horizontal the transcendence
and the immanence of God, and the private and corporate aspects of
worship. ”
This is minimally demonstrated by the following:
• The Pastor takes ultimate responsibility for the content and
atmosphere of the worship service.
• Due to the high priority of worship in a biblical church, worship is
conducted at the “prime time, ” that is, on the Lord's Day.
• The people are encouraged and trained in private and family
worship.
• Worship considers the “unchurched” and seeks to make that activity
intelligible in order that non-Christians may be drawn towards it. ”
These guidelines, as used in submission to the Scriptures and the
subordinate standards, are good and sufficient for directing the development of
worship in a mission church. We note that the overtures do not spell out
specifics about the worship of new Song. We note further that the information
most commissioners may have on the worship of New Song has come through a
video tape and printed materials that have no standing in the church courts.
Nevertheless, based on such reports, it appears that the MNA Committee may
not have adequately required New Song’s original church planter to adhere to
these guidelines.
Therefore, we pledge ourselves to devise regular procedures by which
mission churches under M NA’s guidance are reviewed periodically as to these
guidelines, and to require such remedies as may be necessary to bring them into
conformity.
Since New Song Salt Lake is no longer under MNA/GA support or
supervision, we cannot perform such a review for New Song. However, we

<a id="ga26-p178"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=180 printed_page=178 -->

would hope that Northern California Presbytery would do so. We believe the
above portion of the MNA “Church Essentials” document provides a helpful
guide in working with these mission churches.
We pledge ourselves to reaffirm the following administrative directive to
the MNA Staff and to bring to the October 1998 meeting of the MNA
Committee a recommendation for reaffirmation of the following: We reaffirm
that all of our Staff, Regional Coordinators and Multicultural Movement
Leaders (referred to as “MNA Staff’ from here on) are servants of MNA/GA
and our presbyteries, and possess no authority in themselves as individuals.
They have the responsibility to coordinate church planting ministries of
presbyteries and MNA/GA. In keeping with our theological belief that the Lord
of the church governs us through a plurality of elder sunder the authority of the
Scriptures and our Confessional statements and not through individuals, MNA
Staff are executors of policy determined by presbyteries and MNA/GA, and are
not originators of policy. MNA Staff pledge the following, “I am committed to
developing a church consistent with the above [MNA “Church Essentials”
document] and would welcome periodic evaluation and accountability in that
regard.” In addition, MNA Staff are submissive to their ordination vows -
including vows of approval of biblical polity and subjection to brothers in the
Lord.
In conclusion, we reiterate our desire to submit to the Assembly and our
commitment to provide the leadership to the MNA Committee and Staff that is
necessary to carry out Assembly policy and direction. This includes giving such
guidance to our church planters as is essential to ensure that they conduct
worship in a biblical manner. At the same time, we pray that our brothers will
not put upon MNA a task that the Assembly itself has been unable to perform,
namely, the task of eliminating confusion and conflict as to what biblical
worship is or is not. Conflicts and questions related to worship abound in our
presbyteries and our General Assembly. We will submit to our brothers, but
MNA cannot resolve issues that the Assembly has been unable to resolve. We
pray for patience with one another as we seek the Lord’s direction in being
faithful to Him in what is surely the crown jewel of our Christian life - the
worship of our great God.

##### OVERTURE 17 From Central Georgia Presbytery

“Investigate Worship Practices in Mission Churches in Utah”

Whereas, Central Georgia Presbytery ordained a man in March 1998 to be a
church planter in the Provo, Utah, area, and

Whereas, it has now come to the attention of central georgia presbytery that
some of the PCA mission churches in the utah area are introducing into
worship many questionable practices, including what might be called an
mtv format of rock music, film clips, and video, and

<a id="ga26-p179"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=181 printed_page=179 -->

Whereas, affirming the legitimacy of such practices has been made a test for
service by some in the western region of our denomination, including
those in positions of authority; and

Whereas, one of the prime examples of the type of ministry with which our
evangelist would have had to work is the New Song Salt Lake; and

Whereas, New Song Salt Lake’s approach to ministry can be seen in its internet
website which featured pictures of the pastor and pastoral assistant on
Beavis and Butthead torsos; and

Whereas, New Song Salt Lake regularly employed alternative rock music as
part of “public worship”, and

Whereas, among the lyrics which have been offered in “public worship” are the
following: “What will people think when they hear that I’m a Jesus
freak?/What will people do when they find that that’s true?/I don’t really
care if they label me a Jesus freak/There is no disguising the truth”, and

Whereas, at worship services film clips from secular movies, such as Soul
Food, have been shown, including extremely offensive profanity, and

Whereas, at least two people, including a youth worker from a congregation of
the liberal Presbyterian Church (United states of America), have been
regularly leading worship and supplying the pulpit at New Song Salt
lake even though neither of them has regular ministerial credentials
which would qualify them for that work, and

Whereas, many in the PCA would be offended to know that their benevolence
dollars are being used to promote the type of worship as described
above,

Now therefore, be it resolved, that Central Georgia Presbytery hereby
overtures the 26th General Assembly to do the following:
1 Investigate whether or not such practices have been promoted as
a regular part of the worship of PCA Missions in Utah, and
2. investigate whether or not acceptance of such practices has
become, whether implicitly or explicitly, a requirement for
service in the western region of our denomination, and
3. Remove from employment any MNA personnel who have in any
way encouraged the type of “worship” displayed at New Song
Salt Lake, or who have encouraged making or affirming the
legitimacy of such a test for service, or who, knowing what was
going on, acquiesced in it by failing to bring such to the attention
of the church-at-large
Adopted at the Spring Stated Meeting of Central Georgia Presbytery on April
14,1998 Attested by: /s/ Julian H Davis, Stated Clerk

##### OVERTURE 28 From the Presbytery of Grace

“Expression of support for overtures 16 and 17 Re Enlarging Pacific
Presbytery and Investigating Worship Practices”

Whereas, Grace Presbytery is interested in the work of the church-at-large, and

<a id="ga26-p180"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=182 printed_page=180 -->

Whereas, we are part of a connectional church which seeks to spread into the
entire United States of America; and

Whereas, questionable worship practices have apparently been introduced into
various mission work sunder the sponsorship of the denominational
Mission to North America (MNA) Committee, including the use of
alternative rock music and film clips from secular movies; and

Whereas, at least one of the reasons why our denomination was formed in 1973
out of the Southern Presbyterian Church is because of the experimental
worship that was being sponsored by the Southern Presbyterian home
missions board, and

Whereas, the continuation of such worship practices in our mission works
threatens denominational unity and is offensive to many in the church
who contribute benevolence dollars to the MNA Committee, and

Whereas, Northern California Presbytery has overtured the General Assembly
to expand its bounds to include most of Nevada and all of Utah so that no
other presbytery could plant churches in that area; and

Whereas, this expansion of territory could have a detrimental effect on the
efforts by the church as a whole in the planting of traditional churches in
that vast area, and

Whereas, one of our sister presbyteries, Central Georgia, has adopted two
overtures with regard to these matters;

Now Therefore be it resolved, that Grace Presbytery hereby expresses its
sympathy with and support for the overtures from Central Georgia and
urges the 26th Presbyterian Church in America General Assembly to
adopt them
adopted by the presbytery of grace in its 91st Stated Meeting on the 12th of
May, 1998 Attested by: /s/ Eugene C. Case, Stated Clerk
17. That the following MNA-GA Permanent Committee response adopted at the
June 1, 1998 meeting be approved as the answer to the request from the 25th
General Assembly Adopted
RE: Guidelines for Dividing Presbyteries
That in answer to the request from the 25th GA that MNA/GA work
together with the Stated Clerk’s office to define a process for dividing or
creating new presbyteries which would include the presbyteries and the churches
that will be affected by the change in boundaries ( M25GA , 25-34, III , 11,
p. 162), proposed Guidelines for Dividing Presbyteries were presented to the
MNA Committee at its October 1997 meeting. The Guidelines were adopted
and presented to the Stated Clerk’s office. See Attachment H which follows:
Guidelines for Dividing Presbyteries
As presbyteries grow in the number of congregations and membership they
include, there may come a time at which division of the presbytery maybe

<a id="ga26-p181"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=183 printed_page=181 -->

helpful. Presbyteries should take care that they do not divide prematurely,
causing one or more of the resulting pres by teri es to lack the resources necessary
for their future growth. On the other hand, a presbytery may become so large
that it cannot give adequate attention to the needs of the churches and ministers
within its own membership, and may find even its efforts at church planting and
other growth in ministry difficult.
The 16th General Assembly (1988) established guidelines for the division of
presbyteries, presented below unchanged These guidelines are sufficient in
addressing most situations.
Special care should be taken to ensure that the division of a presbytery is not
made in haste or without adequate consideration of the needs of all parties
involved. Therefore, at th every earliest stages of discussion of a possible
division, those initiating the discussion: (a) should take care to ensure that all
churches and teaching elders (including missionaries and other out of bounds
members) who will possibly be affected are fully informed of the discussion as
early as possible; (b) should communicate with the stated clerk of the
presbytery, who in turn should communicate with the entire presbytery, (c)
should be encouraged to contact General Assembly Mission to North America
and the General Assembly Stated Clerk very early in the process as well, for any
assistance they may be able to offer in making a smooth transition and in giving
advice that may be helpful to the planning process, and (d) should target the first
meeting of a presbytery in the summer or fall of the year so that they may be
able to fully participate in the nominating process of General Assembly without
undue delay.
(Following are Guidelines taken from the MI6GA 1988, p. 143, 16-63, III, 16-
17.)
(1) a presbytery should have a radius of 2 1/2 hours maximum driving
distance.
(2) a presbytery should have a minimum of 10 churches.
(3) a presbytery should have a total communicant membership of at least
1000 .

(4) Presbytery boundaries should not partition metropolitan areas
(5) A presbytery should have regional cohesiveness.
(6) A presbytery should have at least 3 churches each having a membership
of at least 125 communicant members.
(7) Presbytery boundaries should be such that its member churches have a
potential for shared ministries.
(8) Presbytery boundaries should be such that its member churches have a
common commitment to the region within the boundaries and sense their
shared responsibility to cover the region with the Gospel.

<a id="ga26-p184"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=184 printed_page=null -->

(9) When a presbytery reaches 30 churches, it should
consider whether subdivision would lead to more effective
ministry.
Reasons
(i) It is often to the advantage of very large presbyteries to
subdivide for more efficient oversight and closer cooperation
(ii) additional presbyteries are urgently needed inmost of the
United States, other than the South, and in Canada in order to
make the presbyterian system of government operate efficiently.
A presbytery in an area where the PCA is not particularly
strong could be viable with as few as ten churches, and even with
less. (The first presbytery in this land had but seven.)
(10) A presbytery should limit its boundaries to that geographic area for
which it is able to take meaningful responsibility for evangelism and church
development.
Reason M12GA, 12-67, 111,8
(11) We acknowledge the existence of language presbyteries.
(12) We recognize the “ideal nature of guidelines such as these and
understand that several existing presbyteries do not presently meet all of
them.
Approved by the MNA Committee
Oct 97
That Communication #9 from Northern California Presbytery concerning the
appropriateness of Overture 17 from Central Georgia Presbytery be received as
information Adopted
COMMUNICATION 9 From Northern California Presbytery
“A written point of order to the appropriateness of overture 17 of
Central Georgia Presbytery”
It was moved, seconded and passed at the Northern California Presbytery’s
called meeting of June 6, 1998, to send this written objection to Central
Georgia’s Overture 17 on grounds that it is out of order.
The gravamen of Overture 17 is complaining about the work of the court of the
church and is an allegation of offenses directed towards a Presbytery. Such
matters need to be handled under the appropriate rules of discipline of our
BCO [43-1, 43-2, 34-1] Additionally, the use of an overture to complain of
actions of courts or persons is in violation of scriptural principles such as
Matthew 18.

<a id="ga26-p183"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=185 printed_page=183 -->

Adopted at the Called Meeting of Northern California Presbytery on June 6, 1998.
Attested by: /s/ Arthur R. Schick, Stated Clerk

[Stated Clerk’s NOTE: This communication would also apply to Overture 28. PRG]

made to CTS, the PCA and the Church at large. Her contributions in the training of

and the general building up of the church and the spreading of the message of the love
and grace of god has led the trustees of c ts to express their deep gratitude and sincere

<a id="ga26-p185"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=187 printed_page=185 -->

TE Chapell referring to a separate action by the Trustees of CTS and read a resolution expressing thanks to God for the ministry of TE Kennedy Smartt and establishing a Chair of Ministry and Evangelism in his honor.

Whereas, the Reverend Kennedy Smartt has ministered in over 800 of the Presbyterian Church in America’s 1350 churches,

Whereas, the Reverend Kennedy Smartt has exercised godly leadership as a distinguished churchman in the building up of Christ’s body, the Church,

Whereas, the Reverend Kennedy Smartt has sought to extend the borders of God’s kingdom by the power of His enabling grace and with a heart for the lost,

Whereas, the Reverend Kennedy Smartt has testified to the power of God’s munificent grace in his own life and work in evangelism, pastoring, preaching and church extension,

Whereas, we are humbled by the sight of a faithful servant powerfully used by the hand of Almighty God,

Be it Resolved, that the Board of Trustees of Covenant Theological Seminary authorizes The Reverend Kennedy Smartt Chair of Ministry and Evangelism, as a tribute to God’s grace in the life of an important founding father of the Presbyterian Church in America.

Be it Also Resolved, that the Board of Trustees of the national Seminary of the Presbyterian Church in America, Covenant Theological Seminary, does wholeheartedly rejoice in the future prospect of so honoring God’s work through one of His servants, the Reverend Kennedy Smartt. Praise ye the Lord!

TE Robert Rayburn closed the report with prayer and the Assembly rose to sing “All for Jesus”.

Mark Hotel, St. Louis, Missouri, for the purpose of examining Dr L Roy Taylor for the
position of Stated Clerk of the General Assembly After a very thorough examination,
the committee recommended that Dr Taylor be elected by the GA as the new Stated
Clerk.
The committee also examined RE Harry Pinner (Planning), Mr. Scott Raymond
(Student Affairs), and Mr Robert Littlejohn (Academics) for positions as Vice
Presidents at Covenant College. Their examinations being sustained they were
recommended to assume their duties as Vice Presidents at the College.

1998), the following individuals were examined and approved:

Jim McKee, Assistant to the MTW Coordinator

“The Assembly as a body express our repentance for the rude disregard we have
shown toward our brothers and sisters at CE&P, for whom we are grateful
before God, as they attempted to address this Assembly this morning at 11:00
AM, yes even for the contempt that we have shown for Christ, our gracious
Savior and gentle Lord, during the singing of His praise in worship yesterday
afternoon at 4:45 PM, and thus also express our resolution to be more charitable
in the future both toward the brethren and toward our loving God ”

#### I. Business Referred to the Committee

Overtures 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 12, 14, 15, 18, 20, 21, 23, 24, 25, and 26;
Communications 4 and 10

Chaplains and Military Personnel (relative to Overture 26)

#### II. statement of major issues discussed

See above.

#### III. Recommendations

1 That Overture 3 from New River Presbytery be answered in the negative.
Adopted
Grounds: The current practice is consistent with the historic difference between
boards and agencies on the one hand and committees on the other. The agencies
and boards named were granted privileges with respect to nomination as an
exception to a prudentially adopted rule, which rule is designed to avoid an
impetus to committee perpetuation The exception was granted based upon the
peculiar character and responsibility of agencies and boards, as distinguished
from committees, which distinction the overture does not appear to recognize.
Obviously varied treatment grounded in real differences does not amount to the
reprobated partiality referred to in the overture. It is ironic to have the fact that
an exception to a rule was granted be later used as aground to object to the rule
altogether.

<a id="ga26-p190"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=192 printed_page=190 -->

##### OVERTURE 3 From New River Presbytery

“Amend RAO 4, Adding New Section 18, Allowing Nominations
From Committees and Agencies”

Whereas, Article VI of the Bylaws of the PCA permits the Boards of Trustees
of Insurance, Annuities & Relief, Covenant College; Covenant
Theological Seminary and the PCA Foundation to make requests to the
Presbyteries to nominate specific men to their respective boards, and

Whereas, article vi of the bylaws of the PCA permits the boards of trustees
of Covenant College, Covenant Theological Seminary and the PCA
Foundation to submit to the General Assembly Nominating Committee
letters of recommendation concerning particular nominees for their
respective boards, and

Whereas, past actions of the general assemblies of the PCA in approving the
procedures mentioned above presumes that these Boards have good and
valid reasons to be given such permission, and

Whereas, the Bible clearly teaches that there should be no partiality shown in
the work of the church (e.g. Deut. 1:17, 10:17; Mark 10:35-45; 1
Timothy 5:21), and

Whereas, the Book of Church Order (14-1-3) spells out this Biblical principal
when it states: “The work of the Church as set forth in the Great
Commission is one work, being implemented at the General Assembly
level through equally essential committees” (emphasis added), and

Whereas, other Committees and Boards of the PCA may feel that permission to
communicate with Presbyteries and with the General Assembly
Nominating Committee would better enable them to carry out the duties
assigned to them by the General Assembly,

Therefore Be it Resolved that NEW RIVER PRESBYTERY, meeting at New
Martinsville, West Virginia on the 15th day of November, 1997, does
hereby overture the 26th General Assembly to add a new Section 4-18 to
the rules of assembly Operation, as follows:
“Any Committee or Agency of the PCA whose members are
elected by the General Assembly through the report of the assembly
Nominating Committee may, at its own discretion, make requests to the
Presbyteries to nominate specific men to said Committee/Agency and
may submit to the Assembly Nominating Committee letters of
recommendation concerning particular nominees from the Presbyteries.
such requests and recommendations must be made by vote of the full
committee/board of trustees and signed by the Chairman or his designee.
Notwithstanding these requests and recommendations, the Presbyteries
remain free to nominate men of their own choice and the nominating
committee is free to present nominees to the General Assembly of their
own choice.”

<a id="ga26-p191"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=193 printed_page=191 -->

2. That Overture 23 from Tennessee Valley Presbytery be answered in the
affirmative with the following revision. Adopted

##### OVERTURE 23 From the Tennessee Valley Presbytery

“Amend RAO 14-7,c,3 to Require Citing Specific BCO Reference for
Exceptions taken by CRPR”

Whereas, during the proceedings of the General Assembly when Exceptions of
Substance are noted without the citation of specific Scriptural or BCO
references, confusion of interpretation causes a lack of definitive action,
and

Whereas, the endorsement of such an Exception to Substance could represent
careless activity on the part of the General Assembly;

Therefore we respectfully request that Rules of Assembly Operation 14-9,c,3 be
amended to require that whenever Exceptions of Substance are reported
to the General Assembly by the Committee on Review of Presbytery
Records, the report shall in every case, include the Committee’s rationale
for the exception of Substance. In addition, whenever an apparent
violation of the constitution of the PCA is the reason for the exception
of the Substance, the Committee must cite specific Scriptural and BCO
references, that RAO 14-7.c.3 be amended by adding after the words
“General Assembly," the phrase “which presentation shall include
citation of any relevant Scriptural and or Constitutional references, and
provide the Committee's rationale for finding the exception of
Substance. ”
3. That Overture 5 from Central Carolina Presbytery be answered in the
affirmative with the following resolution, and that Overture 18 be answered
with reference to this answer to Overture 5.

Resolved, That the 26th General Assembly erect an Advisory Committee to study
the exegetical, hermeneutical, and theological interpretations of Genesis 1-3 and
the original intent of the Westminster standards ’ phrase ‘in the space of six
days'. the committee of eleven (11) to be appointed by the Moderator within
30 days of the close of the 26th General Assembly, from nominees submitted to
him by the commissioners to the General Assembly. The committee will report,
D.V., to the 27th General Assembly its findings, along with its non-binding
advice and counsel, if any. the committee is to be funded up to $12,000 by
designated donations to the cause. Adopted as amended (521-371)

Grounds:
1. While we appreciate the majority report’s desire that matters such as
these be determined through judicial appeal, yet it has often been the
expressed opinion of many in our assembly that matters such as these ought to
be committed to study groups. Indeed, the majority report raises this means:

<a id="ga26-p192"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=194 printed_page=192 -->

“Further, a study committee could be properly employed with respect to the issues raised by the overtures, were it assigned to study and make recommendations as to whether amendments to the Standards is advisable.”

2. The majority report views that such recommendations would be inexpedient at this time. Yet it is our view that this is indeed the time. Given our last Assembly’s deliberations regarding the Report of the Committee on Review of Presbytery Records, coupled with the minority report to the SJC case 97-5, and these overtures, along with communications to the churches from various subordinate courts and from individuals, all point to the need that this matter must be addressed through all the means available, to include this proposed study committee.

3. We believe the means we propose will be useful to further the purity and peace of the church, and to encourage one another unto “faith and good works” as we face the egalitarian and evolutionistic spirit of the age.

This was presented as a Minority Report prepared by:

|  /s/ TE John O. Butler | /s/ TE Donald Stone | /s/ RE Sam Duncan  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  /s/ TE Scott Reiber | /s/ TE Paul Frick | /s/ RE Frederick Neikirk  |
|  /s/ TE Charles Baldini | /s/ TE Ted Trefsgar | /s/ RE Wilson Barbee  |
|  /s/ TE Mark Duncan | /s/ RE Clyde Herron |   |

**NOTE:** The Majority Report was as follows:

That Overture 5 from Central Carolina Presbytery be answered in the negative, that Overture 18 from Westminster Presbytery be answered in the negative, and that the Assembly address the presbyteries as follows:

Fathers and Brothers,

While acknowledging the great importance of the issues raised by the Overtures, and lauding the evident concern for the glory of God and the good of the church manifested therein, the course proposed in the overtures does not appear to be best adapted to achieve an wholesome end. We grant that the questions raised in the overture are increasingly being raised in our church courts, but such brotherly debate need not, of itself, disturb the peace of the church. "Iron sharpens iron, so one man sharpens another." (Prov. 27:17) In our judgment nothing in these discussions suggests that there is a place in the PCA for unbiblical thinking of any sort. Certainly nothing uttered on the floor of our Assembly with respect to this issue, nor in any other of our courts that we are aware of, can be fairly considered an attack on the veracity of God and His Word. In our judgment, by God's grace, we remain as a denomination "true to the Scriptures, true to the Reformed Faith, and obedient to the Great Commission"

The overtures before us, however, suggest some defect in one of the means our Lord uses to preserve this commitment, that is, Confessional fidelity maintained through the agency of our lower courts in their credentialing responsibilities. We are not persuaded. In our judgment the "clear criteria regarding the allowable exceptions to the

<a id="ga26-p193"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=195 printed_page=193 -->

standards" is already well established in Presbyterian polity and practice, i.e., whatever
in the opinion of the court of original jurisdiction does not violate the fundamentals of
the system of doctrine set forth in the Standards, subject to the review of the higher
courts. consider the testimony of one of the founding fathers of american
Presbyterianism, Samuel Miller:
You will, perhaps, ask me, what shall be done by a man who loves the
Presbyterian Church . . . who regards its confession of faith as by far the best,
in its great outlines, and in all its fundamental articles, that he knows; and who
yet, in some of its minor details cannot entirely concur? Can such an one
honestly subscribe, without any previous explanation of his views? I answer by
no means. Ought he, then, you will ask, to abandon all thoughts of uniting
himself with our Church, when he is in cordial harmony with it in all
fundamental principles, and nearer to it, in all respects, than to any other Church
on earth? i again answer by no means. I know of no other mode of proceeding in
such a case as this which Christian candor, and a pure conscience will justify,
than the following: Let the candidate for admission unfold to the Presbytery
before which he presents himself, all his doubts and scruples, with perfect
frankness; opening his whole heart, as if on oath; and neither softening nor
concealing any thing. Let him cause them distinctly to understand, that if he
subscribe the Confession of Faith, he must be understood to do it inconsistency
with the exceptions and explanations which he specifies. If the Presbytery, after
this fair understanding, should be of the opinion, that the excepted points were
of little or no importance, and interfered with no article of faith, and should be
willing to receive his subscription in the usual way, he may proceed. Such a
method of proceeding will best accord with every principle of truth and honor. .
. . [Samuel Miller, The Utility and Importance of Creeds and Confessions:
Addressed Particularly to Candidates for the Ministry (Philadelphia:
presbyterian board of Publication, 1839), reprinted as Miller On Creeds
(Greenville, SC: A. Press, 1987), pp. 98-102 ]
in place of this historic principle of our polity overture 5 seeks to have the
report of a study committee, adopted by a later Assembly, "clarify parameters for
exceptions to the standards regarding the exegetical, hermeneutical, and theological
interpretations of Genesis 1-3." Similarly overture 18 seeks a "proposed statement of
the PCA's beliefs concerning" the issue of creation, presumably for the same purpose.
Such a procedure suggests that an in thesis statement, adopted by a simply majority of a
single Assembly, could have a virtual Constitutional status, for in order for the
statement to be effective, each court responsible to approve the qualifications of officers
would have to be bound by its provisions. However, under our polity no in thesis
statement of an assembly can prescribe how a court of original jurisdiction interprets
the Constitution. For example, in Overture 18 Westminster Presbytery asserts that any
view which departs from what they take to bethe confessional doctrine of creation in
six 24 hour days "strikes at the fundamentals of the system of doctrines et forth in the
Holy Scriptures." A study committee report to the contrary, adopted by the Assembly,

<a id="ga26-p194"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=196 printed_page=194 -->

could provide no ground to require Westminster to alter this judgment On the other
hand, Potomac Presbytery apparently does not believe other than 24 hour day views so
strike at the fundamentals. A study report adopted by the Assembly declaring otherwise
could provide no ground to require Potomac to conform its judgment to the narrower
view. It is not a happy matter for our purity and peace that such divergences exist from
presbytery to presbytery, but the proposed solution cannot accomplish the worthy end
of bringing unity to our witness.
Historically our tradition has insisted that "Nothing is law, to be enforced by
judicial prosecution, but that which is contained in the Word as interpreted in our
Standards." [The Digest of the Acts and Proceedings of the PCUS, 1861-1965, p 7 1 ]
So too the 22nd General Assembly of the PCA declared:
Were this Assembly, in the abstract, to declare either more or less than the
express statements of our Constitution, it could not in that declaration either add
to, nor take away from, what is constitutional with respect to these doctrines and
duties; nor could such a declaration infringe upon the right and responsibility of
Sessions and Presbyteries to interpret and apply the Constitution as they see
best, subject always to the procedures of review and control, complaint and
appeal.
this view is an expression of sanctified common sense and is essential to the
maintenance of our Constitutional order. Were it otherwise, such Assembly declarations
might effectively amend the Constitution in other than the Constitutionally prescribed
process-a process more deliberate, more laborious and therefore safer for the continued
fidelity and stability of our witness.
beyond the deliberations of the court of original jurisdiction, there is only one
proper procedure for dealing with the question of the meaning of our standards with
respect to their application in the qualification of officers: cases determined on appellate
jurisdiction. F.L. Patton's explanation of traditional presbyterian usage may betaken as
representative:
There is no doubt that there is an area of tolerated divergence from the
Confession of Faith. How large that area is will depend upon the degree of
readiness there may be in the Church to move the ecclesiastical courts, and upon
the decisions reached in the court of last resort. Historical students may tell us
what the Church has thought upon the subject, and dogmatic theologians may
tell us what the Church ought to think; but it is only as the General Assembly
decides concrete cases in appellate jurisdiction, and the principle of stared ec isis
may be supposed to govern subsequent deliverances, that the area of tolerated
divergence can be defined, [From the revision of the confession of Faith. Read
before the Presbyterian Social Union, New York, December 2, 1889, p. 6 ]
Study committees and Assembly declarations have their best use with respect to
the didactic ministry of the Assembly, as we have seen in the papers on "Freemasonry"
and "Divorce and Remarriage." Further, a study committee could be properly employed

<a id="ga26-p195"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=197 printed_page=195 -->

with respect to the issues raised by the Overtures, were it assigned to study and make
recommendation as to whether amendment to the Standards is advisable In our
judgment, however, such a recommendation would be inexpedient at this time.
Let us all continue to study these great questions, to engage in debate with
brotherly affection, to hold with strength and humility our own convictions, while
holding on to brothers with whom we disagree in charity and hope. as we continue to
be committed unswervingly to the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God and to
the Reformed Faith, may we "Prove all things, hold fast that which is good." And may
the God of peace Himself sanctify us all entirely, and may we be preserved complete,
without blame at the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ. Faithful is He who calls us, and
He also will bring it to pass (1 Thes 5:21-23)

##### OVERTURE 5 From Central Carolina Presbytery

“Erect a Study Committee on Exegetical, Hermeneutical and
Theological Interpretation of Genesis 1-3”

Whereas, genesis is the fountainhead of the doctrines grace and the covenants
of promise. The apostle Paul bases his exposition of the imputation of
the righteousness of the Second Adam to God’s elect on the federal
headship of the first Adam over the entire human race. Romans 5:17,
“For if by one man’s offence death reigned by one; much more they
which receive abundance of grace and of the gift of righteousness shall
reign in life by one, Jesus Christ.” 1 Corinthians 15:45, “And so it is
written, The first Adam was made a living soul; the last Adam (was
made) a quickening spirit.”

Whereas, interpretations of genesis 1-3 have a direct bearing on the
interpretation of New Testament passages. For example, Genesis 2:17,
“But of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of
it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die ”, has a
direct bearing on the interpretation of the New Testament passage
regarding rule of death in Romans 5:14, “Nevertheless death reigned
from Adam to Moses, even over them that had not sinned after the
similitude of Adam’s transgression, who is the figure of him that was to
come.”

Whereas, The PCA Digest: Position Papers 1973-1993 of the Presbyterian Church in America does not specifically address allowable exceptions to
the standards regarding the exegetical, hermeneutical and theological
interpretations of Genesis 1-3.

Whereas, Examinations of ruling elders, candidates, licentiates and ordained
ministers by Sessions and Presbyteries both in the areas of biblical
knowledge and theology require clear criteria regarding the allowable
exceptions to the standards regarding the exegetical, hermeneutical and
theological interpretations of genesis 1-3.

Whereas, the thursday evening session of the 25th General Assembly
pointedly debated in the report of the review of presbytery records the

<a id="ga26-p196"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=198 printed_page=196 -->

allowable exceptions to the standards regarding the exegetical,
hermeneutical and theological interpretations of Genesis 1-3. It is an
issue affecting the peace of the church.

Whereas, the proper context fora doctrinal debate is in response to a report of
a study committee’s draft of allowable exceptions to the standards
regarding the exegetical, hermeneutical and theological interpretations of
Genesis 1-3.

Therefore, be it resolved, that Central Carolina Presbytery hereby overtures the
26* General Assembly to form a two year study committee funded with
a budget of $10,000 to clarify parameters for exceptions to the standards
regarding the exegetical, hermeneutical, and theological interpretations
of Genesis 1-3.

##### OVERTURE 18 From Westminster Presbytery

“Appoint Study Committee on Creation”

Whereas, the culture in which we live has been permeated with evolutionary
thinking which is an unbiblical and anti-scientific theory of origins,

Whereas, the issue of the veracity of god and his word are under fierce attack
in our day,

Whereas, it is the position of Westminster Presbytery that the Bible teaches a
six day creation, with day defined as an approximately 24 hour period of
time and that Genesis 2 teaches not another creation account but a more
detailed account of Genesis 1 regarding the creation of man on the 6th
day and the creation of the garden of Eden,

Whereas, westminster presbytery holds that the word of god teaches a 24 hour
day because:
1. In Genesis 1:14-19, the Lord God describes the function of the
sun and the moon to delineate the day from the night, this
referring to a portion of a 24 hour period of time,
2. Where the recurring phrase evening and morning occur in the
Scriptures, it always refers to an approximately 24 hour period of
time,
3. the reason given for observance of the 4th commandment in
Exodus 20:11 is base dona 24 hour day view of creation

Whereas, Westminster Presbytery holds that a 24 hour view of the days of
creation is absolutely necessary if,
1. we are to have a biblical view that man is a spiritual (hands on)
creation of God, Genesis 2:7, 21, 22,
2. we are to have a biblical view of the origin of death, Genesis
2:16, 17, Romans 6:23,

Whereas, Westminster Presbytery rejects the following positions as examples of
views out of accord with the Scripture which has been carefully and
accurately exposited in our standards, the WCF, WLC, and WSC:

<a id="ga26-p197"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=199 printed_page=197 -->

1 The ‘gap theory’ which calls for a gap of time before or between
the days of creation,
2. The so called ‘day age theory’ which holds that the term day in
Genesis 1 does not refer to 24 hours, but to long epochs of time,
3. The ‘poetic’ view of Genesis 1 sometimes known as the
‘framework hypothesis’ view which maintains that Genesis 1
does not state history but an allegorical or symbolic declaration
that somehow God, with the details not being important, created
the world. According to this view, the facts and the order of
events of creation are obscured by the poetic, illustrative
language of the text.
Comments on these views:
Note that there is absolutely nothing in the text to even hint, let alone
explicitly declare these views. All through the Scripture the historicity
of creation is referred to Examples include: Exodus 20:8-11,
Deuteronomy 5:12-15, Psalm 33:6-9, Psalm 104:1-6, Matthew 19:4-6,
Romans 5:12-21, Romans 4:17, Hebrews 11:3, 1 Timothy 2:11-14, 1
Corinthians 3:8-12, I Corinthians 15:21-22, 45-47 This does great
violence to the clear teaching of Scripture. These views appear to be
held merely to seek to accommodate the evolutionary view of origins
that postulates millions of years for the earth to appear as it does If
consistently applied throughout the Word of God, this hermeneutic
renders Scripture uninterpretable in an objective, meaningful way.
these views of scripture are perpetrated by the neo-orthodox liberals of
our day. we repudiate it as heresy. it is the same method of perverting
and twisting the Scriptures that has been championed by denominations
to “justify” the ordination of women to ecclesiastical office, to commend
and approve of homosexuality as an acceptable lifestyle, and to permit
sodomites to hold ecclesiastical offices. It is the same hermeneutic that
has been championed by the liberals of our day to twist the Scriptures to
teach that jesus was not born of the virgin Mary, that propitiation by the
substitutionary atonement of our Lord Jesus Christ is a barbaric relic of
primitive man’s thinking, and that the bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ
is mythical. Other casualties of this hermeneutic include: Moses being
the human author of the Pentateuch, the historic i ty of the book of jonah
(his being swallowed by a big fish and being in its belly three days and
nights), the crossing of the Red Sea rather than the reed sea, the
historic i ty of the ten plagues, etc.

Whereas, Westminster Presbytery does declare and make known to the world
and to all churches, especially our own denomination, our churches, our
presbyteries, our General Assembly and the seminaries from which our
candidates arise, that we will not tolerate these views in any teaching
elder seeking admittance to this Presbytery, or any other man seeking to
be licensed or to become a candidate for the ministry under care of this

<a id="ga26-p198"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=200 printed_page=198 -->

Presbytery. Furthermore, Westminster Presbytery considers that any
view which departs from the confessional doctrine of creation in six 24
hour days strikes at the fundamentals of the system of doctrine set forth
in the Holy Scriptures.

Therefore be it resolved that the 26th General Assembly of the PCA appoint a
study committee, comprised of 4 Teaching Elders and 3 Ruling Elders
from 7 different presbyteries fora period of one year, to study the issue
of Creation and to bring to the 27* General Assembly a proposed
statement of the PCA’s beliefs concerning this issue. This Committee
would be funded from the Administrative Committee budget up to
$ 8, 000 .

4 That the Committee advise the Stated Clerk that Communication 4 from
Westminster Presbytery and Communication 10 from Potomac Presbytery be
VACATED on the grounds that they do not conform to RAO 10-1,2. Adopted
5. That OVERTURE 1 from Potomac Presbytery be answered in the affirmative
with the following substitution (see also Committee on Constitutional Business
report, p. 228). Adopted and sent down to presbyteries for advice and consent

##### OVERTURE 1 From Potomac Presbytery

“Amend BCO 13-5 to Clarify Membership of One Honorably Retired”
[Should be entitled “to clarify membership of a teaching elder without call
Who Needs to Relocate”]

Whereas, the requirement of BCO 13-2, “A minister shall be required to hold
his membership in the Presbytery within whose geographical bounds he
resides...” appears in some cases to be in tension with the direction
offered in BCO 13-5, “Ordinarily, only a minister who receives a call to
a definite ecclesiastical work within the bound sofa particular presbytery
may be received as a member of that Presbytery except in cases where
the minister is already honorably retired, or in those cases deemed
necessary by the Presbytery ”;

Therefore, Potomac Presbytery overtures the Twenty-Sixth General Assembly to amend the Book of Church Order 13-5
by adding a new sentence after the words ‘review of the General Assembly.’, as follows:
'In such cases deemed necessary, which may include the case of
a minister without call whose circumstances appear to require
relocation within the bounds of that Presbytery, the time allotment of
BCO 13-2 shall be counted from the day the minister was first continued
on the roll without call in any Presbytery. ’

<a id="ga26-p199"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=201 printed_page=199 -->

So that the amended paragraph reads,

13-5 ‘Ordinarily, only a minister who receives a call to a definite ecclesiastical work within the bounds of a particular Presbytery may be received as a member of that Presbytery except in cases where the minister is already honorably retired, or in those cases deemed necessary by the Presbytery, subject to the review of the General Assembly. In such cases deemed necessary, which may include the case of a minister without call whose circumstances appear to require relocation within the bounds of that Presbytery, the time allotment of BCO 13-2 shall be counted from the day the minister was first continued on the roll without call in any Presbytery’’

[Note: material in italics is the proposed new language in the BCO.]

6. That Overture 14 from SE Louisiana Presbytery be answered by reference to the General Assembly’s action on Overture 1 (see also item 5, above, and Committee on Constitutional Business, p. 229). Adopted

##### OVERTURE 14 From SE Louisiana Presbytery

“Amend BCO 41 to Clarify Ministerial Service Out of Bounds”

Whereas, The Book of Church Order of the Presbyterian Church in America, chapter 46-1, requires the teaching and ruling elders of the congregation to maintain oversight of a member who has moved beyond the bounds of the local church, as far as possible, and

Whereas, the same chapter requires the teaching and ruling elders of the congregation to notify the teaching and ruling elders of a congregation within whose bounds that member has moved, in order that said member be encouraged to transfer his membership, and

Whereas, this is done for the spiritual benefit of such a member who has moved beyond the bounds of his local congregation, and

Whereas, there are many teaching elders, whose membership is help by the Presbytery rather than the local congregation, who are without call and who have been forced by the requirements of supporting their families to move beyond the geographical bounds of the Presbytery holding their membership, and

Whereas, there is no provision in the Book of Church Order for any Presbytery to receive or take oversight of a teaching elder who is without a call, but who resides within the bounds of said Presbytery, and it is impossible for a Presbytery to provide adequate oversight or counsel from a great distance, and

Whereas, it would seem obvious that a teaching elder in such a situation would be in, at least, as great a need of spiritual oversight and support as any other member of the church.

Therefore, we request that a provision be added to the Book of Church Order which would require a teaching elder, without a call, who has cause to move outside

<a id="ga26-p200"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=202 printed_page=200 -->

the geographical bounds of the Presbytery which holds his membership, to request the Presbytery into whose bounds he has moved, to receive him as an associate member of Presbytery, under similar conditions to the Book of Church Order provisions for associate members of a congregation in chapter 46-4. Such associate members of Presbytery shall be examined by the receiving Presbytery as to their views, shall be permitted to fill the pulpits within the Presbytery upon request, and can serve on committees at the discretion of Presbytery, but may not hold office or chair a committee, nor shall he have the right to vote in the Presbytery until such time as he shall receive a call and become a full member of Presbytery. Presbytery shall provide such oversight, care, and counsel as needful until such time as the associate member shall accept another call and be examined and received by the Presbytery within whose bounds such a call originated, or until divestiture from the ministry takes place. Such divestiture should be accomplished by the Presbytery of which said teaching elder is a full member, and can be done either at the instigation of the Presbytery of last full membership, or by request to that Presbytery from the one holding associate membership, or at the request of the teaching elder.

This provision will eliminate the sad experience of those teaching elders who are without call and far removed from the bounds of the Presbytery holding their membership, requiring them to be out of fellowship through no fault of their own.

7. That Overture 2 from Potomac Presbytery be answered in the affirmative with the editorial corrections noted (see also Committee on Constitutional Business, p. 229). Adopted and sent down to presbyteries for advice and consent

##### OVERTURE 2 From Potomac Presbytery

"Amend BCO 20-2 to Clarify Role of Pastor Search Committee"

Whereas, the Book of Church Order does not specify the duties of a pastoral search committee appointed by the congregation;

Whereas, in newly planted churches where members may not be fully acquainted with Presbyterian practices and procedures such a lack of specification can and has led to confusion among some of our churches; and

Whereas, the Twenty-Fifth General Assembly remanded an overture from Potomac Presbytery for refinement, which work is now accomplished.

Therefore, Potomac Presbytery overtures the Twenty-Sixth General Assembly to amend the Book of Church Order 20-2 by adding a new concluding sentence to the second paragraph, after the words "as designated by the congregation (see BCO 25).", as follows: "The pulpit committee shall, after consultation and deliberation, recommend to the congregation a pastoral candidate who, in its judgment, fulfills the Constitutional requirements of that office (e.g., BCO 8, 13-6 and 21) and is most suited

<a id="ga26-p201"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=203 printed_page=201 -->

to be profitable to the spiritual interests of the congregation (cf. *BCO* 20-6).”

8. That Overture 4 from Potomac Presbytery be answered in the affirmative as amended.
*Adopted*

##### OVERTURE 4 From Potomac Presbytery

“Amend *BCO* 13-12 to Clarify Circumstances for a Special Meeting of Presbytery”

Whereas, the Book of Church Order appears to require an emergency for the calling of a special meeting of a Presbytery (13-12. . . When any emergency shall require a meeting earlier than the time to which the Presbytery stands adjourned, the Moderator shall . . . call a special meeting. . .); and

Whereas, the word *emergency* is defined as “a sudden, generally unexpected occurrence or set of circumstances demanding immediate action” (*Webster’s New World Dictionary*, 3rd ed. (1994), s.v.);

Whereas, there are circumstances with respect to which a special meeting of Presbytery might be convenient, but which could not fairly be called an emergency (e.g., to hear the report of a study committee or a special commission, to examine and receive ministers transferring into the Presbytery, etc.);

Therefore, Potomac Presbytery overtures the Twenty-sixth General Assembly to amend the Book of Church Order 13-12 by replacing the second sentences of *BCO* 13-12 with the following:

&gt; The Moderator shall call a special meeting at the request or with the concurrence of two Teaching Elders and two Ruling Elders from at least three different churches.

9. That Overture 12 from the Session of Bedford Presbyterian Church be answered in the negative.
*Adopted*

**Grounds:** The Overture appears to misunderstand the nature of the “rule” referred to in *BCO* 7-2. In our judgment “rule” refers, not to any exercise of authority in the church, but to the overall exercise of government in the church belonging exclusively to the eldership. Clearly some measure of authority (and therefore corresponding obedience on the part of the people) is required in the deacon’s calling to administer and direct the congregation’s efforts in service (cf. Acts 6; I Tim. 3:12; *BCO* 9-2). The current vow is well-adapted to accommodate this truth, in that it requires that obedience “to which *his office*, according to the Word of God and the Constitution of this church, entitles him.” (emphasis added).

<a id="ga26-p202"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=204 printed_page=202 -->

##### OVERTURE 12 From the Session of Bedford Presbyterian Church

“Amend BCO 24-5 RE Congregational Vows respecting Deacons”

Whereas, BCO 7-2 rightly declares “The office of deacon is not one of rule, but
rather of service both to the physical and spiritual needs of the people,” and

Whereas, this means that it is inappropriate to call the congregation to give
obedience to the deacons,

Therefore the Session of Bedford Presbyterian Church overtures the 26th
General Assembly to add in the question to the congregation in BCO 24-5,
after “obedience”, (for deacons substitute ‘and encouragement’ for
‘encouragement and obedience’)”.
REASON: the PCA is a presbyterian and not a baptist denomination.
Forwarded from the Session of Bedford Presbyterian Church, without the
concurrence of presbytery.
10. That Overture 15 from North Georgia Presbytery be answered in the
affirmative by the following substitute (see also Committee on Constitutional Business, p 229).
Adopted and sent down to presbyteries for advice and consent
