26-72 Committee on Thanks
The Assembly adopted the following Resolution of Thanks:
“Hitherto hath the Lord helped us.”
For 25 years the members and officers of the PCA have looked to the lord jesus
for forgiveness of our sins, have depended upon Him for individual and corporate life,
and have prayed for the extension of his kingdom and the in gathering of his church.
This 26th General Assembly expresses gratitude to our Lord for an able 251H
Anniversary committee, chaired by TE Donald B. Patterson and TE Kennedy Smartt,
who prepared for us to celebrate God’s goodness as each committee and agency
reported His grace in their varied activities. Special commendation to TE Paul and
Georgia Settle was also given.
Retiring moderator RE Samuel Duncan ably concluded his term in office.
TE Kennedy Smartt was elected and served admirably as Moderator. In a
poignant hour, TE Don Patterson, unable to attend because of serious illness, was
elected Honorary Moderator. This is a first in the history of our denomination.
a committee of missouri Presbytery, chaired by TE Jim Drexler and TE Bryant
McKee, hosted us grandly in St. Louis, anticipating our needs and providing for our
desires - more people being involved in service than we can possibly mention.
Stated Clerk TE Paul R. gilchrist was honored by expressions of appreciation
for his 10 years of service, and a prolonged standing ovation underscored the

<a id="ga26-p257"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=259 printed_page=257 -->

Assembly’s approval of his ministry as stated clerk and its support of him in his future
plans.
RE Ross Cook will conclude his service as Business Administrator before the
next General Assembly, and this Assembly wishes to express its appreciation for his 10
years of service.
Only those in the Stated Clerk’s Office, led by TE Paul Gilchrist, RE Ross Cook
and Mrs. Laurel De Bert, know the painstaking work necessary to prepare for this
Assembly’s smooth operation.
cooperation of the management and staff of the Adam’s Mark Hotel was
exemplary.
An array of parliamentarians, time keepers, recording clerks, floor clerks,
permanent and temporary staff, and diligent committees contributed.
Preaching, worship, music, and the extremely positive reports of Committees
and Agencies all stir us to praise God for his continued mercy and favor. May that
favor continue to the 27th Assembly in Louisville and to the decades ahead until the
Lord of the harvest returns.
Respectfully submitted,
/s/ RE Dennis Baxter /s/ TE George Long, Chairman
/s/ TE H. R. Patteson Is/ RE John Spencer

The Assembly allowed the Minutes, Twenty-Sixth General Assembly, Session
Eight to be approved by the Stated Clerk and commissioners from the Atlanta area.

On motion, the Moderator declared the Assembly adjourned at 11:40 AM to
reconvene in Louisville, Kentucky on June 15, 1999. The Assembly joined in singing
Psalm 133 and was led in prayer by TE Frank Smith, who pronounced the benediction.

<a id="ga26-p260"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=262 printed_page=260 -->

## REPORT OF THE STATED CLERK TO THE 26TH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Presbyterian Church in America
JUNE 30 -- JULY 3, 1998

### Fathers and Brethren:

The Lord has given me ten good years as Stated Clerk of the General Assembly. How can I thank the Lord for calling me into the ministry, giving me eleven years in the pastorate, 22 years at Covenant College (including 11 years as part time Stated Clerk of the RPC,ES), and now these 10 years with the PCA. Deep down in my soul I have sought to apply the advice given by the wise elders of Solomon to Rehoboam when he succeeded to the leadership of ancient Israel.

&gt; “And they spoke to him, saying, ‘If you will be a servant to these people today, and serve them, and answer them, and speak good words to them, then they will be your servants forever.’”
&gt; 1 Kings 12:7

### I. GUIDING PRINCIPLES

In my report to the AC in March 1998, I summarized some of the specific guiding principles which have helped me to apply the wisdom of Solomon’s elders with respect to servant leadership.

First, and foremost, is total dependence upon a Sovereign God. No one takes on this office without recognizing that there are going to be problems with which you have to deal. This calls for much prayer and faith in a Sovereign God. I have always held to the principle that Jesus Christ is King and Head of the church. This is His church which He is building. There are things that take place or decisions that are made that one may not particularly like but one has to bow to His Sovereign Lordship. He does all things well.

Second, as Stated Clerk I have tried to be accountable to the AC as well as to the General Assembly. Many times I have had to say that I am a man under authority. I am not one to simply tell the committee to simply rubber stamp recommendations. Rather, I have tried to be a servant. I have worked on the basis of team work and collegiality rather than taking an authoritarian stance. I believe that the Lord has honored this through the years.

<a id="ga26-p261"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=263 printed_page=261 -->

Third, with regard to administrative procedures themselves, I learned long ago that an administrator needs to learn to delegate to others and let them do the work. I am grateful for a staff that has worked diligently on my behalf. They have done so much work for me that all too often I get the credit for it when indeed they deserve the credit. Another principle in this category is the principle of hiring better than yourself. I have worked on the basis of honestly evaluating my weaknesses and then hire people who have greater skills and greater strengths in those areas. I believe the Lord has given us such people and particularly I need to mention Ross Cook, who has excelled as a Business Administrator and a great negotiator. Also Laurel De Bert, who has done excellently as Administrative Assistant. More recently, Bob Fiol, Assistant to the Stated Clerk, who has turned out to be far more valuable than I had hoped for. I praise God for these and the rest of our staff: Peggy Little, Monica Johnston, Mary Bradley Bayne, Sue Campana, and more recently Susan Cullen.

Fourth, I have tried to be a servant to others, a servant to the committees and agencies, their coordinators, a servant to the presbyteries and their clerks in particular. I have especially tried to help our Korean brethren. From the perspective of our Korean friends, they are in a foreign land and they feel it very keenly that they are sometimes viewed as second class citizens. In my contact with them, they have been Godly people and a joy to serve. Then finally, I have tried to be a servant to churches, to pastors and to elders. I have tried to assist ministers in looking for a place of service. I have tried to help churches and elders work through all of the discipline problems that they face. I have done that to the best of my knowledge and to the best of my ability but all as a servant to others.

Fifth, in my instructions on the Book of Church Order, I have primarily looked at the BCO as a book of principles for governance. I have held various conferences or retreats for instruction on PCA polity and the BCO. I have been a counselor to many pastors going through difficult times. I have to constantly acknowledge that from a distance it is very difficult to work through some of the particular issues that people are facing.

Sixth, with regard to PCA Polity, the basic principle for me has been the grassroots concept. As many of you know, I wrote an article that showed how Presbyterianism is not just to be contrasted with Congregationalism on the one side or Episcopacy on the other side, but that within Presbyterianism there are two kinds of polity: one is a more hierarchical form of government and the other a more democratic. In my paper, I point out that there is a legitimate place both historically and theologically for the more democratic concept of governance that the PCA adopted in 1973 and which is imbedded in the Book of Church Order.

## II. PUBLICATIONS

Since much of our work goes from General Assembly to General Assembly, the first matter to report is that the Minutes of the 25th General Assembly went out as

<a id="ga26-p262"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=264 printed_page=262 -->

scheduled in September, distributed to all the commissioners and made available
through the CE&P Bookstore here in Atlanta.
The Directory of Churches (often referred to as the “Blue Directory”) was
completed and distributed in February, it was delayed in going to press due to a new
data base and computer upgrade in the Fall.
By June, we finished the work on the 1998 Yearbook which appears in three
volumes. This is a comprehensive directory with information on presbyteries, General Assembly Committees, churches, statistics, and directory of all our ministers. The first
two volumes include the usual statistics of presbyteries and churches, together with the
directories of current ministers, all as reported by clerks of sessions or presbyteries.
The third volume is only added this year for historical purposes in connection with our
25th Anniversary Celebration. It includes a directory of all former PCA ministers as
they appeared in previous Yearbooks It also includes the Succession of Pastors in our
churches. this last section reflects the rich heritage of s oman yo four churches which
were organized in the 19th century, but some even reach back into the 18th century.
The Commissioners' Handbook for the 26th General Assembly went to press
mid-May, including all materials received up to that point; additional materials for
General Assembly commissioners is in the Supplement which is distributed at the
beginning of the General Assembly. Once again it should be noted that churches not
represented at General Assembly have been sent the report of the Standing Judicial Commission and the Special Judicial Commission [Case 95-11], in accordance with the
requirements of the Book of Church Order.
in . Book of Church Order amendments
There were two amendments to the Book of Church Order that were sent down
to presbyteries by the 25th General Assembly for advice and consent. These are listed
on page 251 of the Commissioners ’ Handbook. at the time of this writing both of these
amendments have passed the required two-thirds votes of the presbyteries. They will
need to be ratified by the General Assembly.
IV. STATISTICS FO R 1997
a five year summary of PCA statistics is attached (Table 1, see page 227). For
1997 we are reporting 1171 particular churches and 185 missions. The total number of
churches is up 1% from a year ago to 1356, an increase of 18. This reflects a 8%
growth in the last 5 years.
As for membership, our statistics show a total of 283,381, which includes
ministers, communicant members, and non-communicants The total membership
represents an increase of 2% for the year, and 13 1% over the last 5 years. Both the rate
of growth of churches and membership show a slow down. I would point out that there
were 2345 adult baptisms in 1997 compared to the low of 1912 adult baptisms in 1996

<a id="ga26-p263"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=265 printed_page=263 -->

and a high of 2607 in 1992. This part of our statistics is the only indicator of the PCA’s evangelistic endeavor.

In many ways, the giving patterns show a remarkable growth rate. Total contributions to churches were up 9.7% to over $369.7 million. The pattern of disbursements is interesting: Total benevolences were up 12.3%, congregational current expenses were up 12% and building expenditures were up 14.5% from the previous year. This represents a healthy development since last year when there was a slow down in total benevolences. It is encouraging to see that presbyteries received an increase of 3.4% and that General Assembly causes were increased by 8.6%

Unfortunately, the statistics still continue to be somewhat soft as long as there are churches for whom we have not received recent statistics. For 1995 there were 330 churches and missions not reporting, while in 1996 there were 356. This year there were 346 not reporting including 104 missions. Table 3 (page 229) lists these churches. I would encourage pastors and elders to see to it that these statistics are made available so that we can get a more accurate figure of what God is doing throughout our denomination.

Table 4 (page 234), gives a summary analysis by Presbyteries of churches contributing to General Assembly causes. The number of churches contributing to one or more General Assembly causes dropped to 72% from 82% average for the last 4 years. This means that fewer churches are contributing to General Assembly causes although they have increased their giving to carry on the programs and work of General Assembly. Only AC and Ridge Haven experienced a one or two percentage increase of the number of churches supporting them. A close look at the support by committees or agencies leaves much room for growth and development. Nevertheless, we are very grateful for the churches which have been faithfully contributing to our denominational committees and agencies.

V. STATISTICS: 1973 TO 1998

In addition to the tables and analysis referred to above, we have provided some specific growth statistics for the 25 years that the PCA has been in existence. Tables 5, Numbers of Churches and Missions (page 238), and Table 6 (page 239) give the membership statistics at the end of every five years, by regions and as totals. It will be noted that the first 10 years saw the fastest growth in the church. In the early years, there still was a large number of churches transferring to the PCA from the PCUS. At the end of the first decade, the statistics reflect the influx of churches through J &amp; R (1982) of the Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod. Even during the 3rd five year period there were almost 100 PCUS churches that took advantage of the escape clause to leave PCUSA and join the PCA. But already the growth in churches was the result primarily of MNA's program to organize new churches and missions. This growth by starting our own churches continues through the efforts of MNA as well as presbyteries.

<a id="ga26-p264"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=266 printed_page=264 -->

Table 7 (page 240) shows the contributions by God’s people to the church and
the distribution of benevolences by the churches to General Assembly causes,
presbytery causes, as well as to their own missionary and benevolent causes. These
statistics show the generosity of our PCA people to the work of the Lord. One caveat
may be mentioned: There is no way to estimate how much our PCA people contribute
to other Christian causes directly to those organizations. Heaven alone keeps those
statistics. May the Lord encourage our hearts as we see God's people respond so
generously to Christ and His Kingdom.
VI. NEW CHURCHES SINCE THE 25th GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Table 5 (page 61) lists 18 churches added to the PCA since June of 1997. These
are the ones that have been reported to our office. It should be noted that most of these
have been organized from mission efforts. we welcome all of these. Following the
tradition long established we will give special recognition during the 26th General Assembly of the commissioners representing these churches.
VII. OTHER ASSEMBLY ASSIGNMENTS
The Stated Clerk has sought to be faithful in fulfilling all the responsibilities of
his office and regularly reports to the chairman of the administrative Committee, under
whose supervision he serves. The clerk has attended presbytery meetings, preached in
churches, conducted seminars, responded to multitudinous questions on the BCO and
other General Assembly documents, has conducted meetings of presbytery clerks,
communicating fairly regularly with sessions and friends of the PCA. He has served as
a resource person to various committees in order to enable them to do their work on
behalf of General Assembly.
VIH. TRANSITION
The AC has asked me to give two additional months to the office to allow for
transition with the new Stated Clerk. This will involve concluding much of post
Assembly matters as well as assisting the new Stated Clerk to hit the road running. We
look forward to making this transition as easy as possible.
IX. GRATEFUL ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
I conclude this report expressing once again my sincere thanks to our Living
Lord for enabling me to serve in this office these past years. It has been an honor and a
pleasure assisting the standing committees of General Assembly down through the
years, namely, the Committee on Constitutional Business, the Interchurch Relations
Committee, the Nominating Committee, the Committee on Review of Presbytery
Records, and the Theological Examining Committee.
One would be remiss not to mention the deep appreciation we have for all the
members of the Administrative Committee. It has been a pleasure working with the
officers who have been so good in helping us sort through issues. This expression of
appreciation extends to the subcommittees who have served so faithfully, namely, the
Capital Building Fund Committee, the PCA Historical Center Committee, as well as

<a id="ga26-p265"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=267 printed_page=265 -->

more recently, the 25th Anniversary Celebration Committee. We praise God for every
remembrance of those who have had a part in this ministry.
Let me express grateful appreciation for the hard-working staff in the Office of
the Stated Clerk without whose help it would be impossible to fulfill the duties of the
office. Above all, the clerk gives thanks to the Lord Jesus Christ, the King and Head of
the Church, who wonderfully rules and overrules in the affairs of his people. We
humbly offer the work of our hands and minds to his glory, honor and praise.
Faithfully submitted,

Paul R. Gilchrist, Stated Clerk

## BOARD OF DIRECTORS
Presbyterian Church in America
June 10, 1997

The Board of Directors of the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) held a regular meeting on June 10, 1997 at the Colorado Springs Marriott hotel, Colorado Springs, Colorado.

The meeting was convened at 2:01 p.m. by the Chairman William F. Joseph, Jr.; TE Steve Meyerhoff led in prayer.

A quorum was declared present with the following in attendance:

|  TE Tim Diehl, Heartland | RE Dwight Allen, CC  |
| --- | --- |
|  TE William A. Fox, Jr., Fellowship | RE Howard Q. Davis, Covenant  |
|  TE R. Grady Love, N. Georgia | RE Edwin Hackenberg, SE Louisiana  |
|  TE G. Fredric Mau, Covenant | RE Robert “Neal” Ham, PCAF  |
|  TE D. Steven Meyerhoff, Westminster | RE William F. Joseph, Jr., SE Alabama  |
|   | RE Ralph Mittendorff, S. Florida  |
|   | RE John E. Spencer, CTS  |
|   | RE Gerald Sovereign, MTW  |
|   | RE Tom Stein, IAR  |

## STAFF:

- TE Paul R. Gilchrist, Stated Clerk
- RE Ross E. Cook, Business Administrator
- TE J. Robert Fiol, Assistant to the Stated Clerk
- RE Jerry Kornegay, Director of PCA Historical Center
- Mrs. Laurel De Bert, Administrative Assistant

## APPROVAL OF MINUTES:

The minutes of the March 14, 1997 BOARD OF DIRECTORS meeting were approved as printed and distributed.

The meeting was adjourned at 2:02 p.m. with RE Howard Q. Davis giving the closing prayer.

Respectfully submitted,

RE William F. Joseph, Jr., Chairman
TE Paul R. Gilchrist, Secretary

BOARD OF DIRECTORS
Presbyterian Church in America
October 10,1997

The Board of Directors of the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) held a
regular meeting on October 10, 1997, at the Holiday Inn Airport North Hotel in Atlanta,
Georgia.
The meeting was convened at 5:18 p.m. by the Chairman William A. Fox, Jr. RE Steve
Fox opened the meeting with prayer.
A quorum was declared present with the following in attendance:
TE Tim Diehl, Heartland RE Dwight Allen, CC
TE William A. Fox, Jr., Fellowship RE Dudley M Barnes, IAR
TE George C. Fuller, New Jersey RE Charles Bobyack, W Carolina
TE J. Allen Herrington, E. Carolina RE Howard Q. Davis, Covenant
TE William C. Hughes, MS Valley RE Stephen M. Fox, CE&P
TE G. Fredric Mau, Covenant RE Edwin Hackenberg, SE Louisiana
TE D. Steven Meyerhoff, Westminster RE Robert “Neal” Ham, PCAF
RE Ralph M ittendorff, S. Florida
RE William Moore, IV, Potomac
STAFF:
TE Paul R. Gilchrist, Stated Clerk
RE Ross E. Cook, Business Administrator
TE J. Robert Fiol, Assistant to the Stated Clerk
Mrs. Laurel De Bert, Administrative Assistant
APPROVAL OF MINUTES:
the minutes of the june 10, 1997 BOARD OF DIRECTORS meeting were approved as
printed and distributed.
BD-10/97-1 ms p that the board of directors adopt the following policy regarding the
PCA®:
PREFACE
Since 1974 the Presbyterian Church in America has used the initials “PCA” to
identify various programs and printed materials. In order to prevent confusion from any
unauthorized use of the PCA initials, the “service mark” was registered with the U.S.
Patent and Trademark Office in 1987. Thus, the Presbyterian Church in America has
the exclusive right to use the PCA® initials in matters relating to educational services,
ministry services and missionary services. The Administrative Committee, which
serves as the board of directors of Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation),
desiring to assist PCA churches in the appropriate use of the PCA® service mark, has
adopted the following policy.

<a id="ga26-p282"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=284 printed_page=282 -->

POLICY
1. Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) grants (either expressly or
tacitly) and will continue to grant local churches and presbyteries the right to use
its mark PCA® in connection with the name of the local church for ordinary
local church activities (i.e. local advertising of the church’s worship services and
other activities, ministries in the local church and its community, local church
newsletters for its members, etc.).
2 Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) requires that uses of the mark
for other than ordinary local church activities be approved in advance by the
Administrative Committee at the denominational level. Approval shall not be
granted unless the Committee is able to assert sufficient control over the mark’s
use to assure the mark is only utilized in a manner consistent with the doctrinal
tenents, existing ministries, and policies of the denomination.
3. Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) requires that any incidents of
possible trademark infringement be immediately reported to the Administrative
Committee at the denominational level. The Committee will determine what
appropriate action should be taken to promptly remedy the infringement.
APPLICATION
Because it is impossible to anticipate every possible situation when the PCA®
might be used, the following are given only as examples of what is appropriate or
inappropriate:
Examples of appropriate uses of the PCA8 mark:
First Presbyterian Church, a congregation of the PCA
PCA Women in the Church
PCA Men’s Rally
examples of inappropriate use soft hep ca mark which imply denominational control
or sponsorship:
PCA Publications (when printed by a local church ora presbytery —
implies publication by the denomination), however, “Covenant PCA News”
would be appropriate for the newsletter of a local church named covenant
Presbyterian Church in America.
PCA Parking (where a local church rents out its parking lot during the
week — not part of the church’s worship services or usual church activities and
also not related to the denomination); however, “Westminster PCA Parking”
would be appropriate on the sign for the parking lot of westminster Presbyterian Church in America.
PCA Christian School (implies denominational control or sponsorship);
however, “ChristChurch PCA School” would be appropriate for the school
operated by ChristChurch PCA.
The next meeting of the Board of Directors will be March 20-21, 1998 in Atlanta.

<a id="ga26-p283"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=285 printed_page=283 -->

TE Al Herrington closed the meeting with prayer at 5:20 p.m.
Respectfully submitted,

Isl TE William A. Fox, Jr , Chairman

Isl TE Paul R Gilchrist, Secretary

<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=286 printed_page=284 -->

# BOARD OF DIRECTORS
## Presbyterian Church in America
### March 20, 1998

The Board of Directors of the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) held a regular meeting on March 20, 1998, at the Holiday Inn Airport North Hotel in Atlanta, Georgia.

The meeting was convened at 8:15 p.m. by the Chairman William A. Fox, Jr. and RE Charles Bobyack opened the meeting with prayer.

A quorum was declared present with the following in attendance:

|  TE Tim Diehl, Heartland | RE Dwight Allen, CC  |
| --- | --- |
|  TE William A. Fox, Jr., Fellowship | RE Eugene Betts, MNA  |
|  TE George C. Fuller, New Jersey | RE Charles Bobyack, W. Carolina  |
|  TE J. Allen Herrington, E. Carolina | RE Howard Q. Davis, Covenant  |
|  TE William C. Hughes, MS Valley | RE Edwin Hackenberg, SE Louisiana  |
|  TE D. Steven Meyerhoff, Westminster | RE Robert “Neal” Ham, PCAF  |
|  TE George Mitchell, CE&P | RE Rick Lindsay, RH  |
|   | RE Ralph Mittendorff, S. Florida  |
|   | RE John Mardirosian, IAR  |
|   | RE William Moore, IV, Potomac  |
|   | RE Gerald Sovereign, MTW  |

## APPROVAL OF MINUTES:
The minutes of the October 10, 1997 BOARD OF DIRECTORS meeting were approved as printed and distributed.

The Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) is currently registered as a foreign corporation in Georgia, Missouri and Mississippi.

<a id="ga26-p285"></a>

prayer.
Respectfully submitted,

/s/ TE William A. Fox, Jr., Chairman

Isl TE Paul R Gilchrist, Clerk

<a id="ga26-p286"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=288 printed_page=286 -->

REPORT OF THE ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE
TO THE
26™ GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

"May the Lord God of your fathers make you a thousand times more
numerous than you are, and bless you as He has promised you! How can
I alone bear your problems and your burdens and your complaints?
Choose wise, understanding, and knowledgeable men from among your
tribes, and I will make them heads over you. ”
Deuteronomy 1:11-13

the divine order of leadership calls for teamwork and delegation of authority.
Moses received such advice from his father-in-law Jethro (Exodus 18:19-23) and this
has served as a pattern for presbyterian governance How grateful we are for those who
serve with us to whom we can be accountable, as well as to our staff working together
to accomplish the work of the Lord

### I. MEETINGS BETWEEN ASSEMBLIES

The Administrative Committee met three times during the year, including the
annual meeting on June 10, 1997 in Colorado Springs, Colorado. Then on October 10,
1997, we had our Stated Fall Meeting and on March 20, 1998 we had our regular Spring
Meeting in Atlanta, Georgia. The Administrative Committee also met as a Board of
Directors at each of those meetings to address civil and corporate matters.
II. SUMMARY OF ACTIONS BY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS
the minutes of the three meetings of the board of directors are attached (see
pages 331-336. these minutes contain the formal actions regarding the filing of proper
documents with the various states where the PCA is incorporated. This also applies to
the RPCES corporate filings.
as of the time of this writing there is no legal suit against an agency of the PCA
nor the PCA.
III. POLICY REGARDING THE PCA SERVICE MARK
The 24th General Assembly adopted a motion with regard to the questions raised
on the PCA registered trademark. The motion is as follows, “The Assembly directed
the Board of Directors to prepare a statement describing what is actually trademark
protected so that there may be a clear understanding of what is or is not permissible

<a id="ga26-p287"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=289 printed_page=287 -->

regarding its usage. This statement is to be distributed to all sessions and ministers of
the PCA.” (M24GA , 24-53, III, 26, p. 241).
The 25th General Assembly adopted the following motion: “That the AC/Board
of Directors continue the study of this matter (i.e., the PCA registered trademark) and
report to the October 1997 meeting any recommendations Grounds: since there are
still pending questions in the civil sector as to how this all applies to the
internet/cyberspace, we have not addressed this matter completely.”
In response to the above motion, at the October 1997 meeting, the Board of
Directors received and adopted the following policy:
PREFACE
Since 1974 the Presbyterian Church in America has used the initials “PCA” to
identify various programs and printed materials. In order to prevent confusion from any
unauthorized use of the PCA initials, the “service mark” was registered with the U.S.
Patent and Trademark Office in 1987. Thus, the Presbyterian Church in America has
the exclusive right to use the PCA* initials in matters relating to educational services,
ministry services and missionary services. The Administrative Committee, which
serves as the board of directors of Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation),
desiring to assist PCA churches in the appropriate use of the PCA® service mark, has
adopted the following policy.
POLICY
1 Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) grants (either expressly or
tacitly) and will continue to grant local churches and presbyteries the right to use
its mark PCA® in connection with the name of the local church for ordinary
local church activities (i.e. local advertising of the church’s worship services and
other activities, ministries in the local church and its community, local church
newsletters for its members, etc.).
2. Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) requires that uses of the mark
for other than ordinary local church activities be approved in advance by the
Administrative Committee at the denominational level. Approval shall not be
granted unless the Committee is able to assert sufficient control over the mark’s
use to assure the mark is only utilized in a manner consistent with the doctrinal
tenents, existing ministries, and policies of the denomination.
3. Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) requires that any incidents of
possible trademark infringement be immediately reported to the Administrative
Committee at the denominational level. The Committee will determine what
appropriate action should be taken to promptly remedy the infringement.
APPLICATION
Because it is impossible to anticipate every possible situation when the PCA®
might be used, the following are given only as examples of what is appropriate or
inappropriate:

<a id="ga26-p288"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=290 printed_page=288 -->

Examples of appropriate uses of the PCA® mark:
- First Presbyterian Church, a congregation of the PCA
- PCA Women in the Church
- PCA Men’s Rally

Examples of inappropriate uses of the PCA mark which imply denominational control or sponsorship:
- PCA Publications (when printed by a local church or a presbytery -- implies publication by the denomination); however, “Covenant PCA News” would be appropriate for the newsletter of a local church named Covenant Presbyterian Church in America.
- PCA Parking (where a local church rents out its parking lot during the week -- not part of the church’s worship services or usual church activities and also not related to the denomination); however, “Westminster PCA Parking” would be appropriate on the sign for the parking lot of Westminster Presbyterian Church in America.
- PCA Christian School (implies denominational control or sponsorship); however, “ChristChurch PCA School” would be appropriate for the school operated by ChristChurch PCA.

This policy was distributed to all churches through the Stated Clerk’s Letter.

## IV. DR. L. ROY TAYLOR NOMINATED FOR STATED CLERK

Following Dr. Gilchrist’s announcement of his intention to retire from this office at the conclusion of the 26th General Assembly, the Chairman at the October meeting appointed a five man Search Committee to find a nominee to be presented to the General Assembly in 1998.

Vice-Chairman RE Edwin Hackenberg of the AC served as Chairman of a Search Committee for the third Stated Clerk of the General Assembly.

They reported to the AC at the March 1998 meeting their recommendation of Dr. L. Roy Taylor. His nomination was unanimously supported by written vote on the first ballot.

The following resolution was also unanimously adopted:

- **Whereas**, Ruling Elder Edwin Hackenberg was appointed in October 1997 to chair a Special Committee charged with the responsibility of presenting to the PCA Administrative Committee a candidate for Stated Clerk of the PCA, and
- **Whereas**, Ruling Elder Hackenberg quickly organized the committee and led it in development of a plan for obtaining prospective candidates, investigating their credentials, screening them, interviewing them and selecting a candidate, and
- **Whereas**, he ably led the committee to promptly complete its assignment in an exemplary manner, and

<a id="ga26-p289"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=291 printed_page=289 -->

Whereas, he has demonstrated a deep devotion to Jesus Christ, the Head of the Church,
and has shown superior gifts of leadership and highly effective communication
and administrative skills,

Therefore, Be it Resolved that the Administrative Committee of the PCA dismiss the
Search Committee with its thanks and commendation for a job well done, with
particular recognition and thanks to RE Edwin Hackenberg for his vital
contributions to the successful completion of the process.
Recognition and thanks were also given to all committee members: TE George
Fuller, TE William Fox, RE William (Bingy) Moore and RE William Joseph.
V. dr gilchrist and mr ross cook commended
Every year, pursuant to RAO 3-3,d, the AC has evaluated the Stated Clerk’s
performance for the past year. This year it was no different. Hence, the AC adopted the
following commendation:
“Upon a systematic and thorough review of the Stated Clerk, Paul R. Gilchrist,
and his performance with regard to his responsibilities and duties and his service
to the Presbyterian Church in America, the AC commends Dr. Gilchrist for his
outstanding and excellent performance and service to the Church and for his
upholding the dignity and integrity of his office. Further, the AC expresses its
deepest appreciation to Dr. Gilchrist and God’s blessing on the eve of his
retirement.”
At the same time, recognizing the great value and service that RE Ross E. Cook
has performed, the AC adopted the following resolution unanimously:

Whereas, Ruling Elder Ross E. cook joined the staff of the office of the stated clerk
of the PCA in 1988 as Business Administrator, and

Whereas, he has been responsible to the Stated Clerk for administering the finances of
the PCA Administrative Committee, and

Whereas, he has also administered and overseen the various facets of the PCA Office
Building, and

Whereas, he has also negotiated accommodations and the myriad other aspects of the
PCA General Assembly held in different locations every year, and

Whereas, RE Cook has continuously served his local church, on the session, pulpit
nominating committee, and many other tasks, both large and small, contributing
to the work of the Kingdom, and

Whereas, he has ably, willingly, cheerfully and effectively performed these duties as
unto the lord and for the honor of jesus Christ, and

Whereas, he is expected to retire from the position of business administrator within
the next year

Therefore, Be it Resolved that the PCA Administrative Committee extend its heartfelt
thanks and highest commendation to RE Ross E. cook in recognition of his
faithful and exemplary service to the Church of Jesus Christ, and, in particular to
the PCA.

<a id="ga26-p290"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=292 printed_page=290 -->

VI. 25 th ANNIVERSARY CELEBRATION COMMITTEE
Pursuant to action by the 24th General Assembly, a committee was appointed by
the Administrative Committee to plan a celebration for the 25th anniversary of the
founding of the Presbyterian Church in America. The intention has been to have a year
long celebration to begin in 1997 and culminating in St. Louis in 1998, possibly even
through December 4, 1998.
Chairman TE Don Patterson and co-Chairman TE Kennedy Smartt have held
monthly meetings. Subcommittees have been at work in various areas of responsibility.
A slogan was selected: “Celebrating the Past, Claiming the Future Under God.”
Among the ideas to be developed as part of the denomination-wide celebration
are: camera-ready bulletin inserts and materials for churches, a video, and special
expanded edition of the Yearbook, special displays at the 25th and 26th Assemblies, and
special panel discussions and speakers. Churches will be asked to help provide
information and financial help to accomplish these projects.
Last year’s General Assembly agreed with the 25th Anniversary Celebration
Committee that the 26th General Assembly (1998) in St. Louis, be designed for a time of
renewal and celebration.
As part of this celebration a number of special worship services and other
activities are planned. The evening worship will center on : “True to the Bible” (Dr.
James M. Boice, speaker), “True to the Reformed Faith” (Dr. Morton H. Smith,
speaker), and “Obedient to the Great Commission of Jesus Christ” (Dr. Frank M.
Barker, speaker). Throughout the Assembly we will pause to give thanks, remember
and celebrate God’s Mighty Acts through some of our permanent Committees and
Agencies. Additionally, there will be several Anniversary panel discussions dealing
with our past, the present and our future.

VII. FINANCIAL
The Lord continues to be gracious to us, providing for our needs during this past
year. We are grateful that this is our third consecutive year that we have concluded the
year in the black. This reflects both an increase of 5% in the number of churches
participating in the Partnership Shares as well as an increase in the dollar amount.
Additionally, we express special gratitude for the WIC Love Gift which was a
significant factor in our positive financial picture. Their gift also has enabled us to
make major upgrades to our office equipment as well as amenities for the entire
building.
We have been encouraged by the report from the auditor who has given a very
glowing report particularly for the manner in which your monies have been handled
both for the AC as well as for the Building Fund. The Lord has been so good to us and

<a id="ga26-p291"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=293 printed_page=291 -->

has blessed us abundantly by giving a Business Administrator, Ross Cook, who has managed the business and financial affairs in a manner that I believe honors the Lord.

While we are encouraged by the increased participation and level of giving, I do want to share my concern that over one third of our churches have missed the opportunity and blessing of participating with us through the Partnership Shares. There are a number of items included in the budget, some of them mandated by GA, that will strain the 1998 budget. Consequently, for those who already contribute we ask you to continue faithfully to support the ministry of the Administrative Committee. And for those not yet participating, this anniversary year would be an ideal time for such a commitment.

## VIII PCA OFFICE BUILDING

Every year we report that the AC has the responsibility for the day-to-day management for the PCA office building as well as the payments on the mortgage. Contributions to the PCA office building for 1997 were $26,908, a 39% decrease as compared to $44,090 in 1996. The first quarter contributions in 1998, in the amount of $6,730, indicate that contributions will remain level throughout this year.

Rental rates were increased 3.2% for 1998 which will just about cover the loss of rent for vacant space and the inflationary increase in expenses. During 1996, the occupancy rate was almost 100%, but it dropped to 93% for 1997 and remains at that level. Our annual surveys show that the rental rates are competitive to rates in the area of Atlanta in which we are located.

The mortgage balance as of June 1, 1998, will be $932,086, down from $1,163,568 in June of 1997. At the present rate of payments the mortgage will be paid off by December 2001. With only $27,000 in contributions, we will not be able to accelerate mortgage payments.

We are grateful for the service of Loyd Strickland, who served faithfully and well as chairman of the Capital Building Fund subcommittee for 11 years until he stepped down this spring. Mr. Bill Joseph was chosen to serve as the new chairman of the subcommittee. It is also with sorrow that we report the death of Mr. L. Allen Morris who not only served all 11 years with the subcommittee but was also directly involved in the negations for, and acquisition of, the office building in 1987.

## IX. PERSONNEL

God has continued to be very gracious to us this past year. The Administrative Committee continues to give thanks to God for the work of the Stated Clerk, Dr. Paul R. Gilchrist. During the past year he has served in a very capable way and we commend him for his excellent service to Christ and His Kingdom. The Committee evaluated him and gave him very high marks. Further, as everyone knows, he is assisted by the Business Administrator, Mr. Ross E. Cook., who oversees the office staff and keeps the

<a id="ga26-p292"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=294 printed_page=292 -->

business and financial affairs of the committee in order. The auditors continue to give
him high marks for his competency in keeping track of the finances of the General Assembly and specifically, of the Office of the Stated Clerk We have been grateful to
have him on our staff and we rejoice in the Lord for his 10 years of faithful service
we are particularly grateful for the services of chaplain J. Robert Fiol as
Assistant to the Stated Clerk. The mandates of the General Assembly upon the Office
of the Stated Clerk have been exceedingly heavy and we now wonder how we managed
before Bob joined the staff. He has provided the Administrative Committee with
representation at presbytery meetings which the Stated Clerk’s work load had not
allowed heretofore He has taken on a significant amount of the responsibilities and
work load of the Office of the Stated Clerk
Mrs. Laurel De Bert continues to serve as Administrative Assistant/Office
Manager and has done exceptionally well during the past year in spite of some changes
in the office secretarial staff and a major upgrade in our computer system. We are
grateful to the Lord for her capable and dedicated 16 years of service to the committee.
The staff in Atlanta continues to fulfill its responsibilities assigned to the AC by the
General Assembly; maintaining good relations with the presbyteries, keeping good
statistical records, including changes in churches and individual records. The office
continues to assist pulpit search committees with Ministerial Data Forms provided by
those looking for God’s call to service In this connection, we are grateful for our
receptionist/pastor search secretary, Mrs. Susan Cullen, who handles the M DF’s and
assists the search committees Mrs. Yoon Hee Park continues to serve us and the
General Assembly in her part time capacity as Bi-lingual Secretary (Korean/English).
Mrs. Monica Johnston and Miss Sue Campana continue to keep presbytery information
and church statistics for the Yearbook Mrs. Peggy Little serves as secretary for SJC
and Dr. Gilchrist, while Mrs. Mary Bradley Bayne, who has served the AC for 15 years,
keeps the financial records in order.
The Administrative Committee also oversees the work of the PCA Historical
Center. As everyone knows, Mr. Jerry Kornegay is the Director of the PCA Historical
Center in St. Louis and we are grateful for his 8 years of service in this capacity
During this time he made significant improvements in the proficiency with which
church and denominational records are processed, including the installation of a
computer system with scanning capabilities. We commend him for his excellent work
for the PCA.
X. INVITATIONS TO FUTURE GA’S
The 25th GA accepted the invitation from Great Lakes Presbytery and approved
the 27th GA meeting in Louisville, Kentucky, June 15-18, 1999. The GA had
previously accepted the invitation from Southwest Florida Presbytery to host the 28th
GA June 20-23, 2000, in Tampa, Florida. Additionally, GA accepted the invitation
from North Texas Presbytery and approved the meeting for the 29th General Assembly

<a id="ga26-p293"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=295 printed_page=293 -->

to meet in Dallas-Fort Worth area, in June 2001. An invitation has been received by
Evangel Presbytery to host the 30th GA in June 2002 in Birmingham, Alabama.
XI NEWS OFFICE
The 25th General Assembly approved the recommendation of the christian
Education & Publications Committee to transfer responsibility for the PCA News
Office to the Administrative Committee
Since that action, a “task force” of individuals, from a full range of media
experience, has met with the AC staff to provide assistance in developing a purpose
statement, objectives, a media policy, a general scope of activities, and priorities.
Chaplain Bob Fiol, Assistant to the Stated Clerk, is serving as Interim News Officer
with the expectation that a full time News Officer will be added to the staff in 1999.
XII SOME IMPORTANT ACTIONS TAKEN
1 Approved new exhibitors for General Assembly subject to space availability:
United Van Lines, Center for Reclaiming America, Trinity International
University, American Family Radio, Crossway Books, Gospel for Asia,
Shepherd Enterprise, and Mascot Ministries/Geddy the Gecko.
2. Approved the following dates for Administrative Committee meetings:
October 2-3, 1998 and March 19-20, 1999 in Atlanta; June 14 of 15, 1999 in
Louisville, KY, October 1-2, 1999 and March 16-17, 2000 in Atlanta.
The AC Established as a policy to hold our stated meetings of the
administrative committee on the third thursday through saturday of march and
on the first friday and saturday of october each year
3. pursuant to the directives of the GA, the Board of Directors approved the
changes in the Bylaws proposed by the PCA Foundation. It should be noted that
the PCA Foundation is presenting these changes to the 26th GA through its
committee of Commissioners.
4 Heard and received progress reports from the 25th Anniversary Celebration
Committee.
5. On recommendation from the PCA Building Fund Subcommittee, the AC has
approved the rental rate increase from $16.25 to $16.75 per square foot for the
1999 calendar year.
XIII RECOMMENDATIONS
1 That the General Assembly elect Dr L. Roy Taylor as the third Stated Clerk of
the General Assembly.
2. That in accordance with RAO 3-4 nominations for Stated Clerk be permitted
from the floor and that a deadline for such nominations be set at 9:30 a mon
Wednesday, July 1, 1998, in order to give time for the Theological Examining
Committee to interview candidates
3. That following a systematic and thorough review by the AC of the Stated Clerk,
Dr Paul R. Gilchrist, and his performance with regard to his responsibilities and
duties and his service to the Presbyterian Church in America, the General

<a id="ga26-p294"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=296 printed_page=294 -->

Assembly commend Dr. Paul R. Gilchrist for his outstanding and excellent
performance and service to the Church and for his upholding the dignity and
integrity of his office. Further, that the General Assembly express its deepest
appreciation to Dr. Gilchrist and God’s blessing on the eve of his retirement
4. That the following overtures be answered in the affirmative: Overtures 9 from
Potomac Presbytery (text on p. 60), Overture 13 from Heartland Presbytery
(text on p. 61), and Overture 19 from New Jersey Presbytery (p. 60 and 62)
“Commendation and Thanks for Dr. Paul Gilchrist’s Exemplary Service as
Stated Clerk.”
5 That the Assembly express its deepest appreciation for the hardworking staff in
the Office of the Stated Clerk, especially for Mr. Ross E. Cook, Business
Administrator, who will be retiring before the end of the year, for Mrs. Laurel
De Bert, Administrative Assistant, for Chaplain J. Robert Fiol, Assistant to the
Stated Clerk, and for Mr. Jerry Kornegay, Director of the PCA Historical Center
in St. Louis, who has resigned his position to move to Knoxville, Tennessee.
6. That the 26th General Assembly express its gratitude to the Lord for the good
spirit of cooperation among the Coordinators, Directors, and presidents of the
committees and agencies.
7. That GA amend the Corporate Bylaws of the PCA, Article VI, Section 5, the
first two paragraphs as follows:
“The Board of Directors of Presbyterian Church in America Foundation, Inc.,
shall consist of ten (10): (a) Six (6) members in classes elected through the
standard nomination and election procedures (the “at large members”); and (b)
one (1) member from each of the following permanent committees or board of
trustees of the agencies: (1) Christian-Education and Publications; (2) Covenant
College, (3 2) Covenant Theological Seminary, (4 5) Mission to the World, and
(S 4) Mission to North America (the “Appointed Members”). the chairman of
the Board of Directors of Presbyterian Church in America Foundation, Inc. shall
bean at large member.
The at-large members shall serve for four (4) year terms, and shall be
divided into four (4) classes, each class being as nearly equal in number as
possible, so that the terms of approximately one-fourth of the at-large members
shall expire each year. Directors shall be eligible for reelection to a second
four-year term after which there must be a one-year interval before further
election.
8 That the 26th GA accept the invitation from Evangel Presbytery to host the 30th
General Assembly in June 2002 in the Birmingham area subject to available
suitable facilities.
9. That the 26th GA respond to Overture 8 from Heritage Presbytery “Cease
Publishing Private Information in Yearbookf ' (text on p. 30) by referring it to the

That Overture 6 from Eastern Carolina Presbytery “Permit Vendors to Sell at

2. monitoring the sales items of the exhibitors to ensure that the items

ATTACHMENT 1
Report of the PCA Historical Center
to the Twenty-Sixth General Assembly
of the Presbyterian Church in America

Purpose: The purpose of the PCA Historical Center is to fulfill the historical
preservation/research needs of the Presbyterian Church in America, it’s committees and
agencies and the churches of the denomination.

Computer System Upgrade: During the Spring and Summer of 1997 the PCA
Historical Center received significant contributions from the WIC Love Gift, Trinity
Presbyterian Church in Montgomery, AL and one individual. These special donations
we reused to purchase anew computer system that included a flatbed photographic
scanner, photographic software packages and the storage space required to develop,
update and maintain a photographic database. Over the next twelve to eighteen months
selected photographs will be scanned into a database from the thousands of photographs
that are currently housed at the Center. This computer system will provide a backup
storage system for preserving significant photos and documents, and permit rapid
retrieval of those images and documents.
The new system also gives the Center an e-mail capability The Historical Center email address is: pcahistoricalctr@MCIONE.com

Records Processing: the majority of the records held by the center have been fully
processed and are available for document retrieval and research needs. The remaining
major, unprocessed collections are now being worked on. one of our volunteers is
currently working on the records of CE&P. The Center’s Director continues to process
the records of world presbyterian missions (RPCES). the processing of these
collections should be completed by December 1998. Additional volunteers provided
additional assistance in the identification of individuals depicted in photographs in the
Covenant Theological Seminary Collection. Many of these photographs are now being
entered in the photo database.

Publications: Work continues on a short, consolidated history of the PCA. Current
research encompasses the role of Presbyterian Evangelistic Fellowship in the
Continuing Church movement. The working outline for this history includes a short
section on the rise of liberalism in the Northern Presbyterian Church from the trial of
Dr. Charles A. Briggs (1891) through the trial of Dr. J. Gresham Machen and the
founding of the presbyterian church of america (1936). the second section of the

<a id="ga26-p298"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=300 printed_page=298 -->

publication will address the spread of liberalism to the South through the founding of
the PCA in 1973. The final section of the book will cover the history of the RPCES to
the Joining and Receiving of 1982.

Celebration of the Founding of the PCA: The PCA will continue the celebration of
its 25th anniversary at this year’s General Assembly. The Historical Center Director
has been an active member of the celebration planning Committee. as part of the
celebration activities the Center will have two display tables at the Assembly. The first
display will feature photographs of the key events leading up to the establishment of the
PCA in December 1973. The second display table will also feature photographs. This
second display will change on a regular basis and commissioners are urged to help
identify the individuals depicted in the photographic display.

Aiken Taylor Award: The Presbyterian Church in America’s Historical Center
presents an annual award for the best paper on American Presbyterian Church History
written by a PCA theological seminary student. This year’s winner of the aiken taylor
Award is Mark Home of Covenant Theological Seminary for his paper: “Legal Fiction
or Real Union?, John Williamson Nevin’s Controversy with Charles Hodge over the
imputation of Adam’s Sin (with a Comparison to Robert L. Dabney).” The second
place award went to Daniel Kunkle, of westminster theological Seminary,
Philadelphia, for his paper, “The apologetics of archibald Alexander.” This year’s
panel of judges included: Mrs. Georgia Settle and Drs. Clair Davis and Gregg Singer.

Newsletter: The Historical Center Newsletter will continue to run articles on the
history of the PCA. the next series of lead articles in the newsletter will be short
biographical sketches of the founding fathers of the PCA and significant individuals of
the RPCES. planned articles will include sketches of the following individuals:
Dr. John E. Richards Dr. G. Aiken Taylor
Rev. William E. Hill Mr. Kenneth S. Keyes
Dr. Francis S. Schaeffer Dr. Robert G. Rayburn
Dr. J. Oliver Buswell
if you have a favorite memory or story of these men, please send it to the Historical
Center for use in developing these sketches.

Staff: the staff of the historical center consists of a full time director and several part
time volunteers The Archives volunteers include Mrs. Florence Graham, Mrs. Laverne
Rayburn and Mr. Robert Thomas.

<a id="ga26-p299"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=301 printed_page=299 -->

Historical Center Subcommittee: The Historical Center Subcommittee provides
oversight of the operations of the center members of the subcommittee are:
Dr. David Calhoun Dr. Donald J MacNair
Mrs. Shirley Duncan Dr. Frederic Mau
Miss Lannae Graham Mr. John Spencer
Mr. Edward S. Harris
Dr. Paul Gilchrist and Mr Ross Cook are ex-officio members of this committee.
Respectfully submitted,

/s/ Jerry Komegay
Director

(1) The PARTNERSHIP SHARE is that portion of the approved expense budget which is
dependent on contributions from the PCA churches and individuals. When the
contributions are less than the amount of the PARTNERSHIP SHARE, the
committees. agencies and institutions cannot fulfill the programs and services which
the General Assembly approved at the June 1997 meeting.
(2) $60,000 of the PCA Foundation budget is included in the budgets of the participating
committees and agencies as their portion of the Foundation's support. Therefore, that
amount is deducted from the Total of the 1999 Expense Budgets.
(3) Institutions are on a July 1 through June 30 Fiscal Year.
(4) The PCA Office building budget is not included in the PARTNERSHIP SHARE. Gifts for
the Office Building Fund are over and above the PARTNERSHIP SHARE and benefit all of
the committees and agencies in Atlanta
(5) The Mission to the World PARTNERSHIP SHARE includes the consolidated portion of the
MTW budget identified as Unrestricted or General Fund It does not include any items
which are considered Restricted or "donor designated" funds, including, but not limited to,
such items as: missionary support, program ministry and project budgets, etc.

ATTACHMENT 3
ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE
1999 PROPOSED BUDGET

BUDGET PHILOSOPHY
The budget is built primarily on the job description of the Stated Clerk in the
RAO, which determines the services that are to be provided by the Office of the Stated
Clerk to the churches, presbyteries, committees and agencies, and to the General Assembly.
GENERAL COMMENTS
many of the activities and responsibilities of the administrative committee are
directly affected by the size and growth of the PCA, which in turn are reflected in
annual budget increases for many line items The economic inflation rate also affects
most budget items and is applied to most line items, except as noted in the Economic
Assumptions shown below.
The budgets are presented in a format to comply with the standards for not-forprofit organizations adopted by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB).
the fa sb standards provide a definition of “supporting activities” which they call
“management and general”. Therefore, compensation for the Stated Clerk and his staff
is allocated according to the estimated time spent by each person in “program,”
administration, and fund raising areas.
1998 ECONOMIC ASSUMPTIONS
A. Stated Clerk/Administration
3.0% PCA growth rate
2.0 % Consumer Price Index (CPI)and inflation rate
3.0 % Postal rates
10.0% Health Insurance premiums
10.0 % Cost for Travel (transportation, food and housing)
5.0 % Workman’s Compensation Insurance
The Full Time Equivalent (FTE) employees budgeted at the beginning and end
of the year will be 11
B. PCA Office Building
3.1 % Rent increase to $16.75 per square foot.
2.0 % Consumer Price Index (CPI) and inflation rate
5.0 % Insurance premiums
The Full Time Equivalent (FTE) employees budgeted at the beginning and end
of the year will be 0 .5

News Officer, in compliance with the General Assembly action in 1997 to transfer the
News Office responsibilities from CE&P to the AC.

<a id="ga26-p307"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=309 printed_page=307 -->

CHRISTIAN EDUCATION & PUBLICATIONS
1999 Proposed Budget

Philosophy:
CE/P’s ministry is driven by the Strategic Faith Plan. The plan defines CE/P’s mission,
goals, strategies and programs The proposed budget is a “program-based” budget and
represents the resources needed to adequately fund the planned activities, which have
been approved and/or directed by the Committee and the General Assembly.
Summary:
The proposed 1999 expense budget represent a $351,295 increase (17.08%) from the
1998 budget. CE/P’s contribution need, likewise increases by $58,622 (4.3%). CE/P
has attempted and continues to utilize other revenues to make up for the continuous
shortfall in church’s support of the partnership Share.
Assumptions:
3% PCA growth rate
2% Consumer Price Index/Inflation Rate
3% Postal Rates
10% Health insurance premiums increase
10% Costs for travel (transportation, food & housing)
5% Workman’s Compensation Insurance
The 1999 Budget is based on 25.05 Full-time equivalent employees. The 1998
Budget was based on 23.05. The increase is due to the addition of a Children’s
Ministry Department (1.5 FTE) and additional temporary staff needed to handle
the workload for the 1999 WIC Conference (.5 FTE).
Notes tline numbers refer to Budget Comparisons Statement):
• Other Revenues (line 3) for 1999 are anticipated as follows:
Bookstore Sales/Postage $615,000
WIC Conference Fees $183,000
Subscriptions and Advertising $ 61,000
Video Library Memberships $ 50,000
Reimbursements (travel, postage, etc.) $ 32,000
Seminar/Conference Fees $ 27,500
• the increase of women in the church (line 5) is due to the 1999 WIC National
conference to beheld in Atlanta. Attendance for this event is conservatively
estimated at 4,000. the majority of the costs associated with this even twill be paid
by revenue generated by conference fees.

<a id="ga26-p308"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=310 printed_page=308 -->

• The decrease of Men of the Covenant (line 6) is due to a reevaluation and
refinement of the anticipated costs associated with staffing such a department.
• The increase in Youth Ministries (line 8) is due to the additional income provided
by the 1998 WIC Love Gift (see line 21). Additional programs will include
developing a network of regional youth trainers and the implementation of a youth
ministry internship program in several local churches.
• The most notable change in CE/P’s program budget is the addition of a Children’s
Ministries department (line 9). Local churches continue to look to CE/P to provide
ministry support in this critical area
• The Bookstore (line 13) continues to serve over 85% of PCA churches. The
proposed increase for 1999 is so the Bookstore can continue to broaden its service to
the PCA.
• Depreciation (line 19) increases proportionally as CE/P adds staff and seeks to
utilize technology to minimize the need for additional personnel.
• Management and General (line 17) is anticipated to increase as CE/P’s mailroom
continues to serve some of the other committees and agencies. This increase is
covered by a commensurate increase in revenues.
• Fund Raising (line 20) expenses represent a decrease based on actual experience in
1997.
• Though CE/P has temporarily been relieved of the responsibility of fund a portion
of the PCA Foundation (line 21), CE/P’s management earnestly desires to
participate in this program once again if funds are available
• The Coordinator, his assistant and related expenses are allocated to the various
expense categories as follows: Training 25%, Fund Raising 10%, Administration
10%, Bookstore 15%, WIC 10%, MOC 5%, Youth 5%, Equip for Ministry 20%

COVENANT COLLEGE
NOTES TO ACCOMPANY 98-99 PROPOSED BUDGET
Accounting system:
The college uses the NACUBO (National association of college and university
Business Officers) definitions of revenue and expense categories. This insures that the
college will be able to directly compare various ratios with other colleges and assess our
effectiveness in accordance with our assessment systems. While the categories do not
exactly parallel the definitions used by the Accounting and Financial Reporting Guide
for Christian Ministries, there is some similarity. NACUBO categories including
Instructional, Academic Support, Library, Student Services, Public Service and Student
Aid could be broadly considered “Program Services.” maintenance of plant and
Institutional Support could be considered “Supporting Activities.” Several major
accounting changes were made in 1996-1997 in accordance with changes in standards
published by the Financial Accounting Standards Board. Included in these are two
which have significantly changed the proposed budget shown First, depreciation is
now an operating expense rather than an expense only in the plant fund Due to this
change, tuition will rise 3% more than otherwise would be the case for the next five
years. These tuition increases will fund depreciation, allow Covenant to show a net
positive bottom line and reduce pressure on future capital campaigns by providing funds
for facility and equipment replacement and repair. This expense is shown divided
between maintenance of plant Depreciation, representing the depreciation costs of the
facilities used for educational purposes, and Auxiliary Depreciation, representing the
depreciation costs of the residence halls, food service and for-profit facilities. Second,
since capital gifts are shown as revenue in the year they are used for construction, but
the construction expense only appears through depreciation over time, capital projects
tend to positively overstate the operating results of the college. In order to show the
results more clearly, the operating net results for the college are shown on the expense
budget at the bottom of the page on the line titled “Increase (Decrease) in Undesignated
Net Assets”. this line wills how a negative figure until the completion of funding the
depreciation expense through the tuition increases mentioned in the previous paragraph.
Proposed revenue budget notes:
1) Traditional tuition assumes FTE enrollment of 758, a 6.2% increase from the 97-
98 actual of 714. housing is projected to beat capacity, even with the addition
of the first phase of maclellan Hall. Tuition will rise 6.8% and room by 6.5%.
Board rates will not increase for 98-99. The combined fee increase will be
5.9%, below last year’s combined increase of 8.3%.
2) Quest profit of $220,000 assumes an enrollment of 160, consistent with this
year’s enrollment.
3) The unrestricted gifts budget, which includes undesignated capital campaign
gifts, is set at $1,640,398, slightly below the budget for 97-98. The annual fund
portion of this budget is set at $1,475,000, a $75,000 increase over 97-98.

<a id="ga26-p312"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=314 printed_page=312 -->

4) Unrestricted investment income is set at $250,000, the same as 97-98 Most of
these earnings are designated by the board for scholarship purposes.
Proposed expense budget notes:
1) Student Aid is projected at 29% of tuition for institutional aid, similar to 97-98.
Endowed and/or restricted gift aid is being aggressively pursued to help students
afford a Covenant education. An unrestricted estate gift of over $1,500,000 last
year was designated by the board to be invested for scholarship support.
2) Instructional costs rise as three additional faculty positions are added to
accommodate increased enrollment
3) Academic support costs increase as enrollment increases.
4) Room and board continues to be budgeted as break even departments after
depreciation funding is considered.
5) Contingency is maintained at 2% of the total Educational and General budget.

<a id="ga26-p313"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=315 printed_page=313 -->

<a id="ga26-p315"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=317 printed_page=315 -->

covenant theological seminary
new budget highlights
FY98-99

I. REVENUE:
A. Tuition and Fees:
1. Tuition in most programs will increase from $220 per credit hour
to $232. This 5.45% increase is less than last year (5.77%). We
are increasing tuition less than called for in our Long Range Plan
(6 8%) to remain competitive with other seminaries. The
increase is necessary to covert he cost of anew director for the
D.Min. program and various new staff positions to continue to
address work loads that are too heavy. Tuition pays only about
46% of the cost to educate a seminarian
2. The cost per credit hour for the Doctor of Ministry program is
increased from $226 to $232 (up 2 65%), again less than
previously projected.
3. enrollment for credit is expected to increase in the fall of 1998 to
712 from 706 in the fall of 1997. We expect the full-time
equivalent enrollment to decrease from 370 to 369. In addition
we expect 162 students in the Fall to be auditing classes and
taking Continuing Education courses across the country.
B endow m ent: Earnings used after management expenses are budgeted to
be 5.9%. This is down from 6.12% actually experienced for FY96-97.
We have not budgeted for new gifts which will come in during the
course of the new budget year as a result of the Capital Campaign.
Ultimately, therefore, we will use even less than 5 9% of the earnings
from funds which will actually be in hand (I project 5.5% or lower). The
budget is based on added Endowment gifts committed to be received in
the remainder of the current budget year. The Capital Campaign will
have a significant positive impact on the new budget. Endowment
income in all areas (unrestricted, apartments, and student aid) to the
operating budget will increase by $140,600 versus $113,700 for this
year.
C. Gifts & Grants: The Gifts & Grants goal (PCA Partnership Share) will
increase from $1,590,000 to $1,630,000. This is a very modest increase
of only 2 5%. This gifts & grants goal is an increasingly smaller
percentage of the total operating budget compared to other typical
seminaries. The increase is $22,685 more than projected last year in our
Long Range Plans. the surprising success of the annual funds o far this
year leads us to believe that this is still a conservative increase. We are
projecting Restricted Gifts of $105,000 for next year. I believe the

<a id="ga26-p316"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=318 printed_page=316 -->

budget of $55,600 for this year is too low, considering historical trends
and receipts of nearly $55,000 through the first five months of this year,
D Student Aid: This source of income will increase to $183,200 from
$151,600. These funds come from Endowment earnings.
E. Other Income We expect this source of income to increase to $92,625
in the new year from $84,500 in the current year Most of this increase
is from higher video and syllabi sales related to the Extension program.
F. Auxiliary Enterprises Rents for the apartments will see higher
increases this year, as shown below. This is being done to conform to
the Board’s wishes that on-campus housing should not be “subsidized.”
Extensive research by the Director of Admissions has shown us that area
apartments are renting at rates significantly higher than we presently
charge, particularly for 3-bedroom units. We intend to “catch up” over
2-3 years Single student housing will be increasing about 2.8%.
__________________97-98 Rent 98-99 Rent Increase
2-Bedroom $475 $500 5.26%
3-Bedroom $590 $640 8.47%
[NOTE: following board approval of the budget, Administration elected to reduce the
increase for 3-bedroom units to $40 instead of $50 per month. This will reduce
revenues by $3,840 This change will be shown in a budget revision in May ]
II. Expenditures:
A. General:
1. The budget assumes overall salary increases of 4%. The increases
will be a mixture of cost of living (2.5% in most cases) and merit
where appropriate.
2. Medical Insurance premiums will change February 1, 1998.
Preliminary quotes reflect a 22% increase, which has been the
experience of other local employers. I have budgeted for 22%,
but expect to achieve a slightly lower increase. We were able to
keep the increase in February 1, 1997 to 6%.
3. General expenses (where appropriate) were increased by 3.0%
based on inflation projection by the Kiplinger Letter.
B. Educational & General:
1 President & Trustees: Nothing unusual to report here.
2 Instruction - General Budget Some hours were added for
adjunct staff, principally for homiletics and counseling courses,
as well as full-year salary for Lecturer (half-year in ‘97-98).
sixty percent oft hed min Director’s salary (see below) is

<a id="ga26-p317"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=319 printed_page=317 -->

shown here A new professor was not added to the budget
because there are no “hot” prospects at this time.
3 Instruction - Doctor of Ministry: a director forth ed min
program has been added, along with a full-time secretary We
expect to see more growth in this program
4 Instruction - Master of Theology: No changes
5 Instruction - Evening Program : Budgeted for 18 3-hour
classes at $700 per credit hour.
6 Instruction - Extension Program With expected continuing
growth in this program, salary is added for a new Director The
student wife who previously directed this area is leaving and we
expect a higher salary will be required to recruit for this position
Money is also added for honoraria for increased site visits by
professors, more IMET mentors and assistants, and general
growth of the program
7 Instruction - Francis Schaeffer Institute Inflationary
increases and added instructor load
8 Instruction - Church Planting Only inflationary increases
9. Registrar’s Office Only inflationary increases
10. Library : Some added staff cost and more student worker hours
to handle increased use of the Library resources. More is spent
on Book and Periodical purchases Our expenditures in this
department will need to continue to increase since our accrediting
agencies watch our expenditures here very carefully
11 Student Services Added salary to pay for more administrative
assistance to help Dean.
12. Family Nurture: Only inflationary increases
13 Student A id:
a With a reallocation of labor and changes in personnel the
salary and benefit portion of this budget increases. A full
time Director of Financial Aid is included
b. Student Aid use is calculated as a percentage of tuition.
In the budget the aid is calculated as 30 7% of tuition
which reflects current use and is up from 30.0% in the
current year
14 Development: Growth in this area calls for increased funding for
wages.
15. Public Relations The purpose of this special designation is to
separate the cost of keeping the seminary constituency generally
informed from the cost and efforts specifically directed toward
fundraising. This budget represents personnel cost, and printing
andmailing cost to send out the “In Covenant” newsletter
Added costs for a larger mailing list and improving “In
Covenant.”

<a id="ga26-p320"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=320 printed_page=null -->

16 Gift & Estate Planning: Lower dues to PCA Foundation and
inflationary increases.
17 Admissions: Higher student labor and increased allocation of
Director’s salary.
18 Electronic Media Production: Increased allocation of
Director’s salary and added funds for audio tapes and equipment.
19 Business Office: The provision made for a Director of Finance
and Human Resources in last year’s budget is removed. This was
mostly offset by a higher allocation o fm is personnel and
expenses to the business office and out of other departments.
An appropriate portion of my time is allocated to the Capital
Campaign Other changes are primarily inflationary.
20 Physical Plant: Allows for the addition of another half a person
to crease a full time secretary. Additional hours were added for
cleaning new areas (FI, S19 and Archaeology). Other changes
are inflationary
Auxiliary Enterprises:
1. Operations: Increases are inflationary.
2. Student Apartments: Inflationary increases for the most part
Some provision is made for merit increases for the Supervisor of
Maintenance & Operations and the Supervisor of Grounds &
Equipment as recognition for the significant savings they provide
the Seminary each year by doing project work in-house with very
professional results.
3 Tape of the Month Club: Inflationary increases and expanding
donor involvement.
Transfers:
1. Educational & General:
a. Some costs of the utility control system are being paid
back to the Plant fund over a 2 to 3 year period Some
costs reflect expansion of our new system to computer
control other campus areas This is the third of three
payments
b Annually money is transferred to the Plant Fund so that
we can replace the President’s auto every four years. His
used vehicle (a mini van) is then used by the Seminary
transportation department for a few more years to provide
for regular vehicle rotations
2. Auxiliary Enterprises:
a $40,800 - is set aside as reserves for future apartment
repairs

<a id="ga26-p319"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=321 printed_page=319 -->

b. $4,000 - is the second of six payments (the first payment
was $3,000) to pay back the purchase of a four wheel
drive vehicle equipped for snow removal. This will allow
us to do our own snow removal each year saving annual
contract cost
E. Contingency: There is no provision for a contingency.

<a id="ga26-p320"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=322 printed_page=320 -->

INSURANCE, ANNUITIES AND RELIEF
PROPOSED BUDGET

STATEMENT OF PURPOSE
the purpose of the board of trustees of the Insurance, annuity and relief funds of the
Presbyterian Church in America is to glorify God through serving His servants who
minister in the Church by providing for the health and welfare, retirement and relief
needs of the denomination’s ministers, missionaries, lay church workers and their
families.

• This budget reflects the costs incurred to administer the trust funds for
Insurance, Retirement, and Relief. This budget does not reflect the
financial activity in those trust funds. (Complete financial activity in the
trust funds may be found in the IAR Annual Report, which includes
audited financial statements.)
• The 1999 budget reflects an overall increase of 5.30% over the 1998
budget. 1997 actuals are unaudited.
• the increase in the retirement portion of support and revenue is a more
accurate representation of the allocation of expenses between supporting
activities.
• The total number of staff budgeted for in 1999 is nine and one-half FTE.
Currently, all of these positions are filled.
• An overall net increase of 3.51% in salaries and benefits is assumed in
1999. Budgeted positions include a 4% cost of living increase and 10%
health insurance increase.

<a id="ga26-p323"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=325 printed_page=323 -->

<a id="ga26-p325"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=327 printed_page=325 -->

MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA
PROPOSED BUDGET

The MNA Mission:
In obedience to and in dependence upon our sovereign God, the purpose of MNA is to
enable PCA churches and presbyteries to start and develop healthy, reproducing
churches in North America by providing vision communication, resource development,
strategic planning, training, support services, and special ministries, so that a
movement of multiplying reformed church is initiated and sustained.
• This budget reflects our continuing emphasis on regionalization - assisting and
working with presbyteries to develop church planting in local areas.
• While the total revenue and expenses will increase 14 5% compared to the 1998
budget, the portion included in the “Partnership Share” will only increase 4%.
• an overall net increase of 5% in salaries and benefits is assumed. That is an
aggregate of cost of living, merit increases and health insurance costs.
• The total number of multicultural and campus (international) missionaries budgeted
for is 9. Between 59 and 77 projects are budgeted to receive direct support in
church planting, multicultural church planting and urban and mercy ministries.
• The total number of full-time equivalent staff budgeted for in the 1998 budget was
27. In 1999 it was 29 Vi. This increase is in campus ministries due to their
expanding ministry. Twenty-two and a half of these positions are currently filled.
• The cost being charged by the Administrative Committee for office space is
expected to increase from $16.25/square foot in 1998 to $16.75/square foot in 1999.
• The complete 1999 budget for Reformed University Ministries (MNA Campus
Ministries and the affiliated presbytery campus committee) is provided for
information a san attachment o fm NA’s 1998 GA report.
