<a id="ga26-p328"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=330 printed_page=328 -->

Mission to the World
PROPOSED CONSOLIDATED 1999 BUDGET

I. Methodology and Background:
The 1999 budget is proposed from an analysis of key factors that influence the income
and expenses of mission to the world operating ina global context with a rapidly
changing global economy. we reviewed the results of 1997 and extended these
indicators into 1998 and 1999.
The year 1997 saw a strong US dollar grow even stronger. For mission work this can
sometimes be helpful. Our overseas missionary expenses in US dollars ended the year
at approximately ninety five percent of budget without any major change to program
content. we also saw a consistent and growing us economy with avery strong growth
in the value of the stock market. The economic environment was positive and this was
reflected in consistent growth in contributions from churches and individuals to our
missionaries and programs. We continued to see additional gifts and trusts coming into
the endowment fund. The total income for the year was just under one hundred eight
percent of budget
The solid US economy, a strong dollar, a growing base of home churches, and an
expanding force of missionaries all contributed to a very positive year for Mission to the
World.
II. Future Directions for 1999:
There are several major goals coming from the New Horizon Project that will be part of
the 1999 program. The church-planting center concept should start to appear in various
forms during the later part of the year. The goal of the church-planting center is to
actively support and involve our missionaries and national pastors in proven methods
and best practices in promoting church-planting movements.
The program for National Worker’s Training, which has received initial funding and
minimal expenditures in 1997 will be fully operating in 1999. The expanded
involvement of national workers in church planting is a major part of the future vision
developed by the New Horizon Project.
By 1999, the Southern Africa and the European Muslim programs should be in full
operation. The goal to open one new field in 2000 has been budgeted. The planning
and selection of potential fields will occur in 1998 and the selection and recruitment of
missionaries in 1999.

<a id="ga26-p329"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=331 printed_page=329 -->

Functional realignment and expanded staffing were completed in 1997 for our peoplecentered efforts in the home office. Plans in the home office are to strengthen recruiting
and ongoing support of the missionary staff because the world is undergoing such rapid
change.
Plans for information technology in 1999 focus mainly on better utilization of global
communications (the Internet) and the integration and sharing of data between the donor
services system, the human resource information system, the contact management
system and the short-term project management system.

Major development efforts will continue to focus on raising endowment funds. The
endowment income will go to reduce the administrative assessment and to fund major
new programs like the church-planting center. Our partner relations teams have also set
goals to continue to strengthen relationships with churches who are the major revenue
source for MTW and a growing factor in funding the home office.
III. Projection Base for 1999;
Projections have been made regarding the number of missionaries, home office staff,
annual income and annual expenses. In making these projections, the following
assumptions were used:
We anticipate that renewed efforts to recruit missionaries in 1997 and 1998 will begin
to show results during 1999. Using 1995 as the comparison year to 1999, the number of
career missionaries grew from 484 to 541. Two-year missionaries grew from 94 to 135,
the intern program grew from 48 to 100, and the two-week Impact grew from 1958 to
4000.
CoMission was at its maximum in 1995, at 48 but will grow from 9 in 1997 to 20 in
1999. Home office staff will increase from 58.5 full time equivalent in 1997 to 62 in
1999. New staff is limited to Impact and the new church-planting center.
We have anticipated that the US dollar will continue to be strong, but will not make
similar gains as in 1997. We expect other global economic factors, such as Asia, to be
unstable in many areas with higher inflation than in the US, but somewhat offset by
currency values.
Income (excluding endowment) and expenses have grown from approximately
$7,800,000 in 1985 to $24,800,000 in 1997 and is projected to grow to $26,900,000 in
1999. Income projections have assumed a continuing strong support for missionaries
from churches and individuals ina reasonably stable us economy and in a gradually
growing PCA denomination. We have projected the support requirements of
missionaries adjusted for inflation as a basis for future income.

<a id="ga26-p330"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=332 printed_page=330 -->

Missionary support accounts with deficit balances continue to decline. Total deficits for
all missionaries have gone from $400,000 in 1994 to $33,000 in 1997 indicating the
strong support of MTW's ministry partners The combined effect of inflation, growth
and currency value, using historic trends, indicates an increase of 3 to 4 percent for
1998 and 4 to 5 percent for 1999.
Partnership share giving for the home office grew from $300,000 in 1994 to $872,000
in 1997 and is projected at $1,007,000 in 1999. We have assumed that good church
relations and increased services to churches will allow some increase to continue.
However, not to the same extent as in 1997.
project and field income is calculated as apart of the support requirements of
missionaries and follows the same trends as mentioned above.
Investment income projections assume that interest rates will remain stable near their
current levels. Some temporarily restricted project funds have been reclassified to
permanently restricted endowment resulting in less interest income available for general
fund allocation However, in 1999, we will see reasonable endowment earnings transfer
to the general fund and the church-planting center to cover operational costs.
The 1999 proposed budget for impact is base dona summer program of 4000
students, an internship program of 100 persons and a two-year missionary staff of 135
missionaries. The following major factors form the basis for projecting the income and
expenses for IMPACT:
A. Office Staff—The cost in the home office reflect several changes and
new staff added during 1997. One additional full time equivalent
position is added for 1999.
B. Occupation costs have increased to reflect the new staff dealing with the
enlarged programs.
CoMission as an organization is undergoing several changes in 1998. We are not sure
of the results from the changes, however we do plan to continue expanding the
evangelistic ministry during 1999 in Ukraine and other areas of Russia and the old
Soviet Union.
The medical insurance fund (MIF) had a high expense year in 1997 but not too far out
of the ordinary. Therefore, we have used it as the base to project 1999. We expect that
costs will increase slightly faster than inflation. The operating capital reserve is very
low at the moment and will produce very little investment income.

With controlled costs in the home office, a slight increase in missionaries and increases
in partnership share giving, we project that the administrative assessment average will
drop slightly below twelve percent of total missionary income.

<a id="ga26-p331"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=333 printed_page=331 -->

Major programs that will influence expenses in 1999 are planned new missionary
growth, the church-planting movement training and mentoring, the expansion of
personnel services with emphasis on recruiting and training, and the expansion of the
summer two week program
There are three tables showing the consolidated income and expenses budget proposed
for 1999 The first table presents a historical perspective showing 1997 and 1998
information. The second shows a functional breakdown between program,
administration and fund raising. The third table shows changes in budget from 1998 to
1999.
In addition to the income and expense budget, the capital expense budget is presented
on the last table in the amount of $80,000. It assumes no major facility improvement
expenses. The major items to be purchased relate to information technology, improved
telecommunication and some office reconfigurations to maximize space utilization
following the implementation of virtual office work arrangements for a few appropriate
staff

<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=334 printed_page=332 -->

PCA FOUNDATION, INC.
1999 PROPOSED BUDGET SUMMARY

GENERAL COMMENTS:
In preparing the PCA Foundation Proposed 1999 Budget, we tried to keep increases
to a minimum where possible However, the budget anticipates the implementation of new
initiatives and, therefore, shows some significant increases in support/revenue and in
expense from 1997 actual levels.
ASSUMPTIONS:
The following economic assumptions were taken into consideration when preparing
our 1999 proposed budget. The Foundation presently employs two full-time employees and
one part-time employee
3 .5% Inflation/Consumer Price Index
5.0% Postal Rate
7.0% Travel Costs
10.0% Health Insurance
5 0% Other Insurance

NOTES TO NATURAL EXPENSE STATEMENT
SUPPORT & REVENUE
1. UNDESIGNATED EARNINGS - These are earnings on funds held by the PCA
Foundation, mainly from Advise & Consult Funds and the PCAF Endowment,
which are used to reduce our budget needs so as to continue reducing the actual cost
to the committees and agencies.
2. C & A SUPPORT - Four committees and agencies (Covenant College, Covenant
Theological Seminary, Mission to North America & Mission to the World) pay
support in 1998 to the Foundation in the amount of $120,000 or $30,000 each. This
will be reduced to $60,000 or $15,000 each in 1999.
3. FEES - Administrative fees charged on funds held for long term administration such
as Charitable Remainder Trusts, Endowments, Annuities, etc. This represents an
approximate 9% annual increase over 1997 actual and a 30% increase over the 1998
budget. This is warranted due to an expected increase in the number of charitable
remainder trusts and endowments
4. PRODUCTION REVENUES NL - This category is for income from churches
related to the stewardship program. No monies have been budgeted since the
continuation of the program is under review.

<a id="ga26-p335"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=337 printed_page=335 -->

5. PRODUCTION REVENUES RI/BI - This figure represents a modest amount for
churches reimbursing the Foundation for any bulletin inserts they may use
6 CONTRIBUTIONS - Gifts made to help underwrite the Foundation Operating
Budget. The budgeted amount is a significant increase over the 1997 actual amount
due to new funding opportunities being planned, which will provide the resources
necessary for the PCAF to properly operate without increased funding from the
Agencies and Committees.
7. INTEREST INCOME - Interest on checking account.
8. MISC. - No miscellaneous income budgeted.
OPERATIONS EXPENSES
9. PRESIDENT'S SALARY - The increase is due to hiring a new president in 1998.
Also, in 1997, the position was filled by an individual who contributed his services
at no cost to the Foundation.
10. PRESIDENT'S BENEFITS - Amount based on 1998 amounts, adjusted for
inflation.
11. STAFF WAGES & BENEFITS - Increased approximately 3.5%.
12. TRAVEL EXPENSE - Overall expense is up 36% over the 1997 actual in
anticipation of additional travel.
13. PROFESSIONAL SERVICES - Increase from 1997 actual and 1998 budget due to
budgeting for a consultant/field representative, partially offset by budgeted
decreases in accounting, legal and other professional expenses.
14. PROMOTION - Slight decrease from 1998 budget, but up substantially over 1997
actual due to significant reductions of promotional activities during 1997 and new
programs in 1999.
15. OFFICE EXPENSE - Increase represents more activity in 1999 than in 1997.
16. POSTAGE/UPS/FED EX - Approximates 1998 budget
17. TAXES & LICENSES -Sam e as 1998 budget.
18. RENT - $16.75 per square foot for 1,416 feet (includes common area)
19. TELEPHONE - Increase compared to 1997 actual and 1998 budget due to increased
usage by president and staff.

<a id="ga26-p336"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=338 printed_page=336 -->

20. DUES & SUBSCRIPTIONS - Slight decrease to 1998 budget due to elimination <
several publications and memberships
21 TRAINING - Increase from 1997 actual and 1998 budget since new president an
consultant/field representative should have training in 1999.
22. BOARD EXPENSE - Modest increase from 1997 actual.
23. SEARCH COMMITTEE - This committee was set up to search for a new presidew
No expense budgeted for 1999, as new president was hired in January 1998
24. SABBATICAL EXPENSE - This was a one time expense for Jack Hudson, forme
president
25 OFFICE INSURANCE - Decrease from 1998 budget, based on actual 199:
premiums paid.
26. GA EXPENSE - Marginal increase over 1998 budget. Meeting will be in St Louis
27. GA NOMINATING COMMITTEE - 6% increase over 1997 actual.
28. MISCELLANEOUS - For miscellaneous eexpenditures.
29. SPECIAL RESERVE - No amount budgeted.
30 DEPRECIATION -
31 CAPITAL EXPENDITURES - Based on our best estimate of needed equipment an<
software.
32 LESS DEPRECIATION -

Ridge Haven 1999 Budget Comparisons Statement Notes

Line 5, Resident Leases - This category includes income from lot leases, lot lease
interest, lot lease maintenance fees (the administrative amount we charge for
keeping all lot information on file and current), water hookups, water usage fees,
and road maintenance fees
Line 5, 1999 Budget figure - The reason for the low budget figure as compared to the
actuals is that we have no expectation of selling/leasing more lots and/or receiving
water hookup fees which are the two main items that give rise to the larger
figures. The figure presented represents the actual projected amount to be
charged for water usage and road maintenance fees for 1999.
Line 6, Other Contributions - This category includes contributions from WIC groups,
other groups, and individuals toward the daily operations of Ridge Haven
Line 7, Non-medical Reimbursements - This category includes amounts paid to Ridge
Haven by individuals or groups for the use of Ridge Haven postage, office copier,
vehicle parts, and fuel
Line 14, Sale of Assets - This is a newly added category The amounts that are reported
or provided are unpredictable. there is no certainty of income from the sale of
assets from year-to-year It has only been in the last year that there has ever been
any activity in this category. An example would be the sale of an old Jeep
cherokee an done of our school buses
Support/Revenue Total - The total in this column corresponds to the December 31, 1997
Financial Income statement minus the total of the designated items and the
income from the Cassette tapes.
Line 17, 1999 Budget - The large amount reflected here, just as last year allows for the
hiring of additional needed staff including a promotions Director, Conference
Director, and Maintenance Assistant. (As long as Ridge Haven continues to
receive such a low percentage of its Partnership Share, we will be unable to add
what is needed j the amount also reflects a cost-of-living adjustment of 3% above
the 1998 budgeted figure and one third of the amount necessary to begin to bring
our employees to the average remuneration for employees in comparable
positions in other area camps and conference ministries
Line 19, Counselors and camp support staff - The 1999 budget figure provides for an
amount as available and needed that will allow us to add counselors for larger
camps and a junior counselor program.

<a id="ga26-p340"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=342 printed_page=340 -->

Line 21, Contract Personnel - We continue to utilize contract personnel in several ways.
However, as indicated by the actual amounts expended, for the last two years, the
expense for these has been included in other line items reflected in the budget.
Examples would be individuals we have contracted with to build a parking lot,
and do our water system testing. It is our anticipation to phase out this
category/line item in the budget for the year 2,000.
Line 22, Worker's Comp Insurance - Both the actual figures from 1996 and 1997 are
estimated and approximate The independent auditor retained by the insurance
company to provide the correct data for this category has informed us that we
overpaid our premiums in 1996 to the point that we have had a credit that carried
us all the way through 1997. Our numbers have been determined according to the
insurance-company-provided formula for calculating workers comp insurance
Line 23, Payroll Taxes - This amount reflects the corresponding increase for the
addition of the Promotions Director, Conference Director, and Maintenance
Assistant added in the line 17, Other Salaries and Benefits.
Line 24, Other Fees - This category includes North Carolina Fees (usually a small
amount), Miscellaneous fees (also usually a small amount), On-site fees (what it
costs us to have/pay climbing guides, etc., Off-site fees (a very large amount for
rafting, etc.), Solid Waste Fees, and Pool Fees (the amounts we pay for county
inspection at the beginning of each season).
Line 35, 1999 Budget - We continue to need additional equipment and furnishings for
several of our buildings. These items are purchased as the money comes
available
Line 36, Grounds - We have several erosion and landscape projects we are seeking to
implement. The amount indicated will allow us to do these as the money comes
in.
Line 39, Maintenance and Tools - Ridge Haven owns and operates a large and varied
inventory of maintenance equipment and tools including a tractor, truck snow
plow attachment, air compressor, tire-changer, bushhog, etc. The needed funds
will allow us to replace and add needed equipment and tools.
Line 41, Vehicles - We have increased this category to allow us not only to maintain
our vehicles but to replace those that fail and to add needed new ones.

<a id="ga26-p341"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=343 printed_page=341 -->

Line 46, Facility Renovations - In the 1996 budget this was called Facilities
Maintenance which may have confused readers with other maintenance
categories. With the identification change to "renovations", which is what this
category refers to, the confusion is removed. All 21 of Ridge Haven
buildings/facilities need renovations from time to time that are not otherwise
provided for in the day-to-day maintenance category The number provided in
this category for the 1999 budget reflects renovations for 9 different buildings that
could use immediate renovations. These renovations and their professionally
estimated projected cost building-by-building are provided on the appended sheet
Line 47, Water Systems - This category was newly inserted in the 1997 budget due to
the large amount expended in 1996 in this specific category Our anticipation is
that it will continue to be an increasing expense. It was previously included under
facilities.
Line 47, Water Systems - We are very thankful that the water system crisis that was
reflected in the 1998 budget figure of $50,000 was averted With the increase of
the number of resident users however, our maintenance costs are also increasing
Currently as this budget is being prepared (February 1998), we have three new
residences under construction that will be utilizing the Ridge Haven water
systems. each of these means an increase in the maintenance costs. In addition,
we believe it is necessary to have on hand an amount that would enable us to
handle various crises involving pump equipment, storage tanks, piping, and well
failure.
Operating Expense Totals - The total in this column corresponds to the December 31,
1997 Financial Expense statement. It should be noted that we were able to
spend more than we took in by dipping into the balance carried forward from
1996.

<a id="ga26-p342"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=344 printed_page=342 -->

<a id="ga26-p344"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=346 printed_page=344 -->

Report to the 26th General Assembly of
Christian Education and Publications
INTRODUCTION
The case statement of Christian Education and Publications reads, “ To a Reformed
Christian, the “world” is more than just a source of temptation It’s a responsibility.”
The statement furthers reads, “In the PCA, the Reformation (i.e. the spirit of the
Reformation) is more than just an echo of the past. It is nothing less than the clearest,
truest expression of the biblical faith.”
Dr. Louis Berkhof once said, “Many children of God are even today living in spiritual
poverty, though they are rich in Christ and heirs of the world, because they have not
been taught to see the greatness and splendor of their spiritual heritage. if we do not
want our children to live as paupers in spiritual penury and want while untold riches of
grace and mercy are at their disposal, we must employ all the means at our comand to
unfold before their very eyes the treasures of divine grace that they are heirs in Christ
Jesus. . . . Through lack of training the whole inheritance is sometimes lost.”
Discipling this generation with the same biblical truths that surfaced in the Protestant
Reformation sets the stage for our agenda. But as we state, “So that instead of simply
memorizing historical creeds, reformed christians can make history of their own.
Applying theology in ways that will transform their lives, their communities, their
world” (CE/P case statement). Our aim is to communicate the spirit of the Reformation
to this generation of people who are described as biblically illiterate, totally pluralistic
and relativistic, and definitely steeped in the “spirit of the age.”
While we rejoice in the growth statistics of the Numbers of Christians from other
countries such as Africa, and some of the former Soviet satellites, we are deeply
concerned that the Christian faith continues to lose ground statistically in the U.S. Our
entire culture has been baptized into a post-modern culture that is relativistic and
ignorant of biblical truth. We believe that Christian Education and Publications is a key
player in the equipping process that enables the PCA to serve God, His Kingdom, and
His people by imparting a biblical world and life view. CE/P is providing teaching,
training materials, and consulting services from a biblical, reformed and evangelical
perspective. it is encouraging and influencing PCA members in their development of a
Christian mind and heart, which bears witness to a Kingdom of God life-orientation and
is reflected in the building of strong interpersonal relationships.
Our mission statement reads: Christian education is a God ordained covenantal
process; therefore Christian Education and Publications, a san agency of the
Presbyterian Church in America, focusing upon the sovereignty of god within the

<a id="ga26-p345"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=347 printed_page=345 -->

covenant context, seeks to glorify God by equipping, training, encouraging
believers, particularly leaders and teachers, to make a difference by proactively living
re dem pt ive christian lives and making disciples of god by :
Knowing the Lord,
Knowing the Word,
Knowing the world,
Knowing ourselves.
With a commitment to understand and interpret the truth in different cultural settings
from a biblical ly reformed world view that enables them to serve the lord with a global
vision by: penetrating this generation with the "prophetic ” presentation of the Gospel,
building strong relationships through which Christ builds His church, extending the
kingdom of god in every area of life and equipping people for actual ministry.
we facet he challenge of an ever-increasing secular humanistic world. Only four
percent of the rising "millennial generation" are projected to be Christians. This same
generation is also predicted to bethe most influential of our time. God has called us
like his servants of old, “to understand the times and what God would have us to do ”
1. review of the past
CE/P works from a carefully designed plan that focuses upon a five year span. That
plan calls for us to study our world, analyzing and evaluating the trends. As a staff and
a committee, we attempt to focus on those trends that demand our attention. Some of
those trends are good and useful; others are dangerous and could divert us from our
mission. One of our sub-committees emphases is on scanning the external
environment—the environment of the world in which God has placed us, the
environment of the general Christian community, and the environment of the PCA.
Within our plan, CE/P’s philosophy of ministry identified nine areas that we believe are
foundational for us. We are: Biblically sound, Reformed in philosophy and theology,
pedagogically sound, world focussed, church related, audience targeted in our services,
sound in management tasks, continually developing our human resources, and fiscally
sound
In 1998, CE/P is in its last year of our current ‘Tive-Year Strategic Faith Plan”. This
plan has dealt with an important five years within the PCA’s brief history. We believe
that the present moment calls for Christians to be as strategic as possible and we have
tried to reflect that in our plan. Christianity is not gaining ground, in numbers or
influence, at least within the North American cultures. While we do not believe we
have been called to be cultural transformers, we do believe God has called us to be “salt
and light”— visible expressions of His Kingdom. Our plan has aimed in that direction
In all of our training we have attempted to understand and utilize the best that God has
made available to us through both common and special grace. We have not ignored or

<a id="ga26-p346"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=348 printed_page=346 -->

isolated ourselves from the world, but have operated on the Augustinian principle
echoed by Anselm that “all truth is God’s truth,” and as Abraham Kuyper has stated,
“There is no area of life over which Christ has not said MINE.” We have not attempted
to take the church back in history to a time impossible to recreate. Our commitment,
following the spirit of the reformation of focusing on the Scriptures, is continually to
be the church for today, speaking the Word clearly to this generation that is bombarded
with constant change and is tempted to keep religion out of the public eye
Through our seminars, conferences, bookstore, video library, and our leadership
consulting, we have continued to promote those things that would strengthen the
church, and encourage and assist families in their Christian responsibilities. Our Great
Commission Publication Sunday school materials, our women’s studies, our men’s
focus, as well as our youth and children’s ministries have given us opportunities to do
just that
We have used every opportunity to encourage people to read and think biblically and to
identify the issues that merit attention. we have used all of our resources, including our
EQUIP for Ministry publication, to put these issues before PCA leaders and people-atlarge Thinking Christianly and encouraging people in this area have driven our plan
forward.
We have continued to expand our men and women’s ministries, encouraging a spirit of
teamwork and careful partnership within local churches. We have seen our youth
ministry programs literally multiply with opportunities, even beyond anything we
planned for five years ago. Not only have put in place youth regional trainers to assist
us from the Atlanta office, but we have helped dozens of churches fill youth ministry
positions. Presently, as the plan established, we are networking with some key churches
to train youth interns in an effort to meet the ever-growing demand in our churches. As
of this report, there are around fifty churches seeking to fill youth ministry positions.
We have increased our regional teacher trainers to ten in order to meet the growing need
and to service the churches throughout North America with their teacher training and
broader Christian education needs. These reflect our commitment to networking the
good resources that God has placed within the PCA.
While a review of the five-year plan encourages us as we have seen God lead us
forward, there were areas of our plan, which we were unable to fulfill. Particularly
these were to hire a director of children’s ministry and to focus more attention on the
men’s ministry and the Christian school movement. We have done many good things,
but these are areas of challenge in our new “Five-Year Strategic Faith Plan”.
Our biggest concern during the past five years has been financial support. As with all
plans, ours must reflect sound fiscal responsibility. While CE/P has generally operated
within its income, it has lacked the resources to develop those areas mentioned above.
In order to help our churches maximize their effectiveness in Christian education and

<a id="ga26-p347"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=349 printed_page=347 -->

training, and in order to be faithful to our understanding of the great commission we
must leave no stone unturned

2. PRESENT CHALLENGES
CE/P, in accordance with its plan, is attempting to develop its intern training program
for youth ministers, and to facilitate Presbyteries’ responsibilities in the required one
year internship prior to ordination of teaching elders We are also seeking to use our
program entitled institute of church ministries to increase the learning process and
develop more effective teachers and leaders This is being offered to individuals in
local churches who desire to move to another level of lay training We have designed a
curriculum that can be used to this end Each participant will be able to take six
"correspondence type" courses, through distance learning, a near na certificate of
completion.
A national conference for Women In the Church is planned for 1999 We are
anticipating between 4,000-6,000 PCA women to come together to focus on mercy
ministries— community and compassion Regional training conferences for youth
leaders are being expanded Conferences for music and worship, pastors and wives, as
well as youth and children are being conducted
developing a financial base from church support and individuals continues to be a
concern for CE/P as is increasing our readership of EQUIP For Ministry. This
publication is designed primarily to add to our training resources and to keep local
leadership and readers aware of resources and opportunities available through CE/P.
we are studying regularly the best means of serving the church through our training
programs, materials, electronic services, our personal contacts, and the various forms of
media. The video presented to this assembly is designed to help us tell our story to the
local churches. We want every church needing our resources to know of their
availability You could be a help to us in your local church in this area
Our GCP Sunday school materials continue to expand and develop New and revised
materials for primaries, juniors and two and three year olds are in various stages of
development and availability Vacation Bible School materials have been published
and sales of the Trinity Hymnal continue to grow in our churches Because biblical
worship differs in its expression from church to church, new worship materials are on
the drawing board in the form of a supplement to the Trinity Hymnal

3. OPPORTUNITIES FOR THE FUTURE
We face a future that we believe is the most challenging in the church’s modern history.
CE/P is developing its Five-Year Strategic Faith plan to focus on a monumental

<a id="ga26-p348"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=350 printed_page=348 -->

challenge The grid through which CE/P is developing its plan is a focus on the rising
“millennial generation ” These children born between the late 70’s and the late 90’s
make up the second largest generation in our history— 72 million strong. This
generation is described as the most educated, and potentially the most influential
generation of our history They are also considered to be the most spiritual, but only
four percent are projected to be Christians
This is the first genuinely post-modern generation that believes that truth is socially or
individually determined Missing a Christian consensus in our country has led to
divorcing religion and morality from public areas of life, believing that truth, if there is
any truth, is neither universal nor absolute, and acceptance of declining antithesis. We
are not giving acceptance of this generation a standard of measurement hence, they are
leaving the church by droves
experts both in and out of the church a retelling us that the key to reaching this
generation is older people— the grandparents and to a lesser degree, the parents They
are hungry for relationships and experiences that touch their lives at their deepest level,
and are reaching out to the “builder” grandparents for relationships. The sad irony is
that the older generation, not understanding the millennial children is often pushing
them away— in their families, their churches, and other significant institutions. The
Apostle Paul’s instruction for older men to teach younger men and older women to
teach younger women, plus all the different approaches to reaching and teaching, listed
in Titus two have never been more significant
The millennial generation is made up of young men and women who enjoy a good time,
but who do not want to be patronized or viewed merely as “potentials in process.” They
process information quite differently than the generations before them, hence requiring
creativity in teaching and preaching They have an understanding of music that is
peculiar to their generation and they want to be loved unconditionally. Their extreme
spirituality is not to be confused with the Bible’s definition of spirituality, but it
provides a building block for reaching out to them.
CE/P is totally committed to doing what we can to sensitize, train, and make resources
available through its men and women’s ministries, its youth and children ministries, and
its leadership training to meet this serious challenge. Because most people who accept
Christ do so prior to their 18th birthday we are faced with a closing twelve year window
of opportunity. Then another generation will be here and these children will be adults
without Christ or a Christian influence, yet they are the ones that are destined to shape
our history for the 21st century
CE/P is and will be, with increasing momentum, conducting training seminars to help
churches and individuals know how to bridge into this millennial generation. Much of
what we do will focus on training older people in this vital ministry. We will be using
our regional teacher trainers and youth leader trainers to assist us in multiplying this
ministry. our recent program used in many of churches entitled “Celebrate the child ”

<a id="ga26-p349"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=351 printed_page=349 -->

will be transformed into a grid of “reach the children and youth ” It will take great
effort to make a difference in the lives of this generation but we believe that in our
biblically reformed worldview and our focus on covenant theology, God has given us
what we need to reach them Berkhof was right, “Through a lack of training the whole
inheritance is sometimes lost.”
we need the help of the churches and individuals in this task we will be assisting in
developing these ministries, networking the resources in our churches and other
biblically based churches, and doing what we can to rally the PCA to this challenge
The stakes have never been higher and we are working with a great burden as we move
forward.
CONCLUSION
CE/P strongly desires to be a catalyst to facilitate the kind of ministry outline above
We want to be positioned to help our churches better reach out to this generation that is
embracing everything from Islamic faith to the New Age Movement. We want our men
and women to be equipped for this task This cannot happen without serious prayer,
financial support, and a willingness to make avery costly but rewarding commitment.
We want to say loudly and clearly, builders, grandparents, your greatest challenge may
be yet before you. Boomer parents realize what your children need more than anything
else Church leaders, be aware of your church’s opportunity to make a difference in
reaching these children and youth.
Dr. Allan Curry academic dean at Reformed Theological Seminary and a CE/P regional
teacher trainer said recently in one of our annual regional teacher training sessions that
the idea of family in the Old Testament is generally broader than mother and father “It
often refers to the extended family and especially within the covenant community.”
With that in mind, we adults are part of God’s family with a responsibility to reach this
generation The vow that we take as a congregation when an infant receives the
covenant sign of baptism bespeaks of this privilege and responsibility.
Therefore, we offer the following recommendations:
1 That the following minutes of the CE/P Committee be approved: June 9,1997,
September 18, 19, 1997, February 12, 13, 1998
2. That the Assembly express its thanks to TE Legree Finch, RE Jack Sullivan, TE
Wallace Tinsley, and RE Robert Whittaker for their service to the PCA by
serving as members of the CE/P Committee
3. That in light of our covenant theology the churches be thanked by the Assembly
for participating in the Celebrate the Child and use that momentum to continue
its ministry to them.
4 That the Women in the Church be thanked for their $110,000 contribution to
CE/P to help develop and expand its youth ministry program.

<a id="ga26-p352"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=352 printed_page=null -->

That churches be encouraged to participate in the 1998 WIC Love Gift program
directed to MNA
That special prayer be offered during the year for the plans being made for the
1999 WIC national conference
That having completed its annual evaluation of Coordinator, TE Charles
Dunahoo that he be elected as Coordinator for the coming year
That Presbyterian Youth of America, a partner in CE/P’s ministry, be received
as part of CE/P’s ministry to youth and that the present board, comprised of
PCA Teaching Elders’ function as an advisory sub-committee to the CE/P
Committee
that as a means of communication, training, resource information, and other
Assembly information, the Assembly encourage local sessions to make Equip
for Ministry available to its leadership
That in light of the above report and the challenge facing the PCA and CE/P,
local sessions be encouraged to pray for CE/P and its needed resources during
the next fiscal year
That Sunday September 13th 1998, be designated as Christian Education Sunday
in the PCA and that local churches utilize the materials from CE/P to assist in
that promotion.
That the budget for 1999 be approved
That the 1997 Audit Report be found in order and approved

<a id="ga26-p351"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=353 printed_page=351 -->

“In All Things Christ Pre-eminent”
COVENANT COLLEGE

April 8, 1998
Greetings in the name of Jesus Christ.
on behalf of the trustees of covenant College, i submit to you a report of the activities
of Covenant College for the year 1996-1997. I hope you appreciate that we are unable
to give you a report for the most current year due to the timing of the school year and
General Assembly.
more than a public relations piece, the document that we are choosing to send you is a
document that is generated by the faculty and staff and approved by the Board. Its
purpose is to evaluate the overall effectiveness of the college in a number of important
areas. Faculty and administration are constantly looking for better ways to assess the
effectiveness of the work that is being done at Covenant College and to improve the
overall educational program.
The relationship between the college and the denomination is a relationship that we
cherish and that we want to nurture and cultivate. We believe the growth in the number
of churches supporting Covenant College and the number of students indicates growing
denominational support it is the sincere desire of the board and the faculty and
administration of the college, to serve Christ’s kingdom, and specifically the
Presbyterian Church in America, by training our future leaders in such a way that they
will have a biblical worldview. We are grateful for the grace of God which is evident in
the progress and growth of Covenant College.
In Christ,
Is/ Joel Belz
Chairman, Board of Trustees
of Covenant College

<a id="ga26-p352"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=354 printed_page=352 -->

The 1996-97
Institutional Effectiveness Report
Office of Planning
January 1998

Introduction

This year’s Institutional Effectiveness Report is divided into five major sections: Academic
Affairs, Student Affairs, Financial Affairs, Advancement, and an update on the 1995-2000 Five-
Year Plan. Each section contains information that provides evidence of the health of the
institution. Some of that evidence is in the form of student outcomes, some as inputs, and some
as results of various performance indicators.
The Academic Affairs section includes information about students, faculty, accreditation, and
academic support functions. The student information deals with both student outcomes and
student profiles. Both types of information give evidence of the type of students who are
attracted to Covenant College and the results of their academic experience Faculty information
provides input evidence of the college’s efforts to provide a quality educational opportunity for
the students. Academic support information demonstrates additional input evidence of an
atmosphere conducive to learning.
The Student Affairs section presents evidence of services provided by the Student Development
Department for our students. This information includes both input and outcome evidence.
The Financial Affairs section of the report furnishes evidence of institutional financial stability
and how resources were used to provide students with learning opportunities. All financial aid
information is included in this section.
The section on Advancement includes all financial development and new student recruitment
information. While this evidence tends to be input in nature, it also demonstrates the
commitment of donors and new students to the institution.
The fifth and final section of the report outlines the progress of the college to meet the goals of
the 1995-2000 Five-Year Plan and any new opportunities that the college has chosen to explore.

Section 1: Academic Affairs
The effectiveness of Covenant College as an institution of higher education can only be seen
through the educational goals of the college. The college Statement of Purpose states that
Students should be acquiring a working knowledge of the scriptures... a broad
appreciation of the various aspects of creation, becoming familiar with valid
methods of inquiry... some depth in one or two academic disciplines... the
capacity for incisive, critical and logical thinking... and the ability to
communicate ideas in both speaking and writing
Assessing a student’s progress toward attainment of these goals is done in many ways. Some
methods of evidence collection are conducted annually and some less frequently. Following is
a collection of the evidence gathered this year to ascertain the effectiveness of the college in
helping students meet these education goals.

STUDENT PROFILE
Institutions of higher education have traditionally demonstrated educational effectiveness by
measuring what goes into the educational experience. Therefore, educational credentials of the
faculty and academic competence of the incoming students have often been two major measures
of academic effectiveness. While this still provides evidence of opportunity, it does not
demonstrate the student’s quality of attainment at the conclusion of the educational experience.
This section of the report will present evidence of quality at both the input and outcome portion
of the process along with other evidence relative to a student’s educational expenence at
Covenant.
SAT Scores
The SAT scores of entering first time freshmen provide a picture of the type student attracted to
Covenant College. Such evidence is considered, by many, as one factor of predictability of the
academic potential of the students. It also demonstrates the reputation of the college in the
marketplace, as rising SAT scores tend to suggest an increase in the number of serious students
desiring to attend Covenant.
The graph, which follows, shows the SAT scores of entering first time freshmen over the last
four years as compared with the national average. The I High School Class Rank
over this four year period have increased by 2.1% as cor
of only .59%.

<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=357 printed_page=355 -->

Class Rank The class rank of new first-time freshmen describes the quality of the entering
students, and is often considered to reflect the reputation of the institution.

Student Retention
Another means of measuring how effectively we are performing is to study the students who
choose to return to Covenant. Parents and students tend to select a particular college based on
whether they believe that college can fulfill its claims and meet the needs of its students. When
they see that happening, the students return. Therefore, an examination of retention trends over
the last few years should demonstrate the perceived effectiveness of the college
The following table shows the percentage of students by class in the falls of 1993
through 1997 who returned to die college as students the following fall
Previous Year’s Returned Returned Returned Returned
Fall 94 Fall 95 Fall 96* Fall 97*
Freshmen 83% 74% 84% 80%
Sophomores 77% 74% 76% 78%
Juniors 89% 95% 90% 84%
College-wide 82% 80% 83% 82%
* Based on name to name retention methodology
With only two years of name to name retention history it is difficult to draw many conclusions.
However, it does appear that retention has leveled off in the low 80’s%.

Graduation Rate
This statistic measures how many students in any given freshman class graduate within 150% of
the planned time. Since Covenant offers baccalaureate degrees based on a four-year stay, the
graduation rate is based on a six-year period. To be considered in the graduation rate, students
who came to Covenant as first time freshmen in the fall of 1991 (n=148) must have graduated
from Covenant by May of 1997.
87-93 88-94 89-95 90-96 91-97
Entering Freshmen 119 160 174 157 148
Graduates from that class 44 69 70 76 67
Graduation Rate 37% 43% 40% 48% 45%
lowing is a breakdown of when the entering freshman of 1991graduated.

N= 2 51 8 6
% who graduated within 1.4% 34.5% 5.4% 4.1%
One difficulty in using graduation (sometimes called completion) rates to measure the
effectiveness of an institution, and more importantly, to use that information to improve the
program, is in the time delay. Completion rate information provides a perspective of quality
that is four to six years old The only way to predict if the rate is improving is to examine the
long-term retention rates. Since good retention rates provide the base for good completion,
following is a table showing the change in retention rate from 1990 to the present
Returned Fall Fresh-Soph Soph-Junior Junior -S r Overall

1993 74% 85% 93% 85%

1997 80% 78% 84% 82%

The increase or decrease in retention rates will be reflected six years later in the
completion rate
Major Fields Achievement Tests Results
Each spring, graduating seniors participate in a national testing program related to their
discipline. The Major Fields Achievement Tests (MFATS) are not available m all fields so not
all our majors are involved in this testing
The results of the tests are reported in several ways Each student receives raw scores, which
can be converted to a percentile ranking of all the students who took the test the previous year
Departmental mean scores are also convertible to percentile ranking of the departmental mean
scores of all other colleges participating in the testing the previous year. Test scores (and
therefore percentile rankings) can also be reported for sub-sections of the test and selected
assessment criteria if there are five or more students taking the test. These sub-scores and
subsequent percentile rankings measure very specific content topics within the test
The reader should be aware that MFATS results provide limited information. These rankings
are comparable to those schools of our type taking the tests that year. It should be remembered
that departmental scores reflect only the results of that year’s students. Therefore, departments
with a small number of students taking the test may show great variance from year to year.
Also, some portions of the tests cover material

Senior Integration Project Evaluations

professors. Scores are based on an eight-point system, the highest score
with one being the lowest possible score and eight being
Master of Education
enrollment m the master of education program reached anew high with sixty-seven students
enrolled The program has six stated goals Following are the goals with the evaluation of each
goal The evaluation processes varied but were based mainly on student feedback Goals 1, 2,
and 4-6 are measured on a five-point scale with a minimal threshold acceptance level set at
three.
Goal Result

FACULTY PROFILE
The faculty of the college provides the major learning input opportunities for our students. A
college should be known and evaluated by the quality of education offered and the learning
outcomes of the students who go through that process. Therefore, an examination of the faculty
teaching at Covenant will provide important evidence of quality opportunities.
An important part of the student’s education aimed at meeting these goals is the core
curriculum. As stated in the academic bulletin, “the core curriculum is based on the faculty’s
belief that a liberal arts education should be broad and inclusive, introducing students to ideas
and values of continuing concern and providing them with historical and spiritual perspectives
on the complex problems of our society.” Covenant demonstrates the importance of the core
curriculum in the educational experience by ensuring that the same professors that teach in the
major curriculum staff these courses. The following analysis includes the entire core as listed in
the catalog except for courses that are sometimes taken for other than core requirements such as
the humanities electives and lab science requirements.
This past year twenty-four faculty members taught in the core curriculum, which represents
51% of the full-time teaching faculty involved in the core. Of those twenty-four, 71% hold
earned doctorates. Adjunct faculty taught only 13%ofthe total credit hours in the core.
An integral ingredient in any educational experience is longevity of high quality faculty. This
should be evidenced through promotion into a higher rank and in length of contract offered.
The Faculty Status Committee, with Board of Trustee approval determines promotion in rank.
Promotion to full professor normally requires possession of an earned doctorate and a scholarly
presentation which demonstrates integration of the discipline and the Christian faith. The length

<a id="ga26-p360"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=362 printed_page=360 -->

of a contract offered to a faculty member at covenant is dependent on quality of teaching, the
longevity of service, and the presentation mentioned for promotion. The Board of Trustees
approves a five-year contract (tenure) when faculty have demonstrated a high level of quality
over a period of time. Following is a table that shows the current status of faculty relative to
these issues:
There have only been three promotions since the fall 1994 Board meeting. Based on the
requirements of promotion, it appears that there are a significant number of professors and
associate professors that should be in the promotion or tenure track
Rank Total 1 Year 3 Year 5 Year The earned doctorate degree is
Contract Contract Contract held by 78 .7% of the full-time

Associate 21 14 6 1 an all-time high for the college.
Assistant 6 6 0 0 Of the ten who do not hold the
Instructor 1 1 0 0 terminal degree, five have started
work on their degree.

and over service of our faculty.

In March of 1997, Where Is The Salt? was published. This festschrift is a collection of essays
and studies written by faculty members honoring Dr. John W Sanderson, Jr., former Dean of
the Faculty.
A nationally used textbook by Covenant Education Professor Dr. Robert Ashlock, Error
Patterns in Computation, is now in its seventh edition.

ACCREDITATION
Since the 1995-96 Institutional Effectiveness Report, Covenant has been granted full
reaffirmation of Accreditation by the Commission on Colleges of the Southern Association of
Colleges and Schools. A visiting team from the Commission reviewed the college’s self-study
and made a total of forty-nine recommendations. The college presented responses to these
recommendations to the Commission and was granted full reaffirmation in the December 1996
meeting. The commission asked for a follow-up report cm eight recommendations That report
was completed and sent to the Commission on September 26, 1997.
As a result of the process of reaffirmation, Covenant has made some changes in several
programs, most notably the Quest program. The visiting committee deemed the program should
not be as interdisciplinary in nature and required changes in the type of credentials needed by
faculty. as a result of the S.A C.S. committee report, eight new Quest instructors with

<a id="ga26-p361"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=363 printed_page=361 -->

credentials appropriate for an Organizational Management program were selected from a field
of 59 applicants These new instructors were all personally oriented and have begun teaching in
the program. The new instructors all have business-related master’s degrees and were able to
meet the personal Christian commitment required of Quest instructors. Dr. David Hoover has
also transferred from the traditional program to the Quest program. He is teaching a full load in
Quest and providing academic oversight

ACADEMIC SUPPORT
The academic administration division provides resources to support the academic activities of
the college. While these academic activities are inputs to the educational experience, they
demonstrate the level and quality of academic opportunities available at Covenant College.
Academic Computing
This year academic computing was able to provide major improvements for faculty. Requests
for over $10,000 were met including nine faculty members who were provided new desktop
computer systems. The computer lab in Mills Hall also received some attention with the
processor upgrade to 133 MHz. A new computer lab was installed in Mills Hall with 26 new
machines. We now have 52 machines available to students, representing an on-campus student
to computer ratio of 13 to 1.
Approval was also given to fund a mobile technology cart, which permits faculty access to
previously unavailable technologies.
Audio-Visuals Department
AV services added TV and VCR units to classrooms, which resulted in all but one classroom on
campus being equipped with TV/VCR units in an av survey, 74% of faculty reported utilizing
AV equipment at least once a week while 66% said that they would like the capability to
incorporate video projection of computer, laser disc, or video into their classroom instruction.
This sentiment is in line with the new multi-media capacity that is being installed in Mills Hall.

Diversity Scholarship Recipient Program
During the 1996-1997 academic year, the program provided $198,610 in financial assistance for
47 students from a broad range of diversity backgrounds This group represents six percent of
the student population. This new program provides special scholarship consideration for
students who come from predominantly non-white areas or who are citizens of or have lived in
other countries. The college considers this broad definition of diversity more preferable to
traditional ethnic designations in creating a culturally diverse campus.
Experiential Studies
During the 1996-97 academic year, a total of thirty-three students participated in semester long
off-campus study programs. Eleven students studied in Oxford, England, one in Washington
dc , two in Colorado Springs, two in Jerusalem, and sixteen in the Czech Republic. The
College also completed negotiations with Tmava University in the Slovak Republic to establish
a new program that is being instituted this fall.

<a id="ga26-p362"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=364 printed_page=362 -->

Forty students participated in Christian service Break-on-Impact programs during spring break.
Two students participated in a work internship program with Astec Industries. Covenant is also
exploring the possibility of developing a formal co-op program.
Library
a library analysis of circulation statistics over the past four years shows a fairly steady rate of
books circulated (18-20) per FTE student per year. This is in accordance with expected usage
for this period. Use of the Internet and full text databases increases each year, and as yet we do
not have an accurate means of measuring this usage. Patterns of use are expected to continue to
change toward more use of electronic media During the period of July 1, 1996 to June 30,
1997, Kresge Library’s interlibrary services managed the following transactions through OCLC
relationships:
377 books borrowed from other libraries
262 photocopies obtained from other libraries
188 books lent to other libraries
827 = total items managed.
Hourly attendance counts show that the library is more than two-thirds full during afternoon and
evening hours at mid-term. The hourly average during October, 1996 was 37 students, an
increase over the average of 32 students for the previous year. Twenty carrels are assigned for
SIP students for the entire semester During spring semesters there is a waiting list for these
carrels.
The Library continues to compare our collection with Books for College Libraries. 3rd edition,
1988, and the annual supplement lists. Departmental comparisons have been completed for
philosophy, psychology, religion, economics, education, history and sociology. The Kresge
Library, for these disciplines, has holdings that range from 2,200 titles with 21% of same titles
for sociology to 1,800 titles with 67% of same titles for religion. Departments annually review
holdings in their area. Library purchases take into consideration faculty collection requests that
result from these reviews
Quest
Quest graduated 117 students in May 1997 and had another excellent recruiting period for the
1996-1997 academic year. The enrollment goal for this year was 273 tuition payments. This
goal was exceeded by 21 tuition payments for a total of 294 Included in this enrollment was a
new group that was set up at the request of TVA at their Sequoyah Nuclear facility location.
The Quest bad debt was approximately $90,000 above expectations, or about twice the norm.
In response to the 1996-1997 bad debt situation, the college has decided to take an aggressive
approach toward collecting tuition money from those who have defaulted. Steps are also being
taken on the front end to attempt to identify potential enrollees who could be bad risks.
The program conducted a one-year post-graduation survey of the 117 May 1996 Quest
graduates to determine how their Covenant education had contributed to their work
performance, career/personal development and world view. Evaluations of the ten Quest
modules resulted in positive impact scores ranging from 84% to 99%. Concepts affecting their
career/personal development resulted in positive scores ranging from 94% to 100%. Eight-five
percent of the students reported that Philosophy of Culture had a positive impact on their world
and life view, with 78% reporting this impact for the Old and New Testament courses.

<a id="ga26-p363"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=365 printed_page=363 -->

Office of Records
For the first time in the college’s history, a waiting list had to be started to ensure that offers of
admission could be honored Because of the expectation of full residence halls, the Admissions
Committee approved a priority merit system. This past fall a new software package was
implemented that should provide better service for students and faculty. It includes a
graduation requirement audit to assist faculty advisors in counseling student course selection
A spring semester survey was conducted with 73 students (approximately 10% of the traditional
student body) to determine their satisfaction with faculty advising Similar results were
achieved compared to the first survey, which was conducted during the spring of 1996. On a
scale of 1-5 where 5 is “very good”, the overall opinion of faculty advising was rated 3.91.
Advisors were rated at 4.62 on how well they know the graduation requirements of the student’s
major
a growing student population has resulted in a new emphasis on space utilization. This fall
73% of MWF classrooms and 52% of TuTh classrooms were utilized during normal class
scheduling hours. It is expected that these percentages will go considerably higher for fall 1997
due to enrollment increases
Women in the Church Lecture Series
A lecture series is now available to the college community through an endowment gift from the
Women in the Church. The program was initiated this year with Covenant alumnus Mr.
Michael Cromartie, of the Ethics and Public Policy Center in Washington DC., serving as guest
lecturer. Mr. Cromartie helped the community examine the issue of what theological principles
should shape the views of Christians concerning the role of government.

Section 2: Student Affairs
Career Development
The Career Development Center (CDC) staff provides programs and services enabling students
to explore God’s calling and implement job search plans. Student assessment of the CDC
services continues to illustrate the importance of career exploration and planning.
Assessment statement of the CDC... S 95 S 96 S 97
counseling/guidance helped me understand the big picture and make 3.2 3.1 3.1
progress on my career path.
made me aware of an/or administered tests that helped me identify 3.1 3 2.9
potential careers related to my strengths, interests and abilities.
is equipped with adequate books and other printed materials 2.8 3.1 3.1
assisted me in establishing goals and accomplishing them through follow- 2.8 3 3
up activities.
provides adequate information/assistance regarding placement services 3 3.2 3
promotes and encourages student participation in its programs and services 3.3 3.4 3.3

<a id="ga26-p364"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=366 printed_page=364 -->

Chapel Program
Chapel remams the single, common, daily learning/worship experience shared by members of
the Covenant College community. Dr. Donovan Graham, the college chaplain, completed his
sabbatical that focused on worship in an educational environment. Re-affirming the importance
of daily chapel and worship, the Board of Trustees approved policy to ensure student presence
and encourage student participation The Student Senate approved assessment policies to
measure student satisfaction with the chapel program. The chapel committee was effective in
reaching established goals achieving greater student leadership in chapel programs, increased
faculty involvement, increased visiting speakers, and increased pastoral involvement in the
preaching of the Word
Residence Life
The official ground breaking ceremony was held for Maclellan Hall as the third major residence
hall of Covenant College. The Resident Advisors are responsible to foster a co-curncular
environment enabling students to develop as responsive learners, become responsible social
participants in and out of the classroom and live a lifestyle in agreement with a christian world
and life view. Student assessment of the residence hall environment positively evaluated the
following five strengths of our Resident Advisors: modeling and enhancing academic
faithfulness, modeling a life as a discipline of jesus Christ, being available when needed, loving
individuals and encouraging and teaching hall community A new Resident Director position
for the student apartments was funded in order to develop a greater commitment to community
learning and academic inquiry. The new Resident Director will also be responsible for the
intramural program.
The Intercollegiate Athletic Program
Covenant’s intercollegiate program recruits academically capable and athletically qualified
students. In the past ten years, the average athlete’s GPA has been equal to or greater than the
average GPA of the student body While there has been a small drop of the average GPA from
3.19 spring, 1996 to 3.06 in spring, athlete GPAs in 1997 remain strong at Covenant. Our
athletes have competed well resulting in post-season district and national tournament
participation. Athletes are also involved m local tutoring ministry with high school students as
well as international evangelism ministries.
Co-curricular Involvement and Learning
Covenant College encourages out-of-class learning experiences that will provide opportunities
for our students to integrate faith in every area of life. Student involvement and participation in
meaningful out-of-class experiences continues to increase.

<a id="ga26-p365"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=367 printed_page=365 -->

Student Assistance Services (SAS)
Covenant College Students receive services that will help them connect a world and life view
with a way of living. Intellectual development and academic pressures often unlock personal or
psychological needs within students that require immediate short-term assistance and
intervention
94% of the students utilizing SAS noted that the services were “helpful” or “somewhat” helpful.
Also, 92% of the Resident Advisors stated that the training improved their ability to serve as
ra s. Our counselors are certified and licensed and are active members in the PCA. Individual
counseling, group counseling, referral services, Bible studies, educational seminars and
workshops are provided for the entire college community. Counselor load is currently at a
maximum and additional services will be provided for the following year.

Section 3: Financial Affairs
Finance / Accounting:
The college remains in good financial condition. The independent auditor issued a clean
opinion regarding the financial statements. Long-term debt continues to decline and now
represents less than 10% of the book value of fixed assets. Institutional aid equaled 31% of
tuition, exceeding the budgeted target of 28% even though virtually no aid was given unless
required by an existing aid program. President’s council budgeted 1997-1998 aid at 29%, up
from 28% for 1996-1997. Depreciation has been added to the budget in a five year phased plan
Cash generated by this addition will be used by the council for capital budget items and building
repair reserves. The 1996-1997 year ended in a surplus under previous fund accounting
principles and statements using the new Financial Accounting Standards Board standards show
an increase in the total fund balance and positive cash generation by operations. Endowment
investment return for 1996-1997 was 26.2 %, ranking the college 30th out of 422 institutions
participating in the national endowment return survey.
Following is a table detailing the sources of revenue for the college during 1996-1997.
95-96 Revenue % 96-97 Revenue %
Tuition $ 5.846,884 47% $ 6,619,943 30%
Church Gifts * $ 592,848 5% $ 636,543 3%
Other Gifts * $ 2,300.662 18% S 9,591,292 44%
Auxiliary Services (Room & Board) $ 1,662,010 13% $ 1,912.189 9%

Investment Income $ 522.216 4% $ 1,402,148 6%
Government Student Grants $ 357.048 3% $ 324,114 2%
Other $ 338.858 3% $ 283.717 1%
TOTAL $ 12.478,164 $ 21,832,986
* Gift figures above include both cash gifts and unconditional pledges in accordance with
FASB regulations Of the 96-97 gift figure, $1,957,140 (including church giving) were
unrestricted gifts to support ongoing operations, $1,564,000 were unrestricted gifts designated
by the board to be invested alongside the endowment fund to support scholarships and faculty
development, $247,095 were gifts to the endowment fund and $6,459,600 were for capital
purposes.

<a id="ga26-p366"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=368 printed_page=366 -->

Auxiliary Services and Independent Operations:
Independent operations recorded record revenue of $1,061,309 and record profit of $276,159.
A new operation, Mountain Adventure Games, did not cover the start up costs for 1996-1997,
but is expected to be profitable for 1997-1998. Food service satisfaction ratings kept Covenant
in the top 20 schools (#18) within the national ARAMARK system. Textbook availability at
registration continues to exceed 95% with used book availability at 29%.
Student Financial Planning:
Packaging for 1997-1998 during spring 1997 went well and all events occurred on schedule. An
ad hoc committee of President’s council, composed of the Student Financial Planning Director,
the Planning Director, the VP of Admissions and Development and the VP for Administration
and Finance, worked well in resolving several financial aid issues. Software provided by the
federal government is workmg acceptably, a marked improvement over 1995-1996.
Following are several key financial aid indicators and a two-year history of financial aid by
category
Type of Aid 1995-96 1996-97
Tuition Discount Rate 28.6% 31.1%
% of Students Receiving Aid 93.1% 94.5%
