TOTAL $ 4,506,056 $ 6,392,299
Technical Services / Programming:
After improvements in the college e-mail system, all faculty, staff and students now have
accounts on the network Our web site was activated in the fall and development work on
enhancements continues. New records and accounting software were successfully installed and
all data converted without loss. Network uptime exceeded the goal of 90% and several upgrades
were done to network software College staff, saving considerable outside repair costs now does
most PC repair. However, recently it appears that the rate of services needed and provided is
far outpacing the ability to properly support the same
Physical Plant:
The design of three new buildings was a major task for 1996-1997. The Maclellan Residence
Hall and the Probasco Alumni Visitor Center are under construction Maclellan Hall should be
open in June 1998 and Probasco Alumni Visitor Center completed by spring of that year A
major upgrade to the fire alarm system now allows improved detection and response to all
major campus buildings from the central Physical Plant. Considerable work continues on the
three soccer fields and design of a spray irrigation system for campus wastewater began.
Deferred mamtenance at June 30, 1996 totaled $2,105,000 and $1,805,000 at June 30, 1997

<a id="ga26-p367"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=369 printed_page=367 -->

Human Resources:
The salary management program was changed to focus on external salary comparisons, rather
than internal. The hiring target for 1997-1998 was set at 70% of the market median for each
particular job, where a market median can be established (staff grades 7-10, administrators and
faculty). Staff grades 2-6 will have the same target as a group Increases are planned over the
next several years until the college reaches 100% of the market median for our employees The
self funded health and dental plans again ended the 1996 plan year with a surplus Premiums
for 1997 were held constant, representing the fifth consecutive year of constant or declining
premiums.

Section 4: Advancement
Admissions
The conversion ratings (matriculation) of students who apply and attend can provide some level
of help in understanding a student’s perception of the value of a given college. This is only true
when the college does not have a waiting list and can provide room for any student who wants
to attend and meets the admissions requirements. Covenant was fortunate to see an increase in
matriculation in a period when colleges are becoming more and more competitive in their
attempts to attract students. Last year, 250 of the 577 students (43%) who applied came to the
college. This year while the number of applications reached an all time high of 616, the
matriculation rating decreased to 40% At present, it is unclear why that shift took place. The
increase in tuition and threat of overcrowding residence halls might have had an impact.
1995 1996 1997
Total Applications 463 577 618
Total Matriculates 185 250 246
Matriculation Rate 40% 43% 40%
Given this year’s significantly larger pool of qualified merit candidates, a matriculation
rate equal to the year before would have produced an additional 40 students. Additional
studies are hoped to explain the decrease in matriculation rate and reverse the trend next
year. However, the college continues to have a strong national representation in
applications, in marked contrast to most private schools our size, with notable increases
in suchstates as Illinois, Missouri, and South Carolina. International and diversity
applications continue to keep pace, in that almost 14% of our applicant pool were
students from other countries and cultures.
Some additional developments in admissions from the 1996-97 year:
• New hires on the support side of admissions have substantially shortened
the time from initial request to receipt of admissions material. In
September over 11,117 pieces of admissions material were mailed from the
admissions office (7043 were sent last year).
• 50% increase in the number of students eligible for the Maclellan
Scholarship

<a id="ga26-p368"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=370 printed_page=368 -->

• New higher minimum standards for participation in the Maclellan
Scholarship: 1250 SAT and 3.5 cumulative GPA
• Covenant sponsored a fall tour of Christian recording artist and Covenant
alum Wes King in 15 sites, to raise awareness in new markets. The tour
was coordinated with RDAs’ travel schedule.
• Hiring of 2 new ARDA’s to replace those moved to development.
Alumni Program
The last two Homecomings have been the biggest in the history of the college. This has been
the result of stressing reunion class years as opposed to encouraging all of the alumni to attend
every Homecoming
In the context of the capital campaign, the Probasco Alumni/Visitor’s Center is the first major
fundraising project to be targeted exclusively at the alumni. This has proven to be a very
exciting project that has involved a large number of alumni, in both a Chattanooga campaign as
well as a National Campaign. At this point, $910,000 has been raised towards the $1.1 million
goal, and the construction has begun. While alumni giving has been very low (8-9%), we hope
the pledge opportunity and the national base of volunteers will have an immediate and
important effect in raising the alumni participation rate.
Church Giving
In the 1996-97 giving year, 578 churches gave to Covenant College. The vast majority (550)
were PCA churches. Of those 578 churches, 194 PCA churches and 15 other churches qualified
for the Church Partnership Promise Plan. Church giving totaled $636,543 this year; an increase
of 7.3% over last year’s giving of $592,848 PCA giving accounted for 93.8% of all church
giving.
Capital Campaign
Giving toward the capital campaign during the 1996-97 year was about 2.1 times greater than
the previous year. The total for the 1996-97 year was $ 3,641,334.
Campaign totals to date are as follows:
Campaign Goals Cost Pledged
Hugh Maclellan Residence Hall $ 7,200,000 $ 4,104,430
Probasco Alumni/Visitor’s Center $ 800,000 $ 800,000
Ashe Activity Center $ 3,000,000 $ 2,650,394
Campus Renovation $ 1,000,000 $ 100,000
Endowment $ 5,000,000 $ 2,185,199
Annual Sustaining Fund $ 7,000,000 $ 1,444,000
Campaign Total $ 24,000,000 $ 11,284,023

<a id="ga26-p369"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=371 printed_page=369 -->

Section 5:Five-Year Plan Update
The 1995-2000 plan goal is to:
INCREASE OUR EFFECTIVENESS IN PREPARING GRADUATES
FOR SERVICE AS REFORMING INFLUENCERS ON SOCIETY
There are two basic strategies that were developed as a means to meet the goal. Each strategy
also has a few objectives and tactics to move us closer toward that goal. Much time and effort
have been given to determine how to best execute tactics that would advance the college toward
meeting our goals. To that end, each of the following actions was thought to provide some level
of improvement in the area in which it was employed.
STRATEGY I:
Provide the academic, leadership and personal opportunities that develop
the whole person with a special emphasis on God’s call to be a reforming
influences
OBJECTIVE 1 Strengthen the educational program
The following activities were designed to strengthen the educational experience at Covenant
College so that our graduates would be better prepared for service as reforming influencers
on society.
Academic
• There have been eight faculty sabbaticals taken since fall. 1995.
• In 1997. $500,000 was added to the Sanderson endowment bringing it to $1.4 million.
• $26,903 spent on faculty development from 95-97. The 97-98 and 98-99 budgets total
$70,900.
• Dr. Ashlock was awarded $1,000 stipend for the 6th edition of his teaching math textbook
• Since the fall of 1995, eight new faculty members have been hired in seven fields, all
holding the Ph.D. All have or are teaching in the core except Dr. Steele who is working on a
new arts core course for the fall of 1998 and Dr. McMillian who directs the Quest program
• Faculty members Dan MacDougall, Steve Kaufmann. and Don Graham have led workshops
on integration of faith and learning for new (and some returning) faculty members.
• Dr. Kaufmann’s teaching load reduced to permit time in faculty development issues Each
faculty member is presenting a plan for personal development funded from the 1998-99
budget.
• Fall 97 Board meeting -new majors in Philosophy. Philosophy and Religion, and Economics
Co-Curricular
• ACPA’s “Student Learning Imperative” implemented which concentrates on intertwining
the concepts of student learning and personal development
• A two-year chapel examination process has been completed which involved students and
faculty members. There is now a greater consensus on the role of chapel and the
development of a Chapel Plan. A policy governing visiting speakers was approved at the
1997 General Assembly.
• Realizing that student involvement improves student maturing and retention, a high level of
student participation in intercollegiate and intramural sports (96/97 - 14% and 65%) has
been continued during these periods of enrollment growth.
• An intramural director (Betsy Matthews) was hired to oversee the program

<a id="ga26-p370"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=372 printed_page=370 -->

• Several student clubs and organizations are more closely connected with the appropriate
academic departments.
• A half-time person has been hired to oversee the practical work and work-study programs in
an effort to maintain the integrity and value of the programs The entire program is being
re-evaluated to see if students are learning the value of work from their service.
• A new multi-media room opened in the fall 1997 in Mills Hall The 98-99 budget funds the
hiring of an instructional technologist to encourage and assist faculty in the use of the new
facilities.
• The Management of Information Systems Committee (MISC) was formed to oversee all
information technology issues at the college.
OBJECTIVE 2 Increase awareness of, and ability to live and work with, people of
a wide variety of culture.
The following positive activities were undertaken in a belief that such would help us attain
this objective
• Diversity was re-defined by the multi-cultural committee. Students qualifying for the
scholarship must demonstrate racial and ethnic identity and influence, personal involvement
in racial reconciliation, a cultural diversity contribution to the college, and potential
influence on the thinking and actions of the majority.
• A program was started to offer financial assistance to Covenant female graduates who
would return to teach at Covenant. The program is being re-evaluated and will probably be
modified to provide more immediate results.
OBJECTIVE 3 In recognition that limited space and resources may require
Covenant to become more selective in admissions, improve the
college’s ability to recruit and admit those students most likely to
benefit from a Covenant education and provide benefit to the
college community.
The college has taken the following enrollment development actions.
New Student Recruitment
• Applicants are now required to submit two essays and be interviewed before acceptance
The application will soon be on the WEB site.
• A waiting list was started to ensure room for the better-qualified students.
• Applications have increased from 577 applications for Fall 96 to 616 for Fall 97.
• Development staff has been increased and well trained.
• A Visitors Center will be completed by April 1 to better facilitate visits.
• New literature has been designed for the recruitment efforts.
Financial Aid
• Financial aid budget has greatly increased since 1990 while still holding the discount rate at
29%.
• The basis for aid has shifted from need to merit while still meeting 100% of need for the
high merit students.
• A system of index points has enabled better identification of the well-rounded scholar most
apt to benefit from a Covenant education.
• Returning students are now awarded before new students.
• A Dean’s scholarship is being awarded to assist students who are mostly self-sufficient,
hold a 3.0 GPA and are interested in civic activities.

<a id="ga26-p371"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=373 printed_page=371 -->

• The Church Partnership Program was changed for the 1998 giving year Students who are
members of qualifying churches will receive up to 100% of tuition based on need. In the
1999 program year, the per member participation rate will be directly related with tuition
increases
Retention Efforts
• The Early Warning System for new students was implemented, resulting in less than 5 %

drop out during the fall semester
• A survey of 124 students indicated a relatively high degree of satisfaction on the part of
students with faculty advising and general campus interaction with faculty.
• An electronic AEGR is now on line to assist the faculty.
The Maclellan Scholars Program
• The 1997-98 budget includes $29,745 in additional funds to keep the award at 60% of
tuition.
• Eligibility requirements have increased from 3.4 GPA to 3.6. and SAT scores from
1200 to 1250*
OBJECTIVE 4 Complete facilities called for in the campus plan as needed for an
enrollment of 850 traditional students.
• The residence hall is now under construction with completion expected in late summer
The Visitor's Center is also under construction and should be completed by April 1. 1998
The activity center has been contracted and construction should begin soon The faculty
lounge has been moved to the library and renovation has been completed.
• Signage, lighting, and landscaping have been enhanced and will continue to develop as new
buildings and parking lots are added
• The second floor of the Barnes Physical Education Center, and Carter Hall exterior tower
renovation are complete Intenor renovation to Carter Hall resulted in additional residence
space available on the second floor, expanded mail services and a student lounge below the
Great Hall.
• The master plan has been updated to cover the needs within the present capital campaign
However, additional need analysis is being completed in the areas of classrooms, faculty
offices, chapel, library, special events, and dining.
• All faculty now have computing hardware. software and access to the network.
STRATEGY II:
Increase involvement to efTect reformation in the dominant structures of our
society.
OBJECTIVE 1 Since Covenant graduates must be prepared to enter a
culture that is openly hostile to Christianity, usually
measuring success on individual desires, often at the cost of
others, the college will equip students and enable faculty to
engage in dialogue with the dominant secular culture.
• An inventory of faculty involvement in various academic and professional associations has
been completed. The Faculty Status Committee will examine the inventory and offer
suggestions on ways in which the administration might provide greater opportunity for
faculty to become actively involved in these associations.
• The Festschrift (Where Is The Salt?) has been completed and published It contains nine
scholarly essays by members of the faculty.

<a id="ga26-p372"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=374 printed_page=372 -->

• a study was conducted that showed great promise fora co-operative study program
OBJECTIVE 2 Increase cooperative involvement with the Church.
• Published four editions of "On-Line" in the 1996-97 academic year. Development also
mailed monthly prayer letters to churches in the 1996-97 academic year.
• Dennis Fogo was hired to co-ordinate all publications and public relations.
• Met with Jed Johnston (Mamie), the RUF minister at UTC, and established a potential
working relationship between Covenant and UTC’s RUF chapter
• Discussions with our Bible Department indicate an interest in developing programs for local
PCA churches. Two possibilities have primarily been discussed: the offering of a Bible
certification program and/or instructional training for Sunday School and lay teachers via
television
• Covenant has recently hosted meetings for representatives of all the agencies of the PCA to
discuss information needs This is a first step in providing the expertise available at the
college to the PCA

Updated February 25, 1998

<a id="ga26-p373"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=375 printed_page=373 -->

Report of
Covenant Theological Seminary
to the
26th General Assembly
of the
Presbyterian Church in America
June 1998

Fathers and Brothers,
The following pages are excerpted and revised from the nearly 200-page report
that Covenant Theological Seminary made to our accrediting agencies this year as part of
the 10-year renewal of our credentials. a si mentioned in last year’s report, this
accreditation renewal is the culmination of two years of work during which we were
chosen to be one of only eight pilot schools (and the only evangelical school) from
across the United States and Canada to take the lead in implementing new standards that
will be used to assess the quality of all seminaries accredited by the Association of
Theological Schools (ATS).
This was a major undertaking for us but we praise God for the opportunity to
represent the Presbyterian Church in America in establishing standards that will be used
for years to come in assessing the quality of theological education throughout North
America.
As you read the report of what the Lord has done here over the last decade,
please rejoice with us that God is using your Seminary in extraordinary ways
Respectfully Submitted,
Bryan Chapell,
President
Covenant Theological Seminary
12330 Conway Rd
St. Louis, MO 63141

<a id="ga26-p374"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=376 printed_page=374 -->

SELF-STUDY REPORT
December 1997

COVENANT
THEOLOGICAL
SEMINARY

Submitted to the
Association of Theological Schools
in the United States and Canada
and to the
Commission on Institutions of Higher Education of the
North Central Association of Colleges and Schools

<a id="ga26-p375"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=377 printed_page=375 -->

Introduction
Purpose and Distinctives
Begun with eleven students in 1956, Covenant now serves over a thousand
students each year through graduate-level programs (credit students) and continuing
education courses (certificate students and auditors), both in St. Louis and at several
extension sites. Over a hundred degrees and certificates are awarded each May to
M.Div., M.A., Th.M., D .M in, and Certificate students. Originally formed to serve a
small denominational constituency, today Covenant remains true to its theological and
ecclesiastical heritage as the Lord pours out His blessing of increased enrollment
The Seminary’s history highlights distinctives that still characterize Covenant
today. Covenant’s roots lie in efforts earlier in this century to maintain an historic
Presbyterian and Reformed church. Faced with what many viewed as doctrinal erosion,
Covenant’s founders affirmed an evangelical view of the inspiration and authority of the
Bible. When some moved toward a strident fundamentalism, the Evangelical Presbyterian
Church1 was formed as an alternative, with Covenant as its denominational seminary
With its parent denomination, Covenant joined the Presbyterian Church in America in
1982 and became the national seminary of the PCA, a rapidly growing denomination
with 280,000 members in 1,200 churches across the US and Canada. The Seminary is
governed by a Board of Trustees elected by the General Assembly of the PCA
Throughout its history, Covenant has been known for excellence in training for ministry.
Additional distinctives have emerged in recent years, making historic
commitments more explicit. First among these is a Christ-centered focus , captured in a
statement that students closely identify with the Seminary’s purpose: “A seminary
education is successful only if—at its end— the student knows Jesus Christ more
intimately than at its beginning.” Closely related is an emphasis on God’s grace as the
foundation for everything we do, an “absolute confidence in God’s acceptance provided
through his redemptive work as the supreme motivation and enablement for love and
holiness” (Mission Statement).
While recognizing the wide variety of ministry roles in which its graduates may
serve, as a denominational seminary Covenant has consciously sought to train students
for pastoral ministry as its primary focus. This training is based on a traditional
theological curriculum, heavy on Bible, biblical languages, theology and history, joined
to a relational emphasis, in which “the relationship between students and faculty
assumes a meaningful place alongside teaching content, to affect the entire character of
the student for ministry” (Mission Statement). To accomplish this, Covenant has sought
to assemble a faculty of pastor-scholars characterized by love for the church and the
ministry as well as by academic competence While it is difficult to find talented scholars
with pastoral experience and outlook, doing so has not only enhanced the quality of
training, but produced a unique degree of faculty unity and morale, a swell as a growing
reputation for academic excellence. Covenant views the Reformed faith not simply as
something to be defended, but to be communicated to an unbelieving world, requiring an
active engagement with the culture in which we live and in which our graduates serve.

1 Unrelated to the different denomination now known by this name. The EPC subsequently became part
of the Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod, which then joined the PCA in 1982.

<a id="ga26-p376"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=378 printed_page=376 -->

We believe that these distinctives, together with growing enrollment and faculty
recognition, have given Covenant a unique opportunity for influence, both in and beyond
our own denomination
Key Developments
Covenant has changed dramatically since its last self-study ten years ago. While its
mission remains the same, Covenant Seminary is in many ways a very different
institution.
The most dramatic change has been enrollment. The last self-study indicated a
need to grow, but Covenant’s growth over the past decade has been far beyond what we
expected. Overall (credit and auditor) enrollment has increased six times to a fall
enrollment of almost 900 students (with nearly 1100 students enrolled annually). Credit
head count has more than quadrupled (to over 700) and FTE has tripled While there has
been considerable growth in D.Min., evening, and extension programs, traditional
daytime enrollment has also increased significantly. Spouses of full-time students prepare
for ministry as a team by taking courses (credit or audit) without charge_____________
Fall Credit Head Count by Program

d Extension
Cl Evening
■D.Min.
B Day_____
1987 1989 1990 1991 1992 1993 1994 1995 1996 1997

Fall Credit FTE by Program

d Extension
□Evening
■D.Min.
■Day_____

The M.Div. remains Covenant’s largest degree program, with 45 percent of the credit
headcount and 54 percent of the credit FTE. To maintain the quality of M.Div. training,
in November 1992 Covenant capped its entering M.Div. class at 80 students per year
(later raised to 90 a year by a faculty that did not want to turn qualified students away).

<a id="ga26-p377"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=379 printed_page=377 -->

This cap will remain in effect until additional faculty resources will permit limited
growth Other programs that do not unduly tax the institution (e.g., evening, extension,
and D.Min.) may continue to grow.___________________________________________
Fall Credit Head Count by Degree

Q Certificate
■D.Min.
□Th.M.
□M . A .
■m .a .c .
■M.Div.

Fall Credit FTE by Degree

□Certificate
■D.Min.
□Th.M.
□M.A.
■M ac .
■M D iv

Ten new faculty have been added during the last decade, joining other regular
faculty who have served for as long as 30 years at the Seminary. The regular faculty is
assisted by as many as twenty adjunct and emeritus faculty We deliberately seek to
enhance training by utilizing gifted ministry practitioners as adjunct faculty in the
disciplines of practical theology.
Changes in federal law required reevaluation of faculty retirement and tenure
policies Under the leadership of Professor David Jones (Dean from 1978-88), the
faculty in 1995 voluntarily gave up tenure for a system of automatically renewable threeyear contracts, with a major evaluation of the faculty member before the Seminary would
offer the third three-year contract. Both faculty and trustees expressed the belief that this
approach preserved job security and faculty retention, while avoiding negative
connotations that tenure has conveyed to certain constituencies.

<a id="ga26-p378"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=380 printed_page=378 -->

The heart of the educational program, M.Div curriculum, was thoroughly
reevaluated and slightly revised in this decade, under David Jones’ leadership as
chairman of the faculty Curriculum Committee. In the revision the long-standing
commitment to teaching Biblical languages was re-affirmed and also increased in the
instruction of the Systematics curriculum; and, the pastoral theology courses were
updated to deal with issues and church realities our graduates have told us they face in
ministry today.
Two new academic programs have begun The video tape based Seminary
Extension Training program (SET) enrolls about 100 credit students each semester
(another 100 non-credit students also take extension courses). the master of arts in
Counseling program opened in the fall of 1993 following the addition of Dr. Richard
Winter as its director, enrollment is limited in order to provide personal attention and
care in practicum placements by Dr Winter and Professor Dan Zink.
The Francis Schaeffer Institute, named for a noted apologist and frequent
campus lecturer, opened in 1989 under the direction of Schaeffer’s long-time colleague
Jerram Barrs, Professor of Christianity and Contemporary Culture. Through special
courses, public lectures and other programs, the Institute seeks to continue Dr.
Schaeffer’s ministry by equipping Christians to understand the culture in which they live
and to communicate intelligently and sensitively to unbelievers around them.
The Seminary’s finances have improved over the last decade. Tuition income
provides a higher and more appropriate percentage of annual revenues, while annual
fund giving provides a smaller percentage— changes envisioned in the 1987 self-study.
Beginning with the completed on-campus apartments, all construction funding includes
endowment for long-term maintenance. After extensive study the Seminary in 1996
launched its most ambitious capital campaign. The “Jewel of Grace, Fire for Ministry”
campaign1 seeks to stabilize finances by increasing endowment and the donor base, so
that endowment income and annual fund giving remain appropriate percentages of
annual revenues. Specifically, the campaign seeks to raise $14 million to endow five
faculty chairs, increase endowment for financial aid, expand library and classroom space,
develop a student center, and construct housing for single students. as of december 1,
1997, the campaign was ahead of scheduled goals with commitments for over $8,7
million (62 percent of the goal).

Annual Fund Gifts
$1,600,000
$1,400,000
1 200,000
$ ,
$1,000,000
$800,000
1987 1988 1989 1990 1991 1992 1993 1994 1995 1996 1997

<a id="ga26-p379"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=381 printed_page=379 -->

report, we are deeply grateful to God

Purpose, Planning, and Evaluation

Mission Statement
the purpose of covenant theological seminary is to train servants of the triune god to
walk with God, to interpret and communicate God's Word, and to lead God’s people.
The above statement expresses the unchanged purpose of Covenant Seminary
since its founding in 1956. a significant expansion of this purpose into a fuller mission
Statement (see below) was made as a result of an extraordinary three-day meeting of the
Board in October 1993 that included representatives from the faculty, students, and
alumni in working groups with trustees, clarified many of the Seminary’s distinctives and
commitments. The resulting statement (as revised in May 1997) expresses the principles
and values which the entire Seminary community agrees is Covenant’s calling
Mission Statement
the purpose of covenant theological seminary is to train servants of the triune
God to walk with God, to interpret and communicate God's Word, and to lead
God's people.

Basic Commitments
The following basic commitments amplify, clarify and protect the Seminary’s
understanding of its purpose:
1 Christ-centered ministry: We believe that a seminary education is successful
only if — at its end — the student knows Jesus Christ more intimately than at
its beginning.

<a id="ga26-p380"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=382 printed_page=380 -->

2 Biblical authority: We believe that the Bible is the Word of God and thus it is
our only infallible rule of faith and practice. We believe in the plenary, verbal
inspiration of the Scriptures by the Holy Spirit and, thus, we affirm the
inerrancy of the original manuscripts whose objective truth it is our
responsibility to interpret in accord with the principles of Scripture and to
proclaim in accord with the imperatives of the Gospel
3 Grace foundation: We believe that the foundation for all that we do must be
the gospel of grace — our absolute confidence in God’s acceptance provided
through his redemptive work as the supreme motivation and the only
enablement for love and holiness.
4 Relational emphasis: We believe that the relationship between students and
faculty must take a meaningful place alongside teaching content, so that we
may affect the entire character of the student for ministry Therefore, we seek
to develop a faculty of pastor-scholars and a staff which both individually and
as a community effectively models what it means to walk with God, interpret
and communicate God’s Word, and lead God’s people.
5. Pastoral training: We believe that our primary task is to train students for
pastoral ministry. this task requires development of a faculty of pastor-
scholars experienced in ministry (as understood by our denominational
standards and institutional history). At the same time, we seek to make the
special resources of the Seminary available to other students and the larger
Christian community to the extent that we can do so without hindering
training for pastoral ministry.
6 Leadership and outreach: We believe that, as the national seminary of the
Presbyterian Church in America (PCA), it is our responsibility to provide
intellectual training and models that are true to the Westminster Standards and
the historic distinctives of Presbyterian orthodoxy, while equipping the next
generation of Christian servants for effective church leadership and outreach in
a changing world At the same time, because we recognize that a seminary
alone can never fully equip students for these tasks, we seek to work in
partnership with local churches to accomplish our purpose.

Objectives
The following specific objectives relate the Seminary’s purpose to its programs
1. Covenant Seminary seeks to fulfill its purpose primarily by preparing students
for pastoral ministry in accordance with the standards of the PCA. As the
national seminary of the PCA, Covenant seeks particularly to prepare students
for pastoral ministry within that denomination. At the same time, Covenant
actively seeks to serve students preparing for ministry ina variety of other
denominational and non-denominational contexts. The Master of Divinity
program is the first professional degree preparing students for pastoral
ministry. the master of theology and doctor of ministry degrees provide
additional academic and practical training for students already holding the
M.Div degree.

<a id="ga26-p381"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=383 printed_page=381 -->

2. Covenant Seminary seeks to fulfill its purpose by preparing students for other
non-vocational and vocational roles in churches and other Christian ministries
the master of arts (Genera! Theological Studies) and the Graduate
Certificate provide biblical and theological training that equips lay people to
bring an informed Christian perspective to a variety of secular occupations and
non-ordained ministries. the master of arts (Exegetical Theology) is an
academic and research degree which prepares students pursuing academic
leadership for advanced study at the doctoral level. The Master of Arts in
Counseling prepares students to work as counselors, primarily in church-
related settings, as well as for further advanced study
3. Covenant Seminary seeks to fulfill its purpose by providing adult continuing
education classes, seminars and publications for churches and individuals, as
well as by providing theological leadership for the Presbyterian Church in America and the Christian community through faculty publications, service on
denominational and professional committees, speaking opportunities, etc

Competencies
In fulfilling this purpose, Covenant seeks to develop the following competencies
in its graduates:
... to train servants of the ... to interpret ... God's ... to... communicate God's
Triune God to walk with God Word Word, and to lead God's
people.

gospel of grace as biblical and and in writing.

standards concepts in historical Relates to others with

spiritual maturity and 5. Theological skills: respect, and

dependence on Christ Bible (in the original values.
3. Servanthood: languages where 9 Leadership: Assists
Demonstrates a heart appropriate) to issues others in growing

Christian theology and counseling,
