approaches

<a id="ga26-p382"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=384 printed_page=382 -->

The expanded Mission Statement was reported to, and highly commended by, the
General Assembly of the PCA

Institutional Integrity
Covenant views ethical integrity as an essential part of its mission. Those who seek to
walk with God must obey him (Genesis 17:1; Micah 6:8, 1 John 1:6) who abounds in
love and faithfulness (Exodus 34:6) and whose Word is truth (John 17:17). The
Seminary cannot train servants of God to walk with him and to lead his people if the
Seminary itself does not exhibit love, faithfulness, and truth in its practices and
relationships.
Under the Seminary Bylaws* trustees and faculty must adhere without
reservation to the Seminary’s doctrinal standards. All faculty and trustees annually sign
their commitment to the Westminster Confession of Faith and the standards of the PCA.
Legal and Financial Practices
The Seminary conducts its legal affairs with integrity A 1993 denominational legal audit
approved its legal practices; the few minor concerns (related to provisions of faculty
contracts, development of a sexual harassment policy, and emergency procedures) were
promptly addressed and the Seminary’s response was approved by the denomination,
with commendation for “evident attention to legal detail over an extended period”
(Minutes, 23rd GA, p 181). The Seminary’s first-ever (in 40 years) U.S. Department of
Education Title IV program review* in 1996 found the Seminary in compliance (penalties
and liabilities assessed for two administrative oversights totalled only $815)
covenant conducts its financial affairs with integrity annual audits of both
general seminary operations* and student financial aid programs1 provide external
verification of the Seminary’s practices The Seminary belongs to and meets standards of
the Evangelical Council for Financial Accountability * Seminary trustees and
administrators sign a conflict of interest statement annually (Bylaws, XV.). The Seminary
honors donor requests as far as the law and ethical practice allow, gifts with
inappropriate restrictions are refused Covenant’s reporting on the expenditure of funds
from the Chatlos Foundation was so thorough that the foundation began asking other
recipients to report as we had done
Relations with Constituencies
Covenant Seminary practices integrity in its relationships with those within the Seminary
community Discipline and grievance procedures are built on Jesus’ words in Matthew
18:15 ff:
If your brother sins against you, go and show him his fault, just between
the two of you. if he listens to you, you have won your brother over But
if he will not listen, take one or two others along, so that “every matter
may be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses.” If he
refuses to listen to them, tell it to the church
We find that when individuals first attempt seriously to work out problems together, few
grievances go further Although our policy, since 1993, has been to keep formal

<a id="ga26-p383"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=385 printed_page=383 -->

grievances on file, there are no formal student, staff, or faculty grievances on file— the
result, we believe, of doing what Jesus said
Faculty and staff are treated fairly. Faculty and staff turnover are low. A spring
1997 staff survey found appreciation for the work environment and equitable treatment
In the fall of 1997, students came to St. Louis from thirty-six states and every
section of the united states (35 percent of M.Div. students from the Southeast, 10
percent from the Northeast, 13 percent from the Southwest or West, 37 percent from the
Midwest), as well as from fifteen other countries.
Ethnic diversity tends to reflect the Seminary’s 95 percent white parent
denomination (particularly for full-time ministry-oriented students, faculty, and trustees),
its suburban location (especially for part-time and evening students, as well as staff), and
the areas around its extension sites Although Covenant actively recruits ethnic students,
only eight percent are African-American, Hispanic, or Asian; another four percent are
international. Almost 20 percent of evening students are from ethnic minorities,
reflecting the Seminary’s ability to attract ethnic students from the community, as well as
the lack of ethnic students in the pool from which full-time day students are drawn The
Seminary community encourages and embraces the presence and participation of ethnic
students: the 1997-98 Student Council President is African-American While four of the
sixteen regular faculty have ethnic (Jewish) or overseas backgrounds (England,
Germany), active efforts to recruit or develop additional ethnic faculty have not to this
point been successful. The Seminary has recruited an African-American candidate whom
it expects to be nominated to the Board

Theological Scholarship
Students at Covenant have traditionally developed relationships with faculty outside, as
well as inside, the classroom. We seek to cultivate this sense of intimacy and community,
because “we believe that the relationship between students and professors must take a
meaningful place alongside teaching content, so that we may affect the entire character
of the student for ministry” (Mission Statement). we seek to develop a faculty of pastor scholars and a staff which both individually and as a community model what it means “to
walk with God, interpret and communicate God’s Word, and lead God’s people”
(Mission Statement). The faculty and administration remain committed to this relational
emphasis, despite recent enrollment growth. Faculty and students capitalize on natural
opportunities to build relationships (such as campus events, local church ministries, and
weekly Covenant Groups).

Activities of Theological Scholarship
Learning
Learning does not only take place in the classroom. The Seminary’s Mission
statement recognizes the key role churches play in preparation of students for ministry.
We continually seek ways to bring classroom learning into the practice of ministry in the
church and community, and vice versa. counseling pr act i cum placements form a C.
students and the supervised ministry requirement for M.Div. students provide such

<a id="ga26-p384"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=386 printed_page=384 -->

integration. Special lecture series, ministry lunches (bringing practitioners to talk
informally with students), family nurture events (equipping spouses for ministry
challenges), and Friday Nights at the Schaeffer Institute provide other opportunities for
learning Students learn from one another through group projects in or for class, and
through voluntary study groups. Students also learn from one another as they build
relationships, particularly in campus housing, in other clusters of apartments where many
students live, and at church, especially as they minister to one another through the
difficult times of life
Teaching
Covenant’s faculty not only have appropriate scholarly qualifications but also have a love
for the church and the ministry. All have missions or church ministry experience.
Because they have been and continue to be involved in the church, they “practice what
they teach ” This brings a ministry orientation into the classroom and directs the whole
educational process toward ministry, from examples and illustrations given in class to the
degree of emphasis given to various course elements to assignments (e.g., sermon
outlines in exegetical courses). The experience of ministry in other cultures and overseas
brings a global perspective to teaching and writing. While some faculty publications are
aimed at other scholars, others are directed more broadly to the church.
As a confessional institution, Covenant Seminary seeks to facilitate understanding
and assessment of the Reformed theological tradition. At the same time, we seek to train
students to interact “critically and respectfully with other approaches” and relate “to
others with evident concern, respect, and sensitivity, even when there are differences of
culture, belief, or values” (Mission Statement). Without embracing a relativism which
regards all viewpoints as equally valid and disregards the concept of objective truth, the
faculty presents alternative viewpoints as accurately and fairly as possible to encourage
students to think critically and constructively. In this way faculty teach by word and
example to treat with respect those who hold different views, we attempt to model
disagreement without disagreeableness. the wi derange of denominational backgrounds
and theological persuasions among the student body provides a natural setting for
students to develop skills in working with those of different convictions. Assigned
readings representative of other theological traditions encourage theological reflection
and respectful conversation This process enhances ministerial readiness and cultivates
emotional and spiritual maturity.
Research
The past decade has seen an increasing scholarly output. In his report to the Board in
January 1997, President Chapell highlighted some of the scholarly and other
contributions made by Covenant faculty during 1996. These included: two books hailed
in reviews as “setting the standard in the discipline for years to come” and “an able and
needed defense of historic orthodoxy”; a two-volume history of Princeton Seminary’s
past commitments (yet held dear by this Seminary and Church) by a Covenant faculty
member that was distributed by Princeton’s President to its Board, previous books by
three faculty being translated into Korean, and textbooks authored by Covenant faculty
in use in at least fifteen other seminaries.
While the Seminary’s primary focus is training students for ministry, we

<a id="ga26-p385"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=387 printed_page=385 -->

recognize that research contributes to good teaching and student learning. As a result,
Covenant faculty continue to be engaged in the practice of their respective disciplines in
the church and in various scholarly settings, whether their field is preaching, counseling,
leadership, exegesis, theology, or history. This combination of theory with practice
brings freshness and credibility to the classroom while enhancing the Seminary’s growing
reputation.
Involvement with Diverse Publics
Covenant works closely with the church. The Seminary’s relationship with and
accountability to the PCA, its parent denomination. This relationship is not merely
formal, but involves numerous contacts with denominational leaders, agencies, and
churches who advise the Seminary on their needs for pastoral training, send us talented
students, and employ our graduates. Faculty and staff serve on Presbytery and
denominational committees. The Seminary’s enrollment growth, placement record, and
growing financial support from PCA churches reflects this close relationship.
Covenant faculty, staff, and students are involved in the ministries of dozens of
local congregations from a variety of denominations. Because we believe that, in the
words of our Mission Statement, “a seminary alone can never fully equip students for
[their] tasks, we seek to work in partnership with local churches to accomplish our
purpose.” Students who have moved to St. Louis to attend Covenant Seminary worship
in and often work for these churches; the churches in turn send us members (and
sometimes pastors) as students. These ties are increasingly important as we implement a
new supervised ministry requirement for M.Div. students and as churches assist us in
assessing students’ readiness for ministry.
Covenant’s faculty and staff have extensive opportunities to serve outside the St.
Louis area. In his report to the Board in January 1997, President Chapell noted some of
their diverse ministry opportunities during 1996: PCA denominational ministries, such as
Mission to the World, Mission to North America, Reformed University Ministries,
Christian Education and Publications, Covenant College, Ridge Haven, and Women in
the Church; lectures at Gordon-Conwell, Reformed, Southern, and Dallas Seminaries,
Trinity Evangelical Divinity School, and the Ockenga, Olford, and Moody Bible
Institutes, preaching and/or teaching in Uganda, Ukraine, Mexico, Chile, Haiti, Brazil,
Israel, Romania, Korea, Slovakia, the Netherlands, Switzerland, England, China, and
India.
The Seminary’s Francis Schaeffer Lectures each semester address cultural and
cross-cultural issues such as art, science, psychology, and government. Recent series
have included a panel on the Jesus Seminar (including a Seminar representative), lectures
on racial reconciliation, science and the truth of Scripture, and the theology of
technology.
Global Perspectives
there is considerable evidence of cross-cultural awareness and concern in this
generation. As a result, Covenant maintains close ties to PCA’s Mission to the World,
led by former Seminary President Dr. Paul Kooistra, including plans to involve students
in short-term mission projects and a joint effort using extension courses to train Latin
and Caribbean pastors in south Florida. One church used its Missions Conference to

<a id="ga26-p386"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=388 printed_page=386 -->

recruit students for its upcoming SET class in missions. In 1988, members of the
Seminary faculty and administration formed Presbyterian Mission International, a mission
board assisting nationals (most often Covenant graduates) working in their home
countries Professor David Calhoun regularly teaches at the Iona Centres in the
Carribean. Vice President for Development Ben Homan leads trustees and donors on an
annual mission trip to Mexico; in the summer of 1997 he also led three students on a
three-week service trip to China, beginning a relationship which his hosts would like to
continue. The Seminary’s reports to PCA presbyteries include a cover letter in Korean
when the report is prepared for a Korean language presbytery. Faculty members
frequently participate in international conferences, outreaches, and missions efforts (see
above) Area ethnic pastors periodically speak in chapel.
This awareness is evident in the instructional program. In Apologetics and
Outreach to Contemporary Culture (required of all master’s level and some certificate
students), students develop a relationship with a non-Christian and write a paper to that
person explaining the gospel in terms that reflect understanding of the non-Christian’s
worldview. All M.Div and many other students take the course, Mission Principles.
An energetic and enthusiastic Student Missions Fellowship leads a highly visible
and well-attended annual missions conference. Students and faculty serve in crosscultural ministries, such as three inter-racial New City Fellowship congregations and a
project to provide theological training for area African-American pastors. Some students
lead youth ministries in area ethnic (Korean and Chinese) churches, others have initiated
informal ministries to the local art and gay communities. Some students participate in
summer missions trips. Graduates are regularly placed in overseas missions positions.
At the same time, more can be done. Covenant serves a predominantly white,
middle-class denomination. The Seminary’s strength, through the Francis Schaeffer
Institute, has been dialogue with Western non-Christian traditions. Covenant should
enhance students’ exposure to non-Westem worldviews and continue efforts to secure
another qualified missions professor.
Curriculum
Our Mission Statement expresses Covenant’s conviction that a seminary alone cannot
fully equip students for the tasks of ministry. While experience in ministry before and
during seminary is helpful, many essential lessons can be learned by only working with
and leading God’s people after graduation. Even the best seminary training cannot
replace the years of experience required to develop a truly effective minister. What the
seminary can do best is to provide a solid academic foundation and a conceptual
framework for the student’s future ministry. This academic foundation consists in
rigorous training in theological knowledge and skills for ministry (particularly disciplines
students are less likely to develop on their own).
Some outside constituents wish Covenant would give more attention to current
debates in the church. While we do not ignore these arguments, we do not focus training
on controversies. We believe we train students best by providing a balanced academic
biblical foundation that will equip them for a positive ministry, as well as enable them to
deal with both present and future controversies.

<a id="ga26-p387"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=389 printed_page=387 -->

Spiritual and Personal Formation
Spiritual and personal formation takes place both in and outside of the classroom.
Because of the Seminary’s commitment to training for ministry and because of the
orientation of the pastor-scholars on its faculty, courses frequently apply principles to life
and ministry. All classes begin with prayer and some with brief devotions or sharing of
requests for prayer. Language and homiletics courses particularly force student self-
assessment, counseling is apart of the “load” in such courses.
Specific courses also address spiritual and personal formation. M.Div. students
take Spiritual and Ministry Formation their first year and Pastoral Theology their last; in
both, they write papers assessing their maturity and gifts for ministry Counseling courses
(core for M Div. and ma C.) explicitly help students deal with issues in their own lives
as part of preparation to counsel others. the counseling pr act i cum helps mac
students develop personally, spiritually, and professionally. Elective courses are also
offered.
Other facets of seminary life also contribute to spiritual and personal formation.
Because faculty are also pastors, students seek them out for counsel and direction and
linger after class to talk. Structured programs such as chapel, Covenant Groups, and Day
of Prayer also play a role. About one hundred students and student spouses work on
campus, providing opportunity to discern and address attitudes and work habits that may
be detrimental to ministry. Student comments indicate that the greatest contribution may
be the overall environment, in which students learn from faculty, staff, and one another,
by example a swell as precept, to know Christ better and to relate to others in ways that
reflect his character—the relational emphasis in our Mission Statement.
Because we believe that “a seminary alone can never fully equip students for
[their] tasks, we seek to work in partnership with local churches to accomplish our
purpose” (Mission Statement) This is particularly important in the area of spiritual and
personal formation, which is, as we understand it, primarily the responsibility of the local
church. The Seminary expects and strongly encourages every student to participate
regularly in the life and ministry of a particular congregation.
The Seminary continues to collect exit data. All graduates complete a survey
form, including their evaluation of the extent to which their time at Covenant contributed
to their growth in the nine competencies. Ratings for overall satisfaction with training
received average 4.5 or higher and almost all competencies are rated above 4.0 for all
degree programs. In addition, the Dean of Students meets with focus groups of
graduates each spring and summarizes their comments in a written report.
The Seminary continues to collect data from alumni In addition to systematic
phone interviews of two- and five-year M.Div. alumni, the Seminary collects data from
informal contacts at the annual PCA General Assembly and in the course of faculty and
staff travel. M.Div. alumni are routinely asked, “What did we give you that was
particularly helpful?” and “What didn’t we give you that we should have?” Perhaps
because of Covenant’s relational atmosphere, graduates keep in touch with faculty and
staff, particularly during the first years of ministry. This informal data closely parallels the
data from alumni surveys.
Other Assessment Measures
Covenant Seminary receives strong support from the church Churches and campus

<a id="ga26-p388"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=390 printed_page=388 -->

ministry staff continue to send us quality students and, in the case of the M.Div., in
record numbers. From 1992 to 1996, the number of entering students and of all students
from the PCA doubled. By 1996, Covenant enrolled 30 percent of all PCA M Div.
students in ATS seminaries (up from 20 percent in 1992). This clearly demonstrates
acceptance and approval, since there is no denominational requirement for students to
attend Covenant. Support from the larger church is evident in results from the 1997
Christianity Today Seminary and Graduate School Awareness and Reception Report, in
which readers rated Covenant first among fifty-nine schools in spiritual atmosphere,
second in doctrinal soundness and career preparation, in the top ten for Christian life
preparation, affordability, faculty reputation and academic reputation, and fourth overall.
Covenant’s graduates are accepted by the church. Since 1990, 96 percent of
M.Div. graduates with seminary recommendation seeking placement have been placed
within six months of graduation; many had multiple offers Nine-two percent of students
from the first three ma C. graduating classes have also been placed.
We believe that this acceptance comes, in part, from the fruitful ministries recent
graduates have enjoyed. Graduates since 1990 who have become solo pastors or church
planters have seen remarkable success, we know of no church plants that have failed or
existing churches that have split or declined significantly. Senior pastors and
denominational campus ministry staff commonly report appreciation for our graduates’
knowledge and pastoral disposition, they often return to us for other candidates.
The larger academic community verifies the quality of our graduates’ academic
preparation. Recent graduates have been admitted to doctoral programs at the
Universities of Aberdeen, Edinburgh, Cambridge, Leiden, Oxford, and Stellenbosch, as
well as at Hebrew Union College, Catholic University, St. Louis University, Marquette
University, Vanderbilt University, the University of Alabama, Trinity Evangelical
Divinity School, Concordia Seminary, and Westminster Theological Seminary.
Spiritual and Personal Formation
Covenant Seminary assists M.Div. students in the areas of spiritual and personal
formation. Contributions noted above apply particularly to the M.Div. program
The Seminary is especially concerned to assess M.Div students’ readiness for
ministry. In consultation with the faculty and administration (particularly with
departments that regularly deal with students over such matters as housing, billing, and
financial aid), the Dean of Students each spring seeks to identify students whose
maturity, behavior, or relational skills may make effective ministry difficult. When
questions arise, the Dean of Students discretely investigates. If this investigation
confirms the presence of significant problems, personal conversations with the student
(followed up in writing) seek to identify the difficulties and discuss strategies for
improvement. Students whose maturity, behavior, or skills continue to be problematic
will be advised that the Seminary may not recommend them for placement. The Seminary
is also exploring ways to include peer assessment and to involve students’ churches
much more directly in this assessment process. The Seminary continues to evaluate and
improve this process, seeking especially to use such assessment positively to produce
growth in students.

<a id="ga26-p389"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=391 printed_page=389 -->

doctor of ministry
Purpose and Content
The Doctor of Ministry (D.Min.) curriculum enhances the practice of ministry among
students who hold the M.Div. degree and who are currently engaged in ministry, and
meets ATS degree requirements (Section F). The thirty semester-hour program includes
a summary project/dissertation.
Courses are offered in intensive one-week formats. Courses are offered in St.
Louis, Baltimore and Birmingham in January and in June (August in Birmingham).
Students may take courses at any of these sites, though all must take at least one course
in St Louis Each course requires substantial reading and writing assignments to be
completed before the course begins, followed by additional written assignments within
two months following the course.
Resources
Since 1992 Covenant has been privileged to have Dr Philip Douglass as its D.Min.
program director. Under his leadership, the program doubled enrollment to an average of
seventy taking classes each term. Dr. Douglass also served as President of the
Association of Doctor of Ministry Education during 1996-97. As Director, Dr. Douglass
advises students, recruits prospective students, plans the course schedule, and maintains
a D.Min. Manual* that outlines requirements and procedures for the students in the
program.
Covenant’s faculty of pastor-scholars is well-suited by pastoral experience as well
as academic training to teach in this program In addition, the Seminary each year draws
on the services of four to six qualified ministry practitioners as adjunct faculty.
A spring 1997 D.Min. student survey found appreciation for: 1) faculty
experience in parish ministry and subject knowledge, 2) the quality of communication
with faculty and the D.Min Director, 3) the availability of help when needed, 4) the
overall learning experience Suggestions focused on possible scheduling adjustments.

Students
In addition to academic preparation, the Seminary seriously considers applicants’
spiritual preparation. All applicants submit a written testimony of their Christian
experience and provide a pastor’s reference (with other references). Because we believe
calling to ministry is confirmed in the context of the church, PCA M.Div. applicants are
strongly encouraged to be under the care of their presbyteries. Applicants who appear to
lack potential for fruitful ministry are steered away from professional degrees (M Div.
and ma C.) toward the M.A.G T.S. or Graduate Certificate, and if necessary, denied
admission. Applicants for professional degrees with questionable or problematic episodes
in their past (e.g., legal, financial, or marital difficulties) are advised during the
application process that the Seminary may not be able to assist in placement.
The Seminary’s growth has not compromised quality in admissions On the
contrary, as interest in Covenant has increased, we have been able to become more
selective, particularly in accepting M.Div. applicants. While acceptance rates declined

<a id="ga26-p390"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=392 printed_page=390 -->

from as high as 95 percent of applicants at the time of the last self-study to a slow as 80
percent, the current rate of about 90 percent excludes the significant number who are
discouraged from applying or are steered toward a more appropriate degree program
Faculty frequently express their belief that the quality of entering students has risen in
recent years. We were particularly pleased that over 25 of the students who entered in
the fall of 1997 came with substantial ministry experience (church, parachurch, or
missions) Among the factors we consider in seeking to maintain quality among M.Div.
applicants are: PCA membership (although we consciously also seek and regularly
receive a significant percentage of quality non-PC A applicants), pastor’s and other
references, length and quality of local church or ministry involvement, sense of calling,
likelihood to complete the degree, past academic performance, and a personal interview.
(There is no priority order among these factors) We continue to seek to improve our
ability to discern the highest quality applicants.
Student Sen’ices
The Seminary regularly and systematically evaluates services to students for
appropriateness, adequacy and use, in keeping with the Seminary’s purpose. This
evaluation includes exit interviews, regular meetings with Student Council
representatives and other students, statistical information, faculty and staff assessments,
focus groups, and a great deal of accessibility to students. Student workers in the
Student Services Office and Student Council officers are in touch with and share
concerns of many of their peers The Dean of Students has also sought to learn from his
counterparts at other institutions
In a spring 1997 survey, students were asked to evaluate the services they receive
from the various Seminary offices. On a five-point scale, staff were rated from 4.3 to 4 7
(for competence, pleasantness, and responsiveness), and services (for adequacy) from
4.2 to 4.6. While these results overall were very encouraging, the Seminary is seeking to
improve services from offices that were rated relatively lower.
Community Life and Formation for Ministry
The relational emphasis in the Seminary’s Mission Statement indicates the importance
we place on community life for the development of students as servants of the triune
God, whose walk with God and relationships with others are wholesome and growing,
and whose ministry skills enable them effectively to lead God's people.
Community Life
The Seminary follows up the very personal attention students receive from the
Admissions Office with an assimilation program for new students A “Welcome Wagon”
program and twice-a-month picnics through the summer help new students feel at home
orientations offered during the summer and at the start of each semester provide
important academic and other information. A formal reception at the beginning of the
semester gives new students and their spouses the opportunity to get to know one
another, as well as the faculty. These activities are most successful for students who have
relocated to attend Seminary and especially for those who live on campus, it seems to
take longer for students living off campus, and particularly their working spouses, to feel
a part of the Seminary community. The Seminary encourages spouses to take advantage

<a id="ga26-p391"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=393 printed_page=391 -->

of the Spouses Tuition Supplement, which enables spouses of full-time students to take
courses (credit or audit) free of charge; taking even one course makes a big difference in
identification with the Seminary. (Fourteen spouses received degrees or certificates
during the past three years.) we provide childcare for two of the three sections of the
first-year M.Div. course, Spiritual and Ministry Formation so that new student couples
can take this important course together. We also provide child care for selected other
courses and special events. We continue to evaluate and seek ways to make the
transition easier for new students.
Other campus events contribute to the sense of community students enjoy. We
cancel classes two days each semester so the whole community can come together for
the day of prayer and for campus day (when we work together on various campus
projects). Weekly communications through the Covenant Courier and other notices
posted around campus bring news of events, ministry opportunities, community needs,
and prayer requests, as well as official announcements. An active Student Council and
other student organizations (e.g., Military Student Fellowship, Student Missions
Fellowship, Archaeology Club, and Women Students’ Fellowship) contribute to the
sense of belonging. Other events include informal sporting events, fall and spring picnics,
a Christmas banquet, family nurture events (to strengthen student family life and prepare
couples for ministry), special lectures, and Friday Nights at the Schaeffer Institute.
Graduating students and their families share special times at the Senior Dessert and the
Senior Wives’ Dinner Exit interviews consistently rate “community” as a high point of
the Seminary experience; the positive relationships and sense of belonging are rated
excellent.
Our ecclesiology is sometimes in tension with this desire for community.
Although the Seminary serves the church and trains students for ministry in and through
the church, the Seminary is not itself a church. Our commitment to developing
community on campus must be balanced with our commitment to student participation in
local congregations. This healthy tension keeps us asking, “Should we do this, or should
we encourage students to find it in their local church?” We continue to strengthen
healthy working relationships with the local churches of various denominations in which
our students participate.
Spiritual Formation
In exit interviews students consistently indicate that they complete their time at Covenant
knowing Jesus Christ better than when they arrived. First and foremost, in exit interviews
and informal conversations students point to faculty as a chief instrument in their own
spiritual development. The faculty’s commitment to the goal of spiritual development of
students is clear in the classroom. Academic subjects are not merely academic—
Covenant’s pastor-scholars speak to students’ hearts. Faculty also spend significant time
with students outside the classroom, from office hours to Covenant Groups, from
brown-bag lunches in faculty offices to meals in faculty homes. Over and above these
things, students are affected by the lives, character, and commitment of Covenant’s
faculty. Because we have deliberately sought pastorally talented faculty, they are
naturally attractive to students, and some students would clearly like more personal
contact with faculty. We are currently seeking to clarify for students what level of
pastoral care and relational ministry the Seminary is able to provide.

<a id="ga26-p392"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=394 printed_page=392 -->

Students also indicate that the Seminary community life (see above) makes a
significant contribution to their spiritual growth and development. Chapel services three
days each week provide opportunities for corporate worship and preaching for the
Seminary community. Chapel services increasingly model a variety of worship styles,
using faculty, staff, and students in worship leadership and music. Attendance patterns
indicate that students prefer Seminary faculty to guest preachers. Although attendance is
not required, participation is high in relation to the number of students on campus at the
time chapel is held (though it declines toward the end of the semester).
Once each semester a student-led Day of Prayer replaces classes and provides a
morning of corporate worship, individual, small group and plenary prayer times,
concluding with lunch together Covenant Groups provide a weekly opportunity for
mutual encouragement, accountability, and prayer ina small group led by a faculty
member. Data collected during the self-study indicated that fewer than 20 percent of day
students were involved in these voluntary groups during 1996-97, fewer than expected.
Although we do not have longitudinal data, we believe this represents a significant
decline from past levels of student involvement and indicates the need to gather data on a
regular basis. While we recognize that some students create their own small groups or
participate in small groups out of their local church, this level of participation is a
concern. The Seminary will continue to assess the degree to which students are involved
in a healthy small group experience.
Students consistently report appreciation for the Seminary's focus on helping to
develop family life. The Spouses Tuition Supplement enables spouses to take courses
without charge, equipping them for ministry as well. Free child care for certain courses
and major seminary events, enhances spouses’ opportunities to grow by participating in
seminary life. Special events, workshops, and conferences also provide practical insight
for spouses preparing for ministry. Additional on-campus “for fee” opportunities for
children include ballet classes and a preschool Play Group program.
Again, Covenant’s understanding of the church means that the Seminary seeks to
work in partnership with local churches. We are seeking to monitor students’ church
involvement more closely so that we can begin to work more intentionally with these
churches in facilitating students’ spiritual development and assess readiness for ministry.
We have particularly sought to cooperate with our own denominational agencies
to enhance the Seminary’s purpose and at the same time support these agencies. We
have been actively engaged in sharing in the co stand development of information
resources to enhance public awareness of the denomination. We work with Reformed
University Fellowship in training leaders for the PCA’s campus ministry. We are
beginning a cooperative effort with Mission to the World (MTW) to facilitate increased
theological training for their missionaries. MTW is now supporting a career-track
missionary who, while a student, will assist the Admissions Office in recruiting short
term missionaries and internationals for theological training, as well generally seek to
increase global awareness on campus. The Dean of Students prescreens candidates from
the Seminary for the PCA’s church planting readiness assessment. In addition, the
Seminary hosted a church planting readiness seminar in November 1997. We are
working with both Mission to the World and Mission to North America by providing
extension class materials for ethnic pastors in the Miami area. We continue to work

<a id="ga26-p393"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=395 printed_page=393 -->

cooperatively with the PCA Foundation to share resources and advance the work of
planned giving at the Seminary and throughout our denomination

Summary

Criterion: Purpose
Covenant Theological Seminary has a clear and publicly stated purpose that has
remained essentially unchanged since its founding. The Seminary’s mission is wellunderstood by faculty, staff, students, and donors who tell us that the misssion plays a
major part in the satisfaction they feel in their association with Covenant.
Criterion: Resources.
Covenant Theological Seminary is governed by a knowledgeable and effective
board of trustees which clearly understands its role as expressed in the Seminary’s
Bylaws. The Seminary is administered by competent administrative leaders using welldefined and understood structures, policies, and procedures. Administrative leadership is
collaborative, involving faculty, student, and other consultation as appropriate; faculty
are consulted on all major decisions.
The Seminary faculty is well-qualified for their responsibilities, possessing both
excellent academic credentials in their fields and experience in ministry. This “pastorscholar” emphasis is one of Covenant’s distinctives. Students consistently cite the faculty
as one of the most significant factors in their experience at Covenant.
The Seminary provides a spiritually nurturing community and services that assist
students in spiritual, professional and academic development. The Seminary’s physical
plant, educational equipment (library, electronic services, etc.), and safe and healthy
environment effectively and adequately support the Seminary’s educational programs
and other purposes.
The Seminary has the financial resources and sound business practices necessary
to accomplish its purposes. Both income sources and expenditure categories
approximate those of comparable institutions.
Criterion: Continued Accomplishment
Covenant Theological Seminary has the financial, physical, human, and
technological resources to continue to accomplish its purposes and strengthen its
effectiveness. As a result of imaginative leadership by the Board and administration, the
Seminary is taking steps through the current capital campaign and other measures to
strengthen resources in each of these areas.
The Seminary has shown that it has decision-making resources that enable it to
respond to unexpected challenges. In particular, the Seminary has successfully responded
to unexpectedly rapid enrollment growth. In the process, the Seminary has taken care to
formalize and strengthen assessment and planning processes. The Seminary has
effectively organized and allocated resources to strengthen both individual programs and
the institution as a whole.

<a id="ga26-p394"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=396 printed_page=394 -->

Conclusion
We do not believe that the credit for these accomplishments belongs to Covenant
Seminary. As noted in chapter 1, we believe that while responsibilities are ours, results
come from God. We are required to be faithful to our calling; fruitfulness in that calling
is the gift of God. It is God in his grace who has provided the human, financial, and
physical resources we employ. It is God in his grace who has entrusted us with so many
students. It is God in his grace who has provided this institution with committed and
talented leadership. It is God in his grace who enables Covenant Seminary “to train
servants of the triune God to walk with God, to interpret and communicate God’s Word,
and to lead God’s people.”

Actions Requested of the 26th General Assembly:

1. That the proposed 1998-9 Fiscal Year Budget for Covenant Theological
Seminary be approved.
2. That the financial audit for Covenant Theological Seminary for the fiscal year
ending June 30, 1997 by Humes and Barrington, CPA's be adopted.
3. that the minutes of the covenant theological seminary board of trustee
meetings be approved for May 16-17, 1997; September 26-27, 1997; and
January 30-31, 1998; and, that the minutes of the executive committee of the
Board of Trustees be approved for December 5, 1997 and March 23, 1998.
4. that the congregations of the Presbyterian Church in America be urged to
support the work and progress of Covenant Theological Seminary through their
giving, prayer, advice and student recommendations, so that the future leadership
of Christ's Church is advanced and safeguarded.
5. That prayer be offered for the ministry of Covenant Theological Seminary.

<a id="ga26-p395"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=397 printed_page=395 -->

REPORT OF THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE
INSURANCE, ANNUITY AND RELIEF FUNDS
TO THE TWENTY-SIXTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

STATEMENT OF PURPOSE
the purpose of the board of trustees of the Insurance, Annuity and Relief Funds of the
Presbyterian Church in America is to glorify God through serving His servants who
minister in the Church by providing for the health and welfare, retirement and relief
needs of the denomination’s ministers, missionaries, lay church workers and their
families.
Introduction
By almost any measure, 1997 was an outstanding year for your PCA benefit plans. Total
assets climbed from $96.2 million to $121.9 million, an increase of 26 7% Our
participants’ retirement plan assets moved ahead significantly from $93.3 million to
$118.3 million, a 26.8% increase These increases can be accounted for by a continued
bull market for stocks and bonds and a significant increase in the number of participants
in the PCA Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan. Over 300 new people joined the plan during
1997 The plan received $1 85 million from other retirement plans through rollovers and
in-service transfers Several large churches and Christian schools enrolled in the plan for
their employees during the year.
The Long-Term Disability Plan increased participation by over 150, as several larger
employers and a number of individuals joined the plan The Optional Life and Term Life
Insurance Plans had slight increases in enrollment
See Attachment One for statistical comparisons of the plans for the past three years
The Christmas Offering, which funds Ministerial Relief, was $398,228 for 1997. The
1996 offering was $355,397
Historical Summary
Since we in the Presbyterian Church in America are celebrating our twenty-fifth
anniversary as a denomination, we thought it appropriate to review the history of the
growth of your benefit plans. When IAR set up separate offices and hired its first full
time director and new staff in late 1981, the denomination was less than ten years old

<a id="ga26-p396"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=398 printed_page=396 -->

Retirement Plans
From 1974-1982 we maintained the Annuity Fund for Ministers, a defined benefit
retirement plan, and the (lay) Employees’ Retirement Fund, which had 530 and 48
participants, respectively, at the end of 1982. The Annuity Fund for Ministers also had
death and disability benefits as part of the plan. Dues paid by the church and the minister
amounted to twelve percent of salary and housing. Dues for the lay plan were nine
percent of salary. Retirement benefits were based on a formula relating to years of
service, compensation and good experience credits added through investment surpluses
The lay plan was a defined contribution plan, with individual accounts.
James L. Hughes was hired to become the Director by the Board of Trustees on
November 1, 1981. A new office was set up in Decatur, Georgia with four additional
staff in January 1982 The operation of the retirement and insurance plans had
previously been handled by the office of the Coordinator (Business Administrator) of the
Committee on Administration in Columbus, Georgia. Dr. Dan Moore held this position
at the time.
The Director worked with the Board during 1982 to revamp the benefit plans and move
from a defined benefit plan to defined contribution retirement plans for ministers,
missionaries and lay employees. A new death and disability plan (D & D) was also
established to replace those benefits which had been a part of the defined benefit plan.
The suggested contributions for the retirement and the death and disability plans
remained at 12 percent, with 9 Vi% for retirement and 2 Vi% for D&D (or 9% and 3%,
respectively, for ministers not covered by Social Security).
At the Tenth General Assembly (1982) held at Calvin College in Grand Rapids,
Michigan, the Joining and Receiving of the Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical
Synod took place. Prior to the Assembly several meetings were held between the
Director and Dr Franklin Dyrness and others on the Board of the Pension Fund Plan of
the RPC,ES to discuss transfer of retirement accounts to the new PCA Tax-Sheltered
Annuity Plan if the J & R was approved. Assets remaining in the RPC,ES Plan at the
end of 1982 ($1,005,198) were transferred to the new TSA plan in early 1983. The
transfer represented the accounts of 144 active participants and 34 retirees
The new retirement plans, which began operation January 1, 1983, were the PCA Money
Purchase Pension Plan (401(a)) and the Voluntary Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan (403(b)).
Because of the ongoing difficulty in communicating to the churches the necessity of
covering all eligible employees and monitoring compliance with the adoption agreements
for the MPP Plan, the board closed that plan to new contributions at the end of 1989.
Initial investment options for the new plans were three - the Growth, Income and
Balanced Funds. The Balanced Fund was composed of 50% each in the Growth and
Income Funds. The Growth Fund was managed by outside firms, under morally

<a id="ga26-p397"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=399 printed_page=397 -->

restricted guidelines. The Income Fund was managed in-house by the Investment
Committee and Staff
In 1989 the Growth Fund was renamed the Equity Fund. Separate managers were hired
to manage the Income and Balanced Funds the same year. For diversification and
competitive reasons, three alternative publicly traded mutual funds were added as
options in 1993. A money market fund was added in 1994. The offerings were
expanded to ten in 1995. Investment categories replaced specific mutual fund names in
1996 to help plan participants better understand the investment objectives of the seven
mutual funds
Assets under the original retirement plans grew from $266,429 at the end of 1974 to $8
million at the end of 1982.
Under the new plans, assets grew from $10 1 million at the end of 1983 to $117.5
million at the end of 1997. Those assets first reached $100 million on Valentines Day
1997. Plan assets as of March 31, 1998 stood at over $132 .6 million Plan participation
during the same period increased from 578 to 3,175, including retirees receiving benefits.
The plans moved from annual to semi-annual to quarterly to daily valuation (1995).
With daily valuation, a toll-free benefits phone was added to allow anytime access to
participant accounts for balance inquiries, modeling or initiating loans and changing
investment options
Realizing the importance of communicating with our participants the Board issued the
first Annual Report in 1983. We later added a quarterly newsletter, the Benefit Bulletin.
In 1991 we changed from sending annual to semi-annual participant account statements.
With this change we initiated the two-page PCA Retirement Funds Update. In 1995 we
moved to quarterly statements and have continued to send the Update with each
statement. The Benefit Bulletin was changed to a semi-annual publication after the
addition of the quarterly Update. In 1996 we produced an audiotape and an asset
allocation worksheet to assist participants to more knowledgeably choose their
investment options. Since 1989 we have published an annual newsletter, Retirement
Planning Notes, for plan participants approaching retirement.
Insurance Plans
Health Plan
The health insurance program was initially insured by Pacific Mutual Life Insurance
Company beginning in late 1973, with a maximum lifetime benefit of $50,000. On
November 1, 1976, the coverage was moved to Connecticut General Life Insurance
Company. Coverage was enhanced to include a maximum lifetime benefit of $250,000.
The deductibles were $100 for the employee and $300 for the family. Co-insurance was
80% after the deductible, with an annual maximum of $500 for the employee and 51,000

<a id="ga26-p398"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=400 printed_page=398 -->

for the family. Premiums were set at $23.17 per month for single coverage and $52.74
for family coverage A $10,000 life and $10,000 AD&D plan accompanied the health
plan. A new Supplementary Term Life Insurance program was made available to
participants in the health plan. Plan A began with $10,000 coverage for those under age
30 and reduced each five years thereafter to $2,800 for those ages 60-65. Plan B began
at $20,000 and reduced to $5,000 for the 60-65 age group.
After one year with Connecticut General the coverage was again returned to Pacific
Mutual, with a $1 million lifetime maximum benefit. Plan C, with $30,000 initial
coverage was added to the Supplementary Term Life Insurance program Plan D
($40,000) was added a year later later plane ($40,000), a non-reducing plan was
added as an option for those age 50 and over.
The Prescription Drug Card was added in 1979. The copay per prescription was set at
$2.00. In the same year a two-person category was added to the health plan. Premiums
were set at $32 26 per month for single coverage, $64 00 for two-person coverage and
$79.91 for a family of three or more. By today’s premium levels these seem very low,
but there was a concern expressed by the Board in their report to the next General Assembly (1980) about ever-rising claims costs - a nearly preview of the spiraling cost
increases in the years ahead
After the 1981 General Assembly the health plan was moved to Mutual of New York
(M.O.N.Y.) under a minimum premium approach, to hold down premium costs The
coverage was changed to a self-funded plan, with Massachusetts Mutual Life Insurance
Company serving as claims administrator and stop-loss insurer in 1983. In 1986 the
claims administration was shifted to Self-Funded Plans, with stop-loss coverage provided
by Lloyds of London. This arrangement continued until January 1990, when, due to
depleting reserves because of a number of large claims, the Board chose Blue Cross/Blue
Shield of Michigan to fully insure the plan. We had come full circle in the return to an
insured plan.
By 1989 enrollment in the plan had risen to over 2,300 employees (plus dependents)
The higher costs for a fully insured plan in 1990 sent many of the larger employers
seeking local health coverage for their employees.
After two years with BC/BS of Michigan, the Trustees again returned the coverage to
Pacific Mutual (PM Group), our original insurer. PM Group provided an indemnity plan
with preferred provider organization (PPO) networks in many locations. Today the plan
has stabilized at about 900 participants
In August 1996, in response to a mandate from the General Assembly, a pilot HMO was
setup with blue choice of georgia for the atlanta area this plan has worked
reasonably well for most participants. In the meantime, PM Group has been working
with Private Healthcare Systems (PHCS), our PPO network provider, to establish a

<a id="ga26-p399"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=401 printed_page=399 -->

network of Point of Service (POS) locations around the country Going forward the
Board will explore the feasibility of making POS alternatives available to our employers
in larger metropolitan areas if premiums are competitive. Point of Service is similar to
Health Maintenance Organization (HMO) coverage, but with more flexibility in choice of
providers.
The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (HIPAA) has made it
easier for those whoa recovered by a group or individual plan to join anew plan without
showing proof of insurability and without pre-existing conditions limitations, in most
cases Our health plans are now required to have annual open enrollments to allow
anyone previously eligible but not participating to secure coverage. The downside is that
any eligibles, including newborns, who fail to enroll during their 30-day eligibility period,
will have to wait until the open enrollment to join the plan.
Life and Disability Plans
As mentioned above, the life and disability coverages were originally included with the
Annuity Fund for Ministers. In 1983 they became the Death and Disability Plan. Both
were insured by outside carriers. From 1984-August 1989 the Long-Term Disability
Plan was self-insured. When it appeared that the health plan was exhausting available
reserves, the Board decided to insure the LTD Plan with UNUM Insurance Company
(August 1989) and use the $1 million plus reserve to pay run-out claims from the health
plan UNUM has been the insurer of the LTD Plan since that time.
the ltd plan has undergone a succession of enhancements over time. it is avery
competitive product with increasing enrollments year after year.
The Term Life Plan was underwritten by Allstate Insurance Company from 1983 until its
group business was absorbed by Metropolitan Life in 1989. Met Life continued as
insurer for this plan as well as the Supplemental Life and Basic Life Plans until the
coverages were moved to John Hancock Mutual Life Insurance Company in May 1994.
To provide more flexibility of coverage amounts the Supplemental Life Plan was
replaced by the Optional Life Plan January 1, 1995.
the term life plan has remained a decreasing term insurance plan with a single premium
over the years. Coverage begins with $250,000 for those under age 30 and decreases
each five years thereafter. The Optional Life Plan provides optional amounts of
coverage (from $20,000 - $250,000), plus spouse and dependent children options with
premiums based on age of the employee.
Trustees and Staff
the board of trustees grew out of a sub-committee for insurance and annuities of the
Committee of Administration set up at the First General Assembly in December 1973.
This sub-committee was composed of Mr. Wright Pearson, Mr. William MacLeod, Jr.,

<a id="ga26-p400"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=402 printed_page=400 -->

Mr. William Swain, Reverend Donald Esty and Reverend E. Crowell Cooley (Chairman).
These men had served on the Insurance Committee of the Organizing Committee for a
Continuing Presbyterian Church prior to the convening of the General Assembly. The
First Assembly elected the following Board of Trustees for the Insurance and Annuity
Fund:
Class of 1976: RE William Joseph (Montgomery, AL)
TE Harry Schutte (Columbia, SC)
class of 1977: RE William T. McLeod (Coral Gables, FL)
TE E. Crowell Cooley (Norfolk, VA)
Class of 1978: RE William Swain (Coral Gables, FL)
RE James E Allen (Jackson, MS)
Mr. Swain was elected Chairman and Mr Joseph, Secretary. The Insurance Subcommittee was composed of Mr. McLeod, Rev. Cooley (Chairman), Mr. Swain and
Rev. Don Esty (a non-Trustee). While all these men are to be commended for their
work, it is evident from minutes of early meetings that E C. “Midge” Cooley did
yeoman’s duty in the early days and deserves much credit for a good foundation laid for
the future success of the work of the Board.
Dr. Dan Moore, Coordinator of the Committee on Administration, served as the
administrator for the Board. Mrs. Barbara Perry served as insurance secretary. Other
COA staff assisted in the work of the Trustees.
By the Third General Assembly, there were eight Trustees, then ten in 1976. At this
point there were two in each class, serving five-year terms, as clarified at the Ninth GA
in 1981. this assembly also approved changing the status of the trustees from a subcommittee (of the Committee on Administration) to “Trustees” and granted permission
for the trustees to hire a director and setup a separate office ina separate location with
a separate staff.
The Trustees established a search committee and hired the first Director on November 1,
1981. Gordon Moore, who previously worked for CO A became the Business Manager.
Three additional new staff were hired: Mrs. Peggy Goodlett, Secretary/Receptionist;
Miss Kathy Vandeford, EDP Specialist; and Miss Patricia Stone, Insurance Associate.
Initially all billings and collections for insurance and annuities were handled in-house. As
the plans and assets grew, so did the staff. By 1988, there were 15 full-time and one
part-time staff. when we returned to a fully-insured health plan in 1990, Blue
Cross/Blue Shield of Michigan used F. W. Grotenhuis Underwriters (now the Grotenhuis
Group) of Grand Rapids, to handle the administrative functions. This change forced a
downsizing of the IAR staff, which gradually reduced to seven, before increasing to nine
in 1997.

<a id="ga26-p401"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=403 printed_page=401 -->

As the benefit plans and assets grew, the Board asked the General Assembly to approve
expanding the number of Trustees from 10 to 12 in 1987. The new structure divided the
Trustees into four classes with three in each class. Terms reduced from five to four
years Trustees may now serve two consecutive terms (if reelected by the GA) before a
mandatory year off.
Over the first 25 years of the PCA the following have served as Trustees and Staff

<a id="ga26-p402"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=404 printed_page=402 -->

Ministerial Relief
At the First General Assembly in December 1973, as part of the Committee on
Administration report the following action was passed: “That the General Assembly
establish an annual offering to be received during the Christmas season for Ministerial
Relief. (This is a continuation of that offering known among some of usa s the joy
Gift )” The “Christmas Gift” was begun in 1974.
The Christmas Gift offerings were modest in the early years but were more than
sufficient to assist retirees, widows, etc The sentiment expressed in early minutes of the
Trustees was that the initial calls on relief funds would be small, since the denomination
was new. The idea was to build the fund so as to have adequate resources in future
years when more ministers would be retired and more widows would have needs.
Minutes of the Board meeting of November 8, 1975 show that the Ministerial Relief
Fund had a balance of $62,260.50.
The Fifth GA (1977) approved a set of policies concerning the “Fund for Ministerial
Relief,” among which was the following: “Relief Funds will be used to provide Medical
Insurance coverage for retired ministers and lay workers, to assist in emergency
situations, and to supplement income of retired personnel and their families when
needed ”
In 1981 the board voted to suspend automatic payment of retirees’ health premiums and
cover these payments only upon request and demonstrated need Then current recipients
were grandfathered Concern for the increasing costs to the Relief Fund as health
insurance premiums spiraled upward brought fiscal reality to a well-intentioned program.
The official name of the Board was changed at the 1982 General Assembly from “The
board of trustees of insurance and annuity fund of the Presbyterian Church in America” to “The Board of Trustees of the Insurance, Annuity and Relief Funds of the
Presbyterian Church in America” in recognition of the importance of the ministerial relief
function assigned the Trustees by the General Assembly. From that time we have
become known as “IAR.”
after the joining and receiving of the reformed presbyterian Church, Evangelical
Synod in 1982, the Trustees became concerned that, because of inadequate assets in the
RPC,ES Pension Fund Plan, there may well be a heavy call on the Relief Fund in future
years. The Board hired former Trustee TE Robert G. Sweet in 1984 to conduct a
research project in an effort to quantify future needs for relief assistance. The 1985
General Assembly received as information excerpts from Sweet’s report entitled “The
Church at Risk” and approved the addition of a staff position of coordinator of
Ministerial Relief Mr. Sweet was hired to fill this position “to work with the
presbyteries to better facilitate ministerial relief at the local level...” Sweet held this
post ion until he left to become editor of the PCA messenger july 1, 1989.

<a id="ga26-p403"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=405 printed_page=403 -->

Mrs Ann Llewelyn, widow of former PCA pastor Thomas Llewelyn, became the
Administrative Secretary for Relief January 3, 1988 She was promoted to Ministerial
Relief Assistant upon Sweet’s departure. Her title was later changed to Relief
Representative. Ann continues to minister to retirees and widows and to assist the
Director and the Relief Committee in administering the Relief Fund
In 1991 the ministerial relief fund was the recipient of the women in the church love
Gift to further assist in the ministry to pastors’ widows There has been a growing
relationship over the years between the WIC and Ministerial Relief The Christmas
Offering for the last several years have exceeded $300,000 each year As the
denomination ages we anticipate an increased Ministerial Relief ministry.
We hope you have enjoyed reading this brief history of IAR Thank you for the privilege
of serving you during the past 25 years And thanks to all the current and former
Trustees and staff for their faithful service to our Lord through the PCA

RECOMMENDATIONS:
1 That the Minutes of Board meetings of August 15, 1997, November 7, 1997 and
March 6, 1998 be approved
2. That the audit report dated December 31, 1997 by Arthur Andersen & Company
be received
3 That the General Assembly approve the use of Capin Crouse LLP, to conduct the
1998 audit.
4 That the 1999 budget be received with the understanding that it is a spending
plan and that adjustments will be made during the year, if necessary, by the
Trustees.
5 That the General Assembly approve the Sixteenth and Seventeenth Amendments
to the Presbyterian Church in America Voluntary Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan
(see Attachment Two) and that the resolutions related to these amendments be
adopted and included in the Minutes of the General Assembly.
6. That the General Assembly urge our member churches to participate in the
annual Christmas Offering or budget regular benevolence giving to support relief
activities through the Ministerial Relief Fund
Respectfully Submitted,
Board of Trustees James L Hughes
/s/ Dudley Barnes, Chairman Director

ATTACHMENT TWO
SIXTEENTH AMENDMENT
TO THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
VOLUNTARY TAX-SHELTERED ANNUITY PLAN

THIS AMENDMENT to the Presbyterian Church in America Voluntary Tax-Sheltered Annum
Plan (hereinafter referred to as the "Plan"), made this 7th day of November. 1997. by the Board of
Trustees of the Insurance. Annuity and Relief Funds of the Presbyterian Church in America (hereinafter
referred to as the "Trustees"), to be effective as noted below;
WITNESSETH:

WHEREAS, the Presbyterian Church in America sponsors and maintains the Plan as a
retirement income account under Section 403(b)(9) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended;
and

WHEREAS. Section 11.01 of the Plan reserves to the Trustees the right to alter or amend the
Plan subject to the approval of the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America; and

WHEREAS, it is the desire of the Trustees to amend the Plan to comply with certain provisions
of the Small Business Job Protection Act of 1996; and

WHEREAS, it is the desire of the Trustees to expand the definition of "compensation' for the
purpose of computing the Section 415 contribution limitation, in accordance with the Small Business
Job Protection Act of 1996;

NOW THEREFORE. IT IS RESOLVED that the Plan be amended in the form attached hereto
as Exhibit A effective as of the date set forth in such Exhibit; and it is

FURTHER RESOLVED, that the Director of the Insurance. Annuity and Relief Funds of the
Presbyterian Church in America is hereby authorized to take any and all actions necessary and desirable
to carry out this and the foregoing resolution.

IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the Trustees have caused this Sixteenth Amendment to be executed
on the day and year first above written.

<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=408 printed_page=406 -->

<a id="ga26-p407"></a>

EXHIBIT A
The Plan is hereby amended as follows, the provisions of paragraph 1 to be effective as of
January 1. 1997. the provisions of paragraph 2 to be effective as of December 12.1994. and the
provisions of paragraph 3 to be effective as of january 1. 1998:
1 Subsection (b) of Section 5.01 shall be amended by adding a new paragraph (4) to the
end, to read as follows:
(4) Self-Employed Ministers. For purposes of this Subsection (b) of this Section
5.01. "Compensation” of a minister who is a self-employed individual (within the
meaning of Code Section 401(c)(1)(B)) shall be determined by reference to the
minister's earned income (within the meaning of Code Section 401(c)(2)) from such
ministry' rather than the amount of compensation which is received from an employer;
and his years of Service shall include the years (and portions of years) in which he was
such a self-employed individual.
2. Section 5.01 shall be amended to read as follows:
(e) Notwithstanding any other provision of this Section 5.01, to the extent this
Plan is governed by chapter 43 of title 38. United States Code (relating to employment
and reemployment rights of members of the uniformed services), contributions
required to be permitted by Code Section 414(u)(2) shall be permitted in accordance
with such Code Section.
3. The following sentence shall be added to the end of Subsection (c) of Section 5.01:
For purposes of this Subsection (c) of this Section 5.01. a Participant's Compensation
shall be deemed to include any elective deferral (as defined in Code Section
402(g)(3)). and any amount which is contributed or deferred by the Employer at the
election of the Participant and which is not includible in the gross income of the
Participant by reason of Code Section 125 or 457.

<a id="ga26-p408"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=410 printed_page=408 -->

SEVENTEENTH AMENDMENT
TO THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
VOLUNTARY TAX-SHELTERED ANNUITY PLAN

THIS AMENDMENT to the Presbyterian Church in America Voluntary Tax-Sheltered Annuity
Plan (hereinafter referred to as the "Plan"), made this 6th day of March, 1998, by the Board of Trustees
of the Insurance, Annuity and Relief Funds of the Presbyterian Church in America (hereinafter referred
to as the "Trustees"), to be effective as noted below;
WITNESSETH:

WHEREAS, the Presbyterian Church in America sponsors and maintains the Plan as a
retirement income account under Section 403(b)(9) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended;
and

WHEREAS, Section 11.01 of the Plan reserves to the Trustees the right to alter or amend the
Plan subject to the approval of the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America; and

WHEREAS, it is the desire of the Trustees to amend the Plan to clarify that United States
military chaplains and other ordained ministers who are not employed by a church or another
organization exempt from federal income tax under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code may
participate in the Plan; and

WHEREAS, it is the desire of the Trustees to expand the definition of “compensation" for the
purpose of computing the Section 403(b) exclusion allowance, in accordance with the Taxpayer Relief
Act of 1997;

NOW THEREFORE, IT IS RESOLVED that the Plan be amended in the form attached hereto
as Exhibit A effective as of the date set forth in such Exhibit; and it is

FURTHER RESOLVED, that the Director of the Insurance, Annuity and Relief Funds of the
Presbyterian Church in America is hereby authorized to take any and all actions necessary and desirable
to carry out this and the foregoing resolution.

EXHIBIT A
The Plan is hereby amended as follows, to be effective as of january 1. 1998:
1 Section 1.09 shall be amended to read as follows:
The term 'Employer” shall mean a Church with respect to which the Trustees have
consented to accept contributions made either at the initiative of the Employer or
pursuant to a Salary Reduction Agreement under which the Church purchases an
Annuity Contract provided by this Plan. The term Employer" shall also include a
duly ordained minister of the Presbyterian Church in America engaged in the exercise
of his ministry with the approval of the appropriate presbytery, with respect to whom
the Trustees have consented to accept contributions made by him on his own behalf
The Trustees may manifest their consent by the act of accepting contributions, and
each Church which or minister who has made contributions to the Plan which have
been accepted by the Trustees shall be deemed to be an Employer.
2. Paragraph (1) of Subsection (b) of Section 5.01 shall be amended by adding a new
Subparagraph (E). to read as follows:
For purposes of this Subsection (b) of this Section 5.01. a Participant's Compensation
shall be deemed to include any elective deferral (as defined in Code Section
402(g)(3)). and any amount which is contributed or deferred by the Employer at the
election of the Participant and which is not includible in the gross income of the
Participant by reason of Code Section 125 or 457
