<a id="ga26-p411"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=413 printed_page=411 -->

EXCERPTS FROM THE MINUTES
OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

WHEREAS, the Board of Trustees of the Presbyterian Church in America Insurance. Annuities
and Relief Funds has approved adoption of the Sixteenth and Seventeenth Amendments to the
Presbyterian Church in America Voluntary Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan, subject to approval by the
General Assembly;

WHEREAS, the General Assembly has determined that these amendments recommended by
the Trustees should be approved;

RESOLVED, that adoption of the amendment is hereby ratified by the General Assembly of the
Presbyterian Church in America

STATED CLERK'S CERTIFICATION
The undersigned Stated Clerk of the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America
hereby certifies that attached hereto is a true and correct excerpt from the minutes of a meeting of the
General Assembly, duly called, convened and held on the day o f________________ . 1998. and
that such Resolution therein contained is still in full force and effect
IN WITNESS WHEREOF, I have hereto affixed my signature this_______ dav of
__________ , 1998.

Stated Clerk

<a id="ga26-p412"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=414 printed_page=412 -->

## REPORT OF THE INTERCHURCH RELATIONS COMMITTEE TO THE TWENTY-SIXTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY

Presbyterian Church in America
June 1998

### I. MEMBERSHIP

|  TE Donald J. MacNair | Missouri | 2000  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  RE James Walters | Calvary | 2000  |
|  TE Robert L. Reymond | S. Florida | 1999  |
|  RE Thomas Sanford | MS Valley | 1999  |
|  TE Henry Lewis Smith | SE Alabama | 1998  |
|  RE R. Meade Guy | SE Alabama | 1998  |
|  TE K. Eric Perrin | Palmetto | Alternate  |
|  RE Philip Anderson | Evangel | Alternate  |
|  TE Paul R. Gilchrist | TN Valley | ex-officio, Stated Clerk  |
|  RE Donald Gahagen | N. Georgia | ex-officio, MTW  |

### II. MEETINGS

|  August 12, 1997 | Conference Call  |
| --- | --- |
|  September 23, 1997 | Atlanta  |
|  April 17, 1998 | Atlanta  |

### III. ITEMS DISCUSSED AND ACTIONS TAKEN

#### A. Paul R. Gilchrist

The Interchurch Relations Committee wishes to express appreciation to Dr. Paul R. Gilchrist for his leadership and wise guidance of the PCA's interchurch relations over the past ten years. Dr. Gilchrist has faithfully represented the PCA at countless meetings of various church bodies and has conducted the affairs of our denomination with grace and strength to the glory of God and the benefit of the PCA. He was instrumental in bringing together the PCA and the RPCES in 1982 and he has worked tirelessly in the interests of improved understanding and cooperation among many different Reformed and evangelical denominations. He is widely respected and admired around the world and in the United States. The PCA owes much more to our Stated Clerk than most of us will ever be aware of and the IRC wishes to give him a resounding "Thanks! Well done!"

#### B. NAPARC

The North American Presbyterian and Reformed Council met for its annual meeting in Atlanta, Georgia, November 18-19, 1997, with the PCA as host. Gordon J.

<a id="ga26-p413"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=415 printed_page=413 -->

Keddie of the RPCNA was elected chairman The most important item of business was
the motion brought by the PCA in 1996, that "NAPARC initiate the process to suspend
the CRCNA from its membership " After debate, the council voted 6-1, with the
Christian Reformed Church voting in opposition, to pass on to the member
denominations the recommendation that the CRC be suspended. Members will have
three years to vote at their national assemblies on the proposal and it will require a twothirds vote of the NAP ARC churches to bring about suspension Some denominations
do not meet every year, but their c anticipates that based on the depth of agreement
among the six member denominations who voted in favor, suspension of the CRC is a
foregone conclusion. The PCA delegation served notice to NAP ARC that, as the 25th
General Assembly directed, if the CRC does not reverse its new position on women in
office within one year after being suspended by NAP ARC, the PCA will bring a motion
for removal of the CRC from that body NAP ARC subsequently drew up a rationale for
its action and has sent that document on to the various denominations
In other action, NAP ARC drew attention to the relief efforts of the Korean
American Presbyterian Church being carried out in North Korea and encouraged
participation by other NAPARC denominations. NAPARC is also exploring the
possibility of creating a web page on the internet to provide a directory of reformed
churches in North America.
C. Christian Reformed Church
The 25th General Assembly instructed the IRC to "initiate conversations with
the cr c with a view toward making a recommendation to the 26th General Assembly
concerning the future relationship between the two denominations " (M25GA , 25-15,
III, 2, p 57) After several informal attempts to arrange for conversations, the IRC
delegation to the national association of evangelicals meeting did meet with leaders
from the CRC on March 4, 1998. As one may suppose, the CRC is somewhat "less than
eager" to pursue a new relationship with the PCA. The conversations were frank and
the end result was a willingness of both denominations to continue to discuss the
parameters of whatever future relationship may be desirable The IRC has no
recommendation to make to the General Assembly concerning the CRC at this time
other than to continue the process of discussions.
The IRC received a letter from the CRC Interchurch Relations Committee dated
February 26 enclosing a rationale for their opening the offices of minister and elder to
women, and asking the PCA (and the other NAPARC denominations) to vote against
suspension from NAPARC. The IRC has prepared a brief response to the CRC letter
and rationale and expects it to be printed in the Commissioners' Handbook Supplement
for the information of the General Assembly
D. National Association of Evangelicals
The NAE held its annual meeting in Orlando, Florida on March 2-4, 1998. The
interesting thing about this meeting was that NAE was coming together for the first time
under its revised and streamlined organizational structure and constitution (The

<a id="ga26-p414"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=416 printed_page=414 -->

statement of faith remains unchanged) President Don Argue announced his resignation
after only three years, so the NAE is once again seeking a leader who will take the
fellowship into the next century NAE still faces many challenges, perhaps the chief
one being how to involve younger leaders in its work and meetings. The average age of
NAE individual supporters is 70.
Nevertheless NAE offers the PCA rare opportunities to meet and talk informally
with leaders from other evangelical denominations. It affords the PCA the means of
standing publicly with other evangelicals so that our voice may be heard in our culture
for righteousness' sake in a way we could never do by ourselves. For example, the
NAE’s resolution on the persecution of Christians adopted two years ago has paved the
way for pending legislation in Congress and led to Don Argue's recent trip to China,
representing the United States, to investigate reports of persecution in that nation The
NAE has enabled the PCA to stand with our fellow believers in an international effort to
pray for persecuted brothers and sisters Without the NAE, the believing church in
America would be much more fragmented and would present an almost completely
divided face to our culture people of our day need to hear the gospel of jesus christ
and observable Christian unity is one of the factors which gives the gospel credibility to
many
The NAE adopted several resolutions that the IRC wishes to refer to the
Committee on Christian Education and Publications. They are as follows:
DECLARATION FOR PUBLIC EDUCATION

Whereas, we as Christians recognize our Biblical duty to teach our children, and as
citizens of the United States recognize today that mothers and fathers desire a
hope and a future for their children;

Whereas, public schools were founded as one of the means of educating future
generations to be capable of assuming their responsibilities as citizens, discover
truth and develop moral character,

Whereas, the churches and all people of faith have an opportunity to assist local
schools and communities to secure a safe learning environment, academic
excellence, meaningful parental involvement, and community participation,

Whereas, Christian churches need not only to provide for the spiritual well-being of
children, parents and educators within their congregations, but also need to see
their involvement in local public schools as part of the churches' vision,

Be it resolved, as followers of Jesus Christ, we accept the responsibility to love all
children as we love ourselves, and to pray for children, educators and public
schools,

Be it further resolved, that we recognize and accept our opportunity as Christians and
citizens to build constructive relationships with local public schools, to pursue
avenues of support for those involved in public education and to encourage and
disciple public school teachers, administrators and students.

<a id="ga26-p415"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=417 printed_page=415 -->

Be it further resolved, this emphasis on public education is not intended to
compromise the value nor question the validity of private, Christian, or home
schools.
PORNOGRAPHY AND OBSCENITY
the national association of evangelicals affirms the declaration of scripture that all
persons are made in the image of God and are therefore to be treated with respect and
dignity. We also affirm that sex is a gift from our loving Father to be enjoyed within
the sacred bonds of marriage between husband and wife
Because of our sin, we as individuals and as a nation continue to experience a decline in
moral values and beliefs. Our sinful behavior has resulted in increasing rates of
unmarried adolescent and adult sexual activity, the birth of children to unwed mothers,
the spread of sexually transmitted diseases and decreasing marital fidelity. These are all
signs of the decline of our culture
Today pornography and obscenity are available through increasingly sexually explicit
movies and televisions programs, magazines, video tapes and, most recently, the
Internet. The Internet brings pornography into our homes, schools and libraries
Everything from soft-core to hard-core to child pornography is now available not just
for adults, but for children. The powerfully corrosive effect within American society is
deeply troubling to evangelicals.
Pornography and obscenity cause harm to those (often women and children) who are
abused in the production of it as well as to those who use it. These materials exploit
persons made in God's image, destroy healthy relationships and distort God's gift of
loving, mutual sexuality between husband and wife.
As members of the National Association of Evangelicals, we are therefore committed to
educating both our members and the broader culture about the harm of pornography.
To facilitate that commitment, we encourage our member denominations to take official
action at their national governing bodies, and to implement an educational process that
will protect God's people from the impact of pornography. We encourage pastors and
congregations to protect their youth and children from exposure to these materials We
call upon leaders of businesses, schools and libraries as well as parents to participate in
an effort to protect children and youth from being harmed by these materials.
We also urge local, state, and federal agencies to prosecute aggressively those who
break the law in producing and distributing illegal pornography or who operate illegal
sex businesses.
Lastly, we call upon all leaders of the broader faith community, all persons of goodwill
and especially representatives of the evangelical community to be "salt and light" in
protecting people from the harm of pornography, obscenity and sex businesses. We

<a id="ga26-p416"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=418 printed_page=416 -->

also call upon these same people to join together in their own communities to win the
battle against pornography and obscenity through education, appropriate law
enforcement efforts and by helping those who have been harmed
E. World Fellowship of Reformed Churches
The World Fellowship of Reformed Churches was formed by the PCA, the
Presbyterian Church of Brazil and the National Presbyterian Church of Mexico in 1994.
In 1995 WFRC formed the regional Confraternidad Latinoamericana de Iglesias
Reformadas (CLIR) as a means of bringing evangelical Presbyterian and Reformed
churches into contact with each other. WFRC has two goals: (1) To build a world wide
fellowship among evangelical Presbyterian and Reformed denominations which enables
churches to work together, mutually supporting missions, evangelism, church planting
and pastor training; and (2) to establish a network where congregations, ministries,
organizations and Christian individuals with ministry resources may empower those
who possess vision but few resources.
In 1998 WFRC placed on the field in Costa Rica an executive director, Rev.
William Green, a minister in the United Reformed Church and church planting
missionary. WFRC is sponsoring several pastor training seminars in Latin America,
three in Chiapas and surrounding territories in southern Mexico, where Presbyterians
are experiencing a great awakening but also much persecution. WFRC also has
received its first member churches outside the Americas, from India and Kenya. WFRC
works closely with MTW and PCA missionaries
F. Report on Categories of Ecclesiastical Relationships with Other Churches
The object of the Presbyterian Church in America's interchurch relations has
been acknowledged by previous general assemblies as one aspect of fulfilling the
Lord's prayer for the visible unity of His church (John 17:21) and to facilitate the
completion of the Great Commission by working together with other like-minded
evangelical and Reformed church bodies. (See MIGA, 1973, 1-58, pp. 39, 40; M2GA,
1974, p. 75, 2-100,11, A, 1)
The PCA was bom twenty-five years ago with a clear vision, to be the
continuing Presbyterian church in the United States. To that end, the PCA raised a clear
witness to the Reformed faith and invited others to join us. God has wonderfully
blessed that witness with growth in numbers and influence in this country and across the
world.
the vision to strengthen and expand the impact of the reformed faith led to the
PCA's role in the formation of the north american presbyterian and reformed council
(NAPARC) in 1978 and the Joining and Receiving with the Reformed Presbyterian
Church, Evangelical Synod in 1982.
At the milestone of its 25th anniversary, the PCA reaffirms its original vision for
interchurch relations, and seeks to establish appropriate relationships with other

<a id="ga26-p417"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=419 printed_page=417 -->

evangelical and Reformed denominations in this country and around the world which
will advance the Great Commission. Our task is to encourage those who believe the
historic and Biblical faith, to offer light to those who require clearer understanding of
Biblical truth, to confirm those who claim the Reformed faith in a stronger
understanding and commitment to the doctrines of grace, and to lead those who share
our passion for the glory of god in a growing unity of mission—that the world should
bow before Christ her King.
The 25th General Assembly noted the need to clarify and update the PCA's
categories of ecclesiastical fellowship. To this end the Interchurch Relations
Committee has sought to make as little change as possible so that we reflect the
continuity of policies which have served us well through the years. (See M2GA, 1974,
p. 76, 2-100, II, A, Z ,MJ2GA, 1984, p. 96, 12-29, III, 10 ,M19GA, 1991, p. 65, 19-19,
III, 2) categories of ecclesiastical relationships areas follows:
1 FRATERNAL RELATIONS
The General Assembly may choose certain Reformed denominations in
North America with whom to enjoy Fraternal Relations. Essential agreement of
doctrine, government and practice is the only basis upon which Fraternal
Relations may be established. Fraternal Relations may be implemented where
practical and desirable by any or all of the following expressions of respect,
fellowship and endorsement:
a. Exchange of Fraternal Delegates at major assemblies. (The PCA
can receive as fraternal delegates, men who subscribe to the
classic Reformed confessions, and whose office in their
communion is on a parity with the offices of ruling and
Teaching Elder in the PCA);

- **b.** Occasional pulpit fellowship,
- **c.** Intercommunion (i.e., fellowship at the table of the Lord),
- **d.** joint action in areas of common responsibility;
- **e.** Communication on major issues of joint concern;
- **f.** Exercise of mutual concern and admonition with a view of promoting the fundamentals of christian unity.
- **g.** exchange of Minutes/Acts of the broadest assemblies.

2 ECCLESIASTICAL FELLOWSHIP
The PCA may maintain Ecclesiastical Fellowship through the
Interchurch Relations Committee, with Reformed denominations in North
America where the PCA shares membership in a larger organized fellowship
(e g , NAPARC, NAE, or others entered by action of the General Assembly).
Although there may not be essential agreement of doctrine, government or
practice, Ecclesiastical Fellowship recognizes certain common interests, the
desire to "get to know" the other denomination, or benefit to the PCA through

<a id="ga26-p418"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=420 printed_page=418 -->

correspondence or cooperation. Ecclesiastical Fellowship may be implemented
by any or all of the following expressions of respect and fellowship:
a. Sending and/or receiving men as Ecclesiastical Fellows at major
assemblies where approved by both bodies;
b joint action in areas of common responsibility,

- **c.** Communication on major issues of mutual concern;
- **d.** Exchanges of correspondence;
- **e.** exchange of Minutes/Acts of the broadest assemblies.

3. CORRESPONDING RELATIONS
The PCA may maintain Corresponding Relations through the Interchurch
Relations Committee with other evangelical churches in North America for the
purpose of exchanging greetings and letters of encouragement. This may
include the exchange of Official Observers at the broadest assemblies and
communications on issues of common concern.
4 INTERNATIONAL FRATERNAL CORRESPONDENCE
The PCA will maintain International Fraternal Correspondence through
the Interchurch Relations Committee with Reformed churches across the world
with whom the PCA has established a cooperative agreement, or who are
members of an international organization the General Assembly has chosen to
enter, such as the world fellowship of reformed Churches. International
fraternal correspondence is not an endorsement of another church's doctrine or
practice. Rather, it is a means for experiencing fellowship and getting to know
other churches with the aim of developing mutually beneficial cooperation.
international fraternal correspondence maybe implemented by any or all of the
following expressions of cooperation:

- **a.** Sending and/or receiving men as Fraternal Representatives at major assemblies as time, geography and finances permit,
- **b.** exchanges of correspondence;
- **c.** Fellowship meetings in conjunction with WFRC;
- **d.** Sending and/or receiving men as representatives at church courts, if agreeable to those courts;
- **e.** establishment of cooperative agreements as approved by the

General Assembly.
G. United Reformed Church
The IRC sent TEs Donald MacNair and Thomas Vanden Heuvel as observers to
the second synod of the united reformed churches of north america (URC) held in
Ontario, Canada, October 21-24, 1997. The URC is a young denomination made up
primarily of churches which have left the christian reformed Church. They have
perhaps 65-70 congregations and possibly 13,000-15,000 members. The URC exhibits
opposition to a unified church government in its early organization. The synod meeting

<a id="ga26-p419"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=421 printed_page=419 -->

offered a good opportunity to exchange information and ideas and establish the
beginnings of informal contacts with the PCA. The IRC recommends to the General Assembly that it authorize the IRC to initiate discussions with the URC perhaps leading
to establishment of ecclesiastical relations, and that it send the following letter to the
URC:
Dear Fathers and Brethren:
Warmest greetings in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ. With joy we have
learned of your fidelity to "the faith once delivered to the saints," and your willing
endurance of the sacrifices necessary to maintain and promote that faith.
As you take your place among the Reformed churches of North America and,
indeed, the world, we welcome you and extend to you aright hand and a warm heart of
fellowship. We anticipate co-laboring with you in the Lord's vineyard, should our Lord
tarry. To that end we invite you to take whatever steps are requisite to develop closer
relations between the united reformed churches of north america and the
Presbyterian Church in America Details at this end would be refined by our
Interchurch Relations Committee and approved by the PCA General Assembly, all in
consultation with your appropriate committee.
We pledge ourselves to pray for you that "your faith [may] grow exceedingly
and the love of everyone of you all abound" to all the brethren in Christ. (1
Thessalonians 1:3)

H. Response To Overtures 11 And 22
The IRC believes that the questions raised in these overtures were settled by the
actions of the 25th General Assembly and should be laid to rest. Year after year,
disputes over the actions of the IRC have been pursued by certain individual through
independent publications and often byway of presbytery overtures. We earnestly
desire peace within the body of the PCA and see no great good coming from a
rehashing of issues that have been reviewed and dealt with by previous General
Assemblies.
The IRC, while made up of different members each year, expresses its
confidence that previous committees have acted honorably and in good faith to carry
out the directives and desires of the General Assembly. The 1997-98 Committee
reaffirms its deep-felt commitment to faithfully serve the General Assembly and to be
subject to our brethren in the Lord. If there is acrimony, it does not come from this
committee.
The basic problem Overtures 11 and 22 seem to be concerned with is "that the
IRC had failed to carry out the directive of a previous General Assembly," by which
they evidently refer to the instruction of the 23rd General Assembly "to use all due
process afforded them to remove the CRC from membership in NAPARC, if the CRC

<a id="ga26-p420"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=422 printed_page=420 -->

does not repent or rescind the action of the 1995 Synod (to open all church offices to
women) at its 1996 Synod." (M23GA , 23-32, III, 12, p 167)
The real issue is a point of emphasis in the 23rd General Assembly's instruction,
namely, did the Assembly intend a rigid adherence to "to remove", that is, was the only
motion the IRC could obediently make that NAPARC immediately "terminate the
membership" of the CRC? Or did the Assembly put the emphasis on "due process"
leading to CRC removal The IRC in all good faith and agreeing with the end goal,
interpreted the Assembly's instruction to mean, "start the process and follow it through."
And this was what it did.
This issue was raised at the 25th General Assembly in the form of four nearly
identical overtures and was thoroughly reviewed by the Committee of Commissioners
on Interchurch Relations Their recommendation, adopted 21-2-0 by the Committee of
Commissioners, was subsequently approved by the General Assembly, namely, "That
the General Assembly affirms its confidence that the IRC acted in good faith with
regard to the directive to seek to remove the CRC by "all due process afforded to them."
Further, the Committee of Commissioners adopted 22-3-0 and the Assembly
approved the recommendation "that the General Assembly accepts the rationale for the
decision of the IRC to proceed with suspension of the CRC from NAPARC as a step
toward expulsion and directs the IRC that, if the CRC does not reverse the action of
Synod 95 regarding women in office within a year of being suspended by NAPARC, at
the next meeting of NAPARC the IRC shall introduce a motion that the CRC be
expelled from NAPARC."
The Committee of Commissioners added (25-0-0) and the Assembly approved
the additional statement, "that the General Assembly accepts the apology of their c for
the failure to get the motion concerning the CRC before NAPARC in a timely and
effective manner." (M25GA , 25-15, III 4, p 57)
The IRC is convinced that the issue has already been settled and that the authors
of Overtures 11 and 22 need to trust the thorough and effective oversight and review
process employed by the General Assembly. As to the remaining allegations of
"numerous factual errors" alleged to be in the IRC's report to the 25th General Assembly, they are quibbles over trivial details or interpretations of events. In any case
the IRC stands by the report of the 1996-97 committee.
The primary issue addressed by Overture 11 is, for all intents and purposes, a
moot point. The motion by the PCA to suspend the CRC from NAPARC has been
adopted by that body with a single dissenting vote, that of the CRC. The 26th General Assembly will have opportunity to affirm the action of NAPARC when it votes on the
recommendation of the IRC to approve suspension Beyond this, the IRC has
communicated its intention to bring a motion to remove the CRC from NAPARC, as the

<a id="ga26-p421"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=423 printed_page=421 -->

25th General Assembly directed, at the annual meeting of NAPARC in the year 1999,
or one year after suspension takes effect, if the CRC does not change its course.

### I. APPOINTMENT OF FRATERNAL DELEGATES

The IRC, in agreement with the other NAPARC denominations and as approved
by the General Assembly {M25GA, 25-15, III, 5, p 61) is now following the pattern of
rotating the sending of Fraternal Delegates. This year the IRC has appointed the
following men as Fraternal Delegates:
* Orthodox Presbyterian Church General Assembly, Grand Rapids, MI, May 27-June 3
TE Walter Lorenz
* reformed presbyterian church of north America, Beaver Falls, PA, June 12-19
TE James L. Smith, Jr.

#### IV. RECOMMENDATIONS

1. That Fraternal Delegates and other distinguished guests be welcomed and
invited to address the Assembly.
2 That the General Assembly express its appreciation to TE Paul Gilchrist for his
labors on behalf of the PCA's interchurch relations over the past ten years and
commend him for a job well done.
3. That the proposal from NAPARC be approved: that the Christian Reformed
Church be suspended from membership in NAPARC.
4 That the NAE resolutions on Public Education and Pornography and Obscenity
be referred to the Committee on Christian Education and Publications for study
and appropriate action.
5. That the Report on Categories of Ecclesiastical Relationships with Other
Churches be approved.
6. That the IRC be authorized to open discussions with the United Reformed
Church leading to a possible ecclesiastical relationship and that the
communication to the URC be approved
7. That the General Assembly respond to Overtures 11 and 22 in the negative in
accordance with the action of the 25th General Assembly (.M25GA , 25-15, III, 4,
p 57) and that the General Assembly urge the Presbyteries which have filed

<a id="ga26-p422"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=424 printed_page=422 -->

overtures on this matter to cease and desist for the sake of the peace and unity of the body of Christ.

Respectfully submitted,
/s/ TE K. Eric Perrin, Chairman

# SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT

## IV. RECOMMENDATIONS

8. That the 26th General Assembly respond to Communication 2 from the Christian Reformed Church as follows:

Esteemed brethren of the CRC:

Greetings in the name of our blessed Lord Jesus Christ.

We have come to you in response to your request to reconsider our decision regarding the membership of the CRCNA in NAPARC.

You have favored us with your rationale for the synodical decision which opened the offices of elder and deacon to women in the CRCNA. We appreciate your openness and your readiness to communicate this with us.

After prayerfully discussing the paper of Dr. John Cooper on the Hermeneutics and Theological Method of the CRC position on women's ordination, we humbly submit to you our response with the hope and prayer that you may receive it as a fraternal expression of Biblical thought and exegesis which springs from our hearts, with the prayer that you may re-evaluate your position at the 2000 Synod in this light.

Dr. Cooper maintains that a good case can be made for both the prohibition and the permissibility of the ordination of women.

Can, however, a Biblically based case be made for both? We do not believe a Biblically base case can be made for the permissibility of women in the authoritative offices of the church in the face of what the Apostle Paul states in I Timothy 2:12: "I do not permit a woman to teach or to have authority over a man; she must be silent."

Dr. Cooper's case rests on the philosophical argument that the theological/ontological equality of man and woman requires functional subordination of woman to man only in the realm of marriage. He submits no Biblical evidence for this. The same Apostle who wrote Galatians 3:28, "There is no Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus" also wrote I Timothy 2:12 (above). He saw no incompatibility between the spiritual unity in Christ and the functional subordination in church office. Furthermore a good Biblical example of ontological equality and functional subordination is the Holy Trinity. The three Persons of the Trinity are one in essence and being, yet the Son, Jesus Christ submitted Himself to the Father as the Apostle Paul says: "Now I want you to realize that the head of every man is Christ, and the head of the woman is man, and the head of Christ is God" (I Corinthians 11:3). The functional subordination of the Son to the Father does not

<a id="ga26-p423"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=425 printed_page=423 -->

compromise their full equality. Dr. Cooper, however, sees any teaching of functional difference to be in tension with equality.

Dr. Cooper also believes that the I Timothy passage deals with marriage and the concern to preserve the difference of husband and wife. However, a careful exegesis of this passage in the context of the whole of I Timothy teaches that it deals not with marriage but with worship and the proper order of the church. "Although I hope to come to you soon, I am writing you these instructions so that, if I am delayed, you will know how people ought to conduct themselves in God's household, which is the church of the living God, the pillar and foundation of the truth" (I Timothy 3:14, 15).

Dr. Cooper speaks of "weighting" text. One of the principles of Professor Louis Berkhof's hermeneutics is that a positive analogy of Scripture is better than a general analogy. When texts speak directly and explicitly to a particular matter, and when they do so with one voice, then their teaching has clear and compelling authority. This is precisely the case regarding ordination. In those texts which deal directly and explicitly with the point at issue, namely, whether women may serve as an ordained officer of the church, the teaching is uniformly "no." This should be more than enough to settle the issue in a church of the Reformed persuasion.

Professor Berkhof insisted on the organic unity of Scripture and the necessary consistency of its teachings. Nowhere does Professor Berkhof countenance the idea that an appeal to the general or positive analogy of Scripture could sustain two equally valid, yet contradictory conclusions: namely, that of prohibiting and that of permitting the ordination of women. Yet that is precisely what Dr. Cooper wants to do.

Dear brothers in the CRCNA, you have asked us to take the paper of Dr. Cooper and once more consider the matter of women's ordination from the perspective of the CRCNA. We have done this and do not find his argument persuasive. We cannot accept the fact that a Biblically based case can be made for two contradictory positions. To use the venerable Professor Berkhof in defense of this is gross injustice to one of the great pillars of the CRCNA. We sincerely hope that you will consider some of our remarks about Cooper's paper to be helpful to you in order that you may re-evaluate your position, since the whole matter of women's ordination will come up again for review at the 2000 Synod of the CRCNA.

Yours in the Name of Him Whose we are and Whom we serve, Christ our Lord,

<a id="ga26-p424"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=426 printed_page=424 -->

THE REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE ON
MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA
TO THE 26™ GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF
THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
I INTRODUCTION
MNA and You: Reaching North America for Christ
For everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.
How, then, can they call on the one they have not believed in?
And how can they believe in the one of whom they have not heard?
And how can they hear without someone preaching to them?
And how can they preach unless they are sent?
As it is written, “How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good newsV’
Romans 10:13-15
In this 25th anniversary year of the Presbyterian Church in America, the Lord gives us a
great opportunity for celebration of His work and for recommitment to what He will do
in the future. Indeed, as we look toward future expectations in light of what the Lord
has thus far done, the promise of jeremiah 29:11 comes readily to mind, ‘Tor I know
the plans I have for you, plans to prosper you and not to harm you, plans to give you
hope and a future.”
The PCA began 25 years ago with a great missionary vision. While much of that vision
was focused toward other nations, our short history demonstrates that the Lord also has
given us vision for reaching North America for Christ.
During 25 years, 1269 particular churches have been added to the PCA. of these, 764
joined by transfer; 505, or 40% of the total, were gained through organization as
particular churches. Further, in the five-year period just concluded, 1993-1997, the
greatest number were organized, at 129 (see Attachment A). In addition, Reformed
University Ministries, having begun in 1971 with one campus, has grown to reach 38
campuses. Here also, the last five years have seen the strongest growth, with ministries
initiated on 11 new campuses.
our prayer is that these numbers represent only the beginning of what the lord will do.
The great future which the Lord promises through Jeremiah includes His people seeking
Him, Then you will call upon me and come and pray to me, and I will listen to you. You
will seek me and find me when you seek me with all your heart. (Jeremiah 29:12-13).
From this, we draw a conclusion that is crucial for the PCA: a spiritually vibrant
future requires our seeking and calling upon the Lord with our whole heart

<a id="ga26-p425"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=427 printed_page=425 -->

Consider the great mission field that North America represents. Church growth
statisticians estimate that in 1900 there were 29 churches for every 10,000 people in the
Untied States. By 1950, the number of churches had shrunk to 17. Today, it is
estimated that there are only about 10 churches per 10,000 people. This suggests that
even during the brief 25-year lifetime of the PCA, the presence of the church in north
America has declined in measurable numbers.
There is only one way to reach North America for Christ. That one way is through a
renewal of ourselves in calling upon “the Lord of the harvest. . .to send out workers into
His harvest field.” (Matthew 9:38). While only the Lord can send out the workers, He
chooses to do this work through us. Hence the rhetorical question of Romans 10:15 is a
call to action for the Presbyterian Church in America as we look toward the next 25
years which we anticipate the Lord, by His grace, will give us, “And how can they
preach unless they are sent?”
The challenges are daunting. However, we expect to see the Lord at work in ever
greater measure as we wait on Him as partners in the ministry of the Gospel, reaching
North America for Christ. what is required as we wait upon the lord of the Harvest?
Prayer: only the Lord can send forth the laborers into the harvest fields. Ministers:
Core groups and unreached cities await church planters. The Armed Forces are calling
for additional PCA chaplains. New opportunities for campus ministry abound.
Financial support: presbyteries, churches and individual church members of the PCA
continue to give increasingly to all MNA ministries. However, each ministry also faces
significant challenges — that is, opportunities for the advancement of the Gospel that
could be seized if additional financial resources were available.
1997 was a year of great blessing in the Lord’s work through MNA. While more detail
for each ministry is given below, we summarize here a few highlights from each:
Church Planting: The great challenge here continues to be that of sending out
additional men. church planters are needed fora number of co regroups an dun reached
cities, some of whom have been awaiting a church planter for several years.
Multicultural Church Planting. The international mission field is indeed among us.
Reaching new immigrants and other long-established minority groups in North America
continues to grow as a priority for the PCA. The great need is for qualified church
planters.
Campus Ministries. Thank the Lord for the growing vision to begin Reformed
University Ministries on new campuses. Pray for campus ministers who are effective in
evangelism and in ministering to covenant children during their college years.

<a id="ga26-p426"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=428 printed_page=426 -->

Urban and Mercy Ministries: Growing numbers of PCA churches are establishing new
urban and mercy ministries, providing opportunity for many PCA members to become
involved in ministry Skilled ministry leadership is the greatest need
Church Vitality: thank the lord fora growing recognition of need for renewal among
PCA churches. Pray that Church Vitality leadership can be multiplied, so that more
churches can be led into a greater vision for ministry.
Chaplain Ministries: Despite the downsizing of the military, all of the armed forces
are calling for more PCA chaplains. Pray for men who can fill the 12 or more chaplain
positions that could be filled by PCA teaching elders during 1998.
We remind the 26th General Assembly of the MNA Vision Statement, with its emphasis
on establishing reproducing churches and presbyteries:

Joyfully depending on our sovereign God, the persistent prayers of God’s people,
a practice of biblical stewardship of time, talent and treasure, we will work
in cooperation with other denominational committees and agencies
to fill the United States and Canada with
reproducing churches and presbyteries.
• These will be churches that are Christ-centered, spiritually healthy, worshipping,
evangelizing theologically sound, culturally relevant, Presbyterian churches of all
sizes— small, medium and large.
• These will be churches and presbyteries that are faithful messengers, dependent on
the Holy Spirit to bring the life-changing power of the gospel of jesus christ to
those living in rural, suburban and urban areas, transforming every arena of life and
culture through preaching grace, doing justice and showing mercy.
• These will be churches and presbyteries that incorporate the varied ethnic, cultural,
socio-economic and language groups throughout the region by cultivating
reconciliation and planting churches.
• these will be churches and presbyteries that serve the whole church of jesus christ
through propagation of campus ministries, chaplain ministries, and mercy
ministries, preaching the gospel and spreading rich biblical teaching throughout
North America.
Our concluding challenge is that we seek the Lord together — that we seek Him
together in reaching North America for Christ. For this to occur, the Lord will have to
send forth the laborers. May we renew our commitment to wait upon Him for those
laborers, and to join together as senders — people of God who wait with expectation for
another 25 years of growth in reaching north america with the Gospel.

<a id="ga26-p427"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=429 printed_page=427 -->

II MINISTRIES
A. Church Planting
In our short 25-year history, the PCA has planted many churches, by God’s grace 505
or 40% of all PCA churches have been organized since the beginning of the
denomination. We rejoice in this, yet we realize we have much more to do!
Our greatest challenges are recruiting new church planters (our most precious
resource!), coaching planters on the field, and “revolving” funding through the church
plants themselves. As of January 1, 1999, MNA will have the direct responsibility of
administrating the Assessment Center and the regional church planter training
conferences, roles formerly carried out by the International Church Planting Center.
In 1995, 61 church planters were sent out by the PCA. In 1996, 53 church planters were
sent out. Approximately 50 church planters were sent out in 1997.
MNA plans to expand church planting support services to the PCA When funds
permit, and the right person is identified, an Associate Coordinator of Church Planting
will be added to the staff This will enable MNA Church Planting to:
• initiate a more effective recruiting system,
• initiate a real coaching system, using experienced church planters themselves,
• oversee the church planting “revolving fund” promotion and repayment.
Recruitment of church planters is beginning to show progress. Over 20 couples are in
progress for 25 strategic sites (core groups and funding in place).
The church planter “revolving fund” has continued to grow from $20,000 per year in
1994, $40,000 in 1995, $60,000 in 1996 and $97,000 in 1997. Twenty more church
planters have committed to give toward the revolving fund. We are hopeful that
$150,000 can be given toward this fund in 1998.
Our vision continues with mobilizing regional church planting by enabling regional
coordinators. In addition to serving busy pastorates and presbytery responsibilities,
these men are coordinating church planting efforts in their regions. Regional meetings
and conferences are now being held throughout North America. Regional Coordinators
are in place for Western Canada (TE Evan Bottomley), Eastern Canada (TE Stephen
Christian), the West (TE Lewis Ruff), the Southeast (TE Gary Cox), the Northeast (TE
Ken Smith), Texas (TE Jim Bland), and the Midwest (TE Ted Powers). We thank TE
Andy Silman for his service as Mid-South Regional Coordinator, and pray for a
successor for this region.

<a id="ga26-p428"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=430 printed_page=428 -->

Most of our presbyteries continue to use the assessment services provided by MNA
through the International Church Planting Center The International Church Planting
Center has served GA MNA with helpful excellence. In 1997, 42 PCA couples were
assessed. Thirty-six PCA couples were qualified or provisionally qualified for church
planting, with an additional four pre-assessed couples attending Church Planter
Orientation. In 1998, 22 couples attended our February 1998 Assessment Center and
Church Planter Orientation. The following are comments from the candidates’
evaluation:
“have grown during this time; wonderful experience; stretching and
sanctifying time ”
“helped a lot to evaluate myself; character reflection ”
“allowed valuable time with my spouse ”
“thank you for being thorough honest and not afraid of what you might
find and willing to use information in the best interest of all concerned”
MNA also offers training services to any church planter, including guided learning
contracts that are geared to enhance the church planter's skills in areas in which he feels
the greatest need. Both recruiting and training services were expanded in 1997. In
1997, the first Church Planter Readiness Seminar was held at Covenant Seminary with a
total of 16 couples in attendance. The Church Planter Bootcamp will be launched in
1998. (Please see next page for an overview of these new training and recruiting
services for church planters in the “Church Planter Development System”). The 1998
church planter training conferences will be held regionally in the West, Southeast
(including Mid-South, and Northeast), Western Canada and Midwest.
Multiply, and MNA Intercessor, have fueled church multiplication very effectively. Our
Regional Coordinators have worked hard to stimulate local communication of the Great
Commission. The MNA Committee has always taken the servant-leader role in helping
MNA Staff.
Church planters supported by MNA during 1997 will be found as attachment b the
Assembly is asked to note that this list does not include the large number of church
planters fielded by local churches or presbyteries with no GA/MNA financial
assistance. Because presbyteries and churches are increasingly active in starting new
churches, the actual PCA church planting force in North America is substantially larger
than this list.

<a id="ga26-p429"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=431 printed_page=429 -->

B Multicultural Church Planting
Purpose and Strategy
The purpose of MNA Multicultural Church Planting is to encourage, facilitate, and
initiate the reformed church planting movements among major cultural/ethnic groups in
North America. The focus of these efforts is to initiate vital congregations that
demonstrate an indigenous expression of the reformed faith. The expectation is that
these vital “resource” churches will in turn be able and desirous of starting new
congregations.
Our primary efforts are directed toward major cultural/ethnic groups including African
Americans, Brazilians, Haitians, Hispanics, and Koreans. Less extensive efforts are
focused on French Canadians (Quebec), Filipinos (Chicago), Japanese (Atlanta),
Russian Jews (New York), and West Indians (Florida).
MNA considers a major ethnic group one with a large enough population to start many
new churches and holds the potential for a church multiplication movement. Factors in
determining which people group MNA will enter include: size of population, receptivity
to the Gospel, spiritual and felt needs we can meet, a qualified church planter from the
same people group with an inner call and burden for his people, and partner churches.
MNA starts first generation churches in the language of the immigrant people group.
MNA also starts second generation churches who use English as the common language
and are usually more multicultural.
Advance in 1997
During the past year we rejoice in God’s good hand upon our efforts:
1. Qualified church planting leaders from various ethnic groups continue to be
evaluated and placed. Since the last General Assembly, MNA assisted presbyteries in
placing the following church planters: Carlos Ireta (South Texas Presbytery) in Laredo,
Texas, Peter Padro (Metro NYC Presbytery) in Manhattan
2. In November 1997, over 30 PCA leaders ministering in multicultural contexts met in
Atlanta to discuss and pray about God’s challenge to reach the large diverse groups that
now compose the American mosaic. Four expectations for the conference were
established, (a) to underscore the compelling vision for reaching major ethnic groups in
North America with the Gospel; (b) to design specific strategies addressing major
obstacles related to ethnic church planting, (c) to envision creative approaches to
provide theological and practical training for ethnic leaders; (d) to make specific
recommendations to the GA MNA Committee.

<a id="ga26-p431"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=433 printed_page=431 -->

Five themes with corresponding issues were highlighted by the group as a continuing
focus for study: context (understanding ethnicity; newness and foreign ne ss of us
culture); strategy (prayer for the lost, differences between first and second generations,
leaders, starting core groups, facilities, time frame), leadership (recruitment, meeting
credentialing standards, optional training approaches, finding visionary movement
leaders); finance (funding church planters and movement leaders, funding training of
leaders, buildings and worship facilities); structure (PCA listen to/learn from ethnic
group, recruiting ethnic MNA staff, flexible approaches to church planting).
Recommendations flowing from the group include the following: (1) that we reaffirm
our commitment as a denomination to ethnic church planting; (2) that we bring leaders
from several existing presbyteries with a large multi-ethnic population to design ways to
intentionally become multicultural; also, to identify major cities to plant new
presbyteries which would begin day one with a multicultural makeup; (3) that we train
new ethnic leaders by providing ministry involvement and ministry training on the local
church and regional church levels as a process to credentialing; that MNA encourage
the starting of centers for theological and ministry training providing materials and
learning models, and (4) that each PCA church designate a percentage of its giving to
ethnic or multicultural church planting.
The study conference concluded with thanksgiving to God for His work among ethnic
populations in North America. Six signs of God’s blessing were listed: (1) God raising
up movement leaders among the Haitian, Brazilian, Asian, and African American
people, (2) the beginning of the first multicultural presbytery in the PCA; namely, New
York Metro, (3) growth of the korean church planting movement; (4) networks within
the PCA that are developing on-site educational and church planter training, (5)
increase of ethnic enrollment at reformed seminaries, and (6) existing churches
diversifying to reach various ethnic groups.
3. The following persons have been assigned specific responsibilities within the MNA
structure: TE Henry Koh, Coordinator of Korean Ministries; TE Louis Wilson,
Movement Leader for African American Ministries; TE Dony St. Germain, Movement
Leader for Haitian Ministries; TE Randy Nabors, Movement Leader for Urban and
Mercy Ministries. Henry Koh will serve full-time as soon as sufficient support is
raised. The others serve in the Movement Leader role on a limited basis and continue to
serve as senior pastors of the irrespective churches.
4. refer to attachment c for the list of MNA missionaries and church planters.

C. Thanksgiving Ministries — Urban and Mercy
“For as the soil makes the sprout come up and a garden causes seeds to grow, so the
Sovereign Lord will make righteousness and praise spring up before all nations ”
These words from Isaiah 61:11 describe the effect of the Spirit’s work as the Gospel is

<a id="ga26-p432"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=434 printed_page=432 -->

preached and received The preaching of the Gospel through PCA congregations is
increasingly accompanied by deeds of mercy, when the audience includes those who
live with great physical and economic needs, in addition to spiritual needs.
MNA Urban and Mercy Ministries’ purpose is to coordinate and encourage the growth
of ministry in addressing the unique needs of our great North America urban centers, as
well as to encourage mercy ministries. three areas of emphasis are ongoing:
1 Building the network of communication and resources: Each year, MNA
sponsors a gathering of PCA members at the annual Christian Community
Development Association (CCDA) conference, the purpose of the MNA
meeting is to bring PCA members together for encouragement and to discover
increasing opportunities for developing urban and mercy ministries in the PCA.
In 1997, at least 175 from PCA related ministries attended the CCDA
conference.
2. Increasing the visibility of urban and mercy ministries in the PCA:
• MNA sponsors a dinner during the General Assembly, at which time reports are
given by some of the urban and mercy ministries of the PCA. The dinner is
open, by advance reservation and until the limit of the facilities is reached, to
any commissioners and guests of the Assembly who wish to attend. We express
our gratitude to the members of new city fellowship in St. Louis for providing
this year’s program.
• A directory of PCA urban and mercy ministries is available on request. This
directory is a valuable resource for any PCA church who is involved in urban
and mercy ministries, or who wishes to begin such ministries in their
community. PCA churches and presbyteries are encouraged to avail themselves
of the great wealth of experience that already exists in the PCA in urban and
mercy ministries as they seek to develop or increase ministry in their own
communities.
3. Leadership development: The Thanksgiving Offering continues to be a highly
effective means of encouraging the development of urban and mercy leadership
in the PCA. During 1996 and 1997, funds from this offering were used to assist
18 ministries in leadership training. Primarily, the funds are used to sponsor
internships for new staff members, seminary students or college students
working in urban and mercy ministries. The great value of this offering is that it
enables local churches to increase their ability to train leaders, thus
strengthening the local ministry. By the end of February 1998, more than
$42,000 had been received in response to the 1997 Thanksgiving Offering
appeal.

<a id="ga26-p433"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=435 printed_page=433 -->

A special highlight during 1997 was the receipt of an anonymous one-time gift of $25,000 for mercy ministries, given by a PCA family through their church. This gift is being used to assist PCA ministries working with families making the transition from welfare to gainful employment. Given to several ministries within the PCA, the funds are used in ways that directly assist the families, rather than for organizational support.

Beginning with the 11th General Assembly in 1983, Bethany Christian Services has been endorsed by the PCA, upon MNA’s annual review and recommendation. We note with gratitude the services of Bethany to thousands of Christian families throughout its 54 years of service, and commend Bethany to the PCA for continued involvement and support. Their report is found as Attachment D.

D. Church Vitality

The purpose of MNA Church Vitality is to prepare the PCA for God-sent revival and to be an instrument for awakening, by enabling local church leaders to properly use spiritual disciplines for personal renewal and to use the means of grace for church vitality resulting in multiplication of new Christians and churches. By grace through faith we watch for revival as we work for more vital churches!

The interest in revival and the conviction that God may soon bring great awakening to the world is growing! Presbyteries are scheduling days of prayer and fasting for their teaching elders. Many local church leaders are encouraging their people to watch for revival by banding together in small groups for prayer, mutual encouragement, study of God’s Word, and classic writings on revival. Key Covenant Teams can be used for this purpose. The Key Covenant is a solemn agreement built around the acrostic TEAMS (T for Truth, E for Equip, A for Accountability, M for Mission, and S for Supplication).

Basic to all Church Vitality efforts is the annual Embers to a Flame Conference at Christ Covenant Presbyterian Church, Matthews, NC. This Conference provides basic and advanced levels of training in biblical principles of vitality that are of great value to all leaders, both teaching elders and ruling elders.

- Paul declares that the first priority for vital churches is prayer (See I Tim. 2:1-2). Church vitality begins with praying leaders and churches that are filled with people of prayer. Based on surveys, it is estimated that in most PCA churches, less than 10% pray daily for the salvation of specific unbelievers. Less than 1% pray weekly for the workers needed to do the work of the ministry. And less than 10% pray daily for their official leaders. The Armorbearer Intercessory Ministry Schools of Prayer deal with the context, definition, focus and practice of prayer. The purpose of these events is to mobilize whole congregations, starting with their leaders, to concentrate their prayer energies on the strategically important targets of the leaders, the captives, and the workers. In 1997, in the ministry of MNA Church Vitality, we saw the following results:

<a id="ga26-p434"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=436 printed_page=434 -->

• Armorbearer’s Schools of Prayer were introduced to 231 churches.
• 921 people attended 13 Armorbearer’s Schools of Prayer
• 2,182 committed to pray for their pastors
• 461 were introduced to Key Covenant Teams
• 4,000 copies of The Hidden Life of Prayer were provided for pastors leading a
Day of Prayer and Fasting

E Campus Ministries
The purpose of Mission to North America's campus ministry (Reformed University
Ministries), "Reaching students for Christ, equipping students to serve", is carried out
by the Church, and in the context of the Church, the living organism commissioned by
Christ to build His Kingdom on earth. Reformed University Ministries is building the
church for today and for tomorrow. The staff of Reformed University Ministries are
breaking ground on the secular culture of college and university campuses through
student-led ministries that reach out to other students. These ministries work to
accomplish their goals in four major areas: Evangelism and Missions, Growth in Grace,
Fellowship and Service, and a Biblical World-and-Life View.
Through local Reformed University Fellowship (RUF) groups, students are reached
with the Gospel and equipped for ministry, not only on the campus, but in the church
and society.
In 1997, new ministries began at Baylor University, Belmont University, Harvard
University, New York University, the university of California-Santa Barbara, and the
University of South Carolina. This brings the total of Reformed University Fellowship
groups to thirty-eight in seventeen states. Plans are being laid to add several more
RUF's in 1998.
The work of campus interns makes possible a much broader outreach on campuses. In
this “learning/ministry” position, young men and women work directly under a campus
minister, receiving on-the-job training in evangelism, small group leadership and oneto-one ministry. a planned program of biblical and theological training accompanies
this practical service. After their internship with Reformed University Ministries,
interns disperse into many walks of life, carrying to their callings, churches, and
families their deepened understanding of God’s Word.
Weekly large group meetings, small groups for study, prayer and fellowship, and oneto-one staff-student sessions provide the framework for campus ministry. Through
these, students are fed a steady diet of Bible exposition, examination and discussion of
Scripture, sharing of concerns, prayer, fellowship, and many opportunities for in-depth
discipling, evangelistic encounters and the building of trust-confidence relationships.

<a id="ga26-p435"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=437 printed_page=435 -->

One-to-one sessions between students and staff members offer in-depth discipling of
Christians, evangelistic encounters, and the building of trust relationships each of the
three parts of this structure supports and strengthens the others.
Spring Break cross-cultural projects are other avenues for learning and ministry
experience Inner-city mission projects in Chattanooga, New Orleans and New York
City challenged students to expand their view of God's world and rethink their own
cultural assumptions about their faith. As they work with missionaries and local church
members, they learn to minister in Christ's name.
Reformed University Fellowship groups offer fall and spring statewide conferences
providing an opportunity for students to disengage from the "busy-ness" of campus life,
reflect upon life, deal with questions, and be taught from God's Word
If the church is to impact the culture of our day, its members must understand how to
apply biblical truth to every area of their personal lives, beginning with their thoughts
and extending into every aspect of their actions one of the major goals of reformed
university ministries is to help students develop a view of life which relates god and
the Bible to all aspects of life, or to develop a biblical world-and-life view. In keeping
with this purpose, on February 21-23 in Memphis, TN, Reformed University Ministries
hosted over 800 people at Life VIEW '97.
Dr Jerram Barrs, Professor of Christian Studies and Contemporary Culture at Covenant
Theological Seminary, was the featured speaker at this year's conference. Using Paul as
an example, Dr. Barrs spoke on how we must communicate truth to an unbelieving,
postmodern generation He exhorted the students to think about how they would make
biblical truth known. Evangelism means having patience and love for the lost because
they a remade in the image of God. He gave insight and hope to those who wish to
reach friends and loved ones for Christ.
One student commented, "I now know that throwing stones from a distance, or talking
at people is not biblical evangelism. Jerram Barrs really encouraged me to have a
more loving and humble attitude toward non-Christians."
An annual highlight is the Summer Conference held near Panama City, Florida.
Reformed University Ministries' seventeenth annual Summer Conference was May 12-
16 Students and staff from Reformed University Fellowship groups across the nation
gathered for a week at the beach to study God's Word, pray, attend seminars and
fellowship with friends Many also found time for swimming, basketball, volleyball,
long walks and naps! For students, the bigger picture emerges of a ministry that
includes their peers in the body of christ from other parts of the country.
TE Scot Sherman, pastor of The Village Church in Greenwich Village, NYC, New
York, spoke each morning on "Sanctification: Growing in Relationship." In the
evenings, TE John Hall, senior pastor of trinity presbyterian church in Charlottesville,

<a id="ga26-p436"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=438 printed_page=436 -->

VA, spoke on "The Gospel According to Hosea: God's Passionate Love." TE David Sinclair, former campus minister at Clemson University, closed our conference Friday evening by speaking on "Grace Alone."

These student comments will give you a glimpse into what they experienced during the week:

I couldn't believe that Scot Sherman and John Hall were so willing to "hold court" and answer so many of our questions. One day, there were about thirty of us crowded around a porch while Scot and John responded to questions and talked. Wow!

The teaching was excellent! We couldn't have asked for better! The seminars helped answer so many of my questions.

What an awesome way to start my summer — great Bible teaching, discussion, singing, fellowship and food!

Reformed University Ministries is equipping Christians for service in God's world and in His church. Approximately a third or more of the students involved in RUF are converted during their college years. Avery high percentage of these students eventually join PCA churches.

## International Students Christian Fellowship

Reformed University Ministries is reaching out to "all nations," sharing the Gospel of Christ and training Christian nationals to serve Him all over the globe through International Students Christian Fellowship (ISCF). Our missionaries are privileged to introduce internationals to Christ. Many of these return to their own countries to build the Church there. One of their goals is to help Christian internationals struggle with how to live for Christ in their own cultures.

Refer to Attachment E for a listing of presbyteries and their affiliated committees, campus staff and location of ministries; and the complete 1998 budget for Reformed University Ministries (MNA Campus Ministries and the affiliated presbytery campus committees) provided for information.

## F. Chaplain Ministries

One of the significant mission fields afforded to the Presbyterian Church in America comes through the chaplaincy: in the military (active and reserve duty), in the Veteran's Administration (VA) hospitals, in the Federal and State Prisons, in civilian hospitals that require denominational endorsement, in the Civil Air Patrol, and in retirement centers and in various other institutions that sponsor chaplain ministry. (See Attachment F for PCA Chaplain Roster)

<a id="ga26-p437"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=439 printed_page=437 -->

God has providentially and graciously provided 179 chaplains for the PCA to endorse
Categorical breakdown is as follows:
Active Duty Army 31 Active Duty navy 22 Active Duty Air Force 5
Army Reserve 21 Navy Reserve 7 Air Force reserve 3
Army National Guard 18 Merchant Marine 2 Air Force Applicants 2
Army Applicants (AD*) 2
Army Chaplain Candidates 5 Navy Chaplain Candidates 4 AF Chaplain Candidates 8
Army Applicants 10 Navy Applicants 2 Air Force Applicants 1
Civil Air Patrol 8 VA (full time) 2 Medical Centers 6
VA (part time) 6
VA applicants 2
Retirement Centers 3
Federal/State Prison 2 Police Department 5 Hospice care 2
♦Active Duty
The MNA Coordinator of Chaplain Ministries also serves as the Executive Director of
the Presbyterian and Reformed Joint Commission on Chaplains and Military Personnel
(PRJC). Primary duties include providing ecclesiastical endorsement and recruiting
chaplains for the following denominations: Orthodox Presbyterian Church (OPC),
Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America (RPCNA), Korean American
Presbyterian Church (KAPC), and Korean Presbyterian Church of America (KPCA)
There is a need to recruit many additional chaplains. Although a specific number has
not been given, all three military branches have requested additional chaplains from the
PCA Also, the federal prison chief of chaplains has requested additional PCA
chaplains The Veterans Administration need for additional chaplains has decreased
due to downsizing, although in some locations they are recruiting. There is greater
opportunity for VA part-time “contract” chaplains. The Director of Market Place
Ministries has requested PCA chaplains for the Industrial/Corporation Chaplaincy.
The greatest challenges for MNA Chaplain Ministries are: (1) to enlist 120 prayer
supporters for all military active duty chaplains, VA (full-time) chaplains, Federal/State
prison chaplains, and other chaplains who are not directly involved with a PCA
congregation, (2) to recruit additional chaplains, and (3) to raise financial support for
MNA Chaplain Ministries, which is supported entirely by designated giving and not by
the Partnership Share/Askings.
To enlist 120 prayer supporters for our chaplains we are requesting PCA churches to
sponsor one of our chaplains. The program is similar to the manner in which
congregations support Mission to the World (MTW) missionaries with the exception
that financial support for chaplains is not required. Think of it! More than 150
chaplains at no cost to the denomination.
There is a great need to pray for our chaplains. In most cases they are ministering in an
environment that is non-Christian and very secular, yet very open to the teaching and
preaching of the Gospel So often they labor alone and in difficult surroundings. For

<a id="ga26-p438"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=440 printed_page=438 -->

example, military chaplains often minister during field training. They live in tents, do
physical training with the troops, and perform under the same harsh environment as the
other military personnel In today’s military an abnormal amount of time is spent away
from the family Service members stationed in Europe, for example, spent an average
of 129 days away from their families during 1997.
During a recent trip to Europe to visit PCA chaplains, the Chaplain Ministries
Coordinator met soldiers who were departing for their third rotation to Bosnia. At the
time of this writing, several PCA chaplains have been alerted for duty in Southeast
Asia. Prison and hospital chaplains, like many other institutional chaplains are often
overwhelmed with counselees who seek out the chaplain as a last resort. Much of their
work takes place after normal duty hours. They need prayer! If your congregation
would like to sponsor a chaplain, please notify the Chaplain Ministries Coordinator.
Sponsored chaplains will provide you with timely updates and specific prayer requests
To recruit additional chaplains, the Coordinator provides information to presbyteries,
churches and seminaries Presbytery MNA Committees could assist the Coordinator by
informing Teaching Elders of the opportunities to serve in the chaplaincy. One of the
ways this could be accomplished would be to appoint a representative on MNA
Committees to represent Chaplain Ministries
The minimum annual budget for Chaplain Ministries is $110,000 which includes
Coordinator salary and travel, printing, administration, and telephone expenses To
meet the budget demands, all PCA congregations are requested to include Chaplain
Ministries in their annual budget. Please be aware that Chaplain Ministries is not
supported by MNA Partnership Share/Askings.
PROJECT VIDEO: One of the greatest needs of Chaplain Ministries is to inform the
denomination of the extensive ministry performed by chaplains. One of the most
effective ways to meet this challenge is through publication of a video. The video will
not only increase denominational awareness of our chaplains and their ministries, but it
will also greatly assist in enlisting prayer and financial support Thank the Lord that an
individual has graciously offered to underwrite the cost of the video.
CHAPLAIN GUARDIAN CORPS: The Chaplain Guardian Corps (CGC) was formed
to increase prayer for chaplains and financial support for MNA Chaplain Ministries
the guardian prayer calendar is published quarterly and is provided to all cgc
members. Teaching Elders are requested to assist in enlisting membership in the CGC
by informing members of their congregation of this opportunity.
There is an overwhelming need for reformed Chaplains. The opportunity for
evangelism is great. The need to pastor and encourage Christians is of utmost
importance Every quarter chaplains are required to send in reports to their respective
presbyteries and MNA. Often they inform us of soldiers, family members, patients,
prisoners, and young people coming to a saving knowledge of Jesus Christ. They tell of

<a id="ga26-p439"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=441 printed_page=439 -->

the need for an evangelical witness and the endless opportunities for ministry. It is a
field of ministry that we as a denomination are privileged to share.
There is a great need to visit, encourage and pastor our chaplains to keep the connection
between the denomination and the chaplain alive. The most obvious need is to increase
the involvement of the denominational membership in this ministry and in the lives of
chaplains and their families.
Ill RECOMMENDATIONS
1 That the General Assembly commend and thank TE Cortez A Cooper, Jr., for his
leadership as MNA Coordinator, and re-elect him for another year; and further, as
TE Cooper plans for retirement from MNA at the time of the 27th GA in 1999, that
the Assembly pray for the Transition/Search Committee appointed to identify a
candidate for Coordinator, to be recommended by the MNA Committee to the 27th
GA
2. that the General Assembly express its gratitude to god for the staff of mission to
North America and for their faithful and effective work.

3 That the General Assembly thank God for the increasing church planting that is

interns. Further, that the General Assembly encourage faithful prayer that the Lord
will send additional laborers through MNA and Reformed University Ministries into
the fields that are ripe for harvest in North America.
4. that PCA churches be reminded of their covenant obligation to give high priority to
giving MNA and all other PCA Committees and Agency Partnership Share/Askings,
so that the Gospel is advanced in greater measure.
5. That the General Assembly express its gratitude to the Lord for a Thanksgiving
Offering of more than $42,000, and commend to the churches of the PCA the
opportunity to support Urban and Mercy Ministries, preferably through an offering
taken during the Thanksgiving season, and further, that churches consider how they
may directly participate in greater measure in Urban and Mercy Ministries.
6. That PCA churches be encouraged to fully support and provide exposure to the
1998 WIC Love Gift video, which specifically supports multicultural church
leadership and development.
7. That the General Assembly express thanks to God for the long and effective
ministry of bethany christian services in the area of pregnancy counseling and
adoption, encourage continued support by churches and presbyteries, and invite the
Bethany representative to speak to the Assembly for five minutes at this, or a more
opportune, time.
8. That the General Assembly commend to the churches these resources and
opportunities for ministry available through MNA:
• The opportunity to seek the Lord’s leading in church planting, campus
ministry or the chaplaincy.

<a id="ga26-p441"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=443 printed_page=441 -->

• The opportunity to support church planting (Anglo and Multicultural),
campus ministries, church vitality, chaplain ministries (especially through
THE CHAPLAIN GUARDIAN CORPS), regional coordinators and
movement leaders, financially (in addition to Partnership Share/Askings
participation) and in prayer
• The opportunity to build relationships of fellowship and ministry with
Korean churches and other ethnic groups within the PCA, as well as with
other true believers in our local communities.
• Utilize the resources of Church Vitality materials and consultation services
• Recommend to church members that they subscribe (at no cost) to MNA
INTECESSOR AND MULTIPLY

9. That Overture #10 from the Northern California Presbytery requesting a change in
its boundaries to include the State of Utah and additional counties in Nevada be
approved if the way be clear, [see p. 31]
10. That Overture #7 from central florida presbytery requesting division of the
presbytery and the formation of the North Florida Presbytery be approved, [see p.
29]
11 Whereas, the Lord has blessed the PCA with a growing zeal for planting churches,
reflected in greater annual numbers of church planters sent out and churches
organized;

Whereas, for many church planting situations, the church planter is well known and
churches and individuals commit readily to his support; on the other hand, there are
a growing number of church planting opportunities in which there is a need to have
a church planting fund from which to draw;

Whereas, annual church-wide offerings have been very effective in many
denominations as a means of providing support for the extension of the church;

Therefore, be it resolved that the 26th General Assembly approve the collection of an
annual offering for MNA Church Planting on Mother’s Day, or another suitable day,
and commend participation in this annual offering to the churches, and that churches
be encouraged to observe this day, or another suitable day, as a day of prayer for
Church Planting in North America.
12. That Overtures 17, 18 and 33 ( M24GA , 24-31, III, 7. P. 196) be answered by the
following:
that reformed university ministries continue its ministry as a component of ga
MNA and not as anew permanent committee of the ga at this time.

<a id="ga26-p442"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=444 printed_page=442 -->

That MNA Campus Ministries be directed to give highest priority to staffing needs
at the national and regional levels, with a view toward establishment as anew
permanent committee of GA.
That MNA be directed to continue the Campus Ministries Study Committee to
evaluate the feasibility of such new permanent committee status with a report and
recommendation made no later than the 29th GA.
13 That the General Assembly note that the entire 1999 budget of MNA campus
Ministries, as well as that of the affiliated campus ministries, is provided to the
General Assembly for its information, and further, that the Assembly note with
thanksgiving the opportunities and challenges represented by the entire 1998
budget.
14. That the General Assembly adopt the 1999 MNA budget and commend such to the
churches for their support.

Respectfully Submitted,
Committee on Mission to North America
