SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT
OF THE COMMITTEE ON MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA
TO THE
26™ GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE PCA

The Committee on Mission to North America submits the following recommendations
as a Supplement to the Report of the Committee on MNA to the 26th General Assembly:
RECOMMENDATIONS:
15. That in response to the exceptions taken to the MNA Minutes of June 1997, as
reported by the Committee of Commissioners for the Committee on Mission to
North America at the 25th General Assembly, the MNA Committee submits the
following:
Exception a
At several points (10-96-24 and 10-45 and 47) a teaching elder’s status is
changed such changes in terms of call need to be approved by the man’s
presbytery. This follows from BCO 20-1 and the precedent of the 23rd GA with
regard to the changes in terms of call for missionaries. there is no evidence in
the minutes of such involvement on the part of the relevant presbyteries. This is
especially serious in 10-96-45 where MNA “will dissolve (a TE’s) pastoral
relationship immediately” if a support deficit continues The TE’s presbytery
must concur in such a dissolution (BCO 23-l,cf.20-l).
the MNA committee is aware that all actions affecting the call of a
teaching elder must be approved by his presbytery , and the MNA
committee operates within that understanding . in the cases cited ,
there is simply a failure to note this understanding explicitly in the
minutes . in the future , such motions will include appropriate
language , such as “p ending presbytery approval , ” or " with the
concurrence of the presbytery . ”

Exception b.
while the minutes of the committee reflect the adoption of the various
recommendations to General Assembly, there is no evidence in the minutes that
the Committee ever saw or approved the “Report of the Committee on Mission
to North America,” nor that they authorized anyone else to write or approve the
report. If it is a committee report it needs to be adopted by the Committee
(Robert’s Rule 50).

<a id="ga26-p444"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=446 printed_page=444 -->

Omission of approval of the full MNA Report to the 25th GA was a clerical error in the recording of the minutes. MNA March 1997 committee meeting agenda materials include a printed recommendation for review and approval of the GA Report by the MNA Committee. Several MNA staff members distinctly remember a motion for approval of the GA Report being passed. In the process of producing the minutes this motion was somehow omitted. Greater care will be taken in the future to prevent this error.

## Exception c.

The Committee has one serving as Secretary who is not a member of the Committee. Grounds: RAO 4-10.a. requires that the chairman of a subcommittee must be a member of the permanent committee. It is irregular that a person not elected by the GA to serve on the MNA Committee is serving as an officer of the Committee. RAO 4-16 states that the secretary is an officer of the permanent committee.

The MNA Committee makes extensive use of the provision in the RAO for using co-opted members in the work of the Committee (RAO 4-10). The secretary in question was such a co-opted member who had previously served as an elected member and who served as secretary to the Committee during the time of his elected term. In view of his faithful and efficient service as secretary, the Committee continued to elect him to this post, beginning several years ago, even though he was a co-opted member. For reasons unknown, no one on the MNA Committee or previous Committees of Commissioners questioned this practice. The MNA Committee acknowledges its error, and will take care to make sure that the Committee elects its officers only from among the elected members.

16. That Overture 16 from Central Georgia Presbytery, “Deny Enlarging Northern California Presbytery” be answered by referring to Recommendation 9 of the MNA Report to the 26th General Assembly.

17. That the General Assembly answer Overture 17 from Central Georgia Presbytery, “Investigate Worship Practices in Mission Churches in Utah”, as follows:

That Overture 17 be remanded to Central Georgia Presbytery for their reconsideration.

**Rationale:** (to be recorded in the minutes of the GA)

1. The governance of a church planter and a mission church properly belongs to the presbytery of which each is a member, and not to the Committee on Mission to North America. The latter is not a court of the church, and therefore is not charged

<a id="ga26-p445"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=447 printed_page=445 -->

with final disposition in substantive matters of theology and
practice.
2. At the same time, as a Program Committee of the General Assembly, the
MNA Committee bears a significant level of influence and
administrative responsibility for those church planters and mission
church projects in which MNA participates For such projects,
Attachment G, “Church Essentials: The healthy PCA Church,” is the
document which describes the parameters within which a church planter,
by virtue of his written commitment, is expected to function. All MNA
Staff members also pledge their commitment to function within these
guidelines.
3. In light of items 1 and 2 above, it is questionable whether this overture is
properly before the General Assembly at all It is written in the form of
an overture, which is an administrative communication However, the
charges that it alleges amount to judicial complaints, and should be
entered in that form if they are substantive.
4 The overture contains serious allegations and questions that have never
been addressed either to Northern California Presbytery, the presbytery
of which New Song Salt Lake is a mission church, or to MNA
Committee or Staff The most appropriate course of action, therefore, is
to refer these matters directly to the parties involved Neither the spirit
nor the letter of Matthew 18 or the BCO have been followed prior to
presenting the overture
5. The General Assembly reminds members of all the church courts that
great care should be exercised that allegations of this grave nature not be
based on hearsay, and that they be addressed directly to the parties
involved prior to their being brought to higher courts This is based on
Matthew 18, from which the following are derived:
• “A complaint shall first be made to the court whose act or decision is
alleged to be in error” [BCO 43-2],
• “If, after considering a complaint, the court alleged to be delinquent or in
error is of the opinion that it has not erred, and denied the complaint, the
complainant may make the complaint to the next higher court” [BCO 43-
3].
6. The Scriptures are our final authority for faith and life The overture
contains no biblical references as the basis for its allegations and
requested actions BCO 29-1 instructs: “Nothing, therefore, ought to be
considered by any court as an offense, or admitted as a matter of
accusation, which cannot be proved to be such from Scripture.”

<a id="ga26-p446"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=448 printed_page=446 -->

18 That Overture 28 from Grace Presbytery expressing support for Overture 16
(Deny Enlarging Northern California Presbytery, and Overture 17 (Investigate
Worship Practices in Mission Churches in Utah) be answered by referring to
Recommendation 9 and 17 of the MNA Report to the 26th General Assembly.
18 That in answer to the request from the 25th GA that MNA/GA work together with
the Stated Clerk’s office to define a process for dividing or creating new
presbyteries which would include the presbyteries and the churches that will be
affected by the change in boundaries (M25GA, 25-34, III., 11, p 162), proposed
Guidelines for Dividing Presbyteries were presented to the MNA Committee at its
October 1997 meeting. The Guidelines were adopted and presented to the Stated
Clerk’s office. (See Guidelines, 26-44, ID, 17, p. 180)

ATTACHMENT D

BETHANY CHRISTIAN SERVICES
Annual Report to the 26th PCA General Assembly
One aspect of Bethany’s Annual Report to the PCA is to provide you with an overview
of our ministry for the past year and to reemphasize our appreciation for your
partnership.
It is with gratitude to the Lord that we report a very fulfilling year Bethany ministered
to over 30,000 people world wide in 1997.
• 12,300 young people and families received care through Bethany Lifeline,
our crisis pregnancy counseling hotline.
• 12,518 people with unplanned pregnancies received personal counseling at
a Bethany branch office.
• 1,075 children joined their families through adoption.
• 351 were Caucasian infants.
• 485 were children with special placement challenges.
• 239 were children born in foreign countries
•

• 856 children were cared for in Bethany foster homes while they awaited
placement with an adoptive family or return to their biological parents.
Some of these children had experienced severe abuse and needed the
temporary safety of a Christian foster family.
In addition, Bethany’s preventive and restorative ministry to families experienced
significant growth. And our outreach ministry to families and children in third world
countries also was richly blessed.
Your prayer, stewardship commitment and encouragement is what has made our
ministry possible. $1,250,000 in support was received by Bethany in gifts given by
PCA church congregations and individuals to support Bethany’s ministry.
In addition to this summary, however, we want to personalize the ministry Bethany
provides in the lives of people. Behind each number is a person who received Christian
care or participated with us as a ministry partner.
Let us share an example with you that demonstrates the ministry Bethany provided in
concert with our supporting community.

<a id="ga26-p453"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=455 printed_page=453 -->

Julie's initial contact with Bethany occurred from a phone booth at her school.
A couple of days earlier she had discovered she was pregnant. She was getting
al! kinds of advice about what to do. Some of her friends were pushing her
toward an abortion. “Get it done with quickly ” they urged her. But then there
were others who couldn't believe she would even think about abortion or for
that matter about adoption. How could she possibly kill her child or give it
away to someone else? And, to top it all off, her parents didn 't even know about
her pregnancy yet.
Julie saw the Bethany Lifeline phone number on a billboard at her school. It
had been placed there by a volunteer. When Julie called, another volunteer
answered Lifeline and spent over an hour talking with her, eventually guiding
her to a Bethany branch office for counseling.
The next eight months were the most difficult Julie and her parents ever
experience. But Christian support and guidance made a tremendous impact on
her. Eventually she decided to give her child life and chose adoptive planning
for her son. She recognized she was in no position to be a parent while she was
still in high school.
So what does this all mean for your membership? Well, all along the way, people of
your denomination were involved in ministering to Julie. She received counseling at a
Bethany office that is staffed by members of your denomination. That same office is
richly supported by your church. And, it is governed by a board which enjoys PCA
membership Most importantly, Julie’s child joined a Christian family who are
members of your denomination.
Praise the Lord that experiences like this occur every day at Bethany
Yes 1997 was a fulfilling year. But now we look ahead to 1998. With God’s blessing
and your support, we anticipate:
• an expanded commitment to finding adoptive families for children with
special placement challenges.
• new branch offices in two or three additional locations.
• increased visibility in your communities through expanded advertising
• Increased service diversification at each of our branch office locations
In John 14:12, Jesus says,
“Verily, Verily I say unto you, he that believes in me, the works that I do, he will
do also; and greater works than these shall he do because I go unto my father

<a id="ga26-p454"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=456 printed_page=454 -->

It is our joy to be your representatives as we continue the work Jesus began on earth It
is our prayer that He will continue to bless Bethany’s ministry and that He will continue
to bless the PCA through your involvement with us.
Glenn De Mots, Executive Director

<a id="ga26-p455"></a>

ATTACHMENT E
REFORMED UNIVERSITY MINISTRIES

The Committee on Mission to North America provides support services through
Reformed University Ministries-M NA to presbyteries whose campus ministries are
affiliated with Reformed University Ministries-MNA. The presbyteries receiving
services make a contribution toward their cost. Presbyteries and their affiliated
committees are completely responsible for the funding of ministries within their area
and for determining the budget for such ministries. Reformed University M inistries-
MNA receives and disburses funds only as directed by the presbyteries and their
affiliated committees.
From January 1997 to December 31, 1997, Reformed University M inistries-M NA
received $2,794,567.67 and disbursed $2,681,271.51 as directed by presbyteries and
their affiliated committees. The funds are received for particular ministries, which are
the responsibility of a presbytery as noted below. The responsible body receives an
audit report of its funds. The following list gives the presbyteries and their affiliated
committees, campus staff, and location of ministries receiving support services accounting through reformed university Ministries-M NA, and other ministries and
staff affiliated with Reformed University Ministries-MNA.

ATTACHMENT G
Church Essentials: The Healthy PCA Church 1

The following statements are an attempt to build broad, positive guidelines for what a
PCA church ought to be. There is room for creative approaches, cultural relevancies, and
different leadership styles. Each church is unique unto itself and circumstances will vary
from community to community. All churches are in the process of growth and matura
tion. This document represents an ideal description (essentials) of a church towards
which every church ought to be striving.

1. Foundations
1.1 The church is saturated with a vital growing relationship with the living God.
Evidences of the presence and power of God are clearly visible in the life of the church.
This is minimally demonstrated in the following:
• The sharing of evidences of God's blessing within the body.
• Joyful celebration of God in all His attributes through worship, prayer,
personal testimony and interaction.
1.2 The Bible is the foundation upon which the church is built and is the only infalli
ble rule of faith and practice.
This is minimally demonstrated in the following:
•The pastor’s sermons are issuing from a careful exposition of Scripture
that is communicated effectually in ways that speak clearly to the needs
of modern man.
•The people are encouraged in their personal growth and in understand
ing, knowing and applying the Scriptures to their own life and culture.
•The pastor spends time in personal devotions and study, maintaining his
knowledge of and growing in his ability to apply the Scriptures to his
own life and the lives of others.
1.3 A biblical understanding of the church saturates the life of the congregation so
that the church is committed to and emphasizes the whole work of the church. Conse
quently, there is no neglect of worship, evangelism, missions, education/discipling, fel
lowship and diaconal concern. Christ is viewed as the head of the church and the church
is viewed as central in the plan of God. There is a healthy respect for unity and diversity
within the body.
This is minimally demonstrated in the following:
• All the five functions of the church (worship, education, outreach, fellow
ship and mercy ministries) are clearly visible.
• The congregation is involved in the church at large through participation
in missions, presbytery and General Assembly activities and in signifi
cant ways demonstrates its unity with the broader Christian church.

<a id="ga26-p466"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=468 printed_page=466 -->

Church Essentials: The Healthy PCA Church 2

2. Leadership
2.1 The organizing pastor is totally committed to Christ in all of his life and reflects
that commitment personally in his family, in the church and in society.
This is minimally reflected in:
• His commitment to spend time in personal devotions.
• His commitment to an active personal prayer life.
• His commitment to leading his family in family worship.
• His commitment, to being salt and light and a catalyst for kingdom expan
sion in his community activities.
2.2 The pastor has an understanding of and is committed to the Reformed Faith as it
is taught in the Westminster Standards. He teaches and contextualizes that faith for his
people. The pastor is committed to equipping the saints to be sound in the faith, doctrinally discerning, and able to apply their faith to the issues that face today's world.
This is minimally reflected in:
• The people are being taught the doctrines of the faith over a period of
time.
• Those doctrines are presented in such a way that people understand the
implication of them for their everyday lives.
•People are guided in how to apply some of those doctrines to the issues of
today's society.
2.3 The church should be distinctly Presbyterian in its church government.
This is minimally reflected in:
•Deliberate discipleship of men in preparation for the offices of elder and
deacon in the church.
•The presence of evidences that elders are sharing in the shepherding re
sponsibilities of the church.
•The people are involved in selecting representatives who will guide their
church.
• The church is carrying out its connectional relationship by supporting
and encouraging the work of presbytery and General Assembly.
2.4 The pastor and the people are involved in biblical stewardship, which includes
supporting and promoting the church locally, denominationally and globally.
This is minimally reflected in:
• A reasonable per-capita contribution among the membership.
• Some instruction in the areas of stewardship of time, talent and finances.
• A budget that reflects commitment to others outside the local congregation.

<a id="ga26-p467"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=469 printed_page=467 -->

Church Essentials: The Healthy PCA Church 3

3. Ministry Functions
3.1 The church is committed to the necessity of biblical worship and the spiritual
growth process of its people. The church is led in vital worship that balances the vertical
and horizontal, the transcendence and eminence of God, and the private and corporate as
pects of worship.
This is minimally demonstrated by the following:
• The pastor takes ultimate responsibility for the content and atmosphere
of the worship service.
• Due to the high priority of worship in a biblical church, worship is con
ducted at the "prime time," that is, on the Lord's day.
• The people are encouraged and trained in private and family worship.
• Worship considers the "unchurched”and seeks to make that activity in
telligible in order that non-Christians may be drawn towards it.
3.2 The pastor and the church are committed to Christian education (making disci
ples) and committed to developing leaders.
This is minimally demonstrated by the following:
• An integrated plan of Christian education.
• The pastor has specific individuals that he is discipling.
• Individuals in the church are being trained in and involved in discipling
others.
• Up to date tools of communication and education are being made availa
ble to the members at large.
• Leaders are being trained and placed in significant roles.
• The church has ample small groups and mid-size groups to facilitate nur
ture, accountability and assimilation.

3.3 The church is characterized by a sense of fellowship— the people feel responsibili
ty and love for one another.
This is minimally demonstrated by the following:
• A sense of joyful interaction when the congregation meets.
• Deliberate acts to respond to the needs of others.
• A quick response to dissipate disharmony within the congregation.
3.4 The church is committed to equipping its members to be involved in local and glo
bal evangelism. The church is characterized by an enthusiasm for the extension of bibli
cal, Reformed Christianity through the PCA. It recognizes that the PCA as a whole has
an important role in changing North America and the world, just as the local church has
an important role in bringing change to its local area.
This is minimally demonstrated by the following:
• There is an integrated plan to mobilize a large percentage of the people
in some form of community outreach.

<a id="ga26-p468"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=470 printed_page=468 -->

Church Essentials: The Healthy PCA Church 4

• There is in place some system for training people in personal evangel
ism.
• People of the church are involved in proclaiming the gospel message.
• The church is involved in the support of home and foreign missions
through prayer and financial support.
• The church is effectively communicating to its members the needs in oth
er places.
• Investment of finances in the whole work and ministry of the PCA.
• Participation through finances, leadership, example, in starting addition
al PCA congregations.
3.5 The church should be characterized by compassion for the needy. Mercy minis
tries should be a vital part of the life and ministry of the church.
This is minimally demonstrated by the following:
• The leadership of the church encourages and facilitates word and deed
ministries that reflect the unique makeup of the congregation.
• The pastor is a model of ministry of compassion.
• The congregation is growing in understanding of and is increasingly in
volved in word and deed activities.
4. Purpose
4.1 The purpose of the church is to glorify God.
This is minimally demonstrated by the following:
• Christians increasingly demonstrate love in their relationships. The
one-another commands of the New Testament are lived out.
•Christians are growing in holiness, individually demonstrating Christlike-
ness.
•Many people have become aware of their spiritual gifts and are serving in
accord with their giftedness.

^
PLEDGE:
i endorse the above description of a healthy PCA congregation . a san organizing pastor
i am committed to developing a church consistent with the above and would welcome
periodic evaluation and accountability in that regard .

signature .

date _______

<a id="ga26-p469"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=471 printed_page=469 -->

* A holy people individually demonstrating christ likeness

The Church is Christ's holy, submitted people redeemed to be worshippers in community in the world to the glory of God.

2. Leadership

1. Foundations
• jesus christ , the chief cornerstone and authoritative head of the church .
• the bible , the source of truth .
• the holy spirit , the divine guide and teach e r.

• A biblical understanding of the church ( local, region a l, nation a l)

Church Planting Center, 1992.

<a id="ga26-p470"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=472 printed_page=470 -->

REPORT FROM
THE COMMITTEE ON MISSION TO THE WORLD
TO THE TWENTY-SIXTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
June 1998

Twenty-five years ago, many detractors felt the Presbyterian Church in America would
not last They predicted internal squabbles and splits, which would shatter the hope and
vision of those who started a Presbyterian denomination which held tenaciously to the
inerrancy of Scripture and orthodoxy of Reformed Theology
It has not happened The main reason, of course, is God’s protective hand on the
church He alone can guard the foundations of the church A secondarily important
reason the PCA has flourished has been our emphasis on the Great Commission
It would have been very easy in the beginning to say we have to get our new house in
order before we can have a world concern for the lost Those who laid the foundation
for the PCA knew that an ingrown church would be almost as dangerous as a liberal
church A pastor in Florida was asked by one of his members why they gave so much to
missions while they were paying such high interest on the building debt? This pastor hit
the nail on the head: We give what we do to missions so that when the building is paid
for, there will be a church in it! The church either reaches out or passes out.
Mission to the World exists only for the purpose of helping local PCA churches fulfill the
great commission some believe the age of missions is over they need to hear Jesus’
words again, and surely I am with you always, to the very end of the age. Missions is
the task of the church until Christ comes again
We want to help your church get more deeply involved Many of us feel the urgency of
building churches around the world We will help you develop or renew your missions
conference Many tell us this is the most exciting week of the year. We will introduce
you to missionaries you can support We have focus trips, which often renew a pastor’s
vision for his own calling and charge. Our MTWIMPACT short-term mission program
grew fifty percent in one year We will help your church plan a great summer adventure
in missions We can provide your church with prayer directories, which have names and
pictures of your missionaries If you or anyone in your congregation believes God may
be calling you into missions, please talk to us. LET US KNOW HOW WE CAN HELP
YOU!

<a id="ga26-p471"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=473 printed_page=471 -->

In 1997, as in the past, we had three goals to help us improve our ministry. We began a
course for our missionaries entitled Living in Christ. We developed an action plan for
the next five years. This process included all of our missionaries on one level or another
God blessed this attempt to bring ownership to our missionary force Finally, we
eliminated the debt in the missionaries’ own restricted accounts In 1997, there were
523 career missionaries, 113 two-year MTW IMPACT missionaries, 10 one-year
CoMission missionaries, and 3100 short-term summer missionaries. The breakdown of
the career missionaries is as follows:
Church-planting Urban Ministry 177
Church-planting Urban Multi-National 28
Church-planting Hinterland Ministry 17
Church-planting Partnerships 45
Church-planting Nurture 31
Subtotal
Technical and Support Cooperative Agreements 149
Theological Education 58
Leave of Absence 21
Subtotal 228
Total 523
The figures below show changes in our missionary force during the past four years:
Date Number of Missionaries Change
12/31/93 500 +4.6%
12/31/94 485 -3.0%
12/31/95 484 -02%
12/31/96 512 +5.8%
12/31/97 523 +2.1%
The 1998 Partnership Share (Askings) for MTW is $3,877,700 or $17.76 per
communicant member This represents a 9% increase in funding over 1997 and a $.95
increase in cost per member. If this figure were met, the administrative assessment to
missionaries would be reduced to zero.
following are brief reports of the activities of them tw missionaries in 1997.

<a id="ga26-p472"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=474 printed_page=472 -->

# ASIA/EUROPE HIGHLIGHTS

## EUROPE

### Bulgaria

Despite the loss of the national who was going to work with Craig Sheppard, the team has continued to move ahead and we have seen some other national leadership rise to the occasion. We're still in the initial stages, but there are some exciting things happening.

### Czech Republic

The children's ministries continue to grow and expand. God has also brought the team into contact with a very small Reformed group working not too far from Prague. They are interested in us helping them move forward. It's interesting to see the number of young people in the college/young professional age interested in getting into Bible study in English, partly because of the English, but well recognizing that it will be an English program with the Bible as its textbook.

### France

Lyon: The team has seen God's gracious intervention in opening the doors there. They have had contacts within the government who are excited about their coming to town, particularly if they are able to start some sort of school. Their opening service was very well attended. The following services still had 50-80 people attending.

Marseille: The team began a new work in the southern part of the city. God has graciously given Dr. John Frederick an opening into the medical community to begin to reach into that level of the French community.

Paris: Because of the loss of most of the personnel there, we are reconsidering what our options are and what we should be doing there. However, the church begun there, currently under Gerard Bos, continues to grow slowly but steadily. Interestingly enough, the Lord has opened doors into the community. Gerard was invited by the mayor of their small suburb of Paris to be in a workshop about life in the area where the church building is located. This is the third indication that the city not only tolerates but also appreciates having them there.

### Germany

Will Traub was given the opportunity to help a group begin to pull out of the state church and form a confessing church. Because of the change in the tax structure in Germany, the state is no longer giving as much money to this Lutheran church, thus people are starting to drop out in droves. One of the questions before us is whether this is the time for a major shift in Germany. We will see.

<a id="ga26-p473"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=475 printed_page=473 -->

Berlin: the team has begun a second work and is looking at the possibility of a third.
Meanwhile, relationships are growing and the first work is going along nicely. Their
influence and appreciation in the denomination continues to grow. Through their
personal contacts, they are regularly seeing people from a truly atheist position coming
to Christ. This is exciting in that it appears that East Germany in the only place in all of
Eastern Europe where atheism truly took root, thus making it a very difficult area to
reach.
Italy
A building was purchased to serve as the Vanguard Center. It was hoped that this would
establish credibility in the community, and in fact, the building has been in constant use,
with people coming to workshops and seminars. God continues to open the doors for
Franco to meet with men in high places and help them see what true ethics is built upon.
Portugal
The team ran an EE program and were overjoyed to see ten people receive Christ. By
American standards, this does not necessarily seem like a lot, but ina country as
hardened to the gospel as Portugal it was phenomenal and it gave the team a new boost.
They have also had one other small church become part of their Reformed fellowship.
That brings the number to six churches.
Slovakia
The contacts that God has given the Lesondaks have been unbelievable. This has
enabled them to do everything from finding a place to live very quickly to being invited
very openly by the Catholic university there to bring Christians in to lecture and have
Bible study discussion times weekly, officially supported
Spain
The church continues to see slow, but steady growth. Often it comes in the form of
reaching the women and the women bringing in their husbands. They have several men’s
groups going such as one connected with Promise Keepers, a swell as a leadership
training program. On a more personal note, they saw one drug user finally realize that
he had come to the end of everything, give his life to Christ, and is meeting twice a week
for Bible study.
Ukraine
At one point, it looked like we were going to face government restrictions, not those
faced in Russia but ones in which our men under humanitarian aid visas could not preach
and teach. The training program for teaching elders was stepped up. The men really
stepped into the gap well and the result was not loss, but a continued growth in outreach
there. Two new church works were started during the year. In addition, a small clinic
which will be nationally run, but have regular visits from U.S. doctors, was begun.

<a id="ga26-p474"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=476 printed_page=474 -->

Sensitive
Again, God continues to open doors as we work with a national pastor there Our first
three team members are on board. One is involved in language study The other two,
having already had language study, have begun helping this national pastor as they look
to start a second work there
ASIA
North Central Asia
The team there has begun a second work The first continues to grow with 80 in regular
attendance. One of our goals is finding additional team members as this work begins to
move at a faster pace
Australia
Brisbane The last church, which we still have a missionary pastoring, is in the process
of looking for and calling their first Australian pastor. Another quite interesting event
was in an unheard of move, the Presbyterian Church of Australia unanimously voted to
pass two resolutions to work cooperatively with the Westminster Presbyterian Church.
The two main areas that they see themselves working together in would be cross-cultural
outreach and theological education. It will be exciting to watch how this develops.
Sydney: The resource center once owned by MTW Limited, and given to the
Westminster Presbyterian Church, is now the home of a church pastored by an
Australian. The building is located directly across the street from one of the major sites
for the 2000 Olympics. It’s also in the center of anew housing project.
India
Grace Academy has completed one of its last building projects Presbyterian Theological
Seminary continues to reach out and train men in India. What was also exciting to see
was two of our retirees go out to teach in the seminary there. It’s great when we can
make use of this kind of experience.
Japan
Chiba: The church at Oyumino went through its first crisis with a major discipline case
Because of the face-saving nature of Japan, it was a very difficult situation for many
weeks. In the end, they followed through and understood the need for discipline, and one
man began looking at membership in the church because the church was willing to take a
stance on discipline. They have also held another church-planting workshop with nearly
90 in attendance from 20 different denominations The team is currently looking at a
new site to begin another church. The team had the largest Christmas attendance ever
At the Christmas service, they had four baptisms of new adult believers.
Nagoya: This team also has selected and begun work in a second site. It will be located
somewhat near a new Toyota plant. They have already begun Bible studies and seeker

<a id="ga26-p475"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=477 printed_page=475 -->

groups in the area Like Chiba, they are also beginning to have significant college
outreach ministries.
Myanmar
we developed a working relationship with a former graduate o fr ts jackson to help
him grow a denomination, train leaders and do new church plants. Paul Taylor has been
into teach on several occasions. On one occasion, he took a Filipino along to be part of
the teaching team
Philippines
The team has taken an additional step in their training of nationals The first two
Filipinos have taken the next step in becoming training supervisors This will allow them
to fill some of the roles previously held by our missionaries. ina shift in leadership,
Richard Wolfe has taken over as Team Leader, leaving Paul Taylor to concentrate more
on his job as Regional Director for that area of the world As a result of that switch,
paul made a trip to thailand with a delegation from park cities in dallas from that
meeting, we are looking at a partnership for developing work on the Thailand/Laos
border
Taiwan
one of the board members, professor tj Tseng, was asked to step in as President and
serve at least on a temporary basis prof ts eng seems to be doing such a good job that
the board has asked him to continue for another two years. This has been a real
encouragement to the board and to the school

AFRICA/LATIN AMERICA HIGHLIGHTS
AFRICA / MIDDLE EAST

Abidjan. Cote d'Ivoire
While there have been some struggles as the national leaders in the original church plant
mature in leadership, the work continues to move ahead A third church plant began
worship services in 1997 and about 30 are in attendance
Ethiopia/Eritrea
Tensions are high, as mission agencies have not been able to renew visas The work
continues, but with no assurance as to how long It was necessary for MTW missionary
Lois Ooms to leave her assignment in Eritrea due to the authorities demanding that no
Christian witness be carried out

<a id="ga26-p476"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=478 printed_page=476 -->

Nairobi. Kenya
Karl and Debbie Dortzbach have established a study center for African church leaders
that will help them apply the gospel to the social issues that face much of Africa today.
This has created considerable enthusiasm in seminaries and training institutions in
Nairobi Tom Austin's Theological Training Center and John Shane's Urban Mission
Research Center are working to put all three ministries in one location for efficiency.
North Africa
After years of seconding MTW missionaries to approved cooperative agencies who are
specialists in Muslim ministries, Paul Meiners brought together some of our missionaries
who set forth a proposal for an MTW-only Muslim church-planting team The staff has
approved this and the team is forming presently to be placed in a sensitive country
sometime in 1998
Dakar. Senegal
An unplanned outreach to some villages outside Dakar has resulted in 48 people praying
to receive Christ Regular follow-up is taking place even though there is some
persecution of these new believers. In addition, a project for making and marketing
insecticide-treated mosquito nets is being started, both to aid the believers economically
and the villages health-wise
South Africa
MTW is partnering with four Reformed entities in South Af.ica to provide leadership,
church planting and missions training. Seminars were held in Johannesburg and Cape
Town to help mission pastors and leaders with preparing and overseeing the multitude of
mission candidates that exist today in South Africa. We will be aiding them with
assessment centers in 1998 In 1997, MTW placed five families in specialized training
institutions in South Africa Oliver Claassen, Regional Director for Southern Africa, has
been called to be principal of the bible institute of south africa - Cape Town, and will
add this to his other responsibilities. MTW is also helping a core group of churches
establish a new Presbyterian denomination.

Pakistan
a video on the birth and life of christ has been prepared for national viewing over a
major TV network. Millions will be exposed to the gospel though this media event One
of our missionaries has been asked to head the follow-up, which will be mainly by phone
or correspondence.

<a id="ga26-p477"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=479 printed_page=477 -->

LATIN AMERICA

Argentina
Henry Clay has seen tremendous response to his conferences on discipleship training
The areas of parenting and developing youth leaders have been well received. He is
writing for a widely circulated Christian magazine covering these topics.
Chile
It has been a difficult year for the Cristo Rey congregation The national pastor has been
disciplined by the presbytery and sued the church and the team, dividing the
congregation. The suit was settled and the pastor left the country Since that time, the
church has come back together, has purchased property, and is planning to build
Attendance that was down to about 180 is backup to 350 The San Marcos church, the
multi-cultural church plant, has called a pastor and is having a great impact on the
expatriate community of eastern Santiago The Renaca church purchased property and
has begun a building program Two other church plants are in process.
Colombia
Political tensions remain high with increased terrorist activities Movement is limited,
making church-planting activities difficult at times. Space is a major problem for both
congregations. The lack of team members is also a limiting factor One of the two
ordained Colombian men trained for leadership is dying of cancer Lack of jobs is a
struggle for many in the churches.
Ecuador
The Quito Presbytery is going through a time of adjusting to total national control
MTW has made two men, Roger Smalling and David Strumbeck, available to the
presbytery for theological education at the presbytery's request Tension has arisen
between several Ecuadorians who have trained at U.S. seminaries and have recently
returned, bringing with them an U.S. perspective on ministry The work in Guayaquil
has slowed somewhat because two families are on HMA.
Mexico
MTW and the national presbyterian church of mexico agreed on 22 new church plants
in 1997. At present there are 44 church-planting projects in process. This has
necessitated considerable emphasis on assessment for Mexican church planters and the
training courses and contracts to move men onto these fields. The MTW personnel have
been hard-pressed to carry out this training and mentoring. There has been some lack of
cooperation within a few presbyteries, but the commitment at most levels is strong The
church plants along the border are beginning to see substantial growth, which is
encouraging. The partnerships between PCA churches and specific church plants in
Mexico seem to be functioning well Only two church plants have been shut down due
to lack of response.

<a id="ga26-p478"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=480 printed_page=478 -->

Pern
The Surco Presbyterian Church was organized in the spring with two elders and three
deacons ordained Attendance continues at about 150 which leaves standing room only
in the church. Problems continue with the Cusco property and the schismatic group
trying to take it over A new church plant in the La Molina area of Lima will begin in
early 1998
HINTERLAND
Belize
Considerable effort has gone into actualizing the agreement between MTW and the
Presbyterian Church of Belize In addition to training and church revitalization projects,
IMPACT has opened a large program that will aid in the medical work as well as
outreach through local schools
Ecuador
Several new MTW families have moved into Chimborazo Quichua work
is in the process of setting up a training center in the Riobamba area. The Scharfs are in
the process of building a ministry center ina salas a ca community, but the response is
minimal
Peru
The Quechua church in Cusco continues to grow as the Adams minister faithfully Ruth
Gutierrez has had a significant ministry with upper class women in Huanta and
Ayacucho They have been ostracized by the Catholic church, yet continue with Ruth in
regular Bible studies Several families continue in training for ministries in tribal areas of
Peru

RECOMMENDATIONS
The Committee on Mission to the World makes the following recommendations to the
Twenty-Sixth General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America:
1 That the month of October be set aside as a month of prayer for world
evangelization and that God would send many more laborers to His harvest field.
2. That the General Assembly urge the churches to set aside a portion of their
giving for the suffering peoples of the world and that, to that end, it be
recommended that a special offering for relief and mercy needs around the world
be taken during 1999.
3. That the Committee report to General Assembly that the Coordinator received an
evaluation rating of excellence on his performance for his service during the past

<a id="ga26-p479"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=481 printed_page=479 -->

year and that CMTW reaffirms its enthusiastic support of Paul Kooistra as MTW
Coordinator.
4 That the proposed budget of MTW, as presented through the Administrative
Committee, be approved.
5 That the minutes of the meeting of March 6-7, 1997 be accepted
6. That the minutes of the meeting of October 2-3, 1997 be accepted.
SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT
ADDITIONAL RECOMMENDATIONS:
7. That the General Assembly instruct the Administrative Committee to consult with
all other committees and agencies within the PCA office building regarding space
needs and suitability of the present office building to meet the needs of each
committee and agency and report back to the 1999 General Assembly
8 That the General Assembly affirm that each committee or agency has the right to
determine it sown space needs in the PCA office building in light of it sown
ministry needs. And that General Assembly interpret the requirement that GA
committees and agencies stay in the PCA office building as including their ability
to use less or more space as available.
9. That the GA go on record as urging the Administrative Committee to give strong
consideration to the professional building study by the Staubach Company, which
concluded:
Our “bottom line” conclusion is as follows: After careful economic and
qualitative analysis of the available real estate solutions, a build-to-suit is the
lowest likely cost and highest qualitative value to the PCA.
10. That the rationale for the above motions be appropriately edited by the MTW
Administration and be attached to these minutes. That they be included in the
argument used before the 1998 Committee of Commissioners for CMTW.

CASE STATEMENT:
Why have these issues arisen on such short notice:
1. On May 20, 1998, a PCA Office Study meeting occurred and minutes were
taken. According to those minutes, based on specific questions raised by MTW
regarding process, only a preliminary report regarding the building studies would
go to the AC Committee when they meet at this 1998 General Assembly. After
General Assembly, on July 17, a formal building study presentation was to be
made to the PCA Capital Building Fund Committee at their regular meeting.
Actions were to be made at that meeting to be taken to the regular October 2-3,
AC Committee Meeting. Any actions to be taken to General Assembly would

<a id="ga26-p480"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=482 printed_page=480 -->

not have occurred until 1999, after a careful procedure of due process when each
of the agencies would have the opportunity to make their case.
2, This process abruptly changed when MTW was notified by a June 3 memo that a
special called telephone meeting of the Capital Building Fund Committee would
be held on June 12. The subject of the telephone meeting was to be the building
studies and M TW ’s notice of our intent to reduce space use in the PCA Building.
Our Coordinator was on a preplanned trip out of the country, so Jim McKee
represented MTW during the call He could represent M TW ’s position, but he
would not be allowed to vote (although, during the course of the telephone
meeting Jim was allowed to vote on only their third motion - given below).
3. MTW was surprised in two ways as a result of the meeting:
a First, the timetable was changed for making formal actions regarding the
building study, so that the AC Committee could take actions to this 1998
General Assembly Our ability to have orderly interaction with the Capital
Building Fund Committee and the AC Committee was thereby eliminated
The Capital Building Fund Committee apparently considered our summary
statement regarding the building studies problematic because our conclusions
differed from the other agencies and because it summarily restated issues we
have been continually open about which have never been fully addressed by
the Capital Building Fund Committee or by the AC Committee.
b Secondly, our space reduction letter apparently surprised the AC Office
because our two-month notification included rent reduction beginning August
1. Paul Gilchrist had told us on March 11 that our space reduction required a
two-month notice. We had understood the two-month notification as a rent
reduction delay. In apartment leases in Atlanta, for instance, a notification
requirement means giving at an appointed time the date for move out and
when rent will stop. We had done our virtual office (the reason why we are
able to reduce our space use) financial planning based on this understanding.
We had ongoing discussions with the AC office about our downsizing. Paul
Gilchrist had suggested floor plans that would allow good access to the
vacated space. When we were unable to provide the old access door to the
original AC office as part of the vacated space Paul Gilchrist negotiated with
MTW to try to find options. One of Paul’s comments to MTW at that time
was that if MTW gave up the entrance that had accessed the original AC
office he would not even need to notify the Capital Building Fund
Committee. MTW, because of egress dead-end issues, changed its
downsizing plans to give another entrance option. Because of these ongoing
downsizing discussions, MTW had no indication there was any problem with
our downsizing plans. Paul Gilchrist (we learned after the June 12, telephone
meeting) had intended to communicate the two-month notification as a time
frame so his office could begin advertising but with no intended financial

<a id="ga26-p481"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=483 printed_page=481 -->

implication. For MTW, the only reason we had asked about notification
requirements was for financial reasons. Had the AC office talked with us
when they received the letter on May 27, the misunderstanding could have
been amicably discussed. Instead our letter was presented to members of the
Capital Building Fund Committee without an opportunity for MTW to
respond. Because of the lost opportunity to interact, the Capital Building
Fund Committee apparently interpreted the actions as MTW trying to act in a
precipitous manner and trying to find creative ways to skirt around the 21st
GA action which prevents agencies from moving out of the PCA Building.
The Capital Building Fund Committee, because of the unresolved
misunderstanding, considered our notification letter, therefore, also
problematic.
4. During the unexpected telephone conference meeting the Capital Building
Fund Committee formulated the following three recommendations to the
AC Committee:
a. That we inform the AC of these facts about partial reduction of space by
MTW and suggest that the GA give clarification of the action in light of
the 21st GA Resolution.
b That the AC, with the responsibility to manage the PCA Office
Building, has been studying location, relocation, renovation, build-to-
suit possibilities of our present facilities during the past year. Presently,
the studies show that relocation or building new facilities are not good
options; however, the Administrative Committee through its Building
Committee will continue to exercise good stewardship by regular study
and evaluation of this matter.
c. That the background of the discussion with MTW regarding notice of
vacating space be investigated and that the Building Committee Officers
be authorized to report to the Administrative Committee.
The primary concern addressed by CM TW ’s first motion:
our primary desire is to delay discussion of this issue at the General Assembly level. We
feel it is unfortunate that this issue is being raised at the General Assembly that is
celebrating the twenty-fifth anniversary of the PCA. As explained above, we had been
clearly told that only a preliminary report on the building study was to go to AC at this
General Assembly and AC would not act on the reports until their October meeting.
Had the communicated schedule been followed, the issue, if ever reaching General Assembly level, would not have come until 1999. We believe the Capital Building Fund
Committee’s actions are precipitous. We want the time to allow orderly interaction with
the Capital Building Fund Committee and the AC Committee.

<a id="ga26-p482"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=484 printed_page=482 -->

The issue addressed by **CMTW’s second motion**: the purposes of MTW’s virtual office move (the “partial reduction of space” referred to in the first recommendation of the Capital Building Fund Committee above):

MTW had come to understand that we could move people out and reduce space use in the building as long as we did not pay rent elsewhere. This understanding grew out of discussions with individual Capital Building Fund Committee members and the AC Office never questioned the concept during months of interaction with them regarding virtual office and our downsizing plans.

1. The first primary purpose for MTW’s virtual office move is the growing difficulty of Atlanta’s traffic commute. The Atlanta metropolitan area [consisting of ten counties] has been the fastest growing for the last ten years. Over the last four years MTW’s center of gravity has shifted toward Suwanee on North 85 and the commute over those four years has lengthened from approximately 35 minutes to a time now close to an hour and a half during the school months. The traffic problem has been the number one news topic in the local papers and has been headline news in papers as far away as Miami, Florida. Every article assures us that if growth projections are correct the situation will only get worse as Atlanta has no coordinated plan to deal with the problem. The front-page headlines in *The Atlanta Journal-Constitution* of May 10, 1998, read “Can’t Get There From Here.” The heart of the article argues that there is no coordinated plan on the horizon, and no one is poised to take a leadership role in developing one. The health of the economy, the health of the real estate market, and the health of the citizens is in jeopardy. The article quotes Sam Williams, President of the Metro-Atlanta Chamber of Commerce, “There has to be acknowledgment that we have a crisis in transportation planning.” Mission to the World believes the problem has become a crisis for the ministry the PCA has entrusted to MTW as well.

There is no question that the problem is greater for MTW than the other committees and agencies because we hire more people and as new people are added to our work team they end up living further and further away from the office. Any organization to thrive has to hire the best people possible and that is already becoming more and more difficult. A year ago we had a missionary return from a foreign field who lived in one of the most congested cities on the planet. After coming and spending two weeks in Atlanta, he decided that he did not want to put his family through the isolation that is created with the separation that Atlanta’s urban sprawl creates.

Second and third level positions are becoming more and more difficult to recruit. MTW is not able to pay high salaries in order to attract second

<a id="ga26-p483"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=485 printed_page=483 -->

and third level personnel. Such personnel, in order to find adequate
housing at a reasonable price, are going to have to find housing which is
increasing beyond 35.5 miles which is now the average commute in
Atlanta (according to the Wall Street Journal, June 18, 1998) [the longest
commute in the United States]. Mission to the World has got to solve this
problem and one of the ways that we have instituted to meet this challenge
is the virtual office.
2. reduction of overhead cost was our second primary purpose. MTW
Senior Staff has made a commitment to PCA Churches and primarily to
MTW missionaries that it will work to find ways to reduce the
administrative fee to missionaries. The goal has been to reduce the
percentage from 15% to 10%. MTW has achieved a part of that goal.
MTW is hindered in its goal because of the rent level of the PCA Building
and because MTW is not allowed to move to available cheaper space.
Workspace should come after vision. The nature and vision of missions
does not support being in a building like the PCA Building. Missions and
the PCA could be better served if MTW could work out of a warehouse
type building with a small office front. PCA churches do not desire to
support a high missions administrative overhead. Their desire is to
support missionaries and the work carried out by those missionaries. They
want a mission’s administration to be cost effective, and to work
efficiently and effectively. In a concerned effort to reduce overhead cost
MTW made a conscious decision as part of the virtual office introduction
to find ways to use its space more efficiently so that less space could be
used and overhead cost could be reduced. We are not allowed to move to
suitable space to achieve greater cost reduction, so we used virtual office
as a way to partially reduce our space cost.
More information regarding CMTW ’s third motion:
As you can see from M TW ’s third recommended motion, the Capital Building Fund
Committee’s second recommendation does not address the conclusion of the study done
by The Staubach Company. Staubach offered, but has not yet been allowed, to interact
directly with the Capital Building Fund Committee regarding the study process and
conclusions. The Allen Morris Company also did a study. They concluded we should
stay in the present building; but, according to minutes taken on May 1, they did not have
the expertise to evaluate the cost of staying at the present location. MTW will bear the
largest portion of the needed renovation cost Staubach had the expertise to evaluate all
possible options. Morris Company’s qualitative analysis was a questionnaire, which
provided predictable results based on popular opinion. Staubach’s qualitative analysis
was a business approach, which was weighted by the relative value of each rated quality

<a id="ga26-p484"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=486 printed_page=484 -->

and according to the amount of space and number of employees of each agency. The
Capital Building Fund Committee apparently did not agree with Staubach’s conclusion
because MTW’s point of view was given extra weight due to size. According to minutes
taken during their June 12th Conference Call meeting, the Capital Building Fund
Committee also questioned many assumptions in the Staubach study, but did not interact
with Staubach to understand why these assumptions were used. In order to receive the
full benefit of the professional study done by Staubach, the Capital Building Fund
Committee and the Administrative Committee should extend the courtesy to Staubach of
allowing them to answer fully and directly all of their questions.
Further background on MTW’s proposed action statements:
MTW’s stake in the building decisions is vital. Yet, MTW has not been given “its day in
court,” so to speak. M TW ’s ability to be stewards of its own human and financial
resources is being prevented by forces to which we feel we have not been given proper
access. MTW, under the current arrangement, does not have the independence needed
or the control over building systems necessary to control its own destiny and to
successfully carry out the mandates placed upon it by General Assembly
The agencies in the PCA Building have no participation in the ownership of the building.
The agencies also are not treated as tenants because we have no written lease agreement
(the rules about our rights or options are addressed only as a situation arises), and
because apparently we cannot shrink or move, as a normal tenant would be allowed to
do. The present system establishes rent without any input from the agencies, which must
pay the rent.
MTW does not know what the rights or options of the committees and agencies are in
the PCA Building. We could give specific examples, but we will state the problem in
general terms instead The AC Committee is acting under the General Assembly
directive as the PCA Building Managers. The AC Committee has delegated building
management to the Capital Building Fund Committee, also allowed by the General Assembly (although it was to be a committee separate from the Capital Building Fund
Committee - COA notes to the financial statements, June 30, 1987). In the day-to-day
operations the AC Office represents the committees and performs the delegated
management functions. Normally one would expect that when a policy question is asked
or if policy issues arise, an agency could go to the AC Office or members of the Capital
Building Fund Committee for policy statements and interpretation. In M TW ’s
experience, the policy statements from the AC Office or members of the Capital Building
fund committee have not agreed with the final word of the capital building fund
Committee as a whole. And, by the time the final word is given, stewardship decisions
have already been long in process. Unless the committees and agencies in the PCA
Building know policy clearly, it is very difficult for them to make intelligent and wise
decisions regarding their space use in the PCA Building.

<a id="ga26-p485"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=487 printed_page=485 -->

The prior paragraph illustrates the other reason why we want to delay any building
decisions until the 1999 General Assembly. We are hopeful that we can sit down with
the Capital Building Fund Committee and the AC Committee to come to some
agreement, which addresses the rights in the PCA Building of MTW and the other
agencies

Respectfully submitted,
/s/ Robert V. Massengill, Chairman
Mission to the World

<a id="ga26-p486"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=488 printed_page=486 -->

MINUTES OF THE NOMINATIONS COMMITTEE
OF THE TWENTY-SIXTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

The General Assembly's Nominating Committee convened in Atlanta, Georgia
at the Holiday Inn Airport North on March 28, 1998, The meeting was called to order
at 9 am with the reading of God’s Word as recorded in Deut 1:9-13 and Acts 6:2-3
Prayer was offered by TE Don Clements, Chairman The roll was taken by the
Chairman and the committee members present with their Presbytery and class follows:
Ascension TE Nick Protos (2000)
Calvary RE John Armstrong (2000)
Central Carolina TE Dewey Murphy (99)
Central Georgia RE Donald Comer (99)
Covenant TE Laurie Jones (2000)
Fellowship TE Scott Hill (2000)
Grace te md conn or (99)
Great Lakes RE Will Billings (99)
Heartland TE Phillip Kayser (99)
Heritage RE John Haldeman (2000)
James River TE Harry Long (2000)
Louisiana RE Volney Pierce (98)
Metro NYC TE Allan Barth (1999)
Mississippi Valley RE J. Edmund Johnston (99)
Missouri RE Mark Belz (99)
Nashville RE Elrod H Camp, III (2000)
New Jersey RE Robert Berry, Jr. (98)
New River TE Don K. Clements (98)
North Georgia TE John Batusic (98)
Northern California TE Lewis Ruff (98)
Pacific RE Robert Taylor(99)
Philadelphia RE Kenneth W Rush (98)
Pittsburgh RE Howard Donahoe (98)
Potomac RE Calvin Clark (98)
Rocky Mountain RE Bill Nardin (2000)
South Texas TE William Fitzhenry (99)
Southeast Alabama RE Irv Wicker (99)
Southern Florida RE Gordon Frost (98)
Southwest Florida TE John Graham (2000)
Tennessee Valley RE Roger Ingvalson (2000)

<a id="ga26-p492"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=494 printed_page=492 -->

Warrior TE Tom Kay, Jr. (2000)
Westminster TE G. Brent Bradley (99)
Dr Paul Gilchrist, Stated Clerk of the General Assembly, was present and acted
as a resource person to the Committee.
A packet of material was distributed to each member which included:
• Agenda for the meeting
• “Errata sheet” to update the Rainbow Book.
• Letters of recommendation concerning particular nominees from:
Covenant College, Covenant Seminary, PCA Foundation.
• Draft copy of the Mamma! of operations for the committee prepared by the
Ad Hoc Committee formed in 1997 for that purpose.
• An Overture submitted by the New River Presbytery and assigned to the
Nominating Committee and Bills & Overtures by the Stated Clerk
A Motion was made: “To modify the agenda by allowing the Committee to
recess for 30 minutes (from 10 a.m. to 10:30 a.m.) so that the Committee members, who
wish to do so, may reflect on the letters of recommendations concerning particular
nominees from the Agencies (as provided for in the PCA Corporate Bylaws) prior to
being dismissed into subcommittees,” Seconded.
An Amendment was made: “To discard the ‘raw vote’ tally sheets for
Covenant College, Covenant Seminary, and PCA Foundation that would be considered
in the subcommittee meetings,” Seconded. Amendment FAILED, Main Motion
Carried
A special subcommittee to review the Overture, at lunch, and report back to the
Committee was formed as follows: RE E Johnson, Chairman, RE P. Berry, TE P
Kayser, and TE B. Fitzhenry.
The Committee was divided into four subcommittees to review the submitted
nominees, evaluate the “raw votes” of the entire Committee and to report their
recommendations to the Committee. The assignments for the subcommittees were as
follows:
• RE Gordon Frost, Chairman
Mission to North America
Ridge Haven
Constitutional Business
• TE John Batusic, Chairman
Christian Education & Publications
Covenant Seminary
Interchurch Relations

<a id="ga26-p493"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=495 printed_page=493 -->

• TE Lewis A. Ruff, Jr., Chairman
Administrative Committee
Standing Judicial Commission
PCA Foundation
Theological Examining Committee
• RE Howard Donahoe, Chairman
Mission to the World
Covenant College
Insurance, Annuities and Relief

A tally sheet to count the “raw votes” was presented by RE H. Donahoe for use
by the subcommittees. The Committee was recessed for 30 minutes, at 9:50 a.m., in
prayer by TE J. Batusic. The subcommittees commenced their work at 10:20 a m.
The Committee reconvened at 11:45 am with a season of prayer and then
recessed for lunch at 12:05 p.m. with closing prayer by TE L. Ruff.
The Committee reconvened at 1:05 p.m. with prayer by TE L. Jones. Each
subcommittee presented the nominees for their assigned Committees and Agencies.
Each individual Committee and Board slate was considered, discussed and voted on by
the Committee The subcommittee reports concluded at 3:20 p.m. The resulting
nominations and the nominee’s biographical summaries are attached.
It was agreed, by consensus, that the Committee would discuss the draft copy of
the Manual of Operations at the next meeting of the Committee at the General Assembly. Since the Ad Hoc Committee had further amended the current copy, the
latest revision will be mailed to members of the Committee for their review prior to the
General Assembly.
The subcommittee to review the submitted Overture presented their
recommendation to ACCEPT [2-1-1], After discussion, the Committee REJECTED the
Overture [4-21-0] and requested the Stated Clerk to communicate this to the Bills &
Overtures Committee.
a resolution was presented b yr eh dona hoe that commends and thanks Mrs.
laurel d ebert and her staff for their labors of love in support of the work of the church
of the lord jesus christ in the many preparations required of the nominating
Committee. ADOPTED UNANIMOUSLY.

<a id="ga26-p494"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=496 printed_page=494 -->

RESOLUTION

WHEREAS, preparations for the Spring Stated Meeting of the Nominating Committee of the PCA entail an extensive amount of paper work, including gathering and examining of forms for accuracy, and the production of the Rainbow Book which calls for extensive copying and collating and ultimately mailing to more than 40 members, and

WHEREAS, preparations for the Spring Stated Meeting of the Nominating Committee of the PCA are always crammed into a very brief space of time, usually conflicting with other important work such as preparations for meetings of the Standing Judicial Commission and the Administrative Committee, and

WHEREAS, preparations for the Spring Stated Meeting of the Nominating Committee of the PCA require an unusual degree of flexibility in planning hotel and travel requirements for such a large committee with frequent turnover of membership, and

WHEREAS, year in and year out, the administrative staff of the Office of the Stated Clerk, and in particular, the lead Administrative Assistant, Mrs. Laurel De Bert, has performed these complex and hectic tasks with diligence and cheerfulness,

THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED that the members of the 1997-1998 Nominating Committee, meeting in Atlanta, Georgia, on Saturday, March 28, 1998, do hereby UNANIMOUSLY commend and thank Mrs. Laurel De Bert and her staff for their labors of love in support of the work of the Church of the Lord Jesus Christ.

/s/ Attested: DON K. CLEMENTS, Chairman

The Committee elected RE Robert Taylor of Pacific Presbytery as Chairman and TE Harry Long of James River Presbytery as Secretary of the 1999 General Assembly Nominating Committee.

By consensus, the Committee will meet at General Assembly on Tuesday, June 30, 1998, at 10 a.m., to discuss and adopt the Manual of Operations and review any vacancies on committees or agency boards that have occurred since the spring meeting. There will be a meeting of the Committee on Wednesday, July 1, 1998, at the call of the Chairman, to consider the names of the nominees from the floor and their eligibility.

The Committee adjourned with prayer by RE R. Taylor at 3:50 p.m.

Respectfully submitted,

/s/ TE Don Clements, Chairman

/s/ RE Robert Taylor, Secretary

<a id="ga26-p495"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=497 printed_page=495 -->

ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE
A. Present Personnel
Teaching Elders: Ruling Elders:
class of 2001
William C. Hughes, MS Valley Charles Bobyack, W. Carolina
Class of 2000
George C. Fuller, New Jersey Ralph Mittendorff, S. Florida
Timothy Diehl, Heartland
class of 1999
G. Fredric Mau, Covenant Edwin Hackenberg, SE Louisiana
G. Paul Jones, C. Georgia
Class of 1998
William A. Fox, Jr., Fellowship Howard Q. Davis, Covenant
D. Steven Meyerhoff, Westminster
Alternates
J Allen Herrington, E. Carolina William (“Bingy”) Moore, Potomac

B. To be Elected:
class of 2002
Two TE Primaries and One TE Alternate
AND One RE Primary and One RE Alternate
C. Nominations:
Class of 2002
J. Allen Herrington, E. Carolina William (“Bingy”) Moore, Potomac
Robert S. Homick, Gulf Coast
Alternates
John P. Jerguson, W. Carolina Hugh Potts, Jr., MS Valley
D. Biographical Sketches:
TE J. Allen Herrington: Eastern Carolina. executive director of presbyterian
Evangelistic Fellowship; has served as principal on the Ridge Haven Board and,
at presbytery level, on MNA, MTW, COA, and CE Committees.
TE Robert S. Homick: gulf coast pastor of warring ton PC, Pensacola, FL Has
served as alternate (1991) and principal (1992-1996) on Administrative
Committee. Serves, since 1987, as Stated Clerk and Chairman of Examination
Co. at Presbytery.

<a id="ga26-p496"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=498 printed_page=496 -->

TE John P. Jerguson: Western. Carolina. Pastor of Dillingham PC, Barnardsville, NC.
Prior to ordination, worked as Accountant and DP Manager for Co. on
Administration (1982-1985). Served on Bills & Overtures and Co. of
Commissioners for AC. Served on many Presbytery committees
RE William H. (Bingy) Moore: Potomac. Has served six years as alternate or principal
on the Administrative Co., and as chairman of the Finance Subcommittee of AC,
and on the Co. of Commissioners for AC 1990 and for B&O 1997; at presbytery
level, has served as Moderator in 1990 and again in 1998 and on numerous
committees including Admin. Committee and Credentials Committee.
RE Hugh Potts, jr : Mississippi Valley. ceo of a $60,000,000 Company with over
250 associates. Served on various community and state wide boards. Most
willing to use his gifts of administration and business expertise to further the
kingdom of God.

C Nominations:
Class of 2002
Don K. Clements, New River Jeff Hall, TN Valley
Rod S. Mays, Calvary Jeff Oxman, Pacific
Alternates
Randy Kimbrough, Grace William R. Stanway, Grace

D. Biographical Sketches:
TE Don K. Clements: New River Pastor of Valley PC, Narrows, VA. Has served on
CE&P (1975-79; 1986-90) with terms as chairman and vice chairman Served
two terms on Board of Directors, Great Commission Publications, two years as
President. Certified Trainer with Evangelical Teacher Training Association.
D.Min Gordon-Conwell; thesis project in Adult Education.
RE Jeff Hall: Tennessee Valley. Currently employed as Director of the Master of
Education Program at Covenant College. Is a certified elementary teacher and
school administrator and has served on committees involving governance and
curriculum at the local, national and international level. Active in curriculum
development in the local Christian day school.
TE Randy Kimbrough: Grace. Pastor of Mount Olive PC and Hopewell PC, Mt. Olive,
MS. Chairman of a presbytery co. BA degree in Journalism and published in
World Magazine . Was on Board of PCA Foundation and serves on Mississippi
Joint Commission on Campus Work.
TE Rod Mays: Calvary. Sr. Pastor, W oodruff PCA, Greenville, SC; has served as
principal on Constitutional Business, Task Force on Campus Ministry, and
MNA (Co-Opt subcommittee on Campus Ministry), at presbytery level, has
served as moderator and on MNA and Campus Ministry.
RE Jeff Oxman: Pacific. Self employed investment advisor, at presbytery level, has
served as principal and chairman of administrative Co. and on Judicial
Commission and Commission as Interim Session, has written and published a
textbook and is currently writing another book, teaches young marrieds and
leads a small group Bible Study.
RE William R. Stanway: Grace. A certified public accountant for 30 years. Taught
Sunday School, is a member of th ec e Co. and Clerk of Session at church
member of board of christian school in Hattiesburg, MS. Commissioner to 4 of
the last 6 GAs.

## C. Nominations:

**Class of 2002**

William T. Iverson, S. Florida

**Class of 2002**

John B. Findlay, Jr., Gulf Coast
Sam Elder, Potomac

## D. Biographical Sketches:

RE Sam Elder: Potomac. Physics professor, US Naval Academy. Served on Presbytery MNA Co. (since 1983). Founding member of church and active in church planting by serving on a borrowed session. Helps recruit church planters and organize core groups.

<a id="ga26-p499"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=501 printed_page=499 -->

TE John Findlay: Gulf Coast Pastor of Pinewoods PC, Cantonment, FL. In seminary,
worked with a co regroup to help plant a church in Louisville, KY, which grew
to 65 members in two years During final year at seminary, helped a church
plant in Clinton, MS. Served as organizing pastor of Pinewoods and during the
last 20 years planted 2 daughter churches Served on Presbytery MNA Co.
RE John Jardine, Jr Heritage Retired CEO of a small utility company which serves
parts of Delaware, Maryland and Florida. until recently he was chairman of the
board for a local college. Served as a principal on the committee (1992-96)
served as chairman of presbytery MNA co
TE Michael L Khandjian: Southern Florida Pastor of Old Cutler PC, Miami, FL.
Became youth minister of Old Cutler PC upon graduation (1983) Served 10
years at a Tallahassee church and the membership grew from 40 to 500
members. Moved back to Old Cutler in 1996 as senior pastor The church is
overseeing an Hispanic plant in Little Havana
TE William T. Iverson: Southern Florida. Evangelist, Education, Church Planter with
PEF, Daniel Iverson Center for Christian Studies, has served on MNA, MTW,
IE, and CE at presbytery level, has a heart for evangelism and church planting,
urban and transcultural experience coupled with a theological approach to the
Great Commission; has planted black and Hispanic churches and helped plant
15 in all; lived among minorities and in the urban scene for 34 years, presently
working with 4 new church plants.
RE Fred Schumpert: Tennessee Valley. Sales. Served Presbytery MNA Co for 10
years and chaired for 3 years Worked with Presbytery coordinator to help plant
churches (5 years) Assists in Presbytery work with RUM at several colleges
Served with MTW/IMPACT in short term overseas missions, training nationals

Ralph L. Ruth, Philadelphia

## Alternates

Whaley (Bill) Barton, Sr., Palmetto
Donald B. Blackburn, C. Georgia

## D. Biographical Sketches:

TE Whaley (Bill) Barton, Jr.: Palmetto. Pastor of St. Andrews PC, Irmo, SC. Initiated active mission program as senior pastor, encouraging all age groups to visit mission fields. Serves as Associate Pastor-at-Large with MTW to pastor missionaries. Served as Moderator of Presbytery and on several committees.

RE Donald B. Blackburn: Central Georgia. Works for factory sales agency. Has served on church MTW Co. (12 years) and chaired it for 8 years. Has been on several mission trips to visit and work with missionaries.

RE Robert A. Caldwell: Calvary. Building contractor. As co-owner, has flexibility to serve church and presbytery. Chairman of Missions Committee at church (12 years). Over the past 13 years, led ten short term missions projects varying from 2 weeks to 13 months. Serves on Presbytery MTW Co.

TE S. Michael Preg, Jr.: Missouri. Executive Minister of Kirk of the Hills, St. Louis, MO. Served as principal of IAR (1987-91); GA Nominating Co. (1986-87), 5-in-5 Loan Co. (1979-82), and chairman of several committees of commissioners. Served on several Presbytery committees. Instituted mission conferences at several churches. Staff resource to missions committee at Kirk of the Hills.

RE Ralph I. Ruth: Philadelphia. Business owner - wholesale and retail distributor; has served presbytery on MTW and Licensure Co.; instrumental in founding 2 PCA churches and served as an initial RE in each; concern for missions since coming to know the Lord; served as missions coordinator for 30 years in 2 different churches; has supported missionaries for 32 years; instrumental in founding 3 new businesses.

<a id="ga26-p501"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=26 pdf_page=503 printed_page=501 -->

TE James H. Stewart: Mississippi Valley. Assistant Pastor of First PC, Jackson, MS. Serves as Minister of Missions, taking a summer mission team to the field each year; chairman of Presbytery MTW Co. Served as a MTW missionary (11 years) as church planting leader in Taiwan. Was MTW missionary in Hong Kong (4 years). Also teaching at Chinese Seminary and RTS. Doctoral candidate for Doctor of Mississippi degree.

## C. Nominations:
